topics.title: The Front End
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Front End

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compiler Front End (KTU Definition):** The *front end* of a compiler comprises all the analysis phases of compilation — namely **Lexical Analysis**, **Syntax Analysis**, **Semantic Analysis**, and **Intermediate Code Generation** — that operate on the **source program** to produce an *intermediate representation (IR)* along with auxiliary structures like the **Symbol Table**. The front end is largely *machine-independent* and is concerned only with understanding the source language.

Mathematically, the front end can be viewed as a mapping:

$$ \text{FrontEnd}: \text{Source Program (String over } \Sigma) \longrightarrow \text{Intermediate Representation} $$

where $\Sigma$ is the alphabet (token alphabet) of the source language. The front end is built upon the *theory of formal languages* — the **Chomsky Hierarchy** — where regular languages govern the lexical level, and **Context-Free Grammars (CFGs)** govern the syntactic level.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In Module 1, the front end is treated as the *analysis half* of the compiler. The back end (covered in later modules) handles **optimization** and **code generation**. The boundary between the two halves is the **Intermediate Representation (IR)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multilingual translator standing at a United Nations podium**:
- The **front end** is like the translator *listening and understanding* a speech in French — they parse the words, check the grammar, verify that nouns and verbs agree, and then jot down the meaning in a structured **"neutral summary" (the IR)**.
- The **back end** is then another translator who takes that neutral summary and *re-writes it in English* (target code) in a polished, optimized form.

The translator's job (front end) is to **comprehend** the input — to extract tokens, build a sentence structure (parse tree), check meaning (semantics), and produce a structured note (IR). The quality of translation depends entirely on the front end's correctness.

In short:
- **Front End = "Understand what the programmer wrote"**
- **Back End = "Translate that understanding into machine code"**

### 1.3 The Four Phases of the Front End

| # | Phase | Input | Output | Key Data Structure |
|---|-------|-------|--------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Lexical Analysis** | Character stream | Token stream | DFA, Transition Diagram |
| 2 | **Syntax Analysis** | Token stream | Parse Tree / AST | CFG, Pushdown Automaton |
| 3 | **Semantic Analysis** | Parse Tree + Symbol Table | Annotated Tree / IR | Symbol Table, Type Records |
| 4 | **Intermediate Code Generation** | Annotated Tree | Three-Address Code (TAC) | SDT, Directed Acyclic Graph |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Symbol Table** is a *central data structure* that is built and used *across all four phases*. It is the "memory" of the front end — storing identifiers, their types, scopes, and memory addresses.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

- **Token**: A symbolic name for a category of lexemes (e.g., `id`, `num`, `keyword`).
- **Lexeme**: The actual character sequence matched by a token's pattern (e.g., `count`, `42`).
- **Pattern**: The rule that describes the set of lexemes for a token (usually a regular expression).
- **Parse Tree**: A tree showing how a string is derived from a grammar's start symbol.
- **Syntax Tree (AST)**: A compressed parse tree representing only the essential syntactic structure.
- **Ambiguity**: A grammar property where a string has *more than one* parse tree — a critical issue the front end must resolve.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis (Scanning)

The **lexical analyzer** (also called the *scanner* or *tokenizer*) reads the input source code character by character and groups them into **lexemes**, producing a stream of **tokens** as output.

**Why is it separated from syntax analysis?**
- **Simplicity of design** — separates concerns.
- **Efficiency** — specialized buffering and pattern matching techniques (DFA) make scanning very fast.
- **Portability** — input alphabet peculiarities (line endings, escape sequences) are localized here.
- **Token standardization** — simplifies the parser's input.

**Token Attributes:**
Each token returned by the scanner has the form:
$$ \text{Token} = \langle \text{token\_name}, \text{attribute\_value} \rangle $$
For example, the lexeme `count` yields `<id, 3>`, where `3` is a pointer to the symbol-table entry for `count`.

**Steps Inside the Scanner:**

1. **Buffering input** (sentinels) to avoid costly I/O per character.
2. **Pattern matching** — the lexeme is matched against a set of regular expressions.
3. **Longest-match + Rule-priority** — the longest prefix matching any pattern wins; ties are broken by rule order.
4. **Token emission** — the chosen pattern's token is emitted with its attribute.
5. **Symbol table interaction** — identifiers are entered/looked up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tokens for an expression like `score = marks + 10;`** typically map as:
> `<id, score>` → `<assign_op, ->` → `<id, marks>` → `<plus_op, ->` → `<num, 10>` → `<semicolon, ->`

**Lexical Errors & Recovery:**
- The scanner cannot fix errors — it can only *detect* them.
- Recovery strategies: **panic mode** (delete characters until a valid token is found), **insert missing character**, **replace a character**, **transpose adjacent characters**.

### 2.2 Phase 2 — Syntax Analysis (Parsing)

The **parser** receives the token stream from the scanner and verifies that the sequence can be generated by the source language's **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**. The output is a **parse tree** (or **AST**).

**Why CFGs?** Regular expressions (used in scanning) cannot express *nested structures* (e.g., `if ... then if ... then ... else ... end end`). CFGs handle recursion and balanced parentheses.

**A Context-Free Grammar (CFG) $G$ is the 4-tuple:**
$$ G = (V, T, P, S) $$
where:
- $V$ = finite set of *non-terminals* (variables).
- $T$ = finite set of *terminals* (tokens).
- $P$ = finite set of *productions* of the form $A \to \alpha$ where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^*$.
- $S$ = *start symbol* in $V$.

**Derivation:** A sequence of production applications rewriting the start symbol into the input string. Two styles:
- **Leftmost derivation** — always rewrites the leftmost non-terminal first.
- **Rightmost derivation** — always rewrites the rightmost non-terminal first (canonical form).

**Parse Tree:** A graphical depiction of a derivation; root = $S$, leaves = terminals in input order.

**Ambiguity:** A grammar is ambiguous if some string has two or more distinct parse trees. KTU 2024 expects you to *detect* and *resolve* ambiguity via:
- Rewriting with precedence/associativity rules.
- Introducing new non-terminals.
- Using *dangling-else* resolution (matching `else` with the nearest `if`).

**Two Major Parsing Strategies:**

| Strategy | Class | Driver | Direction |
|----------|-------|--------|-----------|
| **Top-Down** | LL(1), Recursive Descent | Predict next production | Leftmost derivation |
| **Bottom-Up** | LR(0), SLR(1), LALR(1), CLR(1) | Shift-Reduce | Reverse rightmost derivation |

> [!NOTE]
> The parser uses the **Symbol Table** to look up identifiers and their attributes, and consults the **SDT scheme** to invoke *semantic actions* during parsing.

### 2.3 Phase 3 — Semantic Analysis

The **semantic analyzer** uses the parse tree (or AST) and the **Symbol Table** to perform:
- **Type checking** — verifies that operands of operators are type-compatible.
- **Type coercion / implicit conversion** — e.g., `int + float` → `float + float`.
- **Scope and visibility resolution** — determines which declaration of a name is in force.
- **Uniqueness checks** — variable redeclaration, label uniqueness.
- **Constant folding** — e.g., `2 + 3` → `5` at compile time.

It produces a *typed* or *annotated* tree (or directly emits IR using a *Syntax-Directed Translation (SDT) scheme*).

**Key Concept — Type System:**
A type system is a collection of rules for assigning type expressions to the parts of a program. **Type checking** can be:
- **Static** — at compile time (KTU focus).
- **Dynamic** — at run time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The semantic analyzer handles the constructs that are *syntactically valid* but *semantically meaningless* — e.g., using a variable before declaration, adding a string to a boolean, etc.

### 2.4 Phase 4 — Intermediate Code Generation

After semantic analysis, the front end produces an **Intermediate Representation (IR)** — a machine-independent code that the back end later translates to target machine code. The most common IR is **Three-Address Code (TAC)**.

**Three-Address Code (TAC) properties:**
- Each instruction has **at most three operands**.
- Typical forms:
  - $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$   (binary operation)
  - $x = \text{op}\; y$        (unary operation)
  - $x = y$                    (copy)
  - `goto L`                   (unconditional jump)
  - `if x goto L` / `ifFalse x goto L` (conditional jump)
  - L:                         (label)

> [!NOTE]
> TAC is *easy to generate*, *easy to optimize*, and *easy to translate* to target code. KTU 2024 places heavy emphasis on **TAC generation for expressions, control flow, and procedure calls** (covered in Module 1 and revisited in Module 4).

**Other IR Forms:**
- **Postfix notation** — useful for stack-based evaluation.
- **Syntax tree (AST)** — already a useful IR.
- **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** — a *compressed* AST where common subexpressions are shared; enables **common subexpression elimination**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---------|----------------|-------|
| **Token** | $\text{Token} = \langle \text{name}, \text{attribute} \rangle$ | attribute is a symbol-table pointer or numeric value |
| **Pattern for identifier** | $\text{letter} \; (\text{letter} \;\vert\; \text{digit})^*$ | Most languages use this regex |
| **CFG** | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | $V$ = non-terminals, $T$ = terminals |
| **Derivation step** | $\alpha A \beta \Rightarrow \alpha \gamma \beta$ using $A \to \gamma$ | $\Rightarrow^*$ = zero or more steps |
| **Language of $G$** | $L(G) = \{w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w\}$ | Sentential form if $w$ contains non-terminals |
| **Left Recursion** | $A \to A\alpha$ | Must be **eliminated** for top-down parsing |
| **Left Factoring** | $A \to \alpha\beta_1 \;\vert\; \alpha\beta_2$ → $A \to \alpha A'$, $A' \to \beta_1 \vert \beta_2$ | Used to delay choice in LL parsers |
| **FIRST$(\alpha)$** | Set of terminals that begin strings derivable from $\alpha$ | Computed iteratively |
| **FOLLOW$(A)$** | Set of terminals that can appear immediately after $A$ in some sentential form | Add `\$` for start symbol |
| **LL(1) condition** | For any $A \to \alpha \vert \beta$, FIRST$(\alpha) \cap$ FIRST$(\beta) = \emptyset$ | Predictive parsing possible |
| **Predictive Parsing Table entry $M[A, a]$** | $A \to \alpha$ if $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, or $A \to \alpha$ if $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ and $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Unique entry ⇒ LL(1) |
| **LR(0) item** | $A \to \alpha \cdot \beta$ | Dot indicates parser position |
| **Closure of item set $I$** | Add $B \to \cdot \gamma$ for every non-terminal $B$ appearing immediately after a dot | Standard construction |
| **GOTO$(I, X)$** | Move dot past $X$ in each item of $I$, then take closure | Used in building LR DFA |
| **SLR(1) reduction** | Reduce $A \to \alpha$ on input $a$ only if $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Resolves LR(0) conflicts |
| **TAC for `a + b * c`** | $t_1 = b * c; \quad t_2 = a + t_1$ | $t_1, t_2$ are temporaries |

> [!WARNING]
> For LL(1) tables, an entry must be **unique**. A *multiple-entry* cell ⇒ the grammar is **NOT LL(1)**. Many students confuse "left recursion" with "ambiguity" — they are *separate* properties. A grammar can be unambiguous yet non-LL(1).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **front end is the language-processing heart** of every modern toolchain:

- **GCC, Clang, MSVC** — all have front ends (`gcc - frontc`, `clang - clangFrontend`) that read C/C++/Rust/etc. and emit LLVM IR or GIMPLE.
- **IDE tooling** — IntelliSense, autocomplete, syntax highlighters, and linters are essentially *lightweight compiler front ends*.
- **Static analyzers** (SonarQube, Coverity) — front-end-driven.
- **Transpilers** (Babel for JS, TS compiler) — use a full front end to translate modern code to older dialects.
- **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs)** in compiler construction, hardware design (Verilog → VHDL converters), and database query optimization.

The **LLVM IR**, an industry-standard IR used by Apple, Google, and Intel, embodies exactly the **front-end → IR → back-end** architecture described in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — FIRST and FOLLOW Sets (for LL(1) Construction)

Consider the grammar $G$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\to T E' \\
E' &\to + T E' \;\vert\; \epsilon \\
T &\to F T' \\
T' &\to * F T' \;\vert\; \epsilon \\
F &\to ( E ) \;\vert\; \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

We want to construct the LL(1) parsing table.

**Step A — Compute FIRST sets for all non-terminals:**

**Rule 1:** If $X$ is a terminal, $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{X\}$.
**Rule 2:** If $X \to \epsilon$ is a production, add $\epsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
**Rule 3:** If $X \to Y_1 Y_2 \ldots Y_k$, add $\text{FIRST}(Y_1)$ (excluding $\epsilon$) to $\text{FIRST}(X)$. If $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1)$, add $\text{FIRST}(Y_2)$, and so on. If all $Y_i$ are nullable, add $\epsilon$.

Iterative fix-point application:

- $F$: $F \to (E) \to (E)$ adds `(`, and $F \to \text{id}$ adds `id`. So **FIRST$(F) = \{(\,,\, \text{id}\}$**.
- $T'$: $T' \to * F T'$ adds `*`; $T' \to \epsilon$ adds $\epsilon$. So **FIRST$(T') = \{*,\, \epsilon\}$**.
- $T$: $T \to F T'$. $\text{FIRST}(F) = \{(\,,\,\text{id}\}$, none are $\epsilon$, so FIRST$(T)$ inherits these. **FIRST$(T) = \{(\,,\, \text{id}\}$**.
- $E'$: $E' \to + T E'$ adds `+`; $E' \to \epsilon$ adds $\epsilon$. **FIRST$(E') = \{+,\, \epsilon\}$**.
- $E$: $E \to T E'$. $\text{FIRST}(T) = \{(\,,\,\text{id}\}$, no $\epsilon$, so **FIRST$(E) = \{(\,,\, \text{id}\}$**.

**Step B — Compute FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals:**

**Rule 1:** Put `\$` in FOLLOW$(S)$ (start symbol).
**Rule 2:** If $A \to \alpha B \beta$, then everything in FIRST$(\beta)$ (except $\epsilon$) is in FOLLOW$(B)$.
**Rule 3:** If $A \to \alpha B$, or $A \to \alpha B \beta$ with $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then everything in FOLLOW$(A)$ is in FOLLOW$(B)$.

Iterating:

- FOLLOW$(E)$: $E$ is start symbol → add `\$`. **FOLLOW$(E) = \{\$\,,\,)\}$**.
- FOLLOW$(E')$: $E \to T E'$, so FOLLOW$(E) \subseteq$ FOLLOW$(E')$. **FOLLOW$(E') = \{\$\,,\,)\}$**.
- FOLLOW$(T)$: $E' \to + T E'$, so FIRST$(E') = \{+,\epsilon\}$. Add `+`. Since $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$, also add FOLLOW$(E')$. **FOLLOW$(T) = \{+\,,\,\$\,,\,)\}$**.
- FOLLOW$(T')$: $T \to F T'$, so FIRST$(T') = \{*,\epsilon\}$. Add `*`. Since $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$, add FOLLOW$(T)$. **FOLLOW$(T') = \{*\,,\,+\,,\,\$\,,\,)\}$**.
- FOLLOW$(F)$: $T' \to * F T'$, so FIRST$(T') = \{*,\epsilon\}$. Add `*`. Since $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$, add FOLLOW$(T')$. **FOLLOW$(F) = \{*\,,\,+\,,\,\$\,,\,)\}$**.

**Step C — Construct LL(1) parsing table $M$ using:**

$M[A, a] = A \to \alpha$ if $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, else if $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ and $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, then $M[A, a] = A \to \epsilon$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M[E, (\,]   &= E \to T E' \quad ((\, \in \text{FIRST}(T E')) \\
M[E, \text{id}] &= E \to T E' \quad (\text{id} \in \text{FIRST}(T E')) \\
M[E', +]    &= E' \to + T E' \quad (+ \in \text{FIRST}(+ T E')) \\
M[E', )]    &= E' \to \epsilon \quad (\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\epsilon),\; ) \in \text{FOLLOW}(E')) \\
M[E', \$]   &= E' \to \epsilon \\
M[T, (]     &= T \to F T' \\
M[T, \text{id}] &= T \to F T' \\
M[T', *]    &= T' \to * F T' \\
M[T', +]    &= T' \to \epsilon \\
M[T', )]    &= T' \to \epsilon \\
M[T', \$]   &= T' \to \epsilon \\
M[F, (]     &= F \to ( E ) \\
M[F, \text{id}] &= F \to \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

All cells in the table are **unique** ⇒ Grammar is **LL(1)**. ✓

### 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Construction of NFA from a Regular Expression (Thompson's Construction)

Given the regex for identifiers: $\text{letter} \;(\text{letter} \;\vert\; \text{digit})^*$

Let $L = \text{letter}$, $D = \text{digit}$. Then regex is: $L \cdot (L \;\vert\; D)^*$.

**Thompson's Sub-constructions** (each is a micro-NFA with entry and exit states):

**(a) Basic symbol `a`:** states $s_0 \xrightarrow{a} s_1$

**(b) Union `r \;\vert\; s`:** add new start $s_0$ and new end $s_3$; $\epsilon$-transitions from $s_0$ to the start of $r$ and $s$, and from ends of $r$, $s$ to $s_3$.

**(c) Concatenation `r s`:** $\epsilon$-transition from the end state of $r$ to the start state of $s$.

**(d) Kleene closure `r*`:** new start $s_0$, new end $s_3$. $\epsilon$-edges $s_0 \to \text{start of } r$, $s_0 \to s_3$, $\text{end of } r \to \text{start of } r$, $\text{end of } r \to s_3$.

**Applying (a)–(d) recursively** builds the full NFA for $L(L \;\vert\; D)^*$. The resulting NFA has states labeled with both regex positions and the inserted $\epsilon$-transitions.

**Step (e) — Subset Construction (NFA → DFA):**
The standard algorithm builds DFA states as **sets of NFA states**. For our identifier NFA:
- Start state of DFA = $\epsilon$-closure of NFA start.
- For each DFA state $S$ and each input symbol $a$, the next DFA state is $\epsilon$-closure($\text{move}(S, a)$).
- A DFA state is *accepting* if it contains an NFA accept state.

The resulting minimal DFA for identifiers has only **2 states**: a non-accepting "start" state and an accepting "identifier-in-progress" state — both looping on letters/digits, and the start state also transitioning to the accepting state on letter.

### 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Three-Address Code (TAC) Generation for an Expression

Generate TAC for the expression:

$$ \text{id}_1 + \text{id}_2 * \text{id}_3 \;\uparrow\; \text{id}_4 - 50 $$

where $\uparrow$ is exponentiation. Assuming $\uparrow$ is right-associative and higher precedence than `*` and `+`.

**Step 1 — Annotated parse tree** (using SDT):
- Infix → postfix semantic actions are attached to each production.
- Each operator node emits a TAC instruction using a new temporary.

**Step 2 — TAC Emission (bottom-up):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Emit } t_1 = \text{id}_3 \;\uparrow\; \text{id}_4 \\
&\text{Emit } t_2 = \text{id}_2 * t_1 \\
&\text{Emit } t_3 = \text{id}_1 + t_2 \\
&\text{Emit } t_4 = t_3 - 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final TAC sequence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= \text{id}_3 \;\uparrow\; \text{id}_4 \\
t_2 &= \text{id}_2 * t_1 \\
t_3 &= \text{id}_1 + t_2 \\
t_4 &= t_3 - 50
\end{aligned}
$$

`id1` is treated as `address_of(id1)`; constants are literal; temporaries are fresh names from the symbol table.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Parser Types

| Property | Recursive Descent | LL(1) Table | LR(0) | SLR(1) | LALR(1) | CLR(1) |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|-------|--------|---------|--------|
| **Direction** | Top-down | Top-down | Bottom-up | Bottom-up | Bottom-up | Bottom-up |
| **Power** | Low | Low | Very Low | Medium | High | Highest |
| **Handles Left Recursion?** | No | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Conflict Type Resolved** | – | FIRST/FIRST | – | Shift/Reduce | Reduce/Reduce | None |
| **States (typical)** | n productions | n productions | 100+ | 100+ | 100+ | 1000+ |
| **KTU Frequency** | High | High | High | High | Medium | Low |
| **Tool** | Hand-written | Hand-written | bison/yacc | bison/yacc | bison/yacc | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Questions on **LL(1) parsing table construction** and **LR(0)/SLR(1) item-set construction** appear almost every year. CLR(1) is rarely required beyond the definition.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Compiler Phases — Full Picture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Program] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B -->|Token Stream| C[Syntax Analyzer]
    C -->|Parse Tree| D[Semantic Analyzer]
    D -->|Annotated Tree| E[Intermediate Code Generator]
    E -->|IR Three-Address Code| F[Code Optimizer]
    F -->|Optimized IR| G[Code Generator]
    G -->|Target Code| H[Target Program]
    subgraph ST[Shared Structure]
        SYM[Symbol Table]
    end
    B -.-> SYM
    C -.-> SYM
    D -.-> SYM
    E -.-> SYM
    G -.-> SYM
```

### 4.2 Front End Architecture (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Source Chars] --> LEX[Lexical Analyzer Scanner]
    LEX -->|Tokens| SYN[Syntax Analyzer Parser]
    SYN -->|Parse Tree AST| SEM[Semantic Analyzer]
    SEM -->|Typed AST| SDT[SDT Engine]
    SDT -->|TAC| IR[Intermediate Representation]
    subgraph STSUB[Symbol Table Service]
        INSE[Insert]
        LOOK[Lookup]
        TYPE[Type Check]
    end
    LEX -.-> LOOK
    SYN -.-> LOOK
    SEM -.-> STSUB
    SDT -.-> INSE
    IR[IR TAC Three-Address Code]
```

### 4.3 DFA for Identifier Recognition

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0
    s0 --> s1 : letter
    s1 --> s1 : letter
    s1 --> s1 : digit
    s0 --> [*] : other
    s1 --> [*] : other / ACCEPT
```

### 4.4 Parse Tree for `id + id * id` (Arithmetic Grammar)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E[E] --> T1[T]
    T1 --> F1[F]
    F1 --> id1[id]
    T1 --> TP1[T prime]
    TP1 --> eps1[epsilon]
    E --> EP[E prime]
    EP --> plus[+]
    EP --> T2[T]
    T2 --> F2[F]
    F2 --> id2[id]
    T2 --> TP2[T prime]
    TP2 --> star[*]
    TP2 --> F3[F]
    F3 --> id3[id]
    TP2 --> TP3[T prime]
    TP3 --> eps2[epsilon]
    EP --> EP2[E prime]
    EP2 --> eps3[epsilon]
```

### 4.5 Three-Address Code Generation Flow (SDT)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AST Node] --> B{Node Type?}
    B -->|Binary Op| C[Generate new temp t]
    C --> D[Recurse on left child]
    D --> E[Recurse on right child]
    E --> F[Emit t = left op right]
    B -->|Identifier| G[Emit place = id entry]
    B -->|Constant| H[Emit place = const value]
    F --> I[Append to TAC list]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.6 Symbol Table Organization (Block-Scoped, Hierarchical)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Global Scope] --> S1[Function main]
    S1 --> B1[Block if-then]
    B1 --> B2[Block while]
    S1 --> B2inner[Block for]
    ROOT -.-> ENTRY[Entry: name, type, scope, offset]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping** (used here in place of complex line-drawn schematics): Each **block** represents a logical *unit of work* in the front end. Data flows strictly left → right between phases. The **Symbol Table** is a *cross-cutting service* that every phase consults. This mirrors the way real compilers such as **GCC's GIMPLE** or **LLVM's IRBuilder** are organized internally.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *Differentiate between a token, a lexeme, and a pattern. Give an example for each from a C program statement.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Token:** A pair $\langle \text{token\_name}, \text{attribute\_value} \rangle$ that is a symbolic name for a category of lexemes. Example: For the statement `total = 100;`, the token for `100` is `<num, 100>`.
- **Lexeme:** The actual sequence of characters in the source code that matches a token's pattern. Example: `total`, `=`, `100`, `;`.
- **Pattern:** A rule (usually a regular expression) describing the set of lexemes belonging to a token. Example: Pattern for an identifier is `letter (letter | digit)*`.

(1 mark for each correct definition; 1 mark for the example. KTU expects crisp, textbook-aligned definitions.)

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] *What is left recursion? Why must it be eliminated for top-down parsing? Show the elimination rule.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Left recursion** is a grammar property where a non-terminal $A$ has a production of the form $A \to A\alpha$ (direct) or $A \Rightarrow^+ A\alpha$ (indirect). It causes top-down parsers to enter an **infinite loop**, since the parser keeps expanding $A$ on the leftmost side without consuming any input.
- **Elimination rule for immediate left recursion** $A \to A\alpha \;\vert\; \beta$:

$$ A \to \beta A', \qquad A' \to \alpha A' \;\vert\; \epsilon $$

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the *why*, 1 mark for the elimination formula.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** *For the following grammar, compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets, and construct the LL(1) parsing table. Is the grammar LL(1)? Justify.*
$$ S \to aABb \;\vert\; bA \;\vert\; \epsilon $$
$$ A \to aAb \;\vert\; bS $$
$$ B \to bB \;\vert\; a $$

**(b)** *Generate three-address code (TAC) for the following code segment. Assume all variables are `int`:*

```
if (x < y) {
    z = x + y * 2;
    while (z > 10)
        z = z - 1;
} else
    z = y - x;
```

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — FIRST Sets:**

- $\text{FIRST}(B)$: $B \to bB$ adds `b`; $B \to a$ adds `a`. ⇒ **FIRST$(B) = \{a, b\}$**.
- $\text{FIRST}(A)$: $A \to aAb$ adds `a`; $A \to bS$ adds `b`. ⇒ **FIRST$(A) = \{a, b\}$**.
- $\text{FIRST}(S)$: $S \to aABb$ adds `a`; $S \to bA$ adds `b`; $S \to \epsilon$ adds $\epsilon$. ⇒ **FIRST$(S) = \{a, b, \epsilon\}$**.

**Step 2 — FOLLOW Sets:**

- $S$ is the start symbol ⇒ `\$` ∈ FOLLOW$(S)$.
- $S \to bA$ ⇒ FIRST$($ remaining after $A) = \emptyset$, so add FOLLOW$(S)$. But $A$ is the last symbol of $S \to bA$, so **FOLLOW$(A) \supseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$**.
- $A \to aAb$: $b$ follows $A$ ⇒ $b \in$ FOLLOW$(A)$.
- $S \to aABb$: $b$ follows $B$ ⇒ $b \in$ FOLLOW$(B)$. FIRST$(B)$ follows $A$, so FIRST$(B) = \{a,b\}$ ⊆ FOLLOW$(A)$.

Aggregating:
- **FOLLOW$(S) = \{\$\}$**.
- **FOLLOW$(A) \supseteq \{a, b, \$\}$** ⇒ **FOLLOW$(A) = \{a, b, \$\}$**.
- **FOLLOW$(B) = \{b, \$\}$** (also FOLLOW$(S)$).

**Step 3 — LL(1) Table Construction:**

For each production, populate $M$:

- $S \to aABb$: terminals in FIRST are $\{a\}$ ⇒ $M[S, a] = S \to aABb$.
- $S \to bA$: $M[S, b] = S \to bA$.
- $S \to \epsilon$: $M[S, a] = S \to \epsilon$ (since $a \in$ FOLLOW$(S)$? Actually $a \notin$ FOLLOW$(S)$) — recheck: FOLLOW$(S) = \{\$\}$ ⇒ only $M[S, \$] = S \to \epsilon$.

> **Conflict check at $M[S, a]$:** Only one entry ($S \to aABb$) since FOLLOW$(S) = \{\$\}$ does not contain `a`. No conflict. Similarly for `b`.

- $A \to aAb$: $M[A, a] = A \to aAb$.
- $A \to bS$: $M[A, b] = A \to bS$.
- $B \to bB$: $M[B, b] = B \to bB$.
- $B \to a$: $M[B, a] = B \to a$.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Every cell in $M$ has at most one entry ⇒ **the grammar IS LL(1)**. ✓

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

We assume the input variables are `int`. The control-flow translation uses *backpatching* conceptually, but a direct TAC using labels is acceptable at the KTU level.

**Translated TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{if } x < y \text{ goto } L1 \\
&\text{goto } L2 \\
L1: &\quad t_1 = y * 2 \\
    &\quad z = x + t_1 \\
L3: &\quad \text{if } z > 10 \text{ goto } L4 \\
    &\quad \text{goto } L5 \\
L4: &\quad t_2 = z - 1 \\
    &\quad z = t_2 \\
    &\quad \text{goto } L3 \\
    &\quad \text{goto } L6 \\
L5: & \\
L2: &\quad t_3 = y - x \\
    &\quad z = t_3 \\
L6: &
\end{aligned}
$$

The student should also explain the **SDT scheme** used (1 mark for showing how `if` and `while` are translated into labels/jumps). Allocate marks as: 2 marks for the `if` part, 3 marks for the `while` loop, 1 mark for correct labels, 1 mark for clean TAC syntax.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative

**(a)** *What is the difference between a parse tree and a syntax tree (AST)? Construct the parse tree and the AST for the expression* `a * (b + c) - d / e` *using a standard arithmetic grammar. Identify any common subexpressions.*

**(b)** *Explain the role of the symbol table in each phase of the front end. Describe any one implementation strategy for a block-structured language with nested scopes.*

---

**Model Solution Outline for (a) — 7 marks:**

- **Parse Tree** includes every grammar symbol, every non-terminal, every terminal. **AST** retains only essential operator-operand structure (no chain-of-single-productions).
- For `a * (b + c) - d / e`:
  - Parse tree root: $E$, with children reflecting full grammar expansion.
  - AST root: `-` (subtraction), with left child `*(a, +(b, c))` and right child `/ (d, e)`.
- **Common subexpression**: None in this particular expression. Introduce an example like `(a+b)*(a+b)` to highlight DAG sharing.

(Marks: 2 for definitions, 3 for the trees, 1 each for identification and explanation of common subexpressions.)

---

**Model Solution Outline for (b) — 7 marks:**

- **Lexical Analysis:** Symbol table is *populated* with identifiers and constants encountered; first-occurrence creates an entry.
- **Syntax Analysis:** Symbol table is *queried* to validate identifier use (declared before use in some languages), to support back-patching, and to attach attributes to grammar symbols.
- **Semantic Analysis:** Heaviest user — performs *type checks*, *scope resolution*, *overload resolution*; may insert implicit conversions (coercions) and record them in the table.
- **Intermediate Code Generation:** Uses addresses/offsets stored in the symbol table to emit TAC referring to operands correctly.
- **Block-structured implementation strategy** (5 marks split across explanation):
  1. **Stack of scopes** — each scope has its own hash table; entering a scope pushes a new table; exiting pops.
  2. **Single flat table with scope-level field** — simpler but slower lookup.
  3. **Parent-pointer chaining** — current scope points to enclosing scope; lookup walks up the chain.

(1 mark for the 4-phase summary, 4 marks for the block-structured strategy, 2 marks for the chosen strategy's data structures and complexity.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — The Front End:**
> 1. **FIRST/FOLLOW errors (–2 marks typical):** Most common mistake is forgetting to add `\$` to FOLLOW of the start symbol, or omitting the propagation rule "if $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$ then add FOLLOW$(A)$ to FOLLOW$(B)$".
> 2. **LL(1) table multiple entries (–2 to –3 marks):** Failing to detect a *multiple-entry cell* means the conclusion "grammar is LL(1)" is **wrong by KTU standards**. Always check every filled cell.
> 3. **TAC for control flow (–3 marks):** Students commonly forget the **branch-around-else** structure in `if-then-else`. The pattern is: `if cond goto L_true; goto L_false; L_true: ...; goto L_end; L_false: ...; L_end:`.
> 4. **Symbol table confusion (–1 mark):** Treating the symbol table as a *simple list* of identifiers rather than a *structured table* with scope/typing/offset loses marks. Always mention scope chaining or hash buckets.
> 5. **Left recursion vs ambiguity (–1 mark):** The two are *separate* properties. A grammar can be left-recursive **and** unambiguous, or right-recursive **and** ambiguous. State this clearly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Front End = Analysis = Lex → Syn → Sem → IR**, all driven by the *Symbol Table*.
- ✅ **Tokens** are pairs (name, attribute); **lexemes** are the matched substrings; **patterns** are the rules.
- ✅ **Longest match + rule priority** resolves scanner conflicts.
- ✅ A **CFG** $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is the formal model for the source language.
- ✅ **Ambiguity** = more than one parse tree for a string ⇒ must be eliminated for deterministic parsing.
- ✅ **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets drive LL(1) table construction. A unique-entry table ⇒ grammar is LL(1).
- ✅ **Left recursion must be eliminated** for top-down parsers; **left factoring** is the other key preprocessing step.
- ✅ **Bottom-up parsers (LR family)** use *items*, *closure*, *GOTO*, and *SLR(1) lookahead* via FOLLOW.
- ✅ **Semantic analysis** performs *type checking*, *scope resolution*, and *implicit coercion*.
- ✅ **Three-Address Code (TAC)** has at most three operands per instruction; ideal for the IR stage.
- ✅ **Symbol Table** is built incrementally and queried/updated across *all* front-end phases.
- ✅ **Common subexpression elimination** is naturally enabled by the **DAG** representation.
- ✅ KTU **favourite questions**: FIRST/FOLLOW computation, LL(1) table construction, TAC for expressions & control flow, and brief notes on ambiguity removal.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Optimizer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Optimizer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Optimizer (Code Optimization Phase):** It is the intermediate phase of a compiler that analyzes the intermediate representation (IR) of the source program and transforms it into an equivalent, semantically preserved program that executes with **improved performance metrics** — reduced execution time, smaller memory footprint, and lower power consumption — without altering the program's observable output.

The optimizer operates **transparently** between the intermediate code generator (front-end) and the code generator (back-end). It receives three-address code (TAC), quadruples, triples, or SSA (Static Single Assignment) form and produces an optimized IR that is functionally identical but computationally superior.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBCRT604 – Module 1):**
> The official module descriptor classifies optimization into three concentric layers:
> 1. **Local Optimization** — confined to a single basic block.
> 2. **Global Optimization** — confined to a single procedure/function.
> 3. **Inter-procedural Optimization** — across procedure boundaries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Refiner"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:**
> Imagine you are a head chef who has been handed a working recipe (the intermediate code). The recipe produces a delicious dish, but it wastes time — for instance, it tells the cook to "boil water, then boil the same water again later," or "chop onions three times when once would do." You, the optimizer, do **not** change the dish (semantic equivalence) — you only **remove wasted effort, redundant steps, and inefficient techniques**. The result is the same dish, cooked faster, with fewer pans, and lower gas consumption.

The "wasted effort" in code corresponds to:
- **Redundant computations** (e.g., computing `a+b` twice)
- **Unreachable code** (dead code)
- **Loop-invariant work done inside loops**
- **Expensive operations replaced by cheaper equivalents** (e.g., `x*2` → `x<<1`)

### 1.3 Classification of Optimizations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Classification (must memorize for KTU 2-marker):**

| Dimension | Type 1 | Type 2 |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| **Scope** | Local (intra-basic-block) | Global (intra-procedural) |
| **Machine Dependency** | Machine-Independent | Machine-Dependent |
| **When Applied** | Source-Level / IR-Level | Target-Level (post code-gen) |
| **Time of Application** | Static (compile-time) | Dynamic (run-time / JIT) |
| **Aggressiveness** | Peephole (small windows) | Whole-program / Inter-procedural |

### 1.4 Why Optimization is Essential

> [!NOTE]
> **The Iron Triangle of Compiler Optimization:**
> 1. **Speed** — fewer CPU cycles.
> 2. **Space** — smaller code footprint (important for embedded/IoT KTU application questions).
> 3. **Energy** — critical for mobile and KTU-emphasized embedded systems (EST300-style relevance).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Misconception:**
> "Optimization always makes code faster." — **FALSE.** Aggressive optimization can sometimes *increase* code size (code bloat) or, if incorrectly applied, change semantics. KTU examiners test this nuance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Optimization Pipeline — A Step-by-Step Logical Flow

The KTU 2024 scheme treats the optimizer as a **pipeline of analysis-then-transformation phases**, analogous to the classic Aho/Sethi/Ullman dragon-book model.

**Step 1 — Control Flow Analysis (CFA)**
The optimizer first partitions the intermediate code into **Basic Blocks** and constructs the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**. A basic block is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address statements in which **flow of control enters at the beginning and leaves at the end without halting or branching except possibly at the last instruction**.

**Step 2 — Data Flow Analysis (DFA)**
The optimizer then computes, for every program point, certain *data flow values*:
- `gen[B]` — information generated by block $B$
- `kill[B]` — information killed by block $B$
- `in[B]` — information flowing into block $B$
- `out[B]` — information flowing out of block $B$

**Step 3 — Transformation Phase**
Based on DFA results, the optimizer applies **safe rewrites** that preserve semantics.

**Step 4 — Iterative Refinement**
The process iterates until a **fixed point** is reached (no more changes possible).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Equations You Must Know for KTU 2024 ESE:**

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Leader Identification | Statement $i$ is a *leader* if it is the **first** statement, or the **target** of a conditional/unconditional jump, or the statement **immediately following** a conditional/unconditional jump. | Building basic blocks from TAC |
| 2 | Reach Definition (Forward) | $\text{out}[B] = \text{gen}[B] \cup (\text{in}[B] - \text{kill}[B])$ | Determining which definitions reach a use |
| 3 | Reach Definition (Aggregate) | $\text{in}[B] = \bigcup_{P \in \text{pred}[B]} \text{out}[P]$ | Merging info from predecessors |
| 4 | Available Expressions (Forward) | $\text{out}[B] = \text{e\_gen}[B] \cup (\text{in}[B] - \text{e\_kill}[B])$ | Common subexpression elimination |
| 5 | Live Variable Analysis (Backward) | $\text{in}[B] = \text{use}[B] \cup (\text{out}[B] - \text{def}[B])$ | Dead store elimination |
| 6 | Very Busy Expressions (Backward) | $\text{out}[B] = \text{vb\_gen}[B] \cup (\text{in}[B] - \text{vb\_kill}[B])$ | Code motion / hoisting |
| 7 | Dominance | $D \succ B$ if every path from Entry to $B$ passes through $D$ | Loop-invariant code motion |
| 8 | Natural Loop Condition | A loop with header $h$ contains edge $(a \to b)$ where $b \leadsto a$ and $b \not\succ a$ (or $a$ dominates $b$) | Detecting loops for LICM |
| 9 | Strength Reduction Cost | Replace $x \cdot 2^k$ with $x \ll k$, $x / 2^k$ with $x \gg k$, $x^2$ with $x \cdot x$ | Replacing expensive ops |
| 10 | Fixed-Point Convergence | Iterations terminate when $\text{in}^{(i)}[B] = \text{in}^{(i-1)}[B] \; \forall B$ | DFA termination guarantee |

### 2.3 Detailed Description of the High-Yield Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Mandatory Techniques for KTU Module 1:**

**2.3.1 Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**
If an expression $E$ has been **previously computed** and its operands have **not changed** since, replace the second occurrence with a reference to the saved result.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before:} \quad & a = b + c \\
& d = b + c \quad (\text{recompute}) \\
\text{After:} \quad & a = b + c \\
& d = a \quad (\text{reuse})
\end{aligned}
$$

**2.3.2 Copy Propagation**
After an assignment $x = y$ (a *copy*), replace subsequent uses of $x$ with $y$, until $x$ is reassigned.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before:} \quad & x = y \\
& a = x + 2 \\
\text{After:} \quad & a = y + 2 \quad (\text{and } x \text{ may become dead})
\end{aligned}
$$

**2.3.3 Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**
A variable is *dead* at a point $p$ if its value is never used. After copy propagation, many copies become dead and are removed.

**2.3.4 Constant Folding**
If all operands of an expression are compile-time constants, the computation is performed at compile time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before:} \quad & x = 3 + 5 \\
\text{After:} \quad & x = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**2.3.5 Strength Reduction**
Replace an expensive operation with an equivalent cheaper one.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& x \cdot 2 \;\longrightarrow\; x \ll 1 \\
& x / 4 \;\longrightarrow\; x \gg 2 \\
& x^2 \;\longrightarrow\; x \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

**2.3.6 Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)**
If a computation inside a loop does not depend on any variable modified within the loop, it is **hoisted** to a pre-header block outside the loop.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before:} \quad & \text{for } (i=0; i<n; i++) \{ \\
& \quad t = a + b; \quad \text{(invariant)} \\
& \quad A[i] = t \cdot i; \\
& \} \\
\text{After:} \quad & t = a + b; \quad \text{(hoisted)} \\
& \text{for } (i=0; i<n; i++) \{ \\
& \quad A[i] = t \cdot i; \\
& \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4 Peephole Optimization — A Special Case

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peephole Optimization** examines a small sliding window (the "peephole") of *target/assembly instructions* and replaces inefficient sequences with equivalent, more efficient ones. It is a **target-dependent, local** pass done just before emission.

**Canonical Peephole Transformations:**
- **Redundant load/store elimination:** `MOV R1, M; MOV M, R1` → *delete second*.
- **Constant folding in assembly:** `ADD R1, #0` → `MOV R1, R1` → *delete*.
- **Strength reduction at machine level:** `MUL R1, R1, 2` → `SHL R1, R1, 1`.
- **Branch optimization:** `CMP R1, R1; BEQ L` → `BEQ L` (always taken) or `NOP` if L is next.
- **Unreachable code:** jumps to the *very next* instruction are removed.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry (and KTU's "Engineering Relevance" questions):**
> - **GCC's** optimization levels `-O0`, `-O1`, `-O2`, `-O3`, `-Os`, `-Ofast` correspond exactly to which combination of the above techniques is enabled. `-O3` enables aggressive LICM, vectorization, and inlining.
> - **LLVM IR** is the de-facto modern optimizer input, enabling language-independent optimization (C, Rust, Swift all share the optimizer).
> - **JIT compilers** (HotSpot, V8, PyPy) apply a subset of these techniques *at runtime* using profiling feedback.
> - **Embedded cross-compilers** (Kerala's chip-design firms like InApp, TCS Embedded) prioritize `-Os` (size) over `-O3` (speed).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — DFA Equations at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the matrix KTU examiners expect you to reproduce verbatim for full marks on DFA problems.**

| Analysis | Direction | Domain | Transfer Function | Meet Operation |
|----------|-----------|--------|-------------------|----------------|
| Reach Definitions | Forward | $\mathcal{P}(\text{Defs})$ | $f_B(S) = \text{gen}[B] \cup (S - \text{kill}[B])$ | $\cup$ (Union) |
| Available Expressions | Forward | $\mathcal{P}(\text{Exprs})$ | $f_B(S) = \text{e\_gen}[B] \cup (S - \text{e\_kill}[B])$ | $\cap$ (Intersection) |
| Live Variables | Backward | $\mathcal{P}(\text{Vars})$ | $f_B(S) = \text{use}[B] \cup (S - \text{def}[B])$ | $\cup$ (Union) |
| Very Busy Expressions | Backward | $\mathcal{P}(\text{Exprs})$ | $f_B(S) = \text{vb\_gen}[B] \cup (S - \text{vb\_kill}[B])$ | $\cap$ (Intersection) |
| Reaching Copies | Forward | $\mathcal{P}(\text{Copies})$ | $f_B(S) = \text{c\_gen}[B] \cup (S - \text{c\_kill}[B])$ | $\cup$ (Union) |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Reach Definitions via Dataflow Iteration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most-tested KTU Module 1 problem. Master it.**

**Given TAC (Three-Address Code):**
```
(1)  d = 1
(2)  a = 2
(3)  if a > 0 goto (5)
(4)  goto (9)
(5)  b = a + d
(6)  c = b * 2
(7)  a = c - 1
(8)  goto (5)
(9)  end
```

**Step 1 — Identify Leaders (using the rule from §2.2 Row 1):**

- $L_1$: Statement (1) — first statement.
- $L_2$: Statement (3) — target of the `if` at (3) is itself, and the conditional `goto` makes (3) a leader. *(More precisely: targets of jumps.)*
- $L_3$: Statement (5) — target of `goto (5)` from (3) and from (8).
- $L_4$: Statement (9) — target of `goto (9)` from (4).

**Step 2 — Form Basic Blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \{1, 2\} \\
B_2 &= \{3, 4\} \\
B_3 &= \{5, 6, 7, 8\} \\
B_4 &= \{9\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct CFG:**

Edges:
- $B_1 \to B_2$ (fall-through after (2))
- $B_2 \to B_3$ (if $a>0$, taken at (3))
- $B_2 \to B_4$ (fall-through / explicit goto at (4))
- $B_3 \to B_3$ (back-edge via (8) → (5))
- $B_3 \to B_4$ (when control exits the loop, but in this TAC, the loop is infinite; we keep the back-edge)

**Step 4 — Compute `gen` and `kill` per block:**

Definitions in the program: $d_1:(1)$, $a_1:(2)$, $b_1:(5)$, $c_1:(6)$, $a_2:(7)$.

- $B_1$ defines $d$ at (1) and $a$ at (2). It kills all other defs of $d$ and $a$ (none exist).
  $\text{gen}[B_1] = \{d_1, a_1\}$, $\text{kill}[B_1] = \emptyset$
- $B_2$ defines nothing.
  $\text{gen}[B_2] = \emptyset$, $\text{kill}[B_2] = \emptyset$
- $B_3$ defines $b$ at (5), $c$ at (6), $a$ at (7).
  $\text{gen}[B_3] = \{b_1, c_1, a_2\}$, $\text{kill}[B_3] = \{a_1\}$ (kills $a$'s previous definition)
- $B_4$ defines nothing.
  $\text{gen}[B_4] = \emptyset$, $\text{kill}[B_4] = \emptyset$

**Step 5 — Initialize and Iterate:**

Boundary: $\text{out}[\text{Entry}] = \emptyset$ (or omit for our 4 blocks; we treat $B_1$'s `in` as $\emptyset$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration } i=0: \quad & \text{in}[B_i] = \emptyset \; \forall i \\
& \text{out}[B_1] = \{d_1, a_1\} \\
& \text{out}[B_2] = \emptyset \\
& \text{out}[B_3] = \{b_1, c_1, a_2\} \\
& \text{out}[B_4] = \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration } i=1: \quad & \text{in}[B_1] = \emptyset \\
& \text{in}[B_2] = \text{out}[B_1] = \{d_1, a_1\} \\
& \text{in}[B_3] = \text{out}[B_1] \cup \text{out}[B_3] = \{d_1, a_1, b_1, c_1, a_2\} \\
& \text{in}[B_4] = \text{out}[B_2] = \emptyset \\
& \text{out}[B_1] = \{d_1, a_1\} \quad \text{(unchanged)} \\
& \text{out}[B_2] = \{d_1, a_1\} - \emptyset = \{d_1, a_1\} \\
& \text{out}[B_3] = \{b_1, c_1, a_2\} \cup (\{d_1, a_1, b_1, c_1, a_2\} - \{a_1\}) = \{d_1, b_1, c_1, a_2\} \\
& \text{out}[B_4] = \emptyset
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration } i=2: \quad & \text{in}[B_1] = \emptyset \\
& \text{in}[B_2] = \{d_1, a_1\} \quad \text{(stable)} \\
& \text{in}[B_3] = \{d_1, a_1\} \cup \{d_1, b_1, c_1, a_2\} = \{d_1, a_1, b_1, c_1, a_2\} \quad \text{(stable)} \\
& \text{in}[B_4] = \emptyset \\
& \text{out}[B_2] = \{d_1, a_1\} \quad \text{(stable)} \\
& \text{out}[B_3] = \{d_1, b_1, c_1, a_2\} \quad \text{(stable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Fixed Point Reached:** No more changes. The reach definitions are finalized.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU:**
> Notice that $a_1$ does **not** reach the use of $a$ at statement (3) inside $B_2$ *after* iteration stabilizes, because $B_3$ kills $a_1$. This is a key semantic result — it tells us that within the loop, $a$ always holds the *new* value from statement (7).

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Optimizing a Full Block

**Given TAC of a basic block:**
```
t1 = a + b
t2 = c + d
t3 = a + b        // redundant
t4 = t2 * 2
t5 = t4           // copy
t6 = t5 + 1
t1 = t6           // t1 reassigned → previous t1 is DEAD
t7 = 5 * 3        // constant expression
```

**Step 1 — Constant Folding:** $5 \cdot 3 = 15$
```
t1 = a + b
t2 = c + d
t3 = a + b
t4 = t2 * 2
t5 = t4
t6 = t5 + 1
t1 = t6
t7 = 15
```

**Step 2 — Common Subexpression Elimination:** $t1 = a+b$ already exists; rename $t3 = t1$.
```
t1 = a + b
t2 = c + d
t3 = t1
t4 = t2 * 2
t5 = t4
t6 = t5 + 1
t1 = t6
t7 = 15
```

**Step 3 — Copy Propagation:** $t3$ is a copy of $t1$, $t5$ is a copy of $t4$, $t6 = t5 + 1 = t4 + 1$.
Replace uses of $t3$ with $t1$, $t5$ with $t4$, then $t6$ with $t4+1$:
```
t1 = a + b
t2 = c + d
t3 = t1          // copy, can be deleted if t3 unused
t4 = t2 * 2
t5 = t4          // copy, can be deleted if t5 unused
t6 = t4 + 1
t1 = t6
t7 = 15
```

**Step 4 — Dead Code Elimination:** $t1$ is overwritten at the last step; $t3$ and $t5$ are now unused.
```
t1 = a + b
t2 = c + d
t4 = t2 * 2
t6 = t4 + 1
t1 = t6
t7 = 15
```

**Step 5 — Strength Reduction (optional):** $t2 \cdot 2 \longrightarrow t2 \ll 1$
```
t1 = a + b
t2 = c + d
t4 = t2 << 1
t6 = t4 + 1
t1 = t6
t7 = 15
```

**Result:** Block reduced from **8 statements to 6 statements**, with one strength-reduced multiplication.

### 3.4 Loop-Invariant Code Motion — Formal Derivation

**Theorem (LICM Soundness Condition):**
A statement $S: x = y \text{ op } z$ inside a loop $L$ can be safely moved to a pre-header $H$ (a unique predecessor of the loop header) if **all** the following hold:

1. $S$ *dominates all exits* of $L$ (so any later use gets the correct value).
2. $S$ is the **only** definition of $x$ in $L$, OR $x$ is **not live-out** of $L$ (so the new pre-header $S$ does not clobber the loop's value).
3. No operand of $S$ is defined within $L$ (i.e., $S$ is genuinely invariant).

**Proof Sketch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{If condition 3 holds: the values of } y \text{ and } z \text{ are constant across iterations.} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Result of } S \text{ is the same in every iteration.} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Computing it once before the loop yields the same value as computing it inside.} \\
&\text{If condition 2 holds: any use of } x \text{ after the loop sees the pre-header's value, not a stale one.} \\
&\text{If condition 1 holds: any path exiting the loop passes through } S \text{'s new position,} \\
&\text{so the value is consistent at every exit point.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical check for KTU exam:** Test the three conditions in order. Failing **any one** invalidates the LICM transformation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
