> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Backtrack-Free Parsing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Backtrack-Free Parsing** is a deterministic top-down parsing strategy in which the parser makes parsing decisions by inspecting **only the current input symbol (lookahead)** and the **top of the parsing stack**, **without ever having to retract, rewind, or re-scan** an input token. The canonical formal realization of backtrack-free parsing is the **LL(1)** class of parsers — meaning the input is scanned **Left-to-right**, a **Leftmost derivation** is produced, and **1** symbol of lookahead is used. A grammar is said to be **backtrack-free** (or equivalently, **LL(1)**) if and only if its associated **predictive parsing table** contains **at most one production entry per cell (M[N, a])**. If any cell contains more than one production, the grammar is **ambiguous in that parsing context** and a true backtrack-free parser cannot be constructed directly for it. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Train Signal Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine a railway station where a **signal controller** (the parser) stands at a junction. He must direct an incoming train (the input token) onto the correct track (production rule) so it reaches the destination (start symbol derivation). He can see the train only when it is at the signal (current lookahead), and he cannot recall a train that has already passed (no backtracking). For him to work perfectly, every train arriving at the junction must have a **clear, unambiguous track assignment** based on its identity. The moment two different trains (say, "int" and "id") could both go down the same track, the controller is forced to "guess and reverse" — this is **backtracking**, which is forbidden in a backtrack-free parser. In compiler terms: - The **signal controller** = Predictive Parser - The **train** = Current input token (lookahead `a`) - The **junction map** = Predictive Parsing Table $M$ - The **track assignment** = Grammar Production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ - **Clear assignment** = Backtrack-Free (LL(1)) Grammar ### 1.3 Key Engineering Metric The defining engineering metric is the **Lookahead Count = 1 token**. Any deterministic top-down parser requiring more than one lookahead symbol for a decision loses its classification as a strict backtrack-free parser under the LL(1) definition used by KTU Module 2. > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** Examiners frequently award full marks only when students explicitly mention the three LL(1) conditions — *no left-recursion*, *left-factored grammar*, and *unambiguous predictive table*. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — The 5-Step Backtrack-Free Pipeline A backtrack-free (predictive) parser operates through a deterministic pipeline: 1. **Input Buffer Initialization:** The input is loaded into a buffer terminated by the **end-marker `$`**. A pointer `ip` (input pointer) tracks the current lookahead symbol `a = *ip`. 2. **Stack Initialization:** The parsing stack is initialized with `$` at the bottom and the **start symbol `S`** on top. Formally, the stack initially holds: `[$ , S]`. 3. **Top-of-Stack vs. Lookahead Decision:** The parser continuously inspects `X = TOP(stack)` and `a = *ip`: - If `X = a = $` → **ACCEPT** (successful parse). - If `X = a ≠ $` → **MATCH**: pop `X` and advance `ip`. - If `X` is a non-terminal → consult the predictive parsing table $M[X, a]$. 4. **Table Lookup Resolution:** - If $M[X, a] = \{X \rightarrow \alpha\}$ (single production) → **push** the right-hand side $\alpha$ onto the stack in **reverse order** so the leftmost symbol of $\alpha$ becomes the new top. - If $M[X, a] = \emptyset$ (empty cell) → **ERROR** (syntax error, recovery invoked). - If $M[X, a]$ contains **multiple entries** → grammar is **NOT** backtrack-free (table is multidefined). 5. **Output the Production:** Each applied production is appended to the leftmost derivation sequence — this is the parser's traceable output. ### 2.2 Why is Backtrack-Free Parsing Required? - **Time Efficiency:** Guarantees **O(n)** parsing time where `n` is the number of input tokens — no exponential blow-up from trial-and-error. - **Determinism:** Every parsing state has **at most one** legal action — a hard requirement for hardware/software pipeline implementation. - **Memory Predictability:** Stack depth is bounded by the height of the parse tree, making memory consumption static and analyzable. - **Foundation for Code Generation:** The deterministic order of productions makes intermediate code generation straightforward. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **every formula and set definition** required for backtrack-free parsing problems. Use $\vert$ for cardinality to avoid markdown table breakage. | # | Symbol / Concept | Formal Definition | KTU Usage | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $FIRST(\alpha)$ | Set of terminals that begin strings derivable from $\alpha$. If $\alpha \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$, then $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha)$. | Computes column entries of predictive table. | | 2 | $FOLLOW(A)$ | Set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of non-terminal $A$ in any sentential form. `$` is in $FOLLOW(S)$ where $S$ is the start symbol. | Computes column entries for $\varepsilon$-productions. | | 3 | $LL(1)$ Condition | For every non-terminal $A$ with productions $A \rightarrow \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \ldots \mid \alpha_n$:
$FIRST(\alpha_i) \cap FIRST(\alpha_j) = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$
If $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha_i)$, then $FIRST(\alpha_j) \cap FOLLOW(A) = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$ | Verifies backtrack-free eligibility. | | 4 | Table Entry Rule 1 | For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$: for every terminal $a \in FIRST(\alpha)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, a]$. | Fills non-$\varepsilon$ cells. | | 5 | Table Entry Rule 2 | If $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha)$, for every $b \in FOLLOW(A)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, b]$. | Fills $\varepsilon$-derived cells. | | 6 | Table Entry Rule 3 | If $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha)$ and $\$ \in FOLLOW(A)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, \$]$. | Fills dollar cells. | | 7 | Stack Growth Bound | Maximum stack depth $\leq \vert P \vert \cdot h$ where $\vert P \vert$ is RHS length and $h$ is parse tree height. | Used in complexity proofs. | | 8 | Conflict Types | $FIRST-FIRST$ conflict, $FIRST-FOLLOW$ conflict. | Diagnose non-LL(1) grammars. | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Backtrack-free parsing is the engine behind every production **recursive-descent compiler** in modern languages (LLVM front-ends, GCC's C/C++ parser, and Java compilers). In **domain-specific languages (DSLs)** for hardware description (Verilog/VHDL front-ends) and **query compilers** for databases, LL(1) parsers are preferred because their deterministic behavior enables parallel speculative optimization and real-time error reporting in IDEs. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Iterative Algorithm — Computing $FIRST$ Sets The following iterative fixpoint procedure is mandated by KTU board answers: $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Step 1: Base Initialization} \\ &\text{For every terminal } t: \quad FIRST(t) = \{t\} \\ &\text{If } X \rightarrow \varepsilon \text{ is a production: } \quad \varepsilon \in FIRST(X) \\ & \\ &\textbf{Step 2: Propagation Rule} \\ &\text{If } X \rightarrow Y_1 \, Y_2 \, \ldots \, Y_k \text{ is a production:} \\ &\text{Add } FIRST(Y_1) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \text{ to } FIRST(X). \\ &\text{If } \varepsilon \in FIRST(Y_1), \text{ add } FIRST(Y_2) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}. \\ &\text{Continue until either } Y_i \text{ does not yield } \varepsilon \text{ or all } Y_i \text{ yield } \varepsilon. \\ &\text{If all } Y_1 \ldots Y_k \text{ yield } \varepsilon, \text{ then add } \varepsilon \text{ to } FIRST(X). \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Iterative Algorithm — Computing $FOLLOW$ Sets $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Step 1: Base Initialization} \\ &FOLLOW(S) = \{\$\} \quad \text{where } S \text{ is the start symbol.} \\ &FOLLOW(A) = \emptyset \quad \text{for all other non-terminals } A. \\ & \\ &\textbf{Step 2: Propagation Rules (apply to all productions)} \\ &\textbf{Rule A:} \text{If } A \rightarrow \alpha \, B \, \beta, \text{ then } FIRST(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq FOLLOW(B). \\ &\textbf{Rule B:} \text{If } A \rightarrow \alpha \, B \text{ or } A \rightarrow \alpha \, B \, \beta \text{ with } \varepsilon \in FIRST(\beta), \\ &\qquad\qquad \text{then } FOLLOW(A) \subseteq FOLLOW(B). \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Construction of the Predictive Parsing Table $M$ $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Algorithm: Build } M \text{ for backtrack-free parsing} \\ & \\ &\text{For each production } A \rightarrow \alpha: \\ &\quad \text{For each terminal } a \in FIRST(\alpha): \\ &\qquad M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha \\ & \\ &\text{If } \varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha): \\ &\quad \text{For each terminal } b \in FOLLOW(A): \\ &\qquad M[A, b] = A \rightarrow \alpha \\ & \\ &\text{All unfilled cells are marked } \textbf{error}. \\ & \\ &\textbf{Backtrack-Free Test:} \\ &\text{The grammar is LL(1) (backtrack-free) } \iff \text{no cell } M[A, a] \text{ contains more than one production.} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — Trace a Complete Parse **Grammar G (given):** $$ \begin{aligned} &S \rightarrow a \, B \, A \\ &A \rightarrow b \, A \mid \varepsilon \\ &B \rightarrow c \, B \mid d \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Compute FIRST sets:** $$ \begin{aligned} FIRST(A) &= FIRST(bA) \cup FIRST(\varepsilon) = \{b\} \cup \{\varepsilon\} = \{b, \varepsilon\} \\ FIRST(B) &= FIRST(cB) \cup FIRST(d) = \{c\} \cup \{d\} = \{c, d\} \\ FIRST(S) &= FIRST(aBA) = \{a\} \quad \text{(since } a \text{ is a terminal)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute FOLLOW sets:** $$ \begin{aligned} FOLLOW(S) &= \{\$\} \\ FOLLOW(A) &= FOLLOW(S) \text{ is irrelevant; from } S \rightarrow aBA: \quad FOLLOW(A) = \{\$\} \\ FOLLOW(B) &= \text{from } S \rightarrow aBA: \quad FIRST(A)\setminus\{\varepsilon\} = \{b\}, \text{ and since } \varepsilon \in FIRST(A), FOLLOW(S) = \{\$\} \\ &\Rightarrow FOLLOW(B) = \{b, \$\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Build Predictive Parsing Table $M$:** | | a | b | c | d | \$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **S** | $S \rightarrow aBA$ | | | | | | **A** | | $A \rightarrow bA$ | | | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | **B** | | | $B \rightarrow cB$ | $B \rightarrow d$ | | Every cell contains **at most one** production $\Rightarrow$ **Grammar G is LL(1) (backtrack-free).** **Step 4 — Parse the input string `acdb$`:** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Stack} = [\$ , S], \quad \text{Input} = a \, c \, d \, b \, \$ \\ &\textbf{Move 1:} \quad TOP = S, \quad ip = a \Rightarrow M[S, a] = S \rightarrow aBA \\ &\text{Push in reverse: Stack} = [\$ , B , A , a] \\ &\textbf{Move 2:} \quad TOP = a, \quad ip = a \Rightarrow \text{MATCH, pop } a, \text{ advance } \\ &\text{Stack} = [\$ , B , A], \quad \text{Input} = c \, d \, b \, \$ \\ &\textbf{Move 3:} \quad TOP = B, \quad ip = c \Rightarrow M[B, c] = B \rightarrow cB \\ &\text{Stack} = [\$ , A , B , c] \\ &\textbf{Move 4:} \quad TOP = c, \quad ip = c \Rightarrow \text{MATCH} \\ &\text{Stack} = [\$ , A , B], \quad \text{Input} = d \, b \, \$ \\ &\textbf{Move 5:} \quad TOP = B, \quad ip = d \Rightarrow M[B, d] = B \rightarrow d \\ &\text{Stack} = [\$ , A , d] \\ &\textbf{Move 6:} \quad TOP = d, \quad ip = d \Rightarrow \text{MATCH} \\ &\text{Stack} = [\$ , A], \quad \text{Input} = b \, \$ \\ &\textbf{Move 7:} \quad TOP = A, \quad ip = b \Rightarrow M[A, b] = A \rightarrow bA \\ &\text{Stack} = [\$ , A , b] \\ &\textbf{Move 8:} \quad TOP = b, \quad ip = b \Rightarrow \text{MATCH} \\ &\text{Stack} = [\$ , A], \quad \text{Input} = \$ \\ &\textbf{Move 9:} \quad TOP = A, \quad ip = \$ \Rightarrow M[A, \$] = A \rightarrow \varepsilon \\ &\text{Stack} = [\$] \\ &\textbf{Move 10:} \quad TOP = \$, \quad ip = \$ \Rightarrow \textbf{ACCEPT} \end{aligned} $$ The parse succeeded in **10 moves with zero backtracks** — confirming the parser is fully backtrack-free. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 LL(1) Backtrack-Free Parsing Engine — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Buffer with end marker] --> B[Input Pointer ip] B --> C[Current Lookahead a] D[Parsing Stack] --> E[Top Symbol X] C --> F{Decision Logic} E --> F F -->|X = a = dollar| G[ACCEPT State] F -->|X = a is terminal| H[Match and Pop] F -->|X is Non-Terminal| I[Lookup Table M X comma a] I -->|Single Production| J[Push RHS in Reverse] I -->|Empty Cell| K[Error Recovery Module] I -->|Multiple Entries| L[Grammar is NOT LL1] J --> M[Emit Production to Output] H --> M M --> D K --> D ``` ### 4.2 Predictive Parsing Table — Construction Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Production A to alpha] --> Q1{Is epsilon in FIRST alpha} Q1 -->|No| R1[For each a in FIRST alpha: Set M A a to A to alpha] Q1 -->|Yes| R2[For each a in FIRST alpha minus epsilon: Set M A a to A to alpha] R2 --> S1[For each b in FOLLOW A: Set M A b to A to alpha] S1 --> T1[If dollar in FOLLOW A: Set M A dollar to A to alpha] R1 --> U1[Mark all empty cells as Error] T1 --> U1 U1 --> V1{Any cell has more than one entry} V1 -->|Yes| W1[Grammar is NOT backtrack free] V1 -->|No| X1[Grammar is LL1 Backtrack Free] ``` ### 4.3 Parsing Decision State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> InitializeStack InitializeStack --> ReadInput ReadInput --> CompareTop CompareTop --> Match: Top equals Lookahead CompareTop --> Expand: Top is Non-Terminal CompareTop --> Accept: Top equals dollar and Lookahead equals dollar CompareTop --> Error: Top is Non-Terminal and Cell Empty Match --> ReadInput Expand --> ReadInput Accept --> [*] Error --> [*] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define *backtrack-free parsing*. State the two essential conditions a grammar must satisfy to be parsed backtrack-free. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (2 marks):** Backtrack-free parsing is a deterministic top-down parsing technique in which the parser makes production decisions by examining only the current input symbol and the top of the parsing stack, without ever needing to retract, rewind, or re-scan input tokens. It is formally realized as **LL(1)** parsing — Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, **1** symbol of lookahead. > > **Two Conditions (1 mark):** > 1. The grammar must be **non-left-recursive** (no production of the form $A \rightarrow A\alpha$). > 2. The grammar must be **left-factored** so that the predictive parsing table contains at most one production per cell. **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the role of the $FIRST$ and $FOLLOW$ sets in constructing a predictive parsing table? Why is $\varepsilon$ handled separately? **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **$FIRST$ Set (1.5 marks):** $FIRST(\alpha)$ identifies the set of terminals that can begin any string derived from $\alpha$. It is used to fill predictive table cells $M[A, a]$ for every terminal $a \in FIRST(\alpha)$ when production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ is considered. > > **$FOLLOW$ Set (1 mark):** $FOLLOW(A)$ identifies terminals that can legally appear immediately after non-terminal $A$. It is used to fill cells $M[A, b]$ when the production's RHS derives $\varepsilon$. > > **$\varepsilon$ Handling (0.5 mark):** Since $\varepsilon$ is not a real terminal, it cannot appear in the table. Productions yielding $\varepsilon$ must populate the table using the **parent non-terminal's $FOLLOW$ set** instead of $FIRST$. ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 2 Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — July 2024] **(a)** Compute the $FIRST$ and $FOLLOW$ sets for every non-terminal in the following grammar. **(7 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} &S \rightarrow A \, B \, C \\ &A \rightarrow a \, A \mid \varepsilon \\ &B \rightarrow b \mid B \, b \\ &C \rightarrow c \end{aligned} $$ **(b)** Construct the predictive parsing table and determine whether the grammar is backtrack-free. Parse the input `abc$` and show the stack movements. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1: Compute $FIRST$ sets** (3 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} FIRST(A) &: \text{From } A \rightarrow aA: \{a\}. \text{ From } A \rightarrow \varepsilon: \{\varepsilon\}. \\ &\Rightarrow FIRST(A) = \{a, \varepsilon\} \\ FIRST(B) &: \text{From } B \rightarrow b: \{b\}. \text{ From } B \rightarrow Bb: FIRST(B) = \{b\}. \\ &\Rightarrow FIRST(B) = \{b\} \\ FIRST(C) &: \text{From } C \rightarrow c: \{c\}. \\ &\Rightarrow FIRST(C) = \{c\} \\ FIRST(S) &: \text{From } S \rightarrow ABC: FIRST(A)\setminus\{\varepsilon\} \cup \text{continue if } \varepsilon \\ &\text{Since } \varepsilon \in FIRST(A), \text{ check } FIRST(B) = \{b\}. \text{ Since } b \neq \varepsilon, \text{ stop.} \\ &\Rightarrow FIRST(S) = \{a, b\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute $FOLLOW$ sets** (4 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} FOLLOW(S) &= \{\$\} \quad \text{(start symbol)} \\ FOLLOW(A) &: \text{In } S \rightarrow ABC, A \text{ is followed by } BC. \\ &\text{Add } FIRST(B)\setminus\{\varepsilon\} = \{b\}. \\ &\text{Since } FIRST(B) \text{ does not contain } \varepsilon, \text{ stop.} \\ &\Rightarrow FOLLOW(A) = \{b\} \\ FOLLOW(B) &: \text{In } S \rightarrow ABC, B \text{ is followed by } C. \\ &\text{Add } FIRST(C)\setminus\{\varepsilon\} = \{c\}. \\ &\Rightarrow FOLLOW(B) = \{c\} \\ FOLLOW(C) &: \text{In } S \rightarrow ABC, C \text{ is the last symbol.} \\ &\text{Add } FOLLOW(S) = \{\$\}. \\ &\Rightarrow FOLLOW(C) = \{\$\} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1: Predictive Parsing Table** (3 marks) | | a | b | c | \$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | **S** | $S \rightarrow ABC$ | $S \rightarrow ABC$ | | | | **A** | $A \rightarrow aA$ | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | | | **B** | | $B \rightarrow b$ | $B \rightarrow Bb$ | | | **C** | | | $C \rightarrow c$ | | **Step 2: Backtrack-Free Verdict** (1 mark) All cells contain **at most one** production. **The grammar is backtrack-free (LL(1)).** **Step 3: Parse `abc$`** (3 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Stack: } [\$ , S], \quad ip = a \Rightarrow M[S,a] = S \rightarrow ABC \Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\$ , C , B , A] \\ &\text{Stack: } [\$ , C , B , A], \quad ip = a \Rightarrow M[A,a] = A \rightarrow aA \Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\$ , C , B , A , a] \\ &\text{Stack: } [\$ , C , B , A , a], \quad ip = a \Rightarrow \text{MATCH} \Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\$ , C , B , A], \quad ip = b \\ &\text{Stack: } [\$ , C , B , A], \quad ip = b \Rightarrow M[A,b] = A \rightarrow \varepsilon \Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\$ , C , B] \\ &\text{Stack: } [\$ , C , B], \quad ip = b \Rightarrow M[B,b] = B \rightarrow b \Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\$ , C , b] \\ &\text{Stack: } [\$ , C , b], \quad ip = b \Rightarrow \text{MATCH} \Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\$ , C], \quad ip = c \\ &\text{Stack: } [\$ , C], \quad ip = c \Rightarrow M[C,c] = C \rightarrow c \Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\$ , c] \\ &\text{Stack: } [\$ , c], \quad ip = c \Rightarrow \text{MATCH} \Rightarrow \text{Stack: } [\$], \quad ip = \$ \\ &\text{Stack: } [\$], \quad ip = \$ \Rightarrow \textbf{ACCEPT} \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the architecture of a predictive (backtrack-free) parser. List the inputs required to construct the predictive parsing table. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the grammar: $E \rightarrow T \, E'$, $E' \rightarrow + \, T \, E' \mid \varepsilon$, $T \rightarrow F \, T'$, $T' \rightarrow * \, F \, T' \mid \varepsilon$, $F \rightarrow ( \, E \, ) \mid id$. Construct the predictive parsing table and verify backtrack-free property. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** **Architecture (4 marks):** A predictive parser consists of four interacting components: - **Input Buffer** holding the string terminated by `$` and a pointer `ip`. - **Parsing Stack** holding grammar symbols with `$` at the bottom and the start symbol on top. - **Predictive Parsing Table** $M$ — a 2D matrix indexed by non-terminals (rows) and terminals (columns) mapping $(A, a)$ to a unique production. - **Output** stream emitting the productions applied in order. **Inputs Required for Table Construction (3 marks):** 1. The **grammar** $G = (V, T, P, S)$ with all productions. 2. The **$FIRST$ sets** of all RHS strings. 3. The **$FOLLOW$ sets** of all non-terminals. 4. The **start symbol** $S$ to seed $FOLLOW(S) = \{\$\}$. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1: $FIRST$ sets** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} FIRST(F) &= \{\text{(}, id\} \\ FIRST(T) &= FIRST(F) = \{\text{(}, id\} \\ FIRST(E) &= FIRST(T) = \{\text{(}, id\} \\ FIRST(E') &= \{+, \varepsilon\} \\ FIRST(T') &= \{*, \varepsilon\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: $FOLLOW$ sets** (2 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} FOLLOW(E) &= \{\$, )\} \\ FOLLOW(E') &= \{\$, )\} \\ FOLLOW(T) &= \{+, \$, )\} \\ FOLLOW(T') &= \{+, \$, )\} \\ FOLLOW(F) &= \{*, +, \$, )\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Predictive Parsing Table** (3 marks) | | + | * | ( | ) | id | \$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **E** | | | $E \rightarrow TE'$ | | $E \rightarrow TE'$ | | | **E'** | $E' \rightarrow +TE'$ | | | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | **T** | | | $T \rightarrow FT'$ | | $T \rightarrow FT'$ | | | **T'** | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $T' \rightarrow *FT'$ | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | **F** | | | $F \rightarrow (E)$ | | $F \rightarrow id$ | | Every cell has **at most one production** $\Rightarrow$ **Grammar is backtrack-free (LL(1)).** ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Examiner Insights):** > 1. **Forgetting the dollar sign in $FOLLOW(S)$:** Many students write $FOLLOW(S) = \emptyset$ and lose 1 mark. Always seed $FOLLOW(S) = \{\$\}$. > 2. **Filling the table for $\varepsilon$-productions using $FIRST$ instead of $FOLLOW$:** This is a classic 2-mark error. Productions with $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha)$ must use **$FOLLOW(A)$**, not $FIRST(\alpha)$. > 3. **Stopping $FIRST$ propagation too early:** When computing $FIRST(Y_1 Y_2 \ldots Y_k)$, you must keep including $FIRST(Y_{i+1})$ as long as $\varepsilon \in FIRST(Y_i)$. Skipping this loses 1 mark. > 4. **Not verifying backtrack-free property explicitly:** After table construction, the student must write: *"Every cell has at most one entry $\Rightarrow$ grammar is LL(1) (backtrack-free)."* Without this concluding line, 0.5–1 mark is deducted. > 5. **Stack reversal omission:** When pushing RHS $\alpha$ onto the stack, the symbols must be pushed in **reverse order** so that the leftmost symbol of $\alpha$ ends up on top. Forgetting to mention this loses a mark in parse-trace questions. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Backtrack-Free Parsing = LL(1) Parsing:** Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1-token lookahead. - **Two Pre-conditions:** No left-recursion **AND** Left-factored grammar. - **$FIRST(\alpha)$:** Terminals that can start strings derived from $\alpha$ (includes $\varepsilon$ if applicable). - **$FOLLOW(A)$:** Terminals that can legally appear immediately after $A$ in any sentential form. - **$FOLLOW(S) = \{\$\}$** is the universal starting rule. - **Table Entry Rules:** (i) $a \in FIRST(\alpha) \Rightarrow M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$; (ii) $\varepsilon \in FIRST(\alpha) \Rightarrow M[A, b] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ for all $b \in FOLLOW(A)$. - **Backtrack-Free Test:** Every cell $M[A, a]$ must contain **at most one** production. - **Stack Push Rule:** RHS symbols are pushed in **reverse order** so the leftmost symbol becomes the new top. - **Acceptance Condition:** `$` on stack top **and** `$` as lookahead $\Rightarrow$ ACCEPT. - **Error Condition:** Empty cell $M[X, a]$ where $X$ is a non-terminal $\Rightarrow$ syntax error. - **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ where $n$ is the input length — a strict guarantee for backtrack-free parsers. - **Conflict Types:** $FIRST$-$FIRST$ conflict (two $\alpha_i$ share a terminal) and $FIRST$-$FOLLOW$ conflict ($\varepsilon$-production collides with another $\alpha_j$). - **Recursion Removal:** Direct left-recursion $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$ is rewritten as $A \rightarrow \beta A'$, $A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$ before testing LL(1) eligibility. - **Left Factoring:** Common prefixes $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta \mid \alpha\gamma$ are rewritten as $A \rightarrow \alpha A'$, $A' \rightarrow \beta \mid \gamma$. - **Engineering Use:** Recursive-descent compilers, IDE syntax highlighters, DSL interpreters, query compilers, and hardware-description language front-ends.

topics.title: Left-Factoring to Eliminate Backtracking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Left-Factoring to Eliminate Backtracking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Syntax Analysis – Left-Factoring to Eliminate Backtracking

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Left-Factoring** is a systematic grammar transformation technique used during the construction of a **predictive top-down parser**. It is applied when two or more alternative productions of the same non-terminal share a common **prefix string (α)**. The transformation rewrites the grammar so that the parser is forced to commit to *one unique production* by reading only the common prefix, and then defers the choice of the remaining suffix to a freshly introduced auxiliary non-terminal. This removes the **need to backtrack** on the input.

Mathematically, if a non-terminal $A$ has productions of the form:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha \beta_1 \mid \alpha \beta_2 \mid \alpha \beta_3 \mid \dots \mid \alpha \beta_n$$

where $\alpha$ is the **longest common prefix** of length $\geq 1$ shared by at least two alternatives, then left-factoring produces an equivalent grammar:

$$A \rightarrow \alpha A'$$

$$A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \beta_3 \mid \dots \mid \beta_n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backtracking** in a top-down parser is the act of rescanning input tokens because the parser chose the wrong production rule. Left-factoring **eliminates this ambiguity at the choice point** so that a single token-lookahead is sufficient to pick the correct alternative.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Coffee Order" Metaphor

Imagine a customer at a café counter saying:

> *"I want a **latte**."*
> *"I want a **cappuccino**."*
> *"I want a **mocha**."*

A lazy listener hears *"I want a ..."* and is unsure which drink follows. They might write down *latte*, hear *cappuccino* later, realize they were wrong, **scratch out the previous word, and rewrite it** — that scratch-and-rewrite is *backtracking*.

A smart listener restructures the sentences into:

> *"I want a **[DRINK]**."*
> *"[DRINK] is **latte** OR **cappuccino** OR **mocha**."*

The smart listener **commits to the common prefix** *"I want a"* immediately and only later decides the drink. That is exactly what **left-factoring** does to a grammar: it pulls the common prefix out and defers the variable tail to a brand-new non-terminal.

---

### 1.3 Why Backtracking Is Undesirable in Compilers

| Drawback of Backtracking | Consequence in a Real Compiler |
|---|---|
| Exponential time complexity in worst case | Slow compilation of large source files |
| Parser must re-scan input | Re-execution of the lexer (tokenizer) |
| Cannot guarantee linear parsing | Predictable performance is lost |
| Not compatible with LL($k$) parsers | Modern parsers (e.g., recursive-descent) become unusable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For a grammar to be **LL(1)** (the class accepted by predictive top-down parsers generated by tools like YACC's `%glr-parser` or hand-written recursive descent), it must be free of **both left-recursion AND common-prefix ambiguity** (i.e., left-factored). Left-factoring is therefore a **mandatory preprocessing step** before constructing the LL(1) parsing table.

---

### 1.4 Terminology Bookmark

- **α (alpha)** – the *longest* common prefix string of terminals/non-terminals.
- **β (beta)** – the *suffixes* that differ between alternatives.
- **A′ (A-prime)** – a freshly invented auxiliary non-terminal that absorbs the differing tails.
- **ε (epsilon)** – the empty string, used when one of the β's is empty.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Reasoning – When and Why We Factor

A top-down parser begins with the **start symbol** and tries to *expand* the leftmost non-terminal by picking one of its productions. To pick correctly, it inspects the **next input token** (the *lookahead*). It can only pick unambiguously if the **FIRST sets** of the candidate productions are **pairwise disjoint**.

When two productions start with the same terminal (or the same derivable string), the parser sees the same lookahead for both, gets confused, and is forced to *guess — and backtrack if wrong*. Left-factoring fixes this by **pushing the point of choice deeper**, past the common prefix, so each resulting alternative starts with a *different* first terminal.

---

## 2.2 The Left-Factoring Algorithm (Aho-Sethi-Ullman Form)

For every non-terminal $A$ in the grammar:

1. **Identify** all productions of $A$: $A \rightarrow \gamma_1 \mid \gamma_2 \mid \dots \mid \gamma_n$.
2. **Group** those $\gamma_i$ that share a common prefix. Let the common prefix be $\alpha$.
3. **If no two** $\gamma_i$ share a prefix (i.e., all FIRSTs are already disjoint), leave $A$ alone.
4. **Otherwise**, create a new non-terminal $A'$.
5. **Rewrite** $A \rightarrow \alpha A'$ and $A' \rightarrow \gamma'_1 \mid \gamma'_2 \mid \dots \mid \gamma'_k$, where each $\gamma'_i$ is obtained by **stripping the common prefix $\alpha$** from the corresponding $\gamma_i$.
6. **Repeat** Steps 1–5 for every non-terminal, *including* the newly introduced $A'$, until no common prefixes remain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The algorithm is **iterative** — after introducing $A'$, you must check $A'$ itself for common prefixes, because the $\beta_i$ tails may themselves share a prefix.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Decision Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for deciding whether to left-factor and how.

| # | Condition in Grammar | Action Required | Resulting Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A \rightarrow \alpha \beta_1 \mid \alpha \beta_2$ (with common $\alpha$) | Left-factor | $A \rightarrow \alpha A', \; A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$ |
| 2 | $A \rightarrow \alpha \beta_1 \mid \alpha \beta_2 \mid \alpha$ | Left-factor (treat $\alpha$ as $\beta = \varepsilon$) | $A \rightarrow \alpha A', \; A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \varepsilon$ |
| 3 | $A \rightarrow \alpha \mid \beta$ where $\alpha \cap \beta = \emptyset$ | **No factoring** needed | Keep as is |
| 4 | $A \rightarrow \alpha \beta \mid \alpha$ | Left-factor | $A \rightarrow \alpha A', \; A' \rightarrow \beta \mid \varepsilon$ |
| 5 | $A \rightarrow B \alpha \mid B \beta$ where $B$ is a non-terminal | Left-factor (after FIRST($B$) expands) | $A \rightarrow B A', \; A' \rightarrow \alpha \mid \beta$ |

### 2.3.1 Key Boundary Conditions

- **Length of common prefix:** $\vert \alpha \vert \geq 1$. If $\alpha = \varepsilon$, no factoring is meaningful.
- **ε-production handling:** Whenever one alternative *is* the common prefix itself (i.e., $\beta_i = \varepsilon$), the new non-terminal $A'$ **must** have an $\varepsilon$-production.
- **FIRST-set disjointness post-factoring:** After factoring, you must verify $\text{FIRST}(\beta_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta_j) = \emptyset$ for all $i \neq j$. If still non-disjoint, **iterate**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Left-Factoring |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Front-Ends** (GCC, Clang, javac) | Enables LL(1) and recursive-descent parsers; the ANTLR tool requires left-factored grammars. |
| **Expression Evaluators** | Distinguishes `if-then` from `if-then-else` deterministically. |
| **Data-Format Parsers** (JSON, XML, DSLs) | Allows single-lookahead streaming parsers with bounded memory. |
| **Network Protocol Decoders** | Deterministic parsing without state-rollback. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern parser generators (ANTLR, Bison's LALR mode) often *tolerate* limited backtracking, but **hand-written recursive-descent parsers** used in production compilers (e.g., the Clang C++ front-end) absolutely require the grammar to be left-factored, otherwise manual backtracking code becomes error-prone.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Below are **fully worked examples** of the left-factoring transformation. Every algebraic/logical step is shown explicitly. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Example 1 – The Classic "if-then / if-then-else" Grammar

### Original Grammar $G_1$

$$S \rightarrow iEtS \mid iEtSeS \mid a$$
$$E \rightarrow b$$

Here `i` stands for `if`, `t` for `then`, `e` for `else`, `a` for `assign`, `b` for `boolean expression`.

### Step 1: Identify the offending non-terminal

Non-terminal $S$ has three productions. Productions 1 and 2 share the prefix $\alpha = iEtS$.

### Step 2: Group by common prefix

- Group X (prefix `iEtS`): productions $iEtS$ and $iEtSeS$.
- Group Y (no common prefix with X): production $a$.

### Step 3: Factor out the longest common prefix

Strip `iEtS` from each member of Group X. We obtain the suffixes:

- From $iEtS$ → suffix is $\varepsilon$ (empty).
- From $iEtSeS$ → suffix is $eS$.

### Step 4: Introduce a new non-terminal $S'$

The factored grammar becomes:

$$S \rightarrow iEtS\,S' \mid a$$
$$S' \rightarrow \varepsilon \mid eS$$
$$E \rightarrow b$$

### Step 5: Verify no more common prefixes

Inspect $S'$: the two alternatives start with $\varepsilon$ and $e$ — **disjoint**, so no further factoring is required. Inspect $E$: only one production, nothing to factor.

### Step 6: Final Left-Factored Grammar $G'_1$

$$\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow iEtS\,S' \mid a \\
S' &\rightarrow \varepsilon \mid eS \\
E &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
}$$

### FIRST-set verification (for the LL(1) check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FIRST}(iEtS\,S') &= \{ i \} \\
\text{FIRST}(a) &= \{ a \} \\
\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon) &= \{ \varepsilon \} \\
\text{FIRST}(eS) &= \{ e \}
\end{aligned}
$$

All FIRST sets within each non-terminal are pairwise disjoint — the grammar is now **LL(1)-compatible**.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 – The Three-Way Common Prefix

### Original Grammar $G_2$

$$A \rightarrow abcd \mid abef \mid abgh$$

### Step 1: Find the longest common prefix of all three productions

Comparing token-by-token:
- Position 1: `a, a, a` ✓
- Position 2: `b, b, b` ✓
- Position 3: `c, e, g` ✗ — stop.

So $\alpha = ab$, length $2$.

### Step 2: Strip $\alpha$ to obtain suffixes

- $abcd$ → suffix $cd$
- $abef$ → suffix $ef$
- $abgh$ → suffix $gh$

### Step 3: Create $A'$

$$A \rightarrow ab\,A'$$
$$A' \rightarrow cd \mid ef \mid gh$$

### Step 4: Check $A'$ for common prefixes

The alternatives $cd$, $ef$, $gh$ all start with *different* terminals ($c, e, g$). No further factoring needed.

### Step 5: Final Result

$$\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
A &\rightarrow ab\,A' \\
A' &\rightarrow cd \mid ef \mid gh
\end{aligned}
}$$

---

## 3.3 Example 3 – Nested / Iterative Factoring

### Original Grammar $G_3$

$$X \rightarrow abc\,p \mid abc\,q \mid abc \mid abd\,r$$

### Step 1: First factoring round

Longest common prefix of all four productions? Position 1 is `a, a, a, a` ✓; position 2 is `b, b, b, b` ✓; position 3 is `c, c, c, d` ✗. So among the first three, the common prefix is $abc$; the fourth differs at position 3.

**Group 1** (prefix `abc`): $abc\,p$, $abc\,q$, $abc$.
**Group 2** (no shared prefix with Group 1's tail): $abd\,r$.

### Step 2: First-level factoring

$$X \rightarrow abc\,X' \mid abd\,r$$
$$X' \rightarrow p \mid q \mid \varepsilon$$

### Step 3: Second factoring round (X' check)

$X'$ has alternatives $p$, $q$, $\varepsilon$ — all start with different symbols. Done.

### Step 4: Final Result

$$\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
X &\rightarrow abc\,X' \mid abd\,r \\
X' &\rightarrow p \mid q \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Before vs. After

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix summarizes *what changed* in each example, mapped to the KTU grading rubric: (i) correct identification of $\alpha$, (ii) correct $\beta_i$ derivation, (iii) correct introduction of $A'$, (iv) verification of FIRST-set disjointness.

| Example | Original Common Prefix $\alpha$ | Suffixes $\beta_i$ | New Non-Terminal | FIRST-set Disjoint? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 (if-then-else) | $iEtS$ | $\varepsilon,\; eS$ | $S'$ | ✓ |
| 3.2 (Three-way) | $ab$ | $cd,\; ef,\; gh$ | $A'$ | ✓ |
| 3.3 (Nested) | $abc$ (then $ab$ in $X$) | $p,\; q,\; \varepsilon$ | $X'$ | ✓ |

---

## 3.5 Comparison Table – Left-Factoring vs. Left-Recursion Elimination

| Property | Left-Recursion Elimination | Left-Factoring |
|---|---|---|
| **Problem solved** | Infinite recursion in top-down parsing | Backtracking on common prefix |
| **Trigger condition** | $A \rightarrow A\alpha$ (direct or indirect) | $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$ |
| **Direction of work** | Pushes the recursive call to the right | Pulls the common prefix to the left |
| **Effect on FIRST sets** | Removes $\varepsilon$ ambiguity at $A$ | Forces FIRST-set disjointness for $A$ |
| **Order in compiler design** | Always applied **first** | Always applied **after** left-recursion elimination |
| **Introduces new non-terminal?** | Yes (e.g., $A'$) | Yes (e.g., $A'$) |
| **Preserves language?** | Yes (language $L(G)$ unchanged) | Yes (language $L(G)$ unchanged) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** When asked to "transform the grammar for LL(1) parsing", students must perform **left-recursion elimination FIRST**, then left-factoring. Reversing this order produces a wrong grammar.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart of the Left-Factoring Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START: Input Grammar G] --> nodeB[For each non-terminal A in G]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Collect all productions of A]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Any two productions share a common prefix alpha?}
    nodeD -->|No| nodeE[Keep A unchanged. Move to next non-terminal]
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeF[Identify longest common prefix alpha]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Strip alpha to get beta1, beta2, ... beta k]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Introduce new non-terminal A prime]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Replace A with A arrow alpha A prime]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Define A prime arrow beta1 pipe beta2 ... pipe beta k]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Recursively check A prime for common prefixes]
    nodeK --> nodeB
    nodeB -->|All non-terminals processed| nodeL[END: Output Factored Grammar G prime]
```

---

## 4.2 Before-and-After Parse Tree Schematic (Example 3.1)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[S] --> nodeIF1[i E t S]
    nodeS1 --> nodeA1[a]
    nodeIF1 --> nodeE1[E]
    nodeIF1 --> nodeI1[i]
    nodeIF1 --> nodeT1[t]
    nodeIF1 --> nodeSrec1[S]

    nodeS2[Factored S] --> nodeIF2[i E t S S prime]
    nodeS2 --> nodeA2[a]
    nodeIF2 --> nodeE2[E]
    nodeIF2 --> nodeI2[i]
    nodeIF2 --> nodeT2[t]
    nodeIF2 --> nodeSrec2[S]
    nodeIF2 --> nodeSp[S prime]
    nodeSp --> nodeEps[epsilon]
    nodeSp --> nodeEonly[e S]
    nodeEonly --> nodeEonlyE[e]
    nodeEonly --> nodeEonlyS[S]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Role of Left-Factoring in a Compiler Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Source Code] --> nodeB[Lexical Analyzer]
    nodeB -->|Token Stream| nodeC[Syntax Analyzer]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Grammar Preprocessing}
    nodeD --> nodeE[Left-Recursion Elimination]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Left-Factoring Transformation]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute FIRST and FOLLOW Sets]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Construct LL 1 Parsing Table]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Predictive Parsing Engine]
    nodeI -->|Parse Tree| nodeJ[Semantic Analyzer]

    style nodeF fill:#FFD966,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeI fill:#9DC3E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The yellow-highlighted block **"Left-Factoring Transformation"** is the focus of this module. Notice it sits *between* left-recursion elimination and FIRST/FOLLOW computation in the standard compiler front-end pipeline.

---

## 4.4 Decision Matrix Diagram – When to Left-Factor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Non-terminal A has productions] --> nodeQ1{Do two or more productions share a common prefix of terminals or non-terminals?}
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeOut1[Grammar is already LL 1 compatible for A. Skip.]
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeQ2{Is the prefix alpha non-empty?}
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeOut2[No factoring needed. Skip.]
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeAct[Apply left-factoring: introduce A prime, factor alpha out]
    nodeAct --> nodeLoop[Recursively check A prime for further common prefixes]
    nodeLoop --> nodeQ1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The recursion loop in the diagram reflects the **iterative nature** of the algorithm — you must re-check *every* newly created non-terminal, because the $\beta_i$ suffixes may themselves share a prefix.
