> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Table-Driven LL(1) Parsers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Table-Driven LL(1) Parsers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Table-Driven LL(1) Parser** is a top-down, predictive, non-recursive syntax analyzer that uses a **two-dimensional parsing table (LL(1) Parsing Table)** indexed by non-terminals (rows) and terminals (rows) to decide which production rule to apply at every step of parsing. The "**LL(1)**" name follows the standard compiler notation:

- **First L** → Scans input from **L**eft to Right.
- **Second L** → Produces a **L**eftmost Derivation.
- **(1)** → Uses **1** look-ahead (one input symbol) to make parsing decisions.

The parser employs an explicit **push-down stack**, an **input buffer** holding the input string terminated by the end-marker **`$`**, and the precomputed **parsing table** `M[A, a]`. There is no backtracking, and no recursive function calls — the entire parsing process is driven by table look-ups.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to (i) construct FIRST and FOLLOW sets, (ii) build the LL(1) parsing table, (iii) simulate table-driven parsing, and (iv) verify whether a grammar is LL(1) by checking the table for multiple entries.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The GPS of Syntax"

Imagine you are navigating a road network (the **grammar**) to reach a destination (the **start symbol's derivation of the input**). A **GPS device** is your **parser**:

- The **map database** is the **parsing table** — at every intersection (non-terminal), it tells you which road (production) to take based on the **direction you are currently facing** (look-ahead terminal).
- Your **car's odometer and route list** act as the **stack** — recording where you have been and where you are going next.
- The **traffic signs at the end of the road** act as the **terminals** — including a final "**Finish Line**" marker `$` indicating successful completion.

If the GPS has **two different routes** listed for the same intersection and the same direction — i.e., the table has a **multiple entry** — then the map (grammar) is **ambiguous** and the GPS cannot give a unique path. The grammar is therefore **not LL(1)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Anchor:**
> A grammar is **LL(1)** ⟺ Its parsing table has **at most one entry** in every cell. Multiple entries ⟹ Grammar is **not LL(1)**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights for KTU Board Answers)

- **Parsing Table `M`**: A 2-D matrix `M[Non-terminal, Terminal ∪ {$}]`.
- **Stack**: LIFO structure initialized with `$S` (where `S` is the start symbol).
- **Look-ahead**: The current input symbol pointed to by the input pointer.
- **Conflict** (LL(1) table): Two or more productions in the same cell — a **left-factoring** or **left-recursion elimination** is required.
- **ε-production**: An empty production used when no terminal matches the current non-terminal.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pre-Requisites for an LL(1) Grammar

A grammar must satisfy **two structural properties** before a deterministic table-driven LL(1) parser can be built:

1. **No Left Recursion** — Productions of the form `A → Aα` must be eliminated via the **Left Recursion Elimination Algorithm**.
2. **Left Factored** — Productions of the form `A → αβ₁ ∣ αβ₂` must be rewritten using the **Left Factoring Algorithm** so that common prefixes are extracted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these conditions?**
> Left recursion causes **infinite loops** in top-down parsers. Common prefixes cause the parser to be unable to decide which production to choose using only one look-ahead.

### 2.2 Building Blocks — FIRST and FOLLOW Sets

These two functions are the mathematical heart of LL(1) table construction.

#### FIRST Set Definition
$$\text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \{ a \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* a\beta \} \cup \{ \varepsilon \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon \}$$

In words: FIRST(α) is the set of terminals that can begin any string derivable from α. If α can derive ε, then ε is also in FIRST(α).

#### FOLLOW Set Definition
$$\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{ a \mid S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta \} \cup \{ \$ \mid a = \$ \text{ and } A \text{ can be the rightmost symbol} \}$$

In words: FOLLOW(A) is the set of terminals that can **immediately follow** the non-terminal A in some sentential form. `$` is included if A can be the rightmost symbol of a sentential form derived from the start symbol.

#### Step-by-Step FIRST Computation Rules

For a string of symbols `X₁X₂...Xₙ`:
- **Rule 1**: Add FIRST(X₁) − {ε} to FIRST(X₁X₂...Xₙ).
- **Rule 2**: If ε ∈ FIRST(X₁), add FIRST(X₂) − {ε}. Continue while ε ∈ FIRST of the previous symbol.
- **Rule 3**: If ε ∈ FIRST(Xᵢ) for **all** i = 1 to n, then add **ε** to FIRST(X₁X₂...Xₙ).

#### Step-by-Step FOLLOW Computation Rules

- **Rule 1**: Place `$` in FOLLOW(S), where S is the start symbol.
- **Rule 2**: If `A → αBβ`, then everything in FIRST(β) except ε is placed in FOLLOW(B).
- **Rule 3**: If `A → αB` or `A → αBβ` with ε ∈ FIRST(β), then everything in FOLLOW(A) is placed in FOLLOW(B).

### 2.3 The LL(1) Parsing Table Construction Algorithm (THE Most Important Algorithm in Module 2)

For each production **`A → α`** in the grammar G:

1. For every terminal `a` in **FIRST(α)**, add `A → α` to table cell `M[A, a]`.
2. If **ε ∈ FIRST(α)**, then for every terminal `b` in **FOLLOW(A)**, add `A → α` to `M[A, b]`.
3. If **ε ∈ FIRST(α)** and **`$ ∈ FOLLOW(A)`**, add `A → α` to `M[A, $]`.
4. If after applying the above rules, any cell `M[A, a]` has **more than one production**, the grammar is **NOT LL(1)**.

### 2.4 The Table-Driven Parsing Algorithm

```
Let w be the input string terminated by $.
Initialize stack with $S (S = start symbol, $ on bottom).
Initialize ip to point to the first symbol of w$.

Repeat:
  Let X = top of stack
  Let a = symbol pointed to by ip

  if X == a == $:
      ACCEPT and halt.
  else if X == a (X is a terminal):
      Pop X from stack
      Advance ip to the next input symbol
  else if M[X, a] == X → Y₁Y₂...Yₖ:
      Pop X from stack
      Push Yₖ, Yₖ₋₁, ..., Y₁ onto stack  (Y₁ on top)
      Output the production X → Y₁Y₂...Yₖ
  else:
      ERROR  (blank entry in table)
```

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation | Definition / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| FIRST set | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ | Set of terminals that can start strings derived from α; includes ε if α can derive ε. |
| FOLLOW set | $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Set of terminals that can immediately follow non-terminal A in any sentential form. |
| Table Entry | $M[A, a]$ | For each `a ∈ FIRST(α)`, place `A → α`; for each `b ∈ FOLLOW(A)` if `ε ∈ FIRST(α)`, place `A → α`. |
| LL(1) Test | Conflict in $M$ | If any cell has more than one production, grammar is **not LL(1)**. |
| Top-of-Stack Match | $X = a$ | Pop terminal X, advance input. |
| Error | $M[X,a] = \text{error}$ | Parsing halts; syntax error reported. |
| Accept | Stack `$` = Input `$` | Parsing is successful. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> LL(1) table-driven parsers are the foundation of hand-written **recursive-descent parsers** used in compilers for languages like Pascal, early Java compilers, and many domain-specific languages (DSLs). The table is the **single source of truth** — separating the **grammar (data)** from the **parsing logic (control)** — which is a classic example of the **table-driven architecture** pattern in software engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

### 3.1 Sample Grammar (Standard KTU Examination Grammar)

Consider the following arithmetic expression grammar G:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E  &\rightarrow T\,E' \\
E' &\rightarrow +\,T\,E' \mid \varepsilon \\
T  &\rightarrow F\,T' \\
T' &\rightarrow *\,F\,T' \mid \varepsilon \\
F  &\rightarrow (\,E\,) \mid \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 0 — Verify Pre-conditions:**
- No left recursion ✓
- Left-factored ✓
- Therefore, it is a candidate for LL(1) table construction.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of FIRST Sets

**Apply the rules iteratively until no change occurs.**

**For E:** `E → T E'`
   - FIRST(T E') requires FIRST(T). Add FIRST(T) − {ε}.
   - `T` cannot derive ε. So FIRST(E) = FIRST(T).

**For E':** `E' → + T E'` and `E' → ε`
   - `+ T E'` starts with terminal `+`. Add `+`.
   - `ε` is an alternative, so add `ε`.
   - **FIRST(E') = {+, ε}**

**For T:** `T → F T'`
   - FIRST(F) − {ε} (since T' cannot force ε to enter from F).

**For T':** `T' → * F T'` and `T' → ε`
   - Add `*`. Add `ε`.
   - **FIRST(T') = {*, ε}**

**For F:** `F → ( E )` and `F → id`
   - Add `(`, add `id`. No ε-production.
   - **FIRST(F) = {(, id}**

**Back-substitution:**
- **FIRST(T) = FIRST(F) = {(, id}**
- **FIRST(E) = FIRST(T) = {(, id}**

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\text{FIRST}(E)  &= \{ (, \text{id} \} \\
\text{FIRST}(E') &= \{ +, \varepsilon \} \\
\text{FIRST}(T)  &= \{ (, \text{id} \} \\
\text{FIRST}(T') &= \{ *, \varepsilon \} \\
\text{FIRST}(F)  &= \{ (, \text{id} \}
\end{aligned}}
$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of FOLLOW Sets

**Rule 1:** Add `$` to FOLLOW(E). **FOLLOW(E) ⊇ {$}**

**For E':** `E → T E'`
   - E' is at the **end** of RHS. So add FOLLOW(E) to FOLLOW(E').
   - **FOLLOW(E') ⊇ {$}**

**For T in `E → T E'`:** T is followed by E', so add FIRST(E') − {ε} to FOLLOW(T).
   - FIRST(E') − {ε} = {+}. So **FOLLOW(T) ⊇ {+}**
   - Since ε ∈ FIRST(E'), also add FOLLOW(E) to FOLLOW(T).
   - **FOLLOW(T) ⊇ {$}**

**For F in `T → F T'`:** Same logic.
   - Add FIRST(T') − {ε} = {*}. **FOLLOW(F) ⊇ {*}**
   - Add FOLLOW(T) to FOLLOW(F). **FOLLOW(F) ⊇ {+, $}**

**For T':** T' is at the end of RHS in `T → F T'`.
   - Add FOLLOW(T) to FOLLOW(T'). **FOLLOW(T') ⊇ {+, $}**

**For E in `F → ( E )`:** E is followed by `)`.
   - Add `)` to FOLLOW(E). **FOLLOW(E) ⊇ {), $}**

**Propagate `)`:** E is followed by `)` in `F → ( E )`. Anything in FOLLOW(F) is added to the FOLLOW of E **only if** ε ∈ FIRST(`)`) — which is false, so no propagation.

**Final Fixed-Point FOLLOW Sets:**

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\text{FOLLOW}(E)  &= \{ \$, ) \} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(E') &= \{ \$, ) \} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(T)  &= \{ +, \$, ) \} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(T') &= \{ +, \$, ) \} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(F)  &= \{ *, +, \$, ) \}
\end{aligned}}
$$

### 3.4 Construction of the LL(1) Parsing Table

For **every** production `A → α`, place it in `M[A, a]` for all `a ∈ FIRST(α)`, and for all `b ∈ FOLLOW(A)` if `ε ∈ FIRST(α)`.

**Production 1: `E → T E'`** — FIRST(TE') = {(, id}
   - `M[E, (] = E → T E'`
   - `M[E, id] = E → T E'`

**Production 2: `E' → + T E'`** — FIRST(+TE') = {+}
   - `M[E', +] = E' → + T E'`

**Production 3: `E' → ε`** — ε ∈ FIRST(ε); FOLLOW(E') = {$, )}
   - `M[E', $] = E' → ε`
   - `M[E', )] = E' → ε`

**Production 4: `T → F T'`** — FIRST(FT') = {(, id}
   - `M[T, (] = T → F T'`
   - `M[T, id] = T → F T'`

**Production 5: `T' → * F T'`** — FIRST(*FT') = {*}
   - `M[T', *] = T' → * F T'`

**Production 6: `T' → ε`** — ε ∈ FIRST(ε); FOLLOW(T') = {+, $, )}
   - `M[T', +] = T' → ε`
   - `M[T', $] = T' → ε`
   - `M[T', )] = T' → ε`

**Production 7: `F → ( E )`** — FIRST((E)) = {(}
   - `M[F, (] = F → ( E )`

**Production 8: `F → id`** — FIRST(id) = {id}
   - `M[F, id] = F → id`

### 3.5 Final LL(1) Parsing Table (Rendered as a Matrix)

| NT \ T | **id** | **+** | **\*** | **(** | **)** | **$** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **E** | $E \to TE'$ | | | $E \to TE'$ | | |
| **E'** | | $E' \to +TE'$ | | | $E' \to \varepsilon$ | $E' \to \varepsilon$ |
| **T** | $T \to FT'$ | | | $T \to FT'$ | | |
| **T'** | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to *FT'$ | | $T' \to \varepsilon$ | $T' \to \varepsilon$ |
| **F** | $F \to \text{id}$ | | | $F \to (E)$ | | |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Every non-empty cell contains **exactly one** production. The grammar is therefore **LL(1)**. ✓

### 3.6 Trace — Table-Driven Parsing of Input `id + id $`

We trace the algorithm step-by-step. The format is: **(Stack, Input) → Action**.

| Step | Stack (top → bottom) | Input Buffer | Action |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $E$ | id + id $ | $M[E, \text{id}] = E \to TE'$. Pop E, push E' then T. |
| 2 | $E' T$ | id + id $ | $M[T, \text{id}] = T \to FT'$. Pop T, push T' then F. |
| 3 | $E' T' F$ | id + id $ | $M[F, \text{id}] = F \to \text{id}$. Pop F, push id. |
| 4 | $E' T' \text{id}$ | id + id $ | Top `id` matches input `id`. Pop, advance. |
| 5 | $E' T'$ | + id $ | $M[T', +] = T' \to \varepsilon$. Pop T', push nothing. |
| 6 | $E'$ | + id $ | $M[E', +] = E' \to +TE'$. Pop E', push E', T, +. |
| 7 | $E' T +$ | + id $ | Top `+` matches. Pop, advance. |
| 8 | $E' T$ | id $ | $M[T, \text{id}] = T \to FT'$. Pop, push T', F. |
| 9 | $E' T' F$ | id $ | $M[F, \text{id}] = F \to \text{id}$. Pop F, push id. |
| 10 | $E' T' \text{id}$ | id $ | Match. Pop, advance. |
| 11 | $E' T'$ | $ | $M[T', \$] = T' \to \varepsilon$. Pop. |
| 12 | $E'$ | $ | $M[E', \$] = E' \to \varepsilon$. Pop. |
| 13 | $ | $ | Both `$`. **ACCEPT ✓** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** During the trace, the order in which symbols are pushed onto the stack is **reverse** of the RHS of the production, so the **first symbol of RHS becomes the new top of the stack**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Table-Driven LL(1) Parser Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    grammar["Grammar G"] --> firstset["Compute FIRST sets"]
    firstset --> followset["Compute FOLLOW sets"]
    followset --> tablebuild["Build Parsing Table M A a"]
    tablebuild --> verify{"Multiple entries in any cell"}
    verify -- "Yes" --> notLL1["Grammar is NOT LL1"]
    verify -- "No" --> ready["Grammar is LL1 - proceed"]

    input["Input Buffer w dollar"] --> parse
    stack["Pushdown Stack dollar S"] --> parse
    tablebuild --> parse
    ready --> parse

    parse["Parsing Engine"] --> decision{"Top of Stack X vs Lookahead a"}

    decision -- "X is terminal and X equals a" --> match["Pop X, advance input pointer"]
    decision -- "X is nonterminal and M X a equals A to alpha" --> apply["Pop X, push alpha reversed, output production"]
    decision -- "X equals dollar and a equals dollar" --> accept["ACCEPT - Parsing Successful"]
    decision -- "M X a is error" --> reject["ERROR - Syntax Error Reported"]

    match --> decision
    apply --> decision
    accept --> stop1(["Halt"])
    reject --> stop2(["Halt with Error"])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph pre["Pre-Parse Phase Off-Line"]
        A1["Grammar G"] --> A2["Eliminate Left Recursion"]
        A2 --> A3["Left Factor the Grammar"]
        A3 --> A4["Compute FIRST for all symbols"]
        A4 --> A5["Compute FOLLOW for all non-terminals"]
        A5 --> A6["Construct LL1 Parsing Table M"]
    end

    subgraph runtime["Run-Time Parsing Phase"]
        B1["Input String w dollar"] --> B2["Initialize Stack to dollar S"]
        B6["Parsing Table M"] --> B3["Look up M X a"]
        B2 --> B3
        B3 --> B4{"Match Terminal or Apply Production"}
        B4 -- "Terminal Match" --> B5["Pop and Advance"]
        B4 -- "Apply Production" --> B7["Pop X push RHS reversed"]
        B4 -- "Accept" --> B8["ACCEPT"]
        B4 -- "Blank Entry" --> B9["Syntax ERROR"]
    end

    A6 --> B6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Work for This Topic:**
> The table-driven parser is naturally a **data-driven architecture** — the grammar logic lives in the table (data), while the engine (control) is generic. This is the exact architectural principle used in **Yacc / Bison**, **ANTLR**, and **JavaCC**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define LL(1) grammar. What is the role of the look-ahead symbol in an LL(1) parser?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A grammar G is called **LL(1)** if its parsing table constructed using FIRST and FOLLOW sets contains **at most one production in every cell**, i.e., the parser can always decide which production to apply by looking at **one input symbol (look-ahead)** without ambiguity. The look-ahead symbol is the **current terminal** pointed to by the input pointer; it indexes the **column** of the LL(1) parsing table when the **top of stack** is a non-terminal, thereby disambiguating which production rule to apply. (1 mark definition + 1 mark table property + 1 mark look-ahead role.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Why must a grammar be free of left recursion before constructing an LL(1) parsing table?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A left-recursive grammar contains a production of the form `A → Aα`. During top-down parsing, to expand A, the parser applies this production, which **again brings A on top of the stack** — leading to an **infinite loop** without consuming any input. Since the LL(1) parser has **no backtracking**, it cannot recover. Therefore, **all left recursion must be eliminated** (using the left-recursion elimination algorithm) before building the LL(1) parsing table. (1 mark problem + 1 mark infinite loop + 1 mark elimination needed.)

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2]**

**Q. (a)** Consider the following grammar:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow A\,B\,C \\
A &\rightarrow a \mid \varepsilon \\
B &\rightarrow b\,B \mid \varepsilon \\
C &\rightarrow c
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i)** Compute the **FIRST** sets for all non-terminals. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Compute the **FOLLOW** sets for all non-terminals. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** Construct the **LL(1) parsing table** for the above grammar and verify whether it is LL(1). Parse the input string **"a b c $"** using the table, showing each step of the stack and input buffer. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Model Solution for Question Choice A

#### Part (a)(i) — FIRST Sets (3 marks)

Applying the FIRST rules iteratively:

- **FIRST(C)** = FIRST(c) = **{c}**
- **FIRST(A)** = FIRST(a) ∪ FIRST(ε) = **{a, ε}**
- **FIRST(B)** = FIRST(bB) ∪ FIRST(ε) = **{b, ε}**
- **FIRST(S)** = FIRST(ABC). Apply Rule 1, 2, 3. Add FIRST(A) − {ε} = {a}. Since ε ∈ FIRST(A), add FIRST(B) − {ε} = {b}. Since ε ∈ FIRST(B), add FIRST(C) = {c}. So **FIRST(S) = {a, b, c}**.

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\text{FIRST}(S) &= \{ a, b, c \} \\
\text{FIRST}(A) &= \{ a, \varepsilon \} \\
\text{FIRST}(B) &= \{ b, \varepsilon \} \\
\text{FIRST}(C) &= \{ c \}
\end{aligned}}
$$

#### Part (a)(ii) — FOLLOW Sets (4 marks)

- **Rule 1:** `FOLLOW(S)` ⊇ `{$}`. (S is start symbol)
- **In S → A B C:** A is followed by B. Add FIRST(B) − {ε} = {b} to FOLLOW(A). Since ε ∈ FIRST(B), also add FOLLOW(S) to FOLLOW(A).
- B is followed by C. Add FIRST(C) − {ε} = {c} to FOLLOW(B). Since ε ∉ FIRST(C), do not add FOLLOW(S).
- C is at the end. Add FOLLOW(S) to FOLLOW(C).

Final fixed-point:

$$
\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\text{FOLLOW}(S) &= \{ \$ \} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(A) &= \{ b, \$ \} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(B) &= \{ c \} \\
\text{FOLLOW}(C) &= \{ \$ \}
\end{aligned}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Parsing Table Construction (4 marks) and Parsing Trace (3 marks)

**Parsing Table:**

| NT \ T | **a** | **b** | **c** | **$** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **S** | $S \to ABC$ | $S \to ABC$ | $S \to ABC$ | |
| **A** | $A \to a$ | $A \to \varepsilon$ | | $A \to \varepsilon$ |
| **B** | | $B \to bB$ | $B \to \varepsilon$ | |
| **C** | | | $C \to c$ | |

**Verification:** Every non-empty cell has exactly one entry ⟹ Grammar is **LL(1)** ✓

**Parsing Trace for `"a b c $"`:**

| # | Stack | Input | Action |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $S | a b c $ | $M[S, a] = S \to ABC$ |
| 2 | $C B A | a b c $ | $M[A, a] = A \to a$ |
| 3 | $C B a | a b c $ | Match `a`, advance |
| 4 | $C B | b c $ | $M[B, b] = B \to bB$ |
| 5 | $C B b | b c $ | Match `b`, advance |
| 6 | $C B | c $ | $M[B, c] = B \to \varepsilon$ |
| 7 | $C | c $ | $M[C, c] = C \to c$ |
| 8 | $C c | c $ | Match `c`, advance |
| 9 | $C | $ | $M[C, \$] = \text{error}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **input has a `$` terminator** in the buffer; for `"a b c $"` step 9 expects `$`, but C's table column `$` is blank — meaning the parser **rejects**. The correct test input for this grammar is `"a b c $"` is actually invalid because after consuming `a, b, c`, C's expected terminal `$` is not in FIRST(C). The correct string would be something that exercises C properly, e.g., `"a b c"` after which stack would expect `$` from FOLLOW(C). *(Always re-check the test string during valuation!)*

---

### 5.4 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 2]**

**Q. (a)** For the grammar below, compute the **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets for every non-terminal. Show all iterations. **(7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow A\,B \\
A &\rightarrow a\,A \mid \varepsilon \\
B &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Q. (b)** Build the **LL(1) parsing table** and parse the input string **"a a b $"** by tracing the stack and input pointer at each step. State whether the grammar is LL(1). **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.5 Model Solution for Question Choice B (Outline)

#### Part (a) — FIRST & FOLLOW (7 marks)

- FIRST(A) = {a, ε}; FIRST(B) = {b}; FIRST(S) = {a, b}.
- FOLLOW(S) = {$}; FOLLOW(A) = FOLLOW(S) ∪ FIRST(B)−{ε} = {$, b}; FOLLOW(B) = {$}.

#### Part (b) — Table & Trace (7 marks)

**Table:**

| NT \ T | **a** | **b** | **$** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **S** | $S \to AB$ | $S \to AB$ | |
| **A** | $A \to aA$ | $A \to \varepsilon$ | $A \to \varepsilon$ |
| **B** | | $B \to b$ | |

Grammar is LL(1) ✓. Trace shows A consumes `a a` via two `A → aA` applications, then `A → ε` for `b`, then B consumes `b`. ACCEPT.

---

### 5.6 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in LL(1) Table Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include `$` in FOLLOW(S)** — loses 1 mark. `$` is **always** in FOLLOW of the start symbol.
> 2. **Forgetting the ε-propagation rule** in FOLLOW: "If A → αB and ε ∈ FIRST(β) (or β is empty), add FOLLOW(A) to FOLLOW(B)" — this is the most-skipped rule.
> 3. **Pushing symbols in wrong order** during the trace: students push RHS left-to-right, but the parser pushes **right-to-left** so the first symbol is on top.
> 4. **Ignoring iterative fixed-point**: A single pass over productions is **not** enough; loops must be re-evaluated until no new symbol is added to any FIRST/FOLLOW set.
> 5. **Not checking for multiple entries**: A table with two productions in one cell automatically makes the grammar **not LL(1)** — students often build the table but skip the conflict check.
> 6. **Mixing FIRST(α) with FOLLOW(A)**: In step 1 of the table algorithm, the row is the **LHS non-terminal A**, and the column comes from **FIRST(α) — NOT FOLLOW(A)** (FOLLOW is only used when ε ∈ FIRST(α)).

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LL(1) = Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1 look-ahead.**
- The grammar must be **free of left recursion** and **left-factored** before table construction.
- **FIRST(α)**: terminals that begin strings derivable from α; include ε if derivable.
- **FOLLOW(A)**: terminals that can appear immediately after A; always contains `$` for the start symbol.
- **Table Algorithm**: For `A → α`, fill `M[A, a]` for `a ∈ FIRST(α)`; if ε ∈ FIRST(α), fill `M[A, b]` for `b ∈ FOLLOW(A)`.
- **Multiple entries in a cell ⟹ Grammar is NOT LL(1)**.
- **Stack Operation**: On applying `A → Y₁Y₂...Yₖ`, pop A and push `Yₖ, Yₖ₋₁, ..., Y₁` so that `Y₁` becomes the new top.
- **ACCEPT condition**: Top of stack = `$` AND look-ahead = `$` simultaneously.
- **Error condition**: `M[X, a]` is blank when X is a non-terminal and a is a terminal.
- The **table-driven approach separates grammar (data) from parsing logic (control)** — a foundational idea in compiler construction.
- **Real-world use**: Bison, Yacc, ANTLR, JavaCC all rely on variants of LL/LR table-driven parsing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IR Taxonomy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** IR Taxonomy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Intermediate Representation (IR)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> An **Intermediate Representation (IR)** is a data structure (or set of data structures) used by a compiler to represent the source program between the **front-end** (after parsing) and the **back-end** (before code generation). It is *abstract* in the sense that it is independent of the source language on one side and the target machine on the other.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (PBCRT604), Module 3 explicitly focuses on **Intermediate Code Generation**, of which the classification of IRs — known as the **IR Taxonomy** — forms the conceptual foundation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need an IR at all?**
> A modern compiler (like GCC's GIMPLE, LLVM's LLVM IR, Java's Bytecode) never translates source code *directly* to machine code. It first lowers the high-level language into a structured **IR**, performs **optimisations** on the IR, and then lowers the IR to machine code. This separation enables **retargeting** (same front-end, many back-ends) and **reusable optimisations** across languages.

---

## 1.2 The IR Taxonomy — A Bird's-Eye View

The **IR Taxonomy** is the *formal classification* of intermediate representations based on their **structural organisation** and **level of abstraction**. As per the standard reference taxonomy (Muchnick/Aho–Sethi–Ullman/Waite–Goos), IRs fall into three master families:

### (a) Structural IRs
These preserve the **syntactic/semantic structure** of the program as a graph or tree.
- **Tree-based IRs** → e.g., *Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)*
- **DAG-based IRs** → e.g., *Directed Acyclic Graph for expression representation*
- **Graph-based IRs** → e.g., *Control Flow Graph (CFG)*, *Data Flow Graph (DFG)*, *Call Graph*, *Dependence Graph*

### (b) Linear IRs
These represent the program as a **sequential list (or stream) of instructions** — similar in spirit to assembly, but virtual.
- **Three-Address Code (TAC)** → most popular KTU exam example
- **Stack-based IRs** → e.g., **Java Bytecode (JVM)**, Postfix notation
- **Register-based linear IRs** → e.g., simple two-address code

### (c) Hybrid IRs
Combine a graph representation (e.g., a CFG) where each *node* holds a linear IR (e.g., TAC) — this is the model used by **LLVM IR** and **GCC GIMPLE**.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Translator's "Rough Draft Notebook":**
> Imagine a translator converting a Hindi novel into French.
> 1. First, they understand the *syntactic structure* of Hindi sentences — that's a **tree-based IR (AST)**.
> 2. Then, they rewrite each sentence as simple "Subject → Verb → Object" triplets — that's a **linear IR (TAC)**.
> 3. Finally, they map the flow of who-does-what-when across chapters — that's a **graph IR (CFG)**.
> 
> The IR is the translator's "working language" — neither Hindi nor French, but a structured intermediate form that allows editing, polishing, and rewriting (i.e., optimisation) before producing the final French book (machine code).

---

## 1.4 Formal Taxonomy Hierarchy

$$
\text{IR Taxonomy}
\begin{cases}
\text{Structural IRs} \begin{cases} \text{Tree IR (AST)} \\ \text{DAG IR} \\ \text{Graph IR} \begin{cases} \text{CFG} \\ \text{DFG} \\ \text{Call Graph} \\ \text{Dependence Graph} \end{cases} \end{cases} \\[6pt]
\text{Linear IRs} \begin{cases} \text{Three-Address Code (TAC)} \\ \text{Stack-based IR (Bytecode)} \\ \text{Two-Address Code} \end{cases} \\[6pt]
\text{Hybrid IRs (CFG of TAC blocks — LLVM/GIMPLE)}
\end{cases}
$$

---

## 1.5 Standard KTU-Style Terminology Glossary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Term | Standard Meaning (KTU 2024) |
> |---|---|
> | **AST** | Abstract Syntax Tree — tree of syntactic constructs |
> | **DAG** | Directed Acyclic Graph — merges common subexpressions |
> | **TAC** | Three-Address Code — at most 3 operands per instruction |
> | **CFG** | Control Flow Graph — nodes = basic blocks, edges = control flow |
> | **SSA** | Static Single Assignment — each variable assigned exactly once |
> | **Basic Block** | Maximal sequence of instructions with single entry/single exit |
> | **JVM Bytecode** | Stack-based linear IR used by Java |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Design Properties of an IR

The KTU 2024 syllabus (following Aho et al. & Muchnick) evaluates every IR using **seven canonical design criteria**. An examiner expects you to know each one cold:

| # | Property | Question it answers | Example |
|---|----------|---------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Ease of Generation** | Can the front-end easily produce it? | AST (easy) > TAC (medium) > SSA (hard) |
| 2 | **Ease of Manipulation** | Can optimisations be written on it? | TAC/CFG (easy) > AST (hard) |
| 3 | **Level of Abstraction** | How close is it to source vs. target? | AST (high) < TAC (mid) < Machine code (low) |
| 4 | **Control-Flow Explicitness** | Is control flow visible? | CFG (explicit) < AST (implicit) |
| 5 | **Data-Flow Explicitness** | Are def-use chains visible? | SSA (explicit) < AST (implicit) |
| 6 | **Procedure Size** | Compactness of representation | AST (compact) < TAC (verbose) |
| 7 | **Naming (Anonymous vs Named)** | Does it preserve variable names? | TAC (named) < SSA (uniquely renamed) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** "SSA is *not* a separate IR — it is a *property* of an IR (typically TAC). An IR is said to be in SSA form when every variable is assigned exactly once and every use refers to a unique definition."

---

## 2.2 Linear IR — The Three-Address Code (TAC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (TAC):**
> A **Three-Address Code (TAC)** instruction has the general form:
> $$x = y \;\text{op}\; z$$
> where `x, y, z` are *names* (variables, temporaries, constants), and `op` is a binary operator. The "three addresses" refer to the three operands. Unary ops take two addresses; branch/jump ops are special forms.

### TAC Instruction Set (KTU Standard)

| Form | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `x = y op z` | Binary operation |
| `x = op y` | Unary operation (e.g., `x = -y`, `x = not y`) |
| `x = y` | Simple copy |
| `goto L` | Unconditional jump to label L |
| `if x goto L` / `ifFalse x goto L` | Conditional jump |
| `if x relop y goto L` | Relational branch |
| `param x` / `call p, n` / `y = call p, n` | Procedure call |
| `return y` | Return value |

### TAC Quadruple Representation

Each TAC instruction is stored as a **quadruple (4-tuple)**: `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`. This is the canonical implementation used in KTU problems.

$$
\text{Quadruple} = (\text{op}, \;\text{arg}_1, \;\text{arg}_2, \;\text{result})
$$

---

## 2.3 Stack-Based vs Register-Based Linear IR

| Feature | Stack-Based (e.g., JVM Bytecode) | Register-Based (e.g., TAC) |
|---------|----------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Operand location | Implicitly on operand stack | Explicitly named (registers/temporaries) |
| Push/Pop ops | Required (`iload`, `iadd`) | Not required |
| Code size | **Smaller** (one-byte opcodes) | Larger |
| Optimisation | Harder (no names) | Easier (named operands) |
| Example | `iadd`, `iload_1`, `istore_2` | `t1 = a + b` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stack-based IR rule:** To apply a binary operator like `+`, both operands are first pushed onto the stack; the op pops them, computes, and pushes the result. For `a + b * c` in JVM bytecode: `iload a → iload b → iload c → imul → iadd → istore t`.

---

## 2.4 Structural IRs — AST, DAG, CFG

### 2.4.1 Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
- Each **internal node** = an operator.
- Each **leaf node** = an operand.
- **Abstract** = no punctuation/parens (they are implicit in the tree shape).

### 2.4.2 Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
- A **compressed AST** that *merges* identical subexpressions.
- A variable appearing multiple times = multiple pointers to **one node**.
- Used in KTU to compute **common subexpression elimination** and **value numbers**.

### 2.4.3 Control Flow Graph (CFG)
- **Nodes** = Basic Blocks.
- **Edges** = Possible transfer of control.
- The **entry block** has no incoming edges; the **exit block** has no outgoing edges (conceptually).

---

## 2.5 SSA (Static Single Assignment) Form

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SSA Property:** Every variable is a *target* of assignment *exactly once* in the program text. A re-assignment requires a new name (using a $\phi$-function at control-flow merge points).
> $$\text{Example: } x = x + 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_2 = x_1 + 1$$
> SSA is heavily used in modern optimising compilers (LLVM, GCC) because **def-use chains become trivial** — each use has exactly one definition.

---

## 2.6 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **All Key IR Design Equations & Rules** (KTU 2024)

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| TAC General Form | $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ | Backbone of optimising compilers |
| Quadruple Tuple | $(\text{op}, \arg_1, \arg_2, \text{result})$ | Storage unit in TAC implementations |
| SSA Re-naming | $x \rightarrow x_1, x_2, \ldots$ per def | Enables efficient data-flow analysis |
| DAG Node Count | $\vert N \vert = n_{\text{distinct subexpressions}}$ | Measures CSE benefit |
| Basic Block Size | $n_{\text{stmts in block}}$ (between leaders) | Affects CFG granularity |
| Jump Density | $\rho = \frac{n_{\text{branches}}}{n_{\text{TAC instrs}}}$ | Optimisation cost indicator |
| Stack Op Count | $S(n) = 2n - 1$ for n-ary expression | Predicts JVM bytecode length |
| TAC Operands Limit | $\le 3$ per instruction | Hard constraint of TAC form |
| SSA $\phi$-function | $x_3 = \phi(x_1, x_2)$ at merge | Resolves multiple reaching defs |

> **CRITICAL: KTU explicitly tests** the ability to (i) classify an IR into the taxonomy, (ii) justify *why* by naming its properties, and (iii) compare two IRs along the 7 design axes.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GCC (GNU Compiler Collection)** uses a structural IR called **GIMPLE** (a hybrid: CFG + TAC).
- **LLVM** uses the **LLVM IR** (an SSA-form, hybrid, RISC-like, infinitely-register TAC).
- **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** uses **stack-based bytecode** — a linear IR.
- **MSVC** uses a proprietary **MSIL** (Microsoft Intermediate Language) — a stack-based IR.
- **TensorFlow XLA** uses **HLO IR** (High-Level Optimiser) — a graph IR.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example Master Class:** We will take the same source-level expression and derive it into **three different IRs** — AST, DAG, and TAC — to *prove* why the IR taxonomy matters. The expression is the KTU classic:
> $$a = b \times (-c) + b \times (-c)$$

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)

### Step 1: Identify the syntactic structure
The expression is `a = b * (-c) + b * (-c)`. Precedence: unary `-` > `*` > `+`. Assignment is right-associative.

### Step 2: Build the tree bottom-up
- Innermost: `-c` → node `uminus` with child `c`.
- `b * (-c)` → node `*` with children `b` and `uminus(c)`.
- Two such identical subtrees are present.
- Sum them: `+` node combines the two `*` subtrees.
- Assign to `a`: `=` node with `a` and the `+` subtree.

### Resulting AST
```
         =
        / \
       a   +
          / \
         *   *
        / \ / \
       b  u b  u
          |    |
          c    c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Property demonstrated:** Notice the AST *duplicates* the subtree `-c` and the subtree `b * (-c)`. The AST has **no compactness optimisation** — it represents each syntactic occurrence as a distinct subtree.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)

### Step 1: Re-use identical subexpressions
While building the DAG, when we encounter `-c` the second time, we **do not** create a new node — we add a pointer to the existing `uminus(c)` node. Same for the second `b * (-c)`.

### Step 2: Resulting DAG
```
         =
        / \
       a   +
          / \
         *   *
          \ /
           u
           |
           c
        (shared)        ← Note: b and c may also be reused if multi-occurrences
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property demonstrated:** The DAG has **strictly fewer nodes** than the AST for the same expression. This is the structural foundation of **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**. The number of leaf/internal nodes *reduces* linearly with subexpression redundancy.

### Step 3: Algorithmic value numbering (KTU formula)
$$
N_{\text{DAG}} = N_{\text{AST}} - k
$$
where $k$ is the number of redundant subexpressions *merged*. In our example, the subtree `-c` appears twice and `b * (-c)` appears twice, so:
$$
N_{\text{AST}} = 11, \quad N_{\text{DAG}} = 9, \quad k = 2
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Three-Address Code (TAC)

### Step 1: Apply TAC rules — at most three operands per instruction; introduce temporaries.

### Step 2: Generate TAC by linearising the DAG (post-order traversal)

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= \text{uminus}\; c \\
t_2 &= t_1 \times b \\
t_3 &= \text{uminus}\; c \quad \text{(re-use the same temporary, OR re-compute)}\\
t_4 &= t_3 \times b \\
t_5 &= t_2 + t_4 \\
a   &= t_5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)
Since `t_1` and `t_3` compute the *same* value, we reuse:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= \text{uminus}\; c \\
t_2 &= t_1 \times b \\
t_5 &= t_2 + t_2 \\
a   &= t_5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Killer insight for KTU:** TAC without CSE = 6 instructions. TAC with CSE = 4 instructions. The *same* expression yielded a DAG with 9 nodes and a TAC with 4 instructions. The IR choice has a **direct, measurable impact** on program compactness and optimisation ease.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Stack-Based Bytecode Translation

Take the expression: `a = b + c * d`

### Step 1: Convert to postfix
`b c d * + a =` (Reverse Polish)

### Step 2: Emit JVM-style bytecode

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{iload } b \\
&\text{iload } c \\
&\text{iload } d \\
&\text{imul} \\
&\text{iadd} \\
&\text{istore } a
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Verify the stack semantics
$$
\begin{array}{|c|}
\hline
\text{Stack at each step} \\
\hline
[\;] \\
[\;b\;] \\
[\;b, c\;] \\
[\;b, c, d\;] \\
[\;b, c \times d\;] \\
[\;b + c \times d\;] \\
[\;] \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **stack depth** for an n-operand binary expression tree is equal to the *height* of the expression tree (i.e., the length of its longest root-to-leaf path). This is why deeply nested expressions can cause **stack overflow** in JVMs — a real engineering concern.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven-Axis IR Comparison Table** (this is the **most-asked** KTU 14-mark structure)

| Property | AST | DAG | TAC | SSA | CFG | JVM Bytecode |
|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|--------------|
| **Ease of Generation** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★ |
| **Ease of Manipulation** | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ |
| **Level of Abstraction** | Highest | High | Mid | Mid | High | Low |
| **Control-Flow Explicit?** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| **Data-Flow Explicit?** | ❌ | ❌ | Partial | ✅ (def-use) | Partial | ❌ |
| **Procedure Size** | Compact | Compact | Verbose | Verbose | Medium | Very Compact |
| **Naming** | Named | Named | Named | Unique | Named | Anonymous (stack) |

---

## 3.6 SSA Transformation Algorithm (KTU Step-by-Step)

To convert TAC to SSA, follow this rigorous KTU-expected algorithm:

1. **Insert $\phi$-functions** at every join point (node in CFG with multiple predecessors).
2. **Rename** each definition: at each definition of variable $v$, create a fresh name $v_i$.
3. **Propagate** the new name down the dominance tree: every use of $v$ within a renamed scope refers to the freshest $v_i$ that dominates it.
4. **Fix-up at merges:** uses dominated by multiple $\phi$-function definitions refer to the appropriate $\phi$-output.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A $\phi$-function is *not* an executable instruction — it is a **notational device** to express the *merger of values* from different control-flow paths. In machine code, $\phi$-functions are removed by **register allocation** (called "phi-elimination").

---

## 3.7 CFG Construction for a TAC Block

Given TAC:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&t_1 = a + b \\
&\text{if } t_1 < c \text{ goto } L_1 \\
&t_2 = a \times c \\
&L_1: \; t_3 = t_1 \times t_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step-by-step CFG construction

1. **Identify leaders:**
   - First instruction is a leader (entry).
   - Targets of conditional/unconditional jumps are leaders.
   - Instructions immediately following jumps are leaders.

2. **Group into basic blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &: \quad t_1 = a + b \; ; \; \text{if } t_1 < c \text{ goto } L_1 \\
B_2 &: \quad t_2 = a \times c \; ; \; \text{goto } L_2 \\
B_3 &: \quad t_3 = t_1 \times t_2
\end{aligned}
$$

3. **Draw edges:**
   - $B_1 \rightarrow B_3$ (true branch)
   - $B_1 \rightarrow B_2$ (false branch)
   - $B_2 \rightarrow B_3$ (unconditional fall-through)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CFG is a *graph IR* — its nodes hold linear IR (TAC) → this is exactly the **hybrid IR** model used by LLVM and GIMPLE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master IR Taxonomy Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IR Taxonomy] --> B[Structural IR]
    A --> C[Linear IR]
    A --> D[Hybrid IR]

    B --> B1[Tree IR - AST]
    B --> B2[DAG IR]
    B --> B3[Graph IR]

    B3 --> B3a[Control Flow Graph CFG]
    B3 --> B3b[Data Flow Graph DFG]
    B3 --> B3c[Call Graph]
    B3 --> B3d[Dependence Graph]

    C --> C1[Three Address Code TAC]
    C --> C2[Stack Based Bytecode]
    C --> C3[Two Address Code]

    D --> D1[CFG of TAC blocks]
    D1 --> D1a[LLVM IR]
    D1 --> D1b[GCC GIMPLE]
    D1 --> D1c[MSVC MSIL]
```

---

## 4.2 Seven Design Properties of an IR — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Property 1: Ease of Generation]
    P2[Property 2: Ease of Manipulation]
    P3[Property 3: Level of Abstraction]
    P4[Property 4: Control Flow Explicitness]
    P5[Property 5: Data Flow Explicitness]
    P6[Property 6: Procedure Size]
    P7[Property 7: Naming Convention]

    P1 --> E[Evaluator: KTU 14-mark Comparison]
    P2 --> E
    P3 --> E
    P4 --> E
    P5 --> E
    P6 --> E
    P7 --> E

    E --> R[Result: Best IR for Each Pass]
    R --> R1[Front End: AST]
    R --> R2[Optimisation: SSA on CFG]
    R --> R3[Code Gen: Linear IR]
```

---

## 4.3 TAC Quadruple Storage Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1["Quadruple 1"]
        Q1A[op: ADD]
        Q1B[arg1: a]
        Q1C[arg2: b]
        Q1D[result: t1]
    end

    subgraph Q2["Quadruple 2"]
        Q2A[op: MUL]
        Q2B[arg1: t1]
        Q2C[arg2: c]
        Q2D[result: t2]
    end

    subgraph Q3["Quadruple 3"]
        Q3A[op: ASSIGN]
        Q3B[arg1: t2]
        Q3C[arg2: NIL]
        Q3D[result: x]
    end

    Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3
```

---

## 4.4 SSA Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original TAC Program] --> B[Build Dominator Tree]
    B --> C[Insert Phi-Functions at Join Points]
    C --> D[Rename Variables Along Dom Tree]
    D --> E[SSA Form Validated]
    E --> F[Run Optimisations on SSA]
    F --> G[Phi-Elimination via Register Allocation]
    G --> H[Final Machine Code]
```

---

## 4.5 Hybrid IR Model — LLVM/GIMPLE Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Module["Function Level"]
        direction TB
        subgraph BB1["Basic Block 1"]
            I1["t1 = a + b"]
            I2["t2 = t1 * c"]
            I3["if t2 > 0 goto BB3"]
        end
        subgraph BB2["Basic Block 2"]
            J1["t3 = a - b"]
            J2["goto BB3"]
        end
        subgraph BB3["Basic Block 3"]
            K1["return t2 or t3"]
        end
    end

    BB1 -->|false branch| BB2
    BB1 -->|true branch| BB3
    BB2 --> BB3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering note:** In LLVM IR, each *Basic Block* is itself a list of LLVM instructions (a linear IR), and the **Function** is a graph of these blocks. This is *exactly* the hybrid IR pattern that KTU 2024 examiners expect students to recognise.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define an Intermediate Representation (IR) and list the three main classes of IRs in the IR Taxonomy.**

**Model Answer:**
An Intermediate Representation (IR) is a data structure used internally by a compiler to represent the source program during the translation process, sitting between the front-end and the back-end. It is language-independent on one side and machine-independent on the other, enabling retargeting and reusable optimisations.

The three main classes of IRs in the IR Taxonomy are:
1. **Structural IRs** — preserve the syntactic/semantic structure (e.g., AST, DAG, CFG, DFG).
2. **Linear IRs** — represent the program as a sequential list of instructions (e.g., TAC, stack-based bytecode).
3. **Hybrid IRs** — combine a graph structure where each node contains a linear IR (e.g., LLVM IR, GIMPLE).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is Three-Address Code (TAC)? Why is it the most widely used IR form in compiler textbooks?**

**Model Answer:**
TAC is a linear IR where each instruction has at most three operands, with the general form `x = y op z`. Binary operations, unary operations, copies, conditional and unconditional jumps, and procedure calls are all expressible in TAC.

It is widely used because:
1. It is **easy to generate** from ASTs and DAGs.
2. It is **easy to manipulate** for data-flow analysis and optimisation.
3. It is **machine-independent**, making it ideal for portable optimisers.
4. The **3-operand constraint** mirrors real RISC instruction sets, easing code generation.
5. It supports **named temporaries**, enabling straightforward def-use analysis.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — IR Taxonomy Pitfalls:**
> - Do **not** confuse SSA as a *separate* IR. SSA is a *property* of an IR (typically TAC).
> - Always mention **all 7 design properties** when comparing two IRs; partial lists lose marks.
> - DAG ≠ CFG. DAG is for *expression-level* CSE; CFG is for *program-level* control flow.
> - Java Bytecode is *stack-based*, not register-based. Confusing this loses 2–3 marks.
> - When asked for TAC, **always show the temporaries** explicitly (`t1, t2, t3...`).

---

### 📝 QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
#### **(a)** Explain the IR Taxonomy in detail with suitable examples for each class. Discuss the seven design properties that govern the choice of an IR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The IR Taxonomy classifies intermediate representations used in compilers into three principal families based on their structural organisation:

**1. Structural IRs:**
These preserve the program structure as trees or graphs. Examples include:
- **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST):** Tree representation of the program's syntactic structure, where internal nodes are operators and leaves are operands. Punctuation is implicit.
- **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG):** A compressed AST where common subexpressions are merged into shared nodes, enabling Common Subexpression Elimination.
- **Control Flow Graph (CFG):** Nodes are basic blocks (maximal sequences of straight-line code), and edges represent possible control transfers. Used heavily in data-flow analysis.
- **Data Flow Graph (DFG):** Nodes are operations; edges represent data dependencies between them.
- **Call Graph:** Nodes are procedures; edges represent calling relationships.

**2. Linear IRs:**
These represent the program as a sequential list of instructions. Examples:
- **Three-Address Code (TAC):** Each instruction has the form `x = y op z`, with at most three operands.
- **Stack-based bytecode:** Operand locations are implicit on an operand stack (e.g., JVM bytecode).
- **Two-Address code:** Compact linear IR where the destination is one of the operands.

**3. Hybrid IRs:**
A graph (typically a CFG) where each node holds a linear IR (typically TAC). Used by **LLVM IR** and **GCC GIMPLE**.

**Seven Design Properties:**
1. **Ease of Generation** — How easily the front-end produces the IR.
2. **Ease of Manipulation** — How easily optimisations can be applied.
3. **Level of Abstraction** — How close to source vs. machine.
4. **Control-Flow Explicitness** — Whether control flow is directly visible.
5. **Data-Flow Explicitness** — Whether def-use relationships are explicit.
6. **Procedure Size** — Compactness of representation.
7. **Naming** — Whether variables are named or anonymous (e.g., stack-based IR).

The trade-off is: **high abstraction** (AST) → easy generation, hard optimisation. **Low abstraction** (machine code) → hard generation, easy optimisation. **Hybrid IRs** (LLVM) achieve the best of both worlds.

#### **(b)** Translate the following expression into (i) AST, (ii) DAG, and (iii) TAC with and without Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE):
$$x = (a + b) \times (a + b) - c$$
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) AST:**
```
           =
          / \
         x   -
            / \
           *   c
          / \
         +   +
        / \ / \
       a  b a  b
```

**(ii) DAG:** (Note merging of `(a+b)`)
```
         =
        / \
       x   -
          / \
         *   c
          \ /
           +
          / \
         a   b
        (shared)
```

**(iii) TAC without CSE:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + b \\
t_2 &= a + b \\
t_3 &= t_1 \times t_2 \\
t_4 &= t_3 - c \\
x   &= t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) TAC with CSE:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + b \\
t_3 &= t_1 \times t_1 \\
t_4 &= t_3 - c \\
x   &= t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

> Savings: 5 instructions → 4 instructions. The DAG made CSE *visible*; the TAC applied it.

---

### 📝 QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
#### **(a)** What is Static Single Assignment (SSA) form? Explain with a step-by-step procedure how a simple TAC program is converted to SSA form, including the role of $\phi$-functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**SSA Definition:** SSA is a *property* of an IR (usually TAC) in which every variable is the *target* of an assignment **exactly once** in the program text. A re-assignment requires a fresh name (e.g., $x_1, x_2, \ldots$).

**Conversion Procedure (Step-by-Step):**

Consider the TAC program:
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1: \;& x = 5 \\
& \text{if } x > 0 \text{ goto } L_2 \text{ else goto } L_3 \\
L_2: \;& y = x + 1 \\
& \text{goto } L_4 \\
L_3: \;& y = x - 1 \\
L_4: \;& z = y \times 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Build the CFG and Dominator Tree:**
- Blocks: $B_1$ (entry, $L_1$), $B_2$ ($L_2$), $B_3$ ($L_3$), $B_4$ ($L_4$).
- $B_1$ dominates all. $B_2$ and $B_3$ are dominated by $B_1$ only. $B_4$ is dominated by $B_1$ (not by $B_2$ or $B_3$).

**Step 2 — Insert $\phi$-functions:**
- $\phi$-functions are placed at **join points** (blocks with multiple predecessors).
- $B_4$ has predecessors $B_2$ and $B_3$ → insert $y_3 = \phi(y_1, y_2)$.

**Step 3 — Rename variables along the dominator tree:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1: \;& x_1 = 5 \\
& \text{if } x_1 > 0 \text{ goto } L_2 \text{ else goto } L_3 \\
L_2: \;& y_1 = x_1 + 1 \\
& \text{goto } L_4 \\
L_3: \;& y_2 = x_1 - 1 \\
L_4: \;& y_3 = \phi(y_1, y_2) \\
& \quad z_1 = y_3 \times 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of $\phi$-functions:**
- $\phi$-functions are **notational devices** that denote the merger of values arriving from different control-flow paths.
- They make the **def-use relationship unique**: every use of $y_3$ refers to exactly one definition (the $\phi$-output), enabling trivial data-flow analysis.
- They are eliminated during **register allocation** in the back-end.

**Why SSA?**
- Optimisations like **constant propagation**, **dead-code elimination**, and **strength reduction** become near-trivial on SSA.
- Modern optimisers (LLVM, GCC) operate almost exclusively on SSA.

#### **(b)** Compare Three-Address Code (TAC) with Java Bytecode along the seven design properties. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | TAC | Java Bytecode |
|----------|-----|---------------|
| 1. Ease of Generation | Medium — requires temporary creation & ordering | High — straightforward postfix translation |
| 2. Ease of Manipulation | High — named temporaries support data-flow analysis | Low — operands are on a stack; no names |
| 3. Level of Abstraction | Mid-level | Low-level (close to stack machine) |
| 4. Control-Flow Explicitness | Low (only via goto) | Low (branch opcodes like `ifeq`) |
| 5. Data-Flow Explicitness | High (named vars) | Low (stack-position dependent) |
| 6. Procedure Size | Larger (verbose temporaries) | Smaller (compact opcodes, e.g., `iadd`) |
| 7. Naming | Named temporaries (e.g., `t1`) | Anonymous (stack slots, local variable indices) |

**Additional Comparison Points:**
- TAC is **register-oriented**; Java Bytecode is **stack-oriented**.
- TAC is **machine-independent RISC-like**; Bytecode is **JVM-specific**.
- TAC supports **optimisations** more readily; Bytecode is closer to the target.
- A typical Java compiler first lowers the AST → TAC (or SSA) → **then** lowers TAC to Bytecode in the back-end.

**Trade-off Justification:**
- TAC wins on **manipulability and optimisations** → preferred in compiler IR.
- Bytecode wins on **compactness and portability** → preferred for distribution (`.class` files).

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 The 12 Things You MUST Memorise Before the KTU IR-Taxonomy Question:**

1. **Three classes of IRs:** Structural, Linear, Hybrid.
2. **TAC form:** $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ — at most **3 operands**.
3. **Quadruple tuple:** $(\text{op}, \arg_1, \arg_2, \text{result})$.
4. **AST** = syntactic tree; **DAG** = compressed AST with CSE; **CFG** = nodes = basic blocks, edges = control flow.
5. **SSA property** = each variable assigned exactly once; uses $\phi$-functions at join points.
6. **Stack-based vs Register-based:** JVM = stack; TAC = register-style.
7. **Seven design properties** = generation, manipulation, abstraction, control flow, data flow, procedure size, naming.
8. **JVM Bytecode instructions:** `iload, istore, iadd, imul, imod, isub, idiv, ifeq, ifne, goto`.
9. **DAG node-count formula:** $N_{\text{DAG}} = N_{\text{AST}} - k$ (k = number of merged subexpressions).
10. **Hybrid IR = CFG + TAC per block** (e.g., LLVM IR, GIMPLE, MSIL).
11. **SSA $\phi$-function** is *not executable* — eliminated during register allocation.
12. **LLVM IR & GIMPLE** are real-world examples of hybrid SSA-form IRs.

> **Last-minute mantra:** *AST for parsing, TAC for optimising, Bytecode for distributing, SSA for analysing.* ✍️
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Intermediate Representation (IR)?

An **Intermediate Representation (IR)** is a data structure that lives between the source program (high-level language) and the target machine code. It acts as a *bridge language* inside the compiler, capturing the semantics of the source in a form that is easier to analyze and translate than raw source text.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> "An Intermediate Representation is a structured, typically language-independent, encoding of the information contained in the source program. Graphical IRs represent this information using tree and graph data structures that preserve syntactic, semantic, or control-flow relationships between program constructs."

There are three broad classes of IR used in modern compilers:

| Class | Typical Use | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Graphical (Structural)** | High-level analysis, optimization | AST, DAG, CFG |
| **Linear (Three-Address)** | Data-flow analysis, code generation | TAC, Quadruples, Triples |
| **Hybrid** | Industrial compilers (LLVM IR) | LLVM IR, JVM Bytecode |

## 1.2 Why Graphical IRs?

Graphical IRs make **structural relationships explicit**. Every operator, operand, and control flow is a *node*; dependencies and execution order are *edges*. This visualization is invaluable for:

- **Optimization** (common subexpression elimination, dead code elimination).
- **Semantic analysis** (type checking, scope resolution).
- **Code generation** (instruction selection, register allocation).

## 1.3 The Three Syntax-Related Graphical IRs

Graphical IRs tied to the *syntactic structure* of the program are the focus of KTU Module 3:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Syntax-Related Trees/Graphs (KTU Module 3 Syllabus):**
> 1. **Parse Tree (Concrete Syntax Tree / CST)** — A faithful, line-by-line rendering of every grammar rule applied during parsing. Contains *all* terminals and non-terminals.
> 2. **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** — A *condensed* parse tree in which syntactic sugar (punctuation, parentheses, keywords) is removed. Only the semantically meaningful nodes survive.
> 3. **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** — A further compression of the AST in which identical subtrees are *merged* into shared nodes. Enables common subexpression detection.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Family Photo Album

Imagine your compiler is processing a recipe book:

- **Parse Tree** is the *full transcript* of a cooking show: every "um", "okay", "next step", and the chef's hand gestures are recorded in order. It is **complete but verbose**.
- **AST** is the *clean written recipe*: just the ingredients and actions in a logical tree. Extra chatter is gone, but the structure of the meal is intact.
- **DAG** is the *meal-prep planner*: if "chop onions" appears in three recipes, you don't write it three times — you write it once and *share* it. This is exactly what a DAG does for repeated expressions.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Parse tree = *grammar faithful*, AST = *semantically clean*, DAG = *economically shared*.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Parse Tree (Concrete Syntax Tree)

### Definition
A **Parse Tree** is a rooted, ordered tree in which each interior node represents a **non-terminal**, each leaf node represents a **terminal**, and the children of an interior node represent the right-hand side symbols of a production rule applied during derivation.

### Construction Logic
Given a CFG production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$:
1. The left-hand side $A$ becomes the **parent node**.
2. The symbols $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_n$ become the **children** in that exact order.
3. Recursion continues for every non-terminal child until only terminals remain as leaves.

### Properties
- **Root** = start symbol of the grammar.
- **Leaves** = terminal string (the input).
- **In-order traversal of leaves** = derived sentence.
- A parse tree is a *witness* of a leftmost or rightmost derivation.

### Drawbacks (Why we move to AST)
- Carries the full baggage of grammar symbols: parentheses, commas, keywords.
- For an expression like `( ( (a) + (b) ) )`, the tree is unnecessarily deep.
- Cannot directly identify semantic constructs.

## 2.2 Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)

### Definition
An **Abstract Syntax Tree** is a tree representation of the abstract syntactic structure of source code. Each node denotes a construct occurring in the source; the children of a node represent its meaningful components.

### AST vs. Parse Tree

| Aspect | Parse Tree | AST |
|---|---|---|
| Non-terminals | Present | Removed |
| Punctuation (parens, commas) | Present | Removed |
| Chain productions (single-child nodes) | Preserved | Collapsed |
| Grouping of operators | Implicit (via tree shape) | Explicit (operator nodes) |
| Size | Larger | Smaller |
| Used in | Parser | Semantic phase, optimization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "construct AST", you must (i) show grouping of operands under operators, (ii) drop all parentheses and non-terminals like `E`, `T`, `F`, and (iii) ensure operator precedence is reflected in the tree shape (lower precedence operators are higher in the tree).

### AST Construction Rules
For the classical arithmetic grammar:
- $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$
- $T \rightarrow T \ast F \mid F$
- $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \mathbf{id}$

The AST node types are: **`+`, `*`, `-`, `/`, `id`, `num`**.

| Grammar Production | AST Node Created |
|---|---|
| $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ | `+` node with children = AST($E_1$), AST($T$) |
| $E \rightarrow T$ | reuse AST($T$) |
| $T \rightarrow T_1 \ast F$ | `*` node with children = AST($T_1$), AST($F$) |
| $F \rightarrow (E)$ | reuse AST($E$) — **parentheses discarded** |
| $F \rightarrow \mathbf{id}$ | `id` leaf node |

## 2.3 Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)

### Definition
A **DAG** is a graphical IR in which the underlying structure is still a tree (or forest), but **identical sub-expressions are represented by a single shared node**. It is acyclic, meaning no path leads from a node back to itself.

### Why DAGs Matter
- **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE):** If the same sub-expression appears multiple times, the DAG reuses one node, exposing the redundancy.
- **Compiler optimization:** A DAG makes it obvious which computations are repeated and can be eliminated.
- **Smaller IR footprint:** A program of size $N$ AST nodes may compress to $M < N$ DAG nodes.

### Value Numbering — The Construction Algorithm

The classical Sethi–Ullman algorithm assigns a **value number** to each node so that structurally identical sub-expressions get the same number.

**Procedure:**
1. Maintain a hash table / search structure mapping signatures to node pointers.
2. For each **leaf node** (identifier or constant), look up its name; if missing, create a new node and assign a fresh value number.
3. For each **interior node** with operator $\otimes$ and children $c_1, c_2$:
   - Compute the **signature** $(\otimes, \text{val}(c_1), \text{val}(c_2))$.
   - Search the table for an existing node with this signature.
   - If found: **reuse** the existing node (return its pointer).
   - If not found: **create** a new node with a fresh value number, insert into table, and return its pointer.

### Worked Example — Building a DAG

Construct the DAG for the expression:
$$a + a \ast (b - c) + (b - c) \ast d$$

Step-by-step creation (each unique subtree gets one node):

| Step | Sub-expression | Signature | Action | Node Label |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a$ | leaf `a` | create | $n_1$ |
| 2 | $a$ | leaf `a` | **reuse** $n_1$ | $n_1$ |
| 3 | $b$ | leaf `b` | create | $n_2$ |
| 4 | $c$ | leaf `c` | create | $n_3$ |
| 5 | $b - c$ | `(-, n2, n3)` | create | $n_4$ |
| 6 | $a \ast (b-c)$ | `(*, n1, n4)` | create | $n_5$ |
| 7 | $a + a \ast (b-c)$ | `(+, n1, n5)` | create | $n_6$ |
| 8 | $b - c$ | `(-, n2, n3)` | **reuse** $n_4$ | $n_4$ |
| 9 | $(b-c) \ast d$ | `(*, n4, n_d)` | create | $n_7$ |
| 10 | full expr | `(+, n6, n7)` | create | $n_8$ |

Notice that step 8 reuses $n_4$ instead of creating a new $(b-c)$ node — this is the **CSE detected** by the DAG.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** $\vert$ denotes conditional OR; subscripts denote child index; $\square$ denotes "empty / done".

| # | Concept | Rule / Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parse Tree leaf count | equals length of input string | by definition of derivation |
| 2 | AST size for expression $E$ | $\le$ Parse tree size of $E$ | strict inequality when parens present |
| 3 | DAG node reduction | $N_{\text{DAG}} \le N_{\text{AST}}$ | equality holds when no CSE exists |
| 4 | Value number signature | $\sigma(v) = (\otimes, \text{val}(L), \text{val}(R))$ | for binary interior node $v$ |
| 5 | Leaf signature | $\sigma(\text{leaf}) = \text{name}$ or $\text{const}$ | identifier or literal |
| 6 | CSE savings | $S = 1 - \dfrac{N_{\text{DAG}}}{N_{\text{AST}}}$ | fractional reduction in nodes |
| 7 | Parse tree depth | $d \ge h_{\text{grammar}}$ | where $h$ is height of operator-precedence stack at deepest |
| 8 | AST operator height | $\text{height}(+) = 1 + \max(\text{height}(L), \text{height}(R))$ | for an operator node |
| 9 | Precedence in AST | Lower precedence $\Rightarrow$ higher (closer to root) | e.g., `+` above `*` |
| 10 | Associativity in AST | Left-assoc: left-deep; Right-assoc: right-deep | affects tree shape |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Application | Where the IR is Used | Real Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Static analysis** | Type checking, linting | ESLint, Clang Static Analyzer |
| **Optimization** | CSE, dead code elim, constant folding | LLVM, GCC (GIMPLE) |
| **Code generation** | Instruction selection | LLVM SelectionDAG, GCC RTL |
| **IDE features** | Refactoring, autocomplete | IntelliJ, VS Code (PSI trees) |
| **Decompilation** | Recover source from binary | Ghidra, IDA Pro |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world link:** The **LLVM Compiler Infrastructure** (used by Apple, Google, AMD) uses a DAG internally called the **SelectionDAG** during instruction selection. It is the direct industrial descendant of the textbook DAG IR.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Parse Tree for `a + b * c`

**Grammar (Classic Arithmetic):**
- $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$
- $T \rightarrow T \ast F \mid F$
- $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \mathbf{id}$

**Input:** $a + b \ast c$

**Leftmost Derivation (yielding parse tree):**
$$E \Rightarrow E + T \Rightarrow T + T \Rightarrow F + T \Rightarrow \mathbf{id} + T \Rightarrow \mathbf{id} + T \ast F \Rightarrow \mathbf{id} + F \ast F \Rightarrow \mathbf{id} + \mathbf{id} \ast F \Rightarrow \mathbf{id} + \mathbf{id} \ast \mathbf{id}$$

**Parse Tree Construction (each step creates children):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Root} = E \\
&\quad E \rightarrow E + T \;\Rightarrow\; \text{children: } [E, +, T] \\
&\quad E \rightarrow T \;\Rightarrow\; \text{child: } [T] \\
&\quad T \rightarrow F \;\Rightarrow\; \text{child: } [F] \\
&\quad F \rightarrow \mathbf{id} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{leaf: } [a] \\
&\quad T \rightarrow T \ast F \;\Rightarrow\; \text{children: } [T, \ast, F] \\
&\quad T \rightarrow F \;\Rightarrow\; \text{child: } [F] \\
&\quad F \rightarrow \mathbf{id} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{leaf: } [b] \\
&\quad F \rightarrow \mathbf{id} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{leaf: } [c]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting Parse Tree (textual, 13 nodes):**

```
            E
          / | \
         E  +  T
         |    /|\
         T   T * F
         |   |   |
         F   F   id(c)
         |   |
        id(a) id(b)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The parse tree has nodes `E`, `T`, `F` that carry *no semantic meaning*. They exist purely to satisfy the grammar. The next section shows how the AST removes them.

## 3.2 Derivation II — AST for `a + b * c`

Using the same grammar and the AST construction rules from Section 2.2:

**Recursive AST construction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{AST}(E \Rightarrow E_1 + T) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{new node } +(\text{AST}(E_1),\, \text{AST}(T)) \\
&\text{AST}(E_1 \Rightarrow T) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{AST}(T) \\
&\text{AST}(T \Rightarrow F) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{AST}(F) \\
&\text{AST}(F \Rightarrow \mathbf{id}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{leaf node } a \\
&\text{AST}(T \Rightarrow T_1 \ast F) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{new node } \ast(\text{AST}(T_1),\, \text{AST}(F)) \\
&\text{AST}(T_1 \Rightarrow F) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{leaf node } b \\
&\text{AST}(F \Rightarrow \mathbf{id}) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{leaf node } c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting AST (only 5 nodes — over 60% smaller):**

```
        +
       / \
      a   *
         / \
        b   c
```

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Metric | Parse Tree | AST |
|---|---|---|
| Total nodes | **13** | **5** |
| Operator nodes | 1 | 2 |
| Operand leaves | 3 | 3 |
| Intermediate non-terminals | 8 | 0 |
| Punctuation in tree | yes (`+`, `*`) | yes (as operator nodes) |
| Grammar symbols remaining | E, T, F, id, +, * | id, +, * |

## 3.3 Derivation III — DAG for `a + a * (b - c) + (b - c) * d`

This is the **classical KTU exam problem** — guaranteed to appear in some form.

**Goal:** Identify and merge the common sub-expression $(b - c)$.

### Step 1: Identify the syntactic structure
Rewrite the expression with explicit grouping (parens reveal structure):
$$(a + (a \ast (b - c))) + ((b - c) \ast d)$$

### Step 2: Build the AST first (without merging)

```
            +1
           /  \
          +2    *2
         /  \   /  \
        a   *1  -2  d
           /  \  / \
          a   -1 b  c
               / \
              b   c
```

Notice that sub-trees `-1` and `-2` (both are $b - c$) are *identical* but separate.

### Step 3: Apply value numbering / merge

**Signature table as we traverse:**

| Visit Order | Node | Signature | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a$ | `a` | create $n_1$ |
| 2 | $a$ | `a` | **reuse** $n_1$ |
| 3 | $b$ | `b` | create $n_2$ |
| 4 | $c$ | `c` | create $n_3$ |
| 5 | $b-c$ | `(-, n2, n3)` | create $n_4$ |
| 6 | $a \ast (b-c)$ | `(*, n1, n4)` | create $n_5$ |
| 7 | $a + a \ast (b-c)$ | `(+, n1, n5)` | create $n_6$ |
| 8 | $b-c$ (second) | `(-, n2, n3)` | **reuse** $n_4$ |
| 9 | $(b-c) \ast d$ | `(*, n4, n_d)` | create $n_7$ |
| 10 | full sum | `(+, n6, n7)` | create $n_8$ |

### Step 4: Draw the merged DAG

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_1 &= a \\
n_2 &= b \\
n_3 &= c \\
n_4 &= b - c \quad \text{(shared)} \\
n_5 &= a \ast n_4 \\
n_6 &= a + n_5 \\
n_7 &= n_4 \ast d \\
n_8 &= n_6 + n_7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting DAG (8 nodes — savings: 1 node merged out of 9 AST nodes ≈ 11% saved):**

```
              n8: +
             /   \
           n6:+   n7:*
          /  \    / \
        n1:a n5:* n4:- d
            / \   / \
          n1:a n4:- n2:b n3:c
              / \
            n2:b n3:c
```

### Step 3.4: Optimization — Three-Address Code from the DAG

A topological sort of the DAG gives an **optimized** TAC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= b - c \\
t_2 &= a \ast t_1 \\
t_3 &= a + t_2 \\
t_4 &= t_1 \ast d \\
t_5 &= t_3 + t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

If we **had not used the DAG** (i.e., naïvely translated the AST), the code would have computed $(b-c)$ **twice** — wasting one subtraction at runtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The DAG exposes redundant computation. Three-address code generated from a DAG is *guaranteed* to be free of trivially redundant sub-expressions.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which IR

| Scenario | Best IR | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Need exact grammar derivation | Parse Tree | Required for parser debugging |
| Need semantic information for type checking | **AST** | Compact, operator nodes are explicit |
| Need to detect identical sub-expressions | **DAG** | Merging is automatic |
| Need data-flow analysis | CFG (control-flow graph) | Tracks variable definitions/uses |
| Need instruction selection | **SelectionDAG** | Mirrors machine instructions |
| Need to generate bytecode / virtual code | AST + TAC | Tree-walking codegen is simple |
| Source-to-source translation (transpiler) | **AST** | Easy to pretty-print back to source |

## 4.6 Comparison: AST vs DAG Side-by-Side

| Feature | AST Node | DAG Node |
|---|---|---|
| Has operator label | yes | yes |
| Has ordered children | yes | yes |
| Can have multiple parents | **no** | **yes** (shared) |
| Unique per occurrence | **yes** | **no** (reused) |
| Detects CSE | no | **yes** |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
Differentiate between a *parse tree* and an *abstract syntax tree*. Give one example where they differ.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **parse tree** is a faithful representation of the derivation of a string using a context-free grammar. It contains *all* grammar symbols — non-terminals, terminals, and punctuation. Every production rule application becomes a parent-child relationship.

An **AST (Abstract Syntax Tree)** is a condensed version in which non-terminals and punctuation (parentheses, commas, semicolons) are removed, leaving only operator and operand nodes. The structure of the AST reflects operator precedence directly.

**Example:** For the input `(a + b) * c`:
- Parse tree contains nodes for `(`, `)`, `E`, `T`, `F` along with `a + b * c`.
- AST contains only the `*` node with two children: a `+` node (with leaves `a`, `b`) and the leaf `c`. The parentheses are gone. (3 marks: 1 def parse tree, 1 def AST, 1 example)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
What is a *Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)* in the context of compiler IRs? Why is it preferred over an AST for certain optimizations?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **DAG** is a graphical intermediate representation in which each node represents an operation or operand, and identical sub-expressions are merged into shared nodes. It is *acyclic* (no cycles) and *directed* (edges flow from operands to operators).

It is preferred over an AST for optimizations such as **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** because the DAG's structure *explicitly* reveals redundancy: if a node has multiple parents, that sub-expression is reused and can be computed only once. Translating the DAG to three-address code via topological sort then produces *redundancy-free* code automatically. (3 marks: 1 def, 2 why preferred)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Construct the **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** for the expression:
$$\left(\left(a + b\right) \ast c - d\right) / e$$
using the standard arithmetic grammar. Show clearly how parentheses and non-terminals are eliminated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Sethi–Ullman value numbering algorithm** for DAG construction. Illustrate its working by constructing the DAG for:
$$x - x \ast y + z \ast (x - y)$$
and identify the common sub-expression. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the **parse tree** for the input string `id + id * id` using the grammar:
- $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$
- $T \rightarrow T \ast F \mid F$
- $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$

Then derive the **AST** from the same input and show the reduction in node count. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Construct the **DAG** for the expression:
$$(a - b) \ast c + (a - b) \ast d + (a - b)$$
using value numbering. Hence write the corresponding optimized **three-address code (TAC)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the grammar to `(a + b) * c - d / e`:**

The parser will:
- Group `a + b` first (inside parens).
- Multiply by `c`.
- Divide `d / e`.
- Subtract the two halves.

**Step 2 — Identify operator precedence and associativity (lowest precedence at top):**
1. Division `/` and subtraction `-` are at the same precedence level (left-assoc).
2. Multiplication `*` is higher.
3. Addition `+` is higher (inside the parens).

**Step 3 — Build the AST bottom-up:**

```
Subtraction node "-"
   ├── "*" node
   │     ├── "+" node
   │     │     ├── leaf a
   │     │     └── leaf b
   │     └── leaf c
   └── "/" node
         ├── leaf d
         └── leaf e
```

**Node count check:**
- 1 root `-` node
- 1 `*` node, 1 `/` node
- 1 `+` node
- 5 leaves (`a`, `b`, `c`, `d`, `e`)
- **Total = 9 AST nodes**

**Elimination proof:** The original parse tree had ~21 nodes (including 8 non-terminals and 4 pairs of parentheses). The AST retained only the 2 subtraction operands, 3 binary operator nodes, and 5 leaves — exactly what the KTU answer key requires. **(7 marks: 2 precedence analysis, 3 tree drawing, 2 elimination commentary)**

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**The Sethi–Ullman Value Numbering Algorithm:**

1. Initialize an empty hash table $H$ mapping signatures to node pointers.
2. For each leaf (identifier or constant) with name $n$:
   - if $n \notin H$: create a new node $v$, set $\text{val}(v) = \text{nextVN}$, insert $(n \to v)$ into $H$.
   - else: reuse $v = H[n]$.
3. For each interior node with operator $\otimes$ and children $c_1, c_2$ having value numbers $v_1, v_2$:
   - Compute signature $\sigma = (\otimes, v_1, v_2)$.
   - if $\sigma \in H$: return the existing node (merge).
   - else: create node $v$, set $\text{val}(v) = \text{nextVN}$, insert $(\sigma \to v)$ into $H$, return $v$.

**DAG Construction for $x - x \ast y + z \ast (x - y)$:**

Regroup using precedence:
$$(x - (x \ast y)) + (z \ast (x - y))$$

| Step | Sub-expression | Signature | Value No. | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x$ | `x` | 1 | create $n_1$ |
| 2 | $x$ | `x` | 1 | reuse $n_1$ |
| 3 | $y$ | `y` | 2 | create $n_2$ |
| 4 | $x \ast y$ | `(*, 1, 2)` | 3 | create $n_3$ |
| 5 | $x - x \ast y$ | `(-, 1, 3)` | 4 | create $n_4$ |
| 6 | $x$ (re-used) | `x` | 1 | reuse $n_1$ |
| 7 | $y$ (re-used) | `y` | 2 | reuse $n_2$ |
| 8 | $x - y$ | `(-, 1, 2)` | 5 | create $n_5$ |
| 9 | $z$ | `z` | 6 | create $n_6$ |
| 10 | $z \ast (x-y)$ | `(*, 6, 5)` | 7 | create $n_7$ |
| 11 | full expr | `(+, 4, 7)` | 8 | create $n_8$ |

**Common sub-expression identified:** $(x - y)$ appears once explicitly and contributes to $z \ast (x-y)$. The expression $x - y$ has value number **5**. *(Note: in this specific expression, $x - y$ appears only once as a parenthesized group; however, the value-numbering mechanism would have detected any further redundancy automatically.)*

**Resulting DAG (textual, 8 nodes):**

```
n8: +
   ├── n4: -
   │     ├── n1: x
   │     └── n3: *
   │           ├── n1: x
   │           └── n2: y
   └── n7: *
         ├── n6: z
         └── n5: -
               ├── n1: x
               └── n2: y
```

**Optimized TAC (topological order):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= x \ast y \\
t_2 &= x - t_1 \\
t_3 &= x - y \\
t_4 &= z \ast t_3 \\
t_5 &= t_2 + t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 algorithm explanation, 3 step-by-step value numbering table, 1 DAG drawing, 1 TAC emission)**

---

### 5.4 Model Solution — Question B (Brief Skeleton for Self-Study)

#### Part (a) Sketch
- **Parse tree** has 13 nodes (one for every grammar symbol).
- **AST** reduces to 5 nodes: a `+` root with leaves `a` and a `*` node (children `b`, `c`).
- Show explicit node count comparison in a small table.

#### Part (b) Sketch
- The expression $(a - b)$ appears **three times** — perfect CSE candidate.
- Use value numbering:
  - Step 1: $a$ → $n_1$, $b$ → $n_2$.
  - Step 2: $a - b$ → $n_3$.
  - Step 3: $(a-b) \ast c$ → `(*, n3, n_c)` → $n_4$.
  - Step 4: $a - b$ (2nd) → **reuse $n_3$**.
  - Step 5: $(a-b) \ast d$ → `(*, n3, n_d)` → $n_5$.
  - Step 6: $a - b$ (3rd) → **reuse $n_3$** as a standalone operand in the final `+`.
  - Step 7: full sum → $n_6$.

**Optimized TAC:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a - b \\
t_2 &= t_1 \ast c \\
t_3 &= t_1 \ast d \\
t_4 &= t_2 + t_3 + t_1
\end{aligned}
$$

The DAG forces the compiler to compute $(a-b)$ **once** and reuse the value — a major runtime savings.

---

## 5.5 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Graphical IR Questions (Examiner Insights):**
>
