> 1. **Forgetting to drop parentheses in AST** — The most common error. If you draw a `( )` node or include the parentheses as a node in the AST, you will lose 1–2 marks immediately. *Rule:* parentheses are *never* AST nodes; they affect *shape* only.
>
> 2. **Confusing AST associativity direction** — For `a - b - c`, students often draw `a - (b - c)` (right-assoc). Standard arithmetic is **left-associative**: `(a - b) - c`. The left child of the root `-` should be `(a - b)`, not `a`.
>
> 3. **Reusing an AST node instead of merging in the DAG** — When a sub-expression repeats, students sometimes *reuse* the AST node in the *AST*. The AST **does not** allow multi-parent nodes. The DAG is the only IR where merging happens. Showing shared parents in an AST is a 2-mark penalty.
>
> 4. **Omitting value-numbering table** — A bare DAG without the signature/value-number table will receive only partial credit. KTU examiners reward the *process* of construction, not just the final picture.
>
> 5. **Wrong top node of AST** — For `a + b * c`, the **root must be `+`**, not `*`. Students sometimes invert this. Remember: **lowest-precedence operator is the root**.
>
> 6. **Not labeling edges in DAG** — The DAG edges represent *operand flow*; examiners expect child labels like `L` (left) and `R` (right) or at least clean left-to-right ordering.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This!)**
>
> ✅ **Parse Tree** — Root = start symbol, leaves = input string. Contains *all* grammar symbols.
>
> ✅ **AST** — Drops non-terminals and punctuation. Lower-precedence operators are *closer to the root*. Operator nodes have operand children. Single-parent rule.
>
> ✅ **DAG** — Like AST, but **multi-parent allowed**. Common sub-expressions are merged using *value numbering*. Exposes CSE.
>
> ✅ **Value Numbering** — Signature for leaf = name; signature for interior = (operator, valueNo(left), valueNo(right)). Search the table first; reuse if found.
>
> ✅ **Construction Order** — AST/DAG are usually built *bottom-up* during syntax-directed translation using *semantic actions* attached to productions.
>
> ✅ **Three-Address Code from DAG** — Topological sort of DAG nodes gives a CSE-free TAC.
>
> ✅ **Right Tool for the Job** — Parse tree for parser debugging; AST for semantic analysis & source-to-source; DAG for optimization; TAC for code generation; CFG (next module) for data-flow.
>
> ✅ **Key Relationship** — $\text{Size}_{\text{Parse}} \ge \text{Size}_{\text{AST}} \ge \text{Size}_{\text{DAG}}$.
>
> ✅ **Memory Trick** — *Parse = Verbose*, *AST = Clean*, *DAG = Economical*.
>
> ✅ **Industry Connection** — LLVM's SelectionDAG and GCC's GIMPLE are direct descendants of the textbook DAG IR; you are learning the same foundations that power Clang, Rust, Swift, and Julia compilers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear IRs - Stack-Machine Code, Three-Address Code, and Representing Linear Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Linear IRs - Stack-Machine Code, Three-Address Code, and Representing Linear Codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Linear Intermediate Representation (IR)

A **Linear Intermediate Representation (Linear IR)** is a sequence of simple, low-level instructions that represents the source program as a linear (non-hierarchical) list. Unlike Abstract Syntax Trees (AST) or Control Flow Graphs (CFG) which preserve the syntactic and control hierarchy, a Linear IR flattens the program into a flat list of pseudo-machine instructions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A *Linear IR* is an Intermediate Representation in which the program is expressed as a sequence of simple instructions that can be viewed as pseudo-code for an abstract machine. It serves as the bridge between high-level language constructs (parsed AST) and target machine code generation.

The two principal families of Linear IR studied under **Module 3** of *PBCRT604 – Compiler Construction* are:

1. **Stack-Machine Code** (also called *One-Address Code / Zero-Address Code*) — instructions manipulate an implicit operand stack.
2. **Three-Address Code (TAC)** — every instruction is of the form `x = y op z`, allowing at most **3 operands** per instruction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Recipe Card"**
> - Think of cooking a dish from a recipe. The **AST** is the *chapter heading* "Make Masala Dosa" (hierarchical, structured).
> - The **Linear IR** is the *step-by-step numbered list* on the back of the card: "1. Pour oil 2. Add mustard 3. Add onion…". The hierarchy is gone; it is just a flat sequence of simple actions.
> - **Stack-Machine Code** is like a chef who can only cook using a *single tray* (stack): put ingredients on top, pop them, mix, push result back.
> - **Three-Address Code** is like a chef with **3 labelled bowls** (a destination, and two sources): "Bowl_C = Bowl_A + Bowl_B".

### 1.3 Importance in the KTU 2024 Compiler Pipeline

```
Source Code → Lexer → Parser → Semantic Analysis → AST
        ↓
   [Module 3 Focus]
   Linear IR (Stack-Machine / TAC)
        ↓
   Optimization Phase (Constant Folding, Dead Code Elimination)
        ↓
   Target Code Generation
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Linear IRs are preferred in modern compilers:**
> - Enables **machine-independent optimization** (gcc, LLVM, Java HotSpot JVM all use linear IRs).
> - Simplifies **data-flow analysis** because each instruction has a known effect.
> - Facilitates **register allocation** through temporaries.

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics

The following are standard metrics used to evaluate a Linear IR in Compiler Design:

| Metric | Typical Value / Range | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Max operands per instruction** | 3 (TAC) or 0/1 (Stack) | Defines complexity of the abstract machine |
| **Code expansion ratio** | 3x – 8x | TAC expands source by 3–8 times original lines |
| **Temporary count** | O(n) | Number of compiler-generated temporaries |
| **Instruction size** | 16 – 32 bits | Typical size of a quadruple entry |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stack-Machine Code (Zero- / One-Address Code)

A **Stack-Machine Code** is a Linear IR in which all operands are kept on an implicit *evaluation stack*. Most instructions (arithmetic, logical, relational) take their inputs from the **top of the stack** and push the result back.

#### 2.1.1 Instruction Set of a Stack Machine

| Instruction | Effect | Operand Type |
|---|---|---|
| `PUSH x` | Push the value of `x` onto the stack | One address |
| `POP x` | Pop top of stack into variable `x` | One address |
| `ADD` | Pop `a` and `b`, push `b + a` | Zero address |
| `SUB` | Pop `a` and `b`, push `b − a` | Zero address |
| `MUL` | Pop `a` and `b`, push `b × a` | Zero address |
| `DIV` | Pop `a` and `b`, push `b ÷ a` | Zero address |
| `NEG` | Negate top of stack | Zero address |
| `EQ`, `NE`, `LT`, `GT`, `LE`, `GE` | Comparison; pushes 0/1 | Zero address |
| `JUMP L` | Unconditional jump to label `L` | Label |
| `JUMPF L` | Pop top; jump if false (0) | Label |
| `JUMPT L` | Pop top; jump if true (1) | Label |
| `LOAD x` | Push memory[x] onto stack | One address |
| `STORE x` | Pop top; store into memory[x] | One address |

#### 2.1.2 Why "Zero-Address" and "One-Address"?

> [!NOTE]
> - **Zero-Address instructions** (e.g., `ADD`, `MUL`) reference **no explicit operands** — both inputs come implicitly from the stack top.
> - **One-Address instructions** (e.g., `PUSH x`, `POP x`) reference **one explicit operand** (a variable or constant).
> - The machine is therefore called a *Stack Machine* or *Operand Stack Machine* (e.g., **Java Virtual Machine – JVM** uses exactly this model!).

#### 2.1.3 Advantages and Disadvantages

| Property | Stack-Machine Code |
|---|---|
| Code size | **Very compact** (no operand fields in arithmetic) |
| Ease of code generation | **Trivial** — one recursive pass over AST |
| Execution speed | Slower (frequent stack access) |
| Optimization potential | Limited (implicit data-flow) |
| Real-world use | **JVM Bytecode, WebAssembly (Wasm)**, .NET CIL |

---

### 2.2 Three-Address Code (TAC)

A **Three-Address Code (TAC)** instruction has the general form:

$$x = y \;\text{op}\; z$$

where:
- `x` is the **result name** (a variable or compiler-generated temporary).
- `y` and `z` are **operands** (variables, constants, or temporaries).
- `op` is a **binary operator**.

#### 2.2.1 Instruction Forms of TAC

| Form | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Binary arithmetic | $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ | $t_1 = a + b$ |
| Unary | $x = \text{op}\; y$ | $t_2 = -t_1$ |
| Copy / Move | $x = y$ | $x = t_2$ |
| Conditional jump | `if x goto L` | `if t1 goto L3` |
| Unconditional jump | `goto L` | `goto L2` |
| Indexed copy | $x = y[i]$ | $t_3 = a[i]$ |
| Indexed store | $x[i] = y$ | $a[j] = t_3$ |
| Address & Dereference | $x = \&y$, $x = *y$, $*x = y$ | $t_4 = \&a$ |
| Function call | `param x`, `call p,n` | `call foo,2` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Rule:** Each TAC instruction contains **at most one operator** on the right-hand side. This single rule is what makes TAC *three-address*.

#### 2.2.2 Why "Three-Address"?

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** The instruction has **three names/addresses** — destination `x`, source `y`, source `z`. Even unary operations are considered three-address because two slots are simply unused. This regular form makes data-flow analysis and optimization mathematically tractable.

#### 2.2.3 Advantages and Disadvantages

| Property | Three-Address Code |
|---|---|
| Code size | **Larger** than stack code (operands explicit) |
| Ease of optimization | **Excellent** — explicit def-use chains |
| Execution simulation | Trivial (each instruction is a primitive) |
| Real-world use | **GCC GIMPLE, LLVM IR (pre-SSA), JVM SSA form** |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TAC instruction form** | $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ | Canonical TAC |
| 2 | **Stack-Machine push-pop balance** | $\sum \text{PUSH} - \sum \text{POP}_{\text{expr}} = 1$ for a value | Stack-depth check |
| 3 | **Number of temporaries for `a + b * c`** | $n = (\text{binary ops}) = 2 \implies t_1, t_2$ | TAC conversion |
| 4 | **Quadruple tuple** | $\langle \text{op},\; \text{arg1},\; \text{arg2},\; \text{result} \rangle$ | Linear IR storage |
| 5 | **Triple tuple** | $\langle \text{op},\; \text{arg1},\; \text{arg2} \rangle$ | Position-based |
| 6 | **Indirect triple** | List of pointers $\rightarrow$ triples | Reorderable IR |
| 7 | **Maximum operands / instruction** | $\leq 3$ | Definition of TAC |
| 8 | **Code expansion factor** | $E = \frac{\text{IR instructions}}{\text{Source statements}}$ | Usually $3 \le E \le 8$ |
| 9 | **SSA dominance rule** | A $\phi$-function at join node $n$ for variable $v$ | Advanced TAC form |
| 10 | **Stack depth for `a + b`** | After `PUSH a; PUSH b; ADD` $\rightarrow$ depth = 1 | Verification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **JVM Bytecode** (Stack-Machine) → used in Android, Spring, Kotlin, Scala runtimes.
> - **LLVM IR** (Three-Address) → used in Clang, Swift, Rust front-ends; powers industry-grade optimizers.

---

### 2.4 The `Why` and `How` of Choosing Between Stack and TAC

The decision is fundamentally a **space–time trade-off** mapped onto the compiler's intermediate layer:

- **Why Stack Code?** The abstract machine is *very simple*. Code generation from an AST is a direct **post-order traversal** with no name allocation. Trade-off: data-flow is **implicit**, making advanced optimizations hard.
- **Why TAC?** The **explicit def-use chain** (each instruction defines a single result, used by other instructions) gives every optimization a precise mathematical handle. Trade-off: more temporaries, larger IR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "compare" them, always mention: *compactness* vs *optimizability* as the central trade-off.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, fully-worked-out translations of source expressions and statements into both Stack-Machine Code and Three-Address Code. **Every step is shown explicitly — no skipping.**

---

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Arithmetic Expression

**Source Expression:**
$$x = a + b \times c - d \div e$$

**Step 1: Identify precedence and parenthesize**

Following standard C/Java precedence (`*`, `/` bind tighter than `+`, `-`, evaluated left-to-right within same precedence):

$$x = (a + (b \times c)) - (d \div e)$$

**Step 2: Generate TAC using a fresh temporary counter**

Let $t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots$ be compiler-generated temporaries.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= b \times c \\
t_2 &= d \div e \\
t_3 &= a + t_1 \\
t_4 &= t_3 - t_2 \\
x  &= t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning for each line:**
- Line 1: The deepest sub-expression `b * c` is evaluated first. Result stored in $t_1$.
- Line 2: The other deep sub-expression `d / e` evaluated next. Result stored in $t_2$.
- Line 3: `a + (b*c)` becomes `a + t1`, stored in $t_3$.
- Line 4: Subtract `t2` from `t3`, store in $t_4`.
- Line 5: Copy final temporary to user variable `x`.

**Step 3: Generate Stack-Machine Code**

A stack machine does not need temporaries. We push operands and apply operations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{PUSH } a \\
&\text{PUSH } b \\
&\text{PUSH } c \\
&\text{MUL} \qquad\quad \text{(computes } b \times c\text{, stack: } a,\; b\!\times\!c\text{)} \\
&\text{ADD} \qquad\quad \text{(computes } a + (b\!\times\!c)\text{, stack: } a + b\!\times\!c\text{)} \\
&\text{PUSH } d \\
&\text{PUSH } e \\
&\text{DIV} \qquad\quad \text{(computes } d \div e\text{, stack: } \ldots,\; d/e\text{)} \\
&\text{SUB} \qquad\quad \text{(computes } (a + b\!\times\!c) - d/e\text{, stack: } x\text{)} \\
&\text{POP } x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verification of stack depth at every step**

| Instruction | Stack (top is rightmost) | Depth |
|---|---|---|
| `PUSH a` | `[a]` | 1 |
| `PUSH b` | `[a, b]` | 2 |
| `PUSH c` | `[a, b, c]` | 3 |
| `MUL` | `[a, b·c]` | 2 |
| `ADD` | `[a + b·c]` | 1 |
| `PUSH d` | `[a + b·c, d]` | 2 |
| `PUSH e` | `[a + b·c, d, e]` | 3 |
| `DIV` | `[a + b·c, d/e]` | 2 |
| `SUB` | `[(a + b·c) − d/e]` | 1 |
| `POP x` | `[]` | 0 |

✅ Stack balance is maintained: **starts at 0, ends at 0**, exactly one value produced.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Array Indexing

**Source:**
$$A[i] = B[j] + C[k]$$

**Step 1: TAC generation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= j \times \text{sizeof}(B\text{ element}) \\
t_2 &= \text{addr}(B) + t_1 \\
t_3 &= *t_2 \qquad\qquad\text{(i.e., load } B[j]\text{)} \\
t_4 &= k \times \text{sizeof}(C\text{ element}) \\
t_5 &= \text{addr}(C) + t_4 \\
t_6 &= *t_5 \qquad\qquad\text{(i.e., load } C[k]\text{)} \\
t_7 &= t_3 + t_6 \\
t_8 &= i \times \text{sizeof}(A\text{ element}) \\
t_9 &= \text{addr}(A) + t_8 \\
*t_9 &= t_7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Stack-Machine Code generation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{PUSH } j \\
&\text{PUSH } \text{sizeof}(B) \\
&\text{MUL} \\
&\text{PUSH } \text{addr}(B) \\
&\text{ADD} \\
&\text{LOAD} \qquad\quad\text{(dereference pointer to get } B[j]\text{)} \\
&\text{PUSH } k \\
&\text{PUSH } \text{sizeof}(C) \\
&\text{MUL} \\
&\text{PUSH } \text{addr}(C) \\
&\text{ADD} \\
&\text{LOAD} \qquad\quad\text{(dereference pointer to get } C[k]\text{)} \\
&\text{ADD} \qquad\quad\text{(stack top = sum)} \\
&\text{PUSH } i \\
&\text{PUSH } \text{sizeof}(A) \\
&\text{MUL} \\
&\text{PUSH } \text{addr}(A) \\
&\text{ADD} \qquad\quad\text{(stack top = address of } A[i]\text{)} \\
&\text{POP } \text{tmp} \qquad\text{(save address)} \\
&\text{POP } \text{val} \qquad\text{(save value)} \\
&\text{PUSH } \text{tmp} \\
&\text{PUSH } \text{val} \\
&\text{STORE} \qquad\text{(perform } A[i] = \text{val}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Control Flow: `if-else`

**Source:**
```c
if (a < b) {
    x = a + b;
} else {
    x = a - b;
}
```

**Step 1: TAC generation using labels and jumps**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L1: \quad & \text{ifFalse } a < b \text{ goto } L2 \\
         & t_1 = a + b \\
         & x = t_1 \\
         & \text{goto } L3 \\
L2: \quad & t_2 = a - b \\
         & x = t_2 \\
L3: \quad & \text{(next statement)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Stack-Machine Code generation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{PUSH } a \\
&\text{PUSH } b \\
&\text{LT} \qquad\quad\text{(top of stack now holds } a < b \text{ as 0/1)} \\
&\text{JUMPF } L2 \quad\text{(jump to else if false)} \\
&\text{PUSH } a \\
&\text{PUSH } b \\
&\text{ADD} \\
&\text{POP } x \\
&\text{JUMP } L3 \\
L2: \quad &\text{PUSH } a \\
         &\text{PUSH } b \\
         &\text{SUB} \\
         &\text{POP } x \\
L3: \quad &\text{(continue)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Comparison of Representations

For the simple expression `x = a + b * c`:

| # | Three-Address Code (TAC) | Stack-Machine Code | Quadruple | Triple |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_1 = b \times c$ | `PUSH b` | `( *, b, c, t1 )` | `(0): (*, b, c)` |
| 2 | $t_2 = a + t_1$ | `PUSH c` | `( +, a, t1, t2 )` | `(1): (+, a, t1)` |
| 3 | $x = t_2$ | `MUL` | `( =, t2, _, x )` | `(2): (=, t2, _)` |
| 4 | | `PUSH a` | | |
| 5 | | `ADD` | | |
| 6 | | `POP x` | | |

> [!TIP]
> **Important:** A *Triple* refers to its result by **position number** (e.g., `(0)` for the first triple's result). A *Quadruple* uses an **explicit symbolic name** (`t1`). A *Quadruple* is more powerful for optimization because renaming temporaries is trivial.

---

### 3.5 Derivation — Why TAC uses One Operator per Instruction

Given a source expression with $n$ binary operators, the **number of TAC instructions required** is:

$$\text{IR}_{\text{count}} = n \;+\; 1$$

where the "+1" accounts for the final assignment to the destination variable.

**Example:** For $x = a + b \times c$, the operator count is $n = 2$ (one `+` and one `*`). Hence:

$$\text{IR}_{\text{count}} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ TAC instructions}$$

This matches the 3 lines: $t_1 = b \times c$, $t_2 = a + t_1$, $x = t_2$.

---

### 3.6 Derivation — Stack Depth for a Chain Expression

For a *left-associative* expression of the form $a_1 \circ a_2 \circ a_3 \circ \ldots \circ a_n$ (all $\circ$ identical precedence), the **maximum stack depth** during evaluation is:

$$D_{\max} = n$$

and the **total number of instructions** for a stack machine is:

$$I_{\text{stack}} = n \text{ PUSH} + (n-1) \text{ ops} = 2n - 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> For TAC, the same expression requires $n-1$ temporaries plus the assignment, yielding $n$ instructions. This makes the stack code **shorter by approximately $n - 1$ instructions** at the cost of $O(n)$ live stack slots.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Where Linear IR Fits in a Compiler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Code] --> LEX[Lexical Analyzer]
    LEX --> PAR[Syntax Analyzer]
    PAR --> AST[Abstract Syntax Tree]
    AST --> SEM[Semantic Analysis]
    SEM --> IRGEN[IR Generation Module 3]
    IRGEN --> STK[Stack-Machine Code]
    IRGEN --> TAC[Three-Address Code]
    STK --> OPT1[Optimization]
    TAC --> OPT2[Optimization]
    OPT1 --> CG[Code Generator]
    OPT2 --> CG
    CG --> TM[Target Machine Code]

    classDef irBox fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef procBox fill:#2d5a27,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    class STK,TAC irBox
    class LEX,PAR,SEM,IRGEN,OPT1,OPT2,CG procBox
```

> **Reading aid:** The two blue boxes (`STK`, `TAC`) are the focus of this module. The green boxes are the surrounding phases.

---

### 4.2 Execution Model of a Stack Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Fetch] --> B{Opcode Type?}
    B -- PUSH / LOAD --> C[Pop operand from IR]
    C --> D[Push value onto Operand Stack]
    D --> A
    B -- Arithmetic ADD / SUB / MUL / DIV --> E[Pop right operand y]
    E --> F[Pop left operand x]
    F --> G[Compute x op y]
    G --> H[Push result onto stack]
    H --> A
    B -- POP / STORE --> I[Pop top of stack]
    I --> J[Store into memory variable]
    J --> A
    B -- JUMP / JUMPF / JUMPT --> K[Update Program Counter]
    K --> A
    B -- HALT --> L[End Execution]

    classDef stackBox fill:#5c2d2d,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef jumpBox fill:#2d2d5c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    class D,H stackBox
    class K jumpBox
```

> **Key insight:** The **Operand Stack** is a single shared data structure; all arithmetic operations consume from it and push back.

---

### 4.3 Quadruple, Triple, and Indirect Triple Data Structures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q[Quadruple Table]
        Q1["(0)  op = *  arg1 = b  arg2 = c  result = t1"]
        Q2["(1)  op = +  arg1 = a  arg2 = t1 result = t2"]
        Q3["(2)  op = =  arg1 = t2 arg2 = -  result = x"]
    end

    subgraph T[Triple Table]
        T1["(0)  op = *  arg1 = b  arg2 = (1)"]
        T2["(1)  op = +  arg1 = a  arg2 = (0)"]
        T3["(2)  op = =  arg1 = (1) arg2 = -"]
    end

    subgraph IT[Indirect Triple List]
        IT1["ptr -> (1)"]
        IT2["ptr -> (0)"]
        IT3["ptr -> (2)"]
    end

    IT1 --> T2
    IT2 --> T1
    IT3 --> T3

    classDef quadBox fill:#3a4a5c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef tripleBox fill:#5c4a3a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef indBox fill:#3a5c4a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 quadBox
    class T1,T2,T3 tripleBox
    class IT1,IT2,IT3 indBox
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - A **Quadruple** explicitly names its result (`t1`, `t2`, `x`) → easy to relocate.
> - A **Triple** points to its result by *position number* (e.g., `(0)` is the result of the instruction at index 0) → compact, but renaming is hard.
> - An **Indirect Triple** keeps a list of **pointers** to triples. Reordering instructions is as simple as **swapping pointer entries** without moving the triples themselves — perfect for optimization passes like *peephole* and *basic-block reordering*.

---

### 4.4 Def-Use Chain Visualized in TAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B[b] --> M1[op = *]
    C1[c] --> M1
    M1 --> T1[t1]
    A[a] --> M2[op = +]
    T1 --> M2
    M2 --> T2[t2]
    T2 --> A2[op = =]
    A2 --> X[x]

    classDef nodeBox fill:#2d3a5c,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef defBox fill:#5c2d3a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    class T1,T2 defBox
```

> **Use the diagram to find def-use chains:**
> - `t1` is **defined** at M1 and **used** at M2.
> - `t2` is **defined** at M2 and **used** at A2.
> - `x` is **defined** at A2 and is never **used** (dead-code candidate).

---

### 4.5 Conversion Workflow from AST to TAC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AST Node] --> B{Node Kind?}
    B -- Leaf Constant --> C[Emit PUSH value]
    B -- Leaf Variable --> D[Emit PUSH name]
    B -- Unary Op op X --> E[Recurse on X] --> F[Emit unary op]
    B -- Binary Op X op Y --> G[Recurse on X] --> H[Recurse on Y] --> I[Emit binary op]
    B -- Assignment X = Y --> J[Recurse on Y] --> K[Emit POP X]
    B -- If-Statement --> L[Emit cond JUMPF] --> M[Recurse then-branch] --> N[Emit JUMP] --> O[Recurse else-branch]
    B -- While Loop --> P[Emit label Lstart] --> Q[Emit cond JUMPF Lend] --> R[Recurse body] --> S[Emit JUMP Lstart] --> T[Emit label Lend]

    classDef leafBox fill:#1f4d2d,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef opBox fill:#4d1f2d,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrlBox fill:#2d1f4d,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    class C,D leafBox
    class F,I opBox
    class L,N,P,Q,R,S,T ctrlBox
```

> **Reading aid:** The recursion point is the **key invariant** — a stack machine code generator is essentially a **post-order AST walker** that emits instructions as it *unwinds* the recursion.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)**

---

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Differentiate between Three-Address Code and Stack-Machine Code. Mention one real-world compiler or virtual machine that uses each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Three-Address Code | Stack-Machine Code |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction form** | $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ | Operands implicit on stack; e.g., `ADD`, `PUSH x` |
| **Max explicit operands** | 3 (destination + 2 sources) | 0 or 1 |
| **Code size** | Larger (explicit operand fields) | Compact |
| **Optimization potential** | High (explicit def-use chains) | Limited (implicit data-flow) |
| **Real-world example** | **GCC GIMPLE / LLVM IR** | **Java Virtual Machine (JVM) / WebAssembly** |

> **Awarding marks:** 1 mark for definition of TAC, 1 mark for definition of Stack code, 1 mark for real-world example of each.

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**What is a Quadruple representation in the context of Three-Address Code? Write the Quadruples for the expression `x = (a + b) * (c - d)`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Quadruple** is a record with four fields: $\langle \text{op},\; \text{arg1},\; \text{arg2},\; \text{result} \rangle$. It is the most common way to store TAC.

Quadruples for `x = (a + b) * (c - d)`:

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `+` | `a` | `b` | `t1` |
| 1 | `-` | `c` | `d` | `t2` |
| 2 | `*` | `t1` | `t2` | `t3` |
| 3 | `=` | `t3` | `—` | `x` |

> **Awarding marks:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correct quadruple sequence.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – July 2023]`

**(a)** Translate the following C code segment into **Three-Address Code (TAC)** and **Stack-Machine Code**. (7 marks)

```c
x = (a + b) * c - d / e;
if (x > 0)
    y = x;
else
    y = -x;
```

**(b)** Explain **Quadruple, Triple, and Indirect Triple** representations of TAC with a suitable example. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**TAC generation** (7 marks awarded for correctness and labeling):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + b \\
t_2 &= t_1 \times c \\
t_3 &= d \div e \\
t_4 &= t_2 - t_3 \\
x  &= t_4 \\
\text{ifFalse } &x > 0 \text{ goto } L1 \\
y  &= x \\
\text{goto } &L2 \\
L1: \quad t_5 &= 0 - x \\
\quad y   &= t_5 \\
L2: \quad &\text{(next statement)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stack-Machine Code:**

```
PUSH a
PUSH b
ADD
PUSH c
MUL
PUSH d
PUSH e
DIV
SUB
POP x            ; stack: []  ; x holds the value
PUSH x
PUSH 0
GT               ; stack: [x > 0]
JUMPF L1
PUSH x
POP y
JUMP L2
L1:  PUSH 0
     PUSH x
     SUB          ; stack: [0 - x] = -x
     POP y
L2:  (continue)
```

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**1. Quadruple** $\langle \text{op}, \text{arg1}, \text{arg2}, \text{result} \rangle$

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `+` | `a` | `b` | `t1` |
| 1 | `*` | `t1` | `c` | `t2` |
| 2 | `-` | `t2` | `t3` | `t4` |

- **Advantage:** Easy to relocate and apply optimizations because results have explicit names.
- **Disadvantage:** Larger memory footprint (4 fields per instruction).

**2. Triple** $\langle \text{op}, \text{arg1}, \text{arg2} \rangle$ — result is *implied by position*.

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `+` | `a` | `b` |
| (1) | `*` | `(0)` | `c` |
| (2) | `-` | `(1)` | `t3` |

- **Advantage:** Compact (only 3 fields).
- **Disadvantage:** Moving an instruction requires updating *all references* to its position — expensive.

**3. Indirect Triple** — A list of **pointers** into the triple table.

```
Pointer List:   [ptr → (0), ptr → (1), ptr → (2)]
Triple Table:   (0): (+, a, b)
                (1): (*, (0), c)
                (2): (-, (1), t3)
```

- **Advantage:** Reordering the program is as simple as **swapping pointer entries**; triples themselves never move.
- **Disadvantage:** An extra level of indirection slows down access.

> **Awarding marks for (b):** 1 mark each for definition (3 marks), 2 marks for the example, 1 mark each for advantage and disadvantage of all three (3 marks).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat example, explain the **postfix translation scheme** for generating **Stack-Machine Code** from an arithmetic expression. Show the steps for the expression `a * (b + c) - d / e`. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is **Static Single Assignment (SSA) Form**? Convert the following TAC segment into SSA form. (7 marks)

```
a = b + c
d = a + e
a = d + f
g = a + d
```

---

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Postfix Translation Scheme** — Each grammar production is augmented with a *semantic action* that emits stack-machine code.

| Production | Semantic Action (emit) |
|---|---|
| $E \rightarrow E_1 + E_2$ | `code(E1) ; code(E2) ; ADD` |
| $E \rightarrow E_1 - E_2$ | `code(E1) ; code(E2) ; SUB` |
| $E \rightarrow E_1 \times E_2$ | `code(E1) ; code(E2) ; MUL` |
| $E \rightarrow ( E_1 )$ | `code(E1)` |
| $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ | `PUSH id` |

**Trace for `a * (b + c) - d / e`:**

Step 1: `a` is a leaf → `PUSH a`

Step 2: `(b + c)`:
  - `PUSH b`, `PUSH c`, `ADD`

Step 3: `a * (b + c)`:
  - `PUSH a`, `PUSH b`, `PUSH c`, `ADD`, `MUL`

Step 4: `d / e`:
  - `PUSH d`, `PUSH e`, `DIV`

Step 5: Combine with `-`:
  - `PUSH a`
  - `PUSH b`
  - `PUSH c`
  - `ADD`
  - `MUL`
  - `PUSH d`
  - `PUSH e`
  - `DIV`
  - `SUB`
  - `POP result`

> **Awarding marks:** 2 marks for the translation scheme, 5 marks for the correct trace.

---

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**SSA Definition:**
Static Single Assignment (SSA) is a property of an IR in which **every variable is assigned exactly once**, and every use of a variable refers to its unique definition. Multiple assignments to the same original variable are renamed using subscripts, and **$\phi$-functions** (phi-functions) are inserted at control-flow merge points.

**Conversion of the given TAC:**

Original:
```
a = b + c
d = a + e
a = d + f
g = a + d
```

SSA Form (each definition is unique):
```
a1 = b1 + c1
d1 = a1 + e1
a2 = d1 + f1
g1 = a2 + d1
```

**Explanation:**
- `a` is assigned in line 1 (becomes `a1`) and again in line 3 (becomes `a2`).
- The second use of `a` in line 4 is changed to use the *latest* definition `a2`.
- The first use of `a` in line 2 is changed to use `a1`.
- The two `a`s in the original program have been **disambiguated** by versioning.
- This is the foundation of modern optimizer analyses (constant propagation, dead-code elimination, GVN).

> **Awarding marks:** 2 marks for definition of SSA, 4 marks for correct conversion, 1 mark for identifying that `a` is versioned into `a1` and `a2`.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in Module 3 questions:**
>
> 1. **Operand order in stack code:** For `SUB` and `DIV`, students frequently forget the *order* of popping. The correct semantics is: pop `a` first (right operand), pop `b` second (left operand), then push `b op a`. A common mistake is `a op b` — this costs **2 marks** in long answers.
>
> 2. **Missing `POP` at the end of stack code:** Every expression sequence must end with the stack returning to depth 0. Forgetting `POP x` after computing `x` is a recurring error.
>
> 3. **Confusing `*` (dereference) with `*` (multiply):** In the quadruple table, `*` as an operator field means *multiply*. The dereference operator must be written explicitly as `=[]` or `[]=`.
>
> 4. **Missing temporaries:** For `(a + b) * c`, the correct count is 3 temporaries (`t1 = a+b`, `t2 = t1*c`, `t3 = ...`). Students often write only 2 — losing 1 mark.
>
> 5. **SSA conversion:** Students forget to **rename the uses** of the variable after a re-definition. Only renaming the *definition* is **not** enough — every subsequent use of the old name must point to the new versioned name.
>
> 6. **Indirect triple confusion:** Students write the *triples* inside the pointer list — the pointer list must point **into** the triple table; do not merge them.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Linear IR** = a flat sequence of simple instructions; no tree/graph hierarchy.
- **Stack-Machine Code** uses an *implicit* operand stack; instructions are `PUSH`, `POP`, `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `JUMP`, `JUMPF`. **Real-world user: JVM.**
- **Three-Address Code (TAC)** has the canonical form $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ with **at most one operator** per instruction. **Real-world user: LLVM, GCC.**
- **Quadruple** = $\langle \text{op}, \text{arg1}, \text{arg2}, \text{result} \rangle$ — best for renaming/optimization.
- **Triple** = $\langle \text{op}, \text{arg1}, \text{arg2} \rangle$ — compact, but positions are immutable.
- **Indirect Triple** = pointer list into triple table — best for reordering optimizations.
- **SSA** = every variable assigned exactly once; renames via subscripts; merge nodes need $\phi$-functions.
- **Code generation strategy:** AST → *post-order traversal* → stack code (easy); AST → *recursive* with temporaries → TAC (medium).
- **For `x = a + b * c`:** TAC needs 3 instructions; Stack code needs 6 instructions + 1 `POP`. Stack code is **shorter** but TAC is **more optimizable**.
- **The defining property of TAC:** maximum of **3 addresses (operands)** per instruction.
- **The defining property of Stack-Machine Code:** arithmetic instructions are *zero-address*; data is on the stack.
- **For board exams:** always draw the **quadruple table** in a tabular form; examiners reward visual clarity.
- **Common formula:** Number of TAC instructions = (number of binary operators in source) + 1.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Syntax-Driven Translation: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Syntax-Driven Translation: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Syntax-Directed Translation (SDT)** is a formalism that augments a context-free grammar (CFG) with a set of rules (called *semantic rules* or *translation rules*) attached to each production of the grammar. These rules prescribe how to compute values of *attributes* associated with grammar symbols, and how to emit output strings or intermediate code during the parsing of an input string.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, SDT is formally expressed as a **Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)**, which is a context-free grammar together with:

1. A finite set of **attributes** $A(X)$ for each grammar symbol $X$.
2. A finite set of **semantic rules** of the form $b := f(c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k)$, attached to each production, where $f$ is a function and $b, c_i$ are attributes of symbols occurring in the production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **SDD (Syntax-Directed Definition)** = declarative specification of what to compute (no order prescribed).
> - **SDT (Syntax-Directed Translation Scheme)** = operational specification of *when* to compute and *emit* during parsing (explicit action placement: { ... } between grammar symbols).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are reading a **recipe book written in English**:
- The **grammar** (sentences like "Add sugar", "Stir for 2 minutes") represents the *syntactic structure*.
- The **annotations/notes** written by the chef in the margins — "this is sweet", "cook for 5 more minutes", "serves 4 people" — are like the **semantic actions/attributes**.
- The act of cooking while reading the recipe (performing actions at the right moment) is the **translation**.

Similarly, in a compiler:
- The parser *recognises* the syntactic structure of a program.
- The SDT *embeds* semantic processing (type checking, code generation, etc.) into the parse itself.
- This is called **syntax-driven** because the translation process is completely **directed by the syntax tree** structure.

> [!NOTE]
> A common mistake students make is to confuse SDT with semantic analysis. SDT is the **mechanism/framework**; semantic analysis is one of its many *applications*.

### 1.3 Why Syntax-Directed Translation?

A compiler must do more than *recognise* a program's structure — it must also *translate* it into machine code, intermediate code, or another form. Rather than performing a separate post-parse walk, we **fold** the translation into the parsing process itself. This provides:
- **Efficiency**: One tree walk instead of multiple passes.
- **Locality**: Semantic actions are placed close to the syntactic constructs that trigger them.
- **Clarity**: Grammar + rules becomes a self-documenting specification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Two Views of Translation

| View | What it Specifies | Order of Evaluation | KTU Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SDD** (Syntax-Directed Definition) | *What* attribute values to compute | Not specified (declarative) | Theory, proof, dependency analysis |
| **SDT Scheme** | *How* to implement via embedded actions | Specified (e.g., postfix) | Practical implementation |

### 2.2 Types of Attributes

**A. Synthesized Attributes**
- A *synthesized* attribute of a non-terminal at a parse-tree node is computed only from the attributes of its **children**.
- Information flows **bottom-up** (from leaves toward the root).
- Denoted $S(X)$ for symbol $X$.
- **KTU 2024 Rule**: For production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$, the rule $A.a := f(X_1.b, \ldots, X_n.c)$ is a *synthesized* rule.

**B. Inherited Attributes**
- An *inherited* attribute of a non-terminal on the RHS of a production is computed from the **attributes of the parent and/or siblings**.
- Information flows **top-down** and **left-to-right**.
- Denoted $I(X)$ for symbol $X$.
- Useful for passing context (e.g., type information, symbol table entries) down the tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Terminal attributes** in standard SDT are supplied by the **lexical analyzer** (e.g., `num.val`, `id.name`). They are the *leaves* of the attribute world.

### 2.3 Classification of SDD

**1. S-Attributed Definition (S-SDD)**
- Uses **only synthesized** attributes.
- Can be evaluated naturally in a **single bottom-up pass** (e.g., during LR parsing).
- Always **well-defined** — no cycles possible in the dependency graph.

**2. L-Attributed Definition (L-SDD)**
- Each inherited attribute of $X_j$ (the $j$-th symbol on RHS of $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$) depends only on:
  1. Attributes of the **parent** $A$,
  2. **Inherited attributes** of $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{j-1}$ (left siblings),
  3. Attributes of $X_j$ **itself** (only inherent circularity if it exists, which must be absent).
- Every S-SDD is also an L-SDD, but not vice-versa.
- Can be evaluated during **predictive (top-down) parsing** by placing actions in the right place.

### 2.4 Dependency Graph & Topological Sort

Given a parse tree, we construct a **dependency graph**:
- For each parse-tree node $N$ (corresponding to grammar symbol $X$), draw a node.
- For each semantic rule $b := f(c_1, \ldots, c_k)$, draw directed edges from each $c_i$ to $b$.

A valid evaluation order = **topological sort** of this graph. The SDD is **well-defined** iff the graph is acyclic (DAG) for **every** parse tree.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Notation / Rule | Boundary / Constraint | Application in Compiler |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Synthesized attribute rule | $A.a := f(X_1.b, \ldots, X_n.c)$ | $A$ is on LHS; rule uses only children's attributes | Expression evaluation, type propagation upward |
| Inherited attribute rule | $X_i.a := f(A.b, X_1.c, \ldots, X_{i-1}.d)$ | $X_i$ on RHS; uses parent or left-siblings only | Passing type/name context downward |
| S-SDD condition | All attributes synthesized | No cycles in dep. graph guaranteed | Used in bottom-up (LALR/Yacc) parsers |
| L-SDD condition | Inherited of $X_i$ depends on $\{$ parent, left siblings, self (acyclic) $\}$ | L-SDD ⊃ S-SDD | Used in top-down (LL) parsers |
| Topological sort | Linear order $v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n$ s.t. edge $v_i \rightarrow v_j$ has $i < j$ | DAG property must hold | Defines legal evaluation order |
| Embedded action placement (postfix SDT) | $A \rightarrow X \, \{ \text{action} \}$ | Action executed after $X$ processed | S-SDD implementation in bottom-up parser |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Almost every production compiler (GCC's RTL generation, LLVM's instruction selection, Java's annotation processing pipeline) uses SDT principles. SDT is the conceptual backbone of **attribute grammars** used in **domain-specific languages (DSLs)**, **program analysis tools**, and **query optimisers** in databases.

### 2.6 Applications of SDT

1. **Construction of Syntax Trees** — building nodes via synthesized `ptr` attributes.
2. **Type Checking** — propagating and checking types through inherited/synthesized attributes.
3. **Intermediate Code Generation** — emitting three-address code via postfix actions.
4. **Evaluation of Arithmetic Expressions** — direct bottom-up computation.
5. **Translation to Virtual Machine Code** (JVM, LLVM IR, MSIL).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Arithmetic Expression Evaluation (S-SDD)

**Grammar:**
$$
E \rightarrow E_1 + T \mid T
$$
$$
T \rightarrow T_1 \times F \mid F
$$
$$
F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{num}
$$

**Goal:** Evaluate the value of the arithmetic expression.

**Syntax-Directed Definition (S-SDD):**

| Production | Semantic Rule |
| :--- | :--- |
| $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ | $E.\text{val} := E_1.\text{val} + T.\text{val}$ |
| $E \rightarrow T$ | $E.\text{val} := T.\text{val}$ |
| $T \rightarrow T_1 \times F$ | $T.\text{val} := T_1.\text{val} \times F.\text{val}$ |
| $T \rightarrow F$ | $T.\text{val} := F.\text{val}$ |
| $F \rightarrow (E)$ | $F.\text{val} := E.\text{val}$ |
| $F \rightarrow \text{num}$ | $F.\text{val} := \text{num}.\text{lexval}$ |

**Step-by-step derivation for the input string:** `3 + 4 × 5`

**Step 1 — Build the parse tree** (root $E$, leaves $\text{num}$ terminals). The parser matches the string to the unique parse tree rooted at $E$.

**Step 2 — Annotate leaves from the lexical analyzer.** The lexval of each `num` is its numeric value:
- Leftmost `num` has lexval = 3.
- Middle `num` has lexval = 4.
- Rightmost `num` has lexval = 5.

**Step 3 — Compute bottom-up (synthesized evaluation).**

For each leaf $F \rightarrow \text{num}$, set $F.\text{val} := \text{num}.\text{lexval}$:
$$
F_{\text{left}}.\text{val} = 3, \quad F_{\text{mid}}.\text{val} = 4, \quad F_{\text{right}}.\text{val} = 5
$$

For each $T \rightarrow F$ production:
$$
T_{\text{left}}.\text{val} = F_{\text{left}}.\text{val} = 3
$$
$$
T_{\text{mid}}.\text{val} = F_{\text{mid}}.\text{val} = 4
$$
$$
T_{\text{right}}.\text{val} = F_{\text{right}}.\text{val} = 5
$$

For the multiplication $T \rightarrow T_1 \times F$ (the inner production):
$$
T_{\text{mul}}.\text{val} = T_{\text{mid}}.\text{val} \times F_{\text{right}}.\text{val} = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

For $T \rightarrow T_1$ (pass-up):
$$
T_{\text{top}}.\text{val} = T_{\text{mul}}.\text{val} = 20
$$

For the addition $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$:
$$
E_{\text{root}}.\text{val} = E_1.\text{val} + T_{\text{top}}.\text{val}
$$
$$
E_{\text{root}}.\text{val} = 3 + 20 = 23
$$

**Final answer:** $E.\text{val} = 23$. ✓

**Conversion logic at each step:** Each rule is a *pure function* of the children's attribute values; the synthesized value propagates upward from leaves to the root. This is the canonical S-SDD property.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Constructing a Syntax Tree (Mixed SDD)

**Grammar (declarations and expressions):**
$$
D \rightarrow T \, L \quad ; \quad L \rightarrow L_1, \, \text{id} \quad ; \quad L \rightarrow \text{id}
$$
$$
T \rightarrow \text{int} \quad ; \quad T \rightarrow \text{real}
$$

**Attributes defined:**
- $T.\text{type}$ : synthesized (e.g., `integer` or `real`).
- $L.\text{in}$ : inherited attribute — the type expected for identifiers in this list.
- $L.\text{ptr}$ : synthesized — pointer to a syntax-tree node representing the identifier list.
- $\text{id}.\text{place}$ : supplied by lexer — pointer to symbol-table entry for the identifier.

**Semantic Rules (this is an L-SDD, not S-SDD):**

| Production | Semantic Rules |
| :--- | :--- |
| $D \rightarrow T \, L$ | $L.\text{in} := T.\text{type}$ |
| $T \rightarrow \text{int}$ | $T.\text{type} := \text{integer}$ |
| $T \rightarrow \text{real}$ | $T.\text{type} := \text{real}$ |
| $L \rightarrow L_1, \, \text{id}$ | $L_1.\text{in} := L.\text{in} ; \quad L.\text{ptr} := \text{makenode}(\text{id}.\text{place}, L_1.\text{ptr})$ |
| $L \rightarrow \text{id}$ | $L.\text{ptr} := \text{makenode}(\text{id}.\text{place}, \text{nil})$ |

**Step-by-step evaluation for input:** `int a, b, c`

**Step 1 — Lexer populates** $\text{id}.\text{place}$ for `a`, `b`, `c` with pointers to their symbol-table entries $p_a, p_b, p_c$.

**Step 2 — Build parse tree.** The root production is $D \rightarrow T \, L$. The $L$ subtree recursively derives `id , id , id`.

**Step 3 — Evaluate bottom-up (synthesized) and top-down (inherited).**

Rule $T \rightarrow \text{int}$:
$$
T.\text{type} = \text{integer}
$$

Rule $D \rightarrow T \, L$, using $L.\text{in} := T.\text{type}$:
$$
L_{\text{top}}.\text{in} = \text{integer}
$$

Innermost $L \rightarrow \text{id}$ (for `c`):
$$
L_c.\text{ptr} = \text{makenode}(p_c, \text{nil}) = \text{node}(p_c, \text{null})
$$

Next $L \rightarrow L_1, \text{id}$ (for `b`):
$$
L_b.\text{in} = L_{\text{parent}}.\text{in} = \text{integer}
$$
$$
L_b.\text{ptr} = \text{makenode}(p_b, L_c.\text{ptr}) = \text{node}(p_b, \text{node}(p_c, \text{null}))
$$

Outermost $L \rightarrow L_1, \text{id}$ (for `a`):
$$
L_a.\text{in} = L_{\text{top}}.\text{in} = \text{integer}
$$
$$
L_a.\text{ptr} = \text{makenode}(p_a, L_b.\text{ptr}) = \text{node}(p_a, \text{node}(p_b, \text{node}(p_c, \text{null})))
$$

**Final syntax tree:** A right-leaning linked list representing the identifier list, each node carrying the type `integer` via inheritance. ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Synthesized vs Inherited

| Property | Synthesized | Inherited |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Information flow | Bottom-up | Top-down / left-to-right |
| Computed from | Children of the node | Parent + left siblings |
| Parser compatibility | Bottom-up (LR, LALR) | Top-down (LL) + LR with care |
| Pass count for evaluation | 1 pass (postorder) | Multiple passes or careful action placement |
| Cycle possibility | None | Possible (must check) |
| Use case | Expression value, return type | Type context, symbol-table lookups |
| Notation example | $E.\text{val} := E_1.\text{val} + T.\text{val}$ | $L_1.\text{in} := L.\text{in}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Used Above:** The principle of "**substitution in order of dependency**" — every attribute on the right-hand side of a rule is bound (either inherited from above, synthesized from a child, or supplied by the lexer) *before* the rule on the left-hand side fires. Topological sort is the formal guarantee of correctness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Block Diagram of the SDT Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Program Text]
    lex[Lexical Analyzer]
    tok[Token Stream with Lexical Values]
    parse[Parser with Embedded Actions]
    sdattr[Attribute Computation Engine]
    sdtree[Annotated Parse Tree]
    emit[Output Translation]
    err[Error Reporting Module]

    src --> lex
    lex --> tok
    tok --> parse
    parse --> sdtree
    sdtree --> sdattr
    sdattr --> emit
    parse -.-> err
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Description:**
- **Lexical Analyzer** populates terminal attributes (e.g., `num.lexval`, `id.name`).
- **Parser** builds the parse tree, and **Embedded Actions** (the SDT scheme) trigger at specific points.
- **Attribute Computation Engine** applies the semantic rules in the topological order of the dependency graph.
- **Output Translation** may be three-address code, a syntax tree, type information, or any desired target.
- **Error Reporting** is invoked when semantic rules detect violations (e.g., type mismatch).

---

### 4.2 Dependency Graph Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Parse Tree Node N for Symbol X]
    rules[Retrieve Semantic Rules of Production]
    deps[For Each Rule b := f of c1, c2, ..., ck]
    edges[Draw Directed Edge from c sub i to b]
    cycle[Check for Cycles]
    tsort[Compute Topological Order]
    eval[Evaluate Attributes in Topological Order]
    done[Translation Complete]

    start --> rules
    rules --> deps
    deps --> edges
    edges --> cycle
    cycle -->|Acyclic| tsort
    cycle -->|Cyclic| err2[Report Semantic Error]
    tsort --> eval
    eval --> done
```

---

### 4.3 L-Attributed Definition Visual: Attribute Flow Along a Production

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Abox[A .a parent attribute]
    X1box[X1 .b inherited]
    X2box[X2 .c inherited]
    X3box[X3 .d synthesized]
    Aprod[Production A to X1 X2 X3]

    Abox -- parent input --> X1box
    X1box -- left sibling input --> X2box
    X2box -- left sibling input --> X3box
    X3box -- synthesized result --> Abox
    Aprod -.-> Abox
    Aprod -.-> X1box
    Aprod -.-> X2box
    Aprod -.-> X3box
```

**Reading the diagram:** In an L-attributed definition, $X_2$'s inherited attribute may use $A.a$ (parent) and $X_1.b$ (left sibling), but **never** $X_3.d$ (right sibling). This is the **L-attribution constraint** that guarantees acyclicity for top-down evaluation.

---

### 4.4 Attribute Flow in the Expression `3 + 4 × 5` (S-SDD)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Eroot[E root val]
    Eleft[E1 val]
    Ttop[T top val]
    Tmid[T1 val]
    Tright[T val]
    Fright[F val]
    Fmid[F val]
    Fleft[F val]
    L1[num lexval = 3]
    L2[num lexval = 4]
    L3[num lexval = 5]

    L1 --> Fleft
    Fleft --> Eleft
    L2 --> Fmid
    Fmid --> Tmid
    L3 --> Fright
    Fright --> Tright
    Tmid --> Ttop
    Tright --> Ttop
    Eleft --> Eroot
    Ttop --> Eroot
```

**Diagram description:** Each `num.lexval` is supplied by the lexer; values flow bottom-up as $F \rightarrow T \rightarrow E$. The root attribute $E.\text{val} = 23$ is computed at the top.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*Define a Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD). Differentiate between synthesized and inherited attributes with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)** is a context-free grammar augmented with a set of attributes $A(X)$ for each grammar symbol $X$ and a set of semantic rules of the form $b := f(c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k)$ attached to each production. The rules specify how attribute values are computed in terms of other attributes.

**Synthesized attribute** — computed from attributes of *children* of a parse-tree node. Information flows **bottom-up**.
*Example:* $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$, rule $E.\text{val} := E_1.\text{val} + T.\text{val}$ (here `val` is synthesized).

**Inherited attribute** — computed from attributes of the *parent* and/or *left siblings*. Information flows **top-down** / left-to-right.
*Example:* $D \rightarrow T \, L$, rule $L.\text{in} := T.\text{type}$ (here `in` is inherited; receives the type from the parent $T$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*What is an L-Attributed Definition? State the condition that a production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$ must satisfy to be L-attributed.*

**Model Answer:**

An **L-Attributed Definition (L-SDD)** is an SDD in which each inherited attribute of the $j$-th symbol $X_j$ on the right-hand side of a production may depend only on:

1. The attributes of the **left-side symbol $A$** (the parent).
2. The **inherited attributes** of $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_{j-1}$ (left siblings).
3. Attributes of $X_j$ itself, but only in a way that introduces no cycles.

This restriction ensures the dependency graph is always acyclic and allows natural evaluation during a top-down (LL) parse, or by embedding actions at the right position.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose Either A or B)

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Consider the following grammar for signed numbers. Construct an S-attributed SDD that computes the decimal value of the signed number. Show the parse tree and the annotated attribute values for the input string `− 5 + 2`.*

$$
\text{num} \rightarrow \text{sign} \, \text{num} \mid \text{digit}
$$
$$
\text{sign} \rightarrow + \mid -
$$
$$
\text{digit} \rightarrow 0 \mid 1 \mid 2 \mid 3 \mid 4 \mid 5 \mid 6 \mid 7 \mid 8 \mid 9
$$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define attributes.**
- $\text{digit}.\text{val}$: integer value of the digit (synthesized; derived from `digit.lexval`).
- $\text{sign}.\text{neg}$: boolean — `true` if sign is `−` (synthesized).
- $\text{num}.\text{val}$: integer value of the signed number (synthesized).

**Step 2 — Semantic rules (S-SDD).**

| Production | Semantic Rule |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\text{num} \rightarrow \text{digit}$ | $\text{num}.\text{val} := \text{digit}.\text{lexval}$ |
| $\text{num} \rightarrow \text{sign} \, \text{num}_1$ | $\text{num}.\text{val} := \text{sign}.\text{neg} \,? \, -\text{num}_1.\text{val} : \text{num}_1.\text{val}$ |
| $\text{sign} \rightarrow +$ | $\text{sign}.\text{neg} := \text{false}$ |
| $\text{sign} \rightarrow -$ | $\text{sign}.\text{neg} := \text{true}$ |
| $\text{digit} \rightarrow 0$ | $\text{digit}.\text{val} := 0$ |
| $\ldots$ (similar for 1–9) | $\ldots$ |
| $\text{digit} \rightarrow 9$ | $\text{digit}.\text{val} := 9$ |

**Step 3 — Parse tree for `− 5 + 2`.** The root production is $\text{num} \rightarrow \text{sign} \, \text{num}_1$, where the inner $\text{num}_1$ again applies $\text{num} \rightarrow \text{sign} \, \text{num}_2$ and finally $\text{num}_2 \rightarrow \text{digit}$.

**Step 4 — Annotated bottom-up evaluation.**

- $\text{digit}_\text{2}.\text{val} = 2$ (from $\text{digit} \rightarrow 2$).
- $\text{sign}_2.\text{neg} = \text{false}$ (from $\text{sign} \rightarrow +$).
- $\text{num}_2.\text{val} = \text{sign}_2.\text{neg} ? -\text{num}_2.\text{val} : \text{num}_2.\text{val} = 2$ (positive sign).
- $\text{digit}_1.\text{val} = 5$.
- $\text{sign}_1.\text{neg} = \text{true}$ (from $\text{sign} \rightarrow -$).
- $\text{num}_1.\text{val} = \text{true} ? -5 : 5 = -5$.
- $\text{sign}_\text{root}.\text{neg} = \text{false}$ (the outermost is implicit positive).
- $\text{num}.\text{val} = \text{false} ? -\text{num}_1.\text{val} : \text{num}_1.\text{val} = -5$.

**Final result:** $\text{num}.\text{val} = -5$. ✓

**(b)** [7 Marks] *Construct the dependency graph for the production $A \rightarrow B \, C$ with semantic rules:*
1. *B.i := A.s*
2. *C.i := B.i + A.s*
3. *A.s := B.s + C.i*

*Is the SDD cyclic? Justify.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify attributes per symbol.**
- $A$: synthesized $s$.
- $B$: inherited $i$, synthesized $s$.
- $C$: inherited $i$, synthesized $s$.

**Step 2 — Build the dependency graph (one parse-tree instance of $A \rightarrow B \, C$).**

Edges drawn from each RHS of a rule to the LHS:

1. Rule 1: $B.i := A.s$ → edge $A.s \rightarrow B.i$.
2. Rule 2: $C.i := B.i + A.s$ → edges $A.s \rightarrow C.i$ and $B.i \rightarrow C.i$.
3. Rule 3: $A.s := B.s + C.i$ → edges $B.s \rightarrow A.s$ and $C.i \rightarrow A.s$.

**Step 3 — List all edges:**
$$
A.s \rightarrow B.i, \quad A.s \rightarrow C.i, \quad B.i \rightarrow C.i, \quad B.s \rightarrow A.s, \quad C.i \rightarrow A.s
$$

**Step 4 — Cycle detection.** A cycle requires a directed path from a node back to itself.
- Starting at $A.s$: $A.s \rightarrow B.i \rightarrow C.i \rightarrow A.s$ → **CYCLE FOUND!**
- $A.s \rightarrow C.i \rightarrow A.s$ is already a smaller cycle (length 2).

**Conclusion:** The SDD is **cyclic** (has a circular dependency), so it is **not well-defined** and cannot be evaluated unambiguously. The SDD is **invalid** for translation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw all five edges** — every rule contributes at least one edge. Skipping rule 2's $A.s \rightarrow C.i$ edge is the most common mistake.
> 2. **Mistaking the LHS of a rule for the source of the edge.** The convention is: *arrow points from the attribute on the RHS of `:=` to the attribute on the LHS*. Reversing this inverts the dependency direction.
> 3. **Claiming "cyclic ⇒ no topological sort" without writing the explicit cycle** — the examiner wants the cycle path explicitly.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** [7 Marks] *Define an S-attributed definition. Show, using a dependency graph, why every S-SDD is naturally evaluated in a single bottom-up pass during LR parsing.*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition.** An **S-Attributed Definition (S-SDD)** is a syntax-directed definition in which *every* attribute of *every* non-terminal is a **synthesized** attribute. That is, the only semantic rules have the form $A.a := f(\text{attributes of } X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ where the LHS is the LHS non-terminal and the RHS involves only its children's attributes.

**Why one bottom-up pass suffices — proof by structure of dependency graph:**

Consider a parse tree node $N$ corresponding to non-terminal $A$ with children $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ (parse-tree children). Since $A.a$ depends *only* on the children, every edge in the dependency graph at $N$ points from a child attribute to the parent attribute $A.a$.

**Inductive argument:**
- *Base case:* The leaves of the parse tree are terminals. Their attribute values are supplied by the **lexical analyzer** — they are the *starting* points and have no incoming edges from within the tree.
- *Inductive step:* Suppose all children $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ of an internal node $N$ have had all their attribute values computed. Then the synthesized attribute $A.a$ at $N$ can be computed, because the rule $A.a := f(\text{children's attributes})$ has all its inputs available.

Since a bottom-up (postorder) traversal of a tree visits every child **before** the parent, all inputs to a synthesized rule are ready at the moment we visit the parent. Hence a **single postorder traversal** of the parse tree (which is exactly what an LR parser implicitly performs using its stack) computes *all* attributes. This is the principle behind tools like **Yacc/Bison**, where mid-rule actions are placed in postfix form to align with stack-pop order.

**(b)** [7 Marks] *Convert the following SDD into an SDT scheme that can be implemented in a bottom-up parser (postfix embedding of actions):*
- $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$, rule $E.\text{val} := E_1.\text{val} + T.\text{val}$
- $E \rightarrow T$, rule $E.\text{val} := T.\text{val}$
- $T \rightarrow T_1 \times F$, rule $T.\text{val} := T_1.\text{val} \times F.\text{val}$
- $T \rightarrow F$, rule $T.\text{val} := F.\text{val}$
- $F \rightarrow (E)$, rule $F.\text{val} := E.\text{val}$
- $F \rightarrow \text{num}$, rule $F.\text{val} := \text{num}.\text{lexval}$

**Model Solution:**

Since this is an S-SDD (only synthesized attributes), actions can be safely placed at the **end** of each production (postfix position). The action is performed exactly when the parser reduces by that production, which is when the synthesized attribute of the LHS becomes ready.

**Postfix SDT Scheme:**

| Production with Embedded Action |
| :--- |
| $F \rightarrow \text{num} \quad \{\, F.\text{val} = \text{num}.\text{lexval}; \,\}$ |
| $F \rightarrow ( \, E \, ) \quad \{\, F.\text{val} = E.\text{val}; \,\}$ |
| $T \rightarrow F \quad \{\, T.\text{val} = F.\text{val}; \,\}$ |
| $T \rightarrow T_1 \times F \quad \{\, T.\text{val} = T_1.\text{val} \times F.\text{val}; \,\}$ |
| $E \rightarrow T \quad \{\, E.\text{val} = T.\text{val}; \,\}$ |
| $E \rightarrow E_1 + T \quad \{\, E.\text{val} = E_1.\text{val} + T.\text{val}; \,\}$ |

**Why this works in a bottom-up parser:**

In a shift-reduce (LR) parser, a reduction by $A \rightarrow \alpha$ is performed only after the entire RHS $\alpha$ has been shifted and is on top of the stack. At that moment, the attribute values of every symbol in $\alpha$ are available in the value-stack (a parallel stack maintained by the SDT-enabled parser). Therefore, the action placed at the end of the production has all the inputs it needs and can immediately compute the LHS synthesized attribute, which is pushed onto the value-stack in place of the popped RHS values.

**Validation for input `3 + 4 × 5`:** the parser reduces `4 × 5` first (multiplying), then `3 + 20` (adding), giving `23` — exactly matching the S-SDD evaluation from Section 3.1. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Placing actions *before* the RHS symbols in an S-SDD** — this is the most common error. For an S-SDD, actions **must** be in postfix (suffix) position to align with the parser's reduce step.
> 2. **Forgetting the action for unary productions** like $E \rightarrow T$ and $T \rightarrow F$ — these trivial passthroughs *still* need explicit actions (e.g., `E.val = T.val;`) to propagate the attribute up the stack.
> 3. **Using inherited attributes in an S-SDD** — the moment an inherited attribute is introduced, it is no longer S-attributed and the postfix placement rule breaks down.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SDD vs SDT:** SDD = *what* to compute; SDT = *how/when* to emit actions during parsing.
- **Synthesized attribute**: bottom-up; uses only children → supports S-SDD; safe in LR parsers.
- **Inherited attribute**: top-down / left-to-right; uses parent + left siblings → supports L-SDD; requires careful action placement.
- **S-SDD ⊂ L-SDD** — every S-attributed definition is L-attributed, but the reverse is not true.
- **L-attributed condition** for production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$: the inherited attribute of $X_j$ may depend only on $A$, $X_1, \ldots, X_{j-1}$, and $X_j$ itself (acyclic).
- **Dependency graph** must be acyclic for the SDD to be *well-defined*; a topological sort gives a valid evaluation order.
- **Postfix SDT scheme** is the standard way to implement an S-SDD inside a bottom-up (LR/LALR/Yacc) parser.
- **Order of actions in LR parsing**: action fires **on reduction** — i.e., at the *end* of the production; this is the foundation of Yacc-style translation.
- **Common KTU trap**: an SDD with even one cycle is invalid; the examiner will look for the *explicit cycle path* in the dependency graph.
- **Real-world link**: SDT is the conceptual basis for tools like **Yacc/Bison**, **ANTLR**, **attribute-grammar engines**, and the front-end of production compilers (GCC, LLVM).
- **High-yield memory point**: "**Synthesized = children, Inherited = parent/left**" — remember this one-liner to instantly classify any attribute in the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Translating Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Translating Expressions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Translating Expressions** is the intermediate code generation phase of a compiler in which high-level source language expressions (arithmetic, boolean, relational, and control-flow) are converted into an intermediate representation (IR) — typically **Three-Address Code (TAC)** — suitable for target code generation and optimization.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic belongs to the **Syntax-Directed Translation** paradigm where a **Translation Scheme** (a Context-Free Grammar augmented with semantic actions) is used to map expressions into a linearized, low-level form.

The key intermediate representations discussed are:

| IR Form | Description | Address Limit |
|---|---|---|
| **Postfix Notation** | Operator placed after operands | Stack-machine friendly |
| **Three-Address Code (TAC)** | At most **3 addresses** per instruction | Unlimited temporaries |
| **Quadruples** | A record `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | Indexed by quad number |
| **Triples** | Uses instruction numbers instead of temporaries | Compact form |
| **Indirect Triples** | List of pointers to triples | Easier optimization |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Recipe Translator 🍳**
> Imagine you are a chef who only understands the language of a microwave oven. When your friend gives you a high-level English recipe ("crack two eggs into a bowl and whisk them"), you don't give that recipe directly to the microwave. Instead, you **translate** it into tiny atomic microwave instructions: "Press power-level 5, set timer 30 sec, beep when done." The microwave can only execute **one or two operands at a time** — exactly like a CPU that processes **three-address instructions**.

- **Source expression** = High-level English recipe
- **Intermediate code** = Atomic microwave steps
- **TAC instruction** = A single, simple machine-friendly step
- **Temporary variable (t1, t2, t3...)** = The microwave's scratchpad memory

## 1.3 Position in Compiler Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the translation of expressions occurs **after** parsing and **before** code optimization. It is the **semantic action phase** of a syntax-directed translation scheme, riding on the **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** built during syntax analysis.

**Pipeline Position:**
$$\text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Lexer} \rightarrow \text{Parser} \rightarrow \text{AST} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Expression Translation (TAC)}} \rightarrow \text{Optimizer} \rightarrow \text{Target Code}$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three-Address Code (TAC) — The Heart of Expression Translation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Three-Address Code (TAC)** instruction has the general form:
> $$x = y \;\text{op}\; z$$
> where `x`, `y`, `z` are addresses (names, constants, or compiler-generated temporaries) and `op` is a binary operator.

### 2.1.1 Allowed Instruction Forms (KTU Standard)

The complete set of TAC forms used for translating expressions:

1. **Binary assignment:** $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$
2. **Unary assignment:** $x = \text{op}\; y$
3. **Copy:** $x = y$
4. **Unconditional jump:** $\text{goto}\; L$
5. **Conditional jump:** $\text{if}\; x \;\text{relop}\; y \;\text{goto}\; L$
6. **Indexed copy (arrays):** $x = y[i]$, $x[i] = y$
7. **Address/pointer:** $x = \&y$, $x = *y$, $*x = y$
8. **Procedure call:** $\text{param}\; x$, $\text{call}\; p,\;n$, $x = \text{call}\; p$

### 2.1.2 Why "Three-Address"?

- The **left side (LHS)** is *one* address (the destination).
- The **right side (RHS)** has at most *two* addresses (operands `y` and `z`).
- Total = **3 addresses** per instruction maximum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This restriction exists because the target machine (or interpreter of TAC) handles only **simple register-level operations** — typically one or two source operands and one destination.

## 2.2 Translation Schemes for Expressions

### 2.2.1 The SDT for Binary Expressions

Production rule with embedded semantic actions (using **syntax-directed translation**):

$$E \rightarrow E_1 + T \quad \{\;E.\text{addr} = \text{newTemp}();\;\text{gen}(E.\text{addr} = E_1.\text{addr} + T.\text{addr})\;\}$$

$$E \rightarrow E_1 - T \quad \{\;E.\text{addr} = \text{newTemp}();\;\text{gen}(E.\text{addr} = E_1.\text{addr} - T.\text{addr})\;\}$$

$$E \rightarrow T \quad \{\;E.\text{addr} = T.\text{addr}\;\}$$

### 2.2.2 The `newTemp()` Function

$$\text{newTemp}() : \text{returns a fresh name } t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots \text{ each time it is called}$$

The `gen()` function appends a TAC instruction to the instruction stream.

## 2.3 Postfix Notation (Reverse Polish Notation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Postfix notation** places the operator **after** its operands. It eliminates the need for parentheses and can be directly evaluated by a **stack machine**.

**Example Conversion Table:**

| Infix Expression | Postfix Form |
|---|---|
| $a + b$ | $a\;b\;+$ |
| $(a+b) * c$ | $a\;b\;+\;c\;*$ |
| $a + b * c$ | $a\;b\;c\;*\;+$ |
| $-(a+b) * (c-d)$ | $a\;b\;+\;\text{uminus}\;c\;d\;-\;*$ |

## 2.4 Quadruple Representation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **quadruple** is a 4-tuple record: `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`
> - `op` — operator
> - `arg1`, `arg2` — operand addresses (may be empty for unary ops)
> - `result` — destination temporary

**Worked Example** for `a + b * c`:

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | b | c | t1 |
| (1) | `+` | a | t1 | t2 |

## 2.5 Triple Representation

A **triple** uses the **instruction number** of a previous instruction instead of a temporary name:

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | b | c |
| (1) | `+` | a | (0) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Triples are more compact** but make **optimization harder** because moving an instruction requires updating all references to its number.

## 2.6 Indirect Triples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **indirect triple** adds a **pointer list** on top of triples, so the triples themselves can be moved/reordered (e.g., during peephole optimization) without renumbering.

The pointer list looks like:
$$\text{instruction} = \text{triples}[\text{list}[i]]$$

## 2.7 Translation of Boolean Expressions

> [!NOTE]
> **Boolean expressions** are used in:
> - **Logical conditions** (`if (E)`, `while (E)`)
> - **Computed boolean values** stored in variables

### 2.7.1 Two Methods

| Method | Description | Used When |
|---|---|---|
| **Numerical Method** | Treat TRUE=1, FALSE=0; evaluate like arithmetic | Assigning bool to a variable |
| **Control-Flow Method** | Use conditional jumps; short-circuit | `if`, `while`, `for` |

### 2.7.2 Numerical Method Translation

For $a < b \;\text{OR}\; c < d \;\text{AND}\; e < f$:

$$t_1 = a < b$$
$$t_2 = c < d$$
$$t_3 = e < f$$
$$t_4 = t_2 \;\text{AND}\; t_3$$
$$t_5 = t_1 \;\text{OR}\; t_4$$

### 2.7.3 Short-Circuit Code (Control-Flow)

For the same boolean using short-circuit `||` and `&&`:
- `||` means: if left is **TRUE**, skip evaluation of right.
- `&&` means: if left is **FALSE**, skip evaluation of right.

The control-flow translation for $a < b \;\text{OR}\; c < d \;\text{AND}\; e < f$:

```
L1: if a < b goto L2
    if c >= d goto L3
    if e < f goto L2
L3: t = 0
    goto next
L2: t = 1
next:
```

## 2.8 Backpatching

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Backpatching** is a technique to fill in the *jump targets* of boolean/control-flow code in a **single left-to-right pass**, avoiding the need for multiple passes.

### 2.8.1 Key Data Structures

- `true_list` — list of TAC instructions that *jump to true* (yet to be filled)
- `false_list` — list of TAC instructions that *jump to false* (yet to be filled)
- `next_quad` — index of the **next** TAC instruction to be emitted
- `makelist(i)` — create a new list containing index `i`
- `merge(p1, p2)` — concatenate two lists
- `backpatch(p, i)` — fill target `i` into all instructions in list `p`

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This cheat sheet captures all exam-critical formulas and rules for **Translating Expressions**.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TAC general form | $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ | Max 3 addresses |
| 2 | Postfix of $a + b * c$ | $a\;b\;c\;*\;+$ | Operator after operands |
| 3 | Quadruple tuple | $(op, arg_1, arg_2, result)$ | 4 fields |
| 4 | Triple tuple | $(op, arg_1, arg_2)$ | Uses instr. # |
| 5 | newTemp counter | $t_i,\; i = i+1$ | Fresh name each call |
| 6 | Boolean TRUE/FALSE | $1 / 0$ | Numerical encoding |
| 7 | Short-circuit `&&` | left FALSE → skip right | Control-flow form |
| 8 | Short-circuit `\|\|` | left TRUE → skip right | Control-flow form |
| 9 | Array element $a[i]$ | Base + $i \times width$ | Address arithmetic |
| 10 | Backpatch | `backpatch(L, target)` | Fills jump targets |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Expression translation is **the backbone of every modern compiler**:
> - **GCC, Clang, LLVM** all lower expressions to an IR similar to TAC (LLVM IR is a 3-address form).
> - **JVM bytecode** and **WebAssembly** are stack-based postfix-like codes derived from expression translation.
> - **Just-In-Time (JIT) compilers** (e.g., V8, HotSpot) re-translate expressions at runtime.
- Database query optimizers translate SQL expressions to relational algebra + TAC-like plans.
- Static analyzers (SonarQube, Coverity) use TAC to do data-flow analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example #1 — Arithmetic Expression

**Expression:** $x = a + b * c - d / e$

**Step 1 — Identify the parse tree and operator precedence:**

The grammar-driven parse order is:
1. `b * c` (highest precedence, `*` and `/`)
2. `d / e`
3. `(b*c) + a` (lower precedence `+`)
4. `((b*c) + a) - (d/e)`

**Step 2 — Issue temporaries one by one:**

| Step | Operation | TAC Issued |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Multiply b and c | $t_1 = b * c$ |
| 2 | Divide d by e | $t_2 = d / e$ |
| 3 | Add a and t1 | $t_3 = a + t_1$ |
| 4 | Subtract t2 from t3 | $t_4 = t_3 - t_2$ |
| 5 | Assign to x | $x = t_4$ |

**Step 3 — Render the complete TAC sequence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= b * c \\
t_2 &= d / e \\
t_3 &= a + t_1 \\
t_4 &= t_3 - t_2 \\
x &= t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation:** Each binary operation produces exactly one TAC instruction. Every intermediate subexpression receives a **fresh temporary** ($t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$) via `newTemp()`. The final result flows into the LHS variable `x`.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example #2 — Array Element Translation

**Expression:** $x = a[i] + b[j]$

**Step 1 — Address computation for $a[i]$:**

In TAC, array access is broken into two parts: a **type-checked** element access and an address computation. The standard form is:

$$a[i] \;\text{is computed as}\; \text{addr}(a) + i \times \text{width}$$

**Step 2 — Issue TAC using the standard translation scheme:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= i * \text{width}_a \\
t_2 &= a[t_1] \quad \text{(reads a[i])} \\
t_3 &= j * \text{width}_b \\
t_4 &= b[t_3] \quad \text{(reads b[j])} \\
t_5 &= t_2 + t_4 \\
x &= t_5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation:** 
- $t_1 = i * \text{width}_a$ computes the **byte offset** of the $i$-th element.
- $a[t_1]$ uses `t1` as the **index** — this is TAC's `x = y[i]` form where `y = a` and `i = t1`.
- The same pattern repeats for `b[j]`.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example #3 — Boolean Expression (Numerical)

**Expression:** `x = (a < b) AND (c > d) OR (e == f)`

**Step 1 — Generate relational sub-results:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a < b \\
t_2 &= c > d \\
t_3 &= e == f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply logical operators in precedence order (`AND` before `OR`):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_4 &= t_1 \;\text{AND}\; t_2 \\
t_5 &= t_4 \;\text{OR}\; t_3 \\
x &= t_5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation:** Each relational operator produces a 0/1 value, and logical operators treat them numerically.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example #4 — Short-Circuit Boolean Code

**Expression:** `if (a < b OR c < d) then S1 else S2`

**Step 1 — Plan the jumps:**

- We want to enter `S1` if **either** `a < b` **or** `c < d` is true.
- We want to enter `S2` if **both** are false.

**Step 2 — Emit the control-flow TAC:**

```
L1:  if a < b goto L_true
L2:  if c < d goto L_true
     goto L_false
L_true:  (code for S1)
         goto L_next
L_false: (code for S2)
L_next:  ...
```

**Step 3 — Symbolic representation with labels:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{if}\; a < b \;\text{goto}\; L_{true} \\
&\text{if}\; c < d \;\text{goto}\; L_{true} \\
&\text{goto}\; L_{false} \\
L_{true}&: \quad \text{[code for S1]} \\
&\quad\;\;\text{goto}\; L_{next} \\
L_{false}&: \quad \text{[code for S2]} \\
L_{next}&:
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation:**
- First condition is tested; if TRUE, jump straight to the THEN branch.
- Second condition is tested only if the first is FALSE (this is the short-circuit).
- If both are FALSE, fall through to ELSE.

---

## 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example #5 — Backpatching Translation

**Expression:** `if (a < b AND c < d) then x = 1 else x = 2`

**Step 1 — Grammar augmented with backpatching actions:**

| Production | Semantic Action |
|---|---|
| $E \rightarrow E_1 \;\text{AND}\; M\; E_2$ | `backpatch(E1.true, M.quad); E.true = E2.true; E.false = merge(E1.false, E2.false)` |
| $E \rightarrow E_1 \;\text{OR}\; M\; E_2$ | `backpatch(E1.false, M.quad); E.true = merge(E1.true, E2.true); E.false = E2.false` |
| $E \rightarrow \text{not}\; E_1$ | Swap true_list and false_list |
| $E \rightarrow (E_1)$ | Inherit lists |
| $E \rightarrow \text{id}_1 \;\text{relop}\; \text{id}_2$ | Emit `if id1 relop id2 goto _` (true) and `goto _` (false) |

Where `M` is a **marker non-terminal** that records the next quad index before parsing $E_2$.

**Step 2 — Trace through the input `a < b AND c < d`:**

- After reading `a < b`:
  - `next_quad = 100` (say)
  - Emit: `100: if a < b goto _` → put 100 in true_list of $E_1$
  - Emit: `101: goto _` → put 101 in false_list of $E_1$
- `M` now records `M.quad = 102`
- After reading `c < d`:
  - Emit: `102: if c < d goto _` → true_list
  - Emit: `103: goto _` → false_list
- Apply AND rule: `backpatch(E1.true, 102)` — this rewrites instruction 100 to `if a < b goto 102`.

**Step 3 — Resulting backpatched code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
100&:\; \text{if}\; a < b \;\text{goto}\; 102 \\
101&:\; \text{goto}\; \_ \;(\text{fill later}) \\
102&:\; \text{if}\; c < d \;\text{goto}\; \_ \;(\text{fill later}) \\
103&:\; \text{goto}\; \_ \;(\text{fill later})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Extend with THEN/ELSE code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
104&:\; t_1 = 1 \\
105&:\; \text{goto}\; 107 \\
106&:\; t_1 = 2 \\
107&:\; x = t_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final backpatched assembly:**

- `backpatch(102, 104)` — instruction 102 jumps to THEN
- `backpatch(103, 106)` — instruction 103 jumps to ELSE
- `backpatch(101, 106)` — instruction 101 jumps to ELSE
- Result: a complete, linear TAC program.

**Logic explanation:** Backpatching **eliminates the need for a second pass** to resolve jump targets. All jumps are left as placeholders (`_`) and filled in at the end using `backpatch()`.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Postfix | TAC | Quadruples | Triples | Indirect Triples |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Readability | Medium | High | High | Medium | High |
| Compactness | High | Low | Low | High | Medium |
| Reordering ease | Hard | Medium | Easy | Hard | Easy |
| Optimization support | Low | High | High | Low | High |
| Used in real compilers? | JVM, WASM | LLVM, GCC | Many IRs | Rare | Rare |
| Handles jumps? | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Best for | Stack machines | General IR | Optimization | Compact IR | Optimization |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
