## 4.1 Translation Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Expression: a + b * c] --> B[Lexical Analysis tokens]
    B --> C[Parser builds AST]
    C --> D[Syntax Directed Translation SDT]
    D --> E[Postfix Generation]
    D --> F[Three Address Code TAC]
    D --> G[Quadruples]
    D --> H[Triples]
    E --> I[Stack Machine Execution]
    F --> J[Optimization Phase]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    J --> K[Target Code Generation]
    K --> L[MIPS x86 ARM bytecode]
```

## 4.2 TAC Generation Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Source Code] --> LEX[Lexer]
    LEX --> PAR[Parser]
    PAR --> AST[Abstract Syntax Tree]
    AST --> SDT[Syntax Directed Translator]
    SDT --> NEWT[newTemp Function]
    SDT --> GEN[gen Function]
    NEWT --> TAC[Three Address Code Stream]
    GEN --> TAC
    TAC --> OPT[Optimizer]
    OPT --> TGT[Target Code]
```

## 4.3 Backpatching State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Parse_Expression
    Parse_Expression --> Emit_True_Jump
    Emit_True_Jump --> Emit_False_Jump
    Emit_False_Jump --> Check_Operator
    Check_Operator --> AND_Action: operator is AND
    Check_Operator --> OR_Action: operator is OR
    AND_Action --> Backpatch_True_List
    Backpatch_True_List --> Merge_False_Lists
    OR_Action --> Backpatch_False_List
    Backpatch_False_List --> Merge_True_Lists
    Merge_False_Lists --> Parse_Expression
    Merge_True_Lists --> Parse_Expression
    Parse_Expression --> [*]: end of expression
```

## 4.4 Quadruple / Triple / Indirect Triple Block Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q[Quadruple Table]
    Q1[op = +] --> Q2[arg1 = a]
    Q2 --> Q3[arg2 = b]
    Q3 --> Q4[result = t1]
    end
    subgraph T[Triple Table]
    T1[op = +] --> T2[arg1 = a]
    T2 --> T3[arg2 = b]
    end
    subgraph IT[Indirect Triple]
    P1[pointer 0] --> PT1[triple 0]
    P2[pointer 1] --> PT2[triple 1]
    end
    Q --> T
    T --> IT
```

## 4.5 Translation Scheme Recursion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start E]) --> E1[Match E1]
    E1 --> OpPlus{Operator}
    OpPlus -->|+| T1[Match T]
    OpPlus -->|-x| End1([Return E.addr])
    T1 --> Tcall[newTemp]
    Tcall --> Gen1[gen E.addr = E1.addr op T.addr]
    Gen1 --> Return1([Return new addr])
    T --> Factor[Match F]
    Factor --> FactorCall[newTemp if needed]
    FactorCall --> ReturnF([Return F.addr])
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Three-Address Code (TAC). List any four common TAC instruction forms with examples.**

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **TAC Definition:** Three-Address Code is an intermediate representation in which each instruction contains at most three addresses — one destination and at most two source operands.

**Four common TAC forms:**
1. **Binary op:** $t_1 = a + b$
2. **Copy:** $x = y$
3. **Unconditional jump:** $\text{goto}\; L$
4. **Conditional jump:** $\text{if}\; x < y \;\text{goto}\; L$

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a quadruple? How does it differ from a triple representation of three-address code?**

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> A **quadruple** is a 4-field record `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` where `result` is a fresh temporary name.

| Quadruples | Triples |
|---|---|
| 4 fields: `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | 3 fields: `(op, arg1, arg2)` |
| Uses temporary names like `t1`, `t2` | Uses instruction numbers like `(0)`, `(1)` |
| Easier to move code during optimization | Harder — moving an instruction requires renumbering |
| Larger memory footprint | More compact |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Translate the following arithmetic expression into (i) Postfix notation, (ii) Three-Address Code, (iii) Quadruples, and (iv) Triples:**
$$x = (a + b) * (c - d) + e / f$$

#### Part (a) — Postfix and TAC (7 Marks)

**Postfix Notation Conversion:**

Following operator precedence `*, /, +, -` and left-to-right associativity:

$$
x\;a\;b\;+\;c\;d\;-\;*\;e\;f\;/\;=
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

| Step | Stack / Output | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output: `x` | Emit variable x |
| 2 | Output: `a b` | Emit a then b |
| 3 | Output: `a b +` | Emit `+` after operands |
| 4 | Output: `a b + c d` | Emit c then d |
| 5 | Output: `a b + c d -` | Emit `-` |
| 6 | Output: `a b + c d - *` | Emit `*` |
| 7 | Output: `a b + c d - * e f` | Emit e then f |
| 8 | Output: `a b + c d - * e f /` | Emit `/` |
| 9 | Output: `a b + c d - * e f / =` | Emit `=` (assignment) |

**Three-Address Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + b \\
t_2 &= c - d \\
t_3 &= t_1 * t_2 \\
t_4 &= e / f \\
t_5 &= t_3 + t_4 \\
x &= t_5
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Quadruples and Triples (7 Marks)

**Quadruples Table:**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `+` | a | b | t1 |
| (1) | `-` | c | d | t2 |
| (2) | `*` | t1 | t2 | t3 |
| (3) | `/` | e | f | t4 |
| (4) | `+` | t3 | t4 | t5 |
| (5) | `=` | t5 |  | x |

**Triples Table:**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `+` | a | b |
| (1) | `-` | c | d |
| (2) | `*` | (0) | (1) |
| (3) | `/` | e | f |
| (4) | `+` | (2) | (3) |
| (5) | `=` | x | (4) |

**Logic explanation:** Quadruples use named temporaries (`t1`–`t5`) which makes the IR **self-documenting**; triples reuse instruction numbers in `( )` which makes the IR **compact** but **less readable**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Generate three-address code using backpatching for the following statement:**
$$\text{if}\;(a < b \;\text{AND}\; c < d)\;\text{OR}\;(e = f)\;\text{then}\; x = 1 \;\text{else}\; x = 2$$

#### Part (a) — Grammar & Marker Setup (7 Marks)

**Augmented Grammar with backpatching actions:**

| # | Production | Semantic Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S \rightarrow \text{if}\; (E)\; M\; S_1\; N\;\text{else}\; S_2$ | `backpatch(E.true, M.quad); backpatch(E.false, N.quad); S.next = merge(S1.next, S2.next)` |
| 2 | $E \rightarrow E_1 \;\text{AND}\; M\; E_2$ | `backpatch(E1.true, M.quad); E.true = E2.true; E.false = merge(E1.false, E2.false)` |
| 3 | $E \rightarrow E_1 \;\text{OR}\; M\; E_2$ | `backpatch(E1.false, M.quad); E.true = merge(E1.true, E2.true); E.false = E2.false` |
| 4 | $E \rightarrow \text{id}_1\;\text{relop}\;\text{id}_2$ | `E.true = makelist(next_quad); E.false = makelist(next_quad+1); gen('if id1 relop id2 goto _'); gen('goto _')` |
| 5 | $M \rightarrow \epsilon$ | `M.quad = next_quad` |
| 6 | $N \rightarrow \epsilon$ | `N.next = makelist(next_quad); gen('goto _')` |

**Trace starting at `next_quad = 100`:**

| Token Read | Action | next_quad | Lists |
|---|---|---|---|
| `a < b` | Emit 100 (true-jump), 101 (false-jump) | 102 | $E_1.\text{true}=\{100\}$, $E_1.\text{false}=\{101\}$ |
| `AND M` | $M.\text{quad} = 102$ | 102 | — |
| `c < d` | Emit 102, 103 | 104 | $E_2.\text{true}=\{102\}$, $E_2.\text{false}=\{103\}$ |
| Apply AND rule | `backpatch({100}, 102)`; merge false lists | 104 | $E_A.\text{true}=\{102\}$, $E_A.\text{false}=\{101, 103\}$ |
| `OR M` | $M.\text{quad} = 104$ | 104 | — |
| `e = f` | Emit 104, 105 | 106 | $E_3.\text{true}=\{104\}$, $E_3.\text{false}=\{105\}$ |
| Apply OR rule | `backpatch({101,103}, 104)`; merge true lists | 106 | $E.\text{true}=\{102, 104\}$, $E.\text{false}=\{105\}$ |

#### Part (b) — Final Code Generation (7 Marks)

**Continuing the trace for THEN/ELSE:**

| Step | Action | next_quad |
|---|---|---|
| `then x = 1` | Emit 106: `t1 = 1`; Emit 107: `x = t1` | 108 |
| `N` | $N.\text{next} = \{108\}$; emit 108: `goto _` | 109 |
| `else x = 2` | Emit 109: `t2 = 2`; Emit 110: `x = t2` | 111 |
| Apply S rule | `backpatch(E.true, 106) = backpatch({102,104}, 106)`; `backpatch(E.false, 109) = backpatch({105}, 109)`; `backpatch(N.next, 111) = backpatch({108}, 111)` | 111 |

**Final Backpatched Three-Address Code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
100&:\; \text{if}\; a < b \;\text{goto}\; 102 \quad\quad \text{(was: goto \_; backpatched by AND rule)} \\
101&:\; \text{goto}\; 104 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\text{(was: goto \_; backpatched by OR rule)} \\
102&:\; \text{if}\; c < d \;\text{goto}\; 106 \quad\quad\text{(backpatched to THEN block)} \\
103&:\; \text{goto}\; 109 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\text{(backpatched to ELSE block)} \\
104&:\; \text{if}\; e = f \;\text{goto}\; 106 \quad\quad\text{(backpatched to THEN block)} \\
105&:\; \text{goto}\; 109 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\text{(backpatched to ELSE block)} \\
106&:\; t_1 = 1 \\
107&:\; x = t_1 \\
108&:\; \text{goto}\; 111 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\text{(backpatched to next label)} \\
109&:\; t_2 = 2 \\
110&:\; x = t_2 \\
111&:\;
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic explanation:** 
- Lines 100 and 102 were initially `goto _`. The AND rule filled 100's target with 102, the address of $E_2$.
- Lines 101 and 103 were filled by the OR rule, with the address of $E_3$ (104).
- The S-rule's `backpatch(E.true, 106)` and `backpatch(E.false, 109)` redirect the true/false jumps of the **entire** boolean expression to the THEN/ELSE blocks.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Translating Expressions:**
> 1. **Operator Precedence Mistake** — Converting $a + b * c$ as `a b + c *` instead of `a b c * +`. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting Fresh Temporaries** — Reusing $t_1$ for two different subexpressions corrupts semantics. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 3. **Missing Assignment Instruction** — Ending TAC without `$x = t_n$` for the final value. (Lose 1 mark)
> 4. **Backpatch Order Confusion** — Forgetting to `backpatch` the false_list of an OR before merging true_list. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 5. **Quadruple vs Triple Mixing** — Writing `(op, arg1, arg2)` in quadruple table (missing `result` column). (Lose 1 mark)
> 6. **Label Numbering** — Using the same label twice or forgetting to update `next_quad` after `M → ε`. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 7. **Array Index Arithmetic** — Skipping the multiplication by `width` when computing addresses. (Lose 2 marks)

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Translating Expressions**

- ✅ **Three-Address Code (TAC)** is the canonical IR for expression translation — at most **3 addresses** per instruction.
- ✅ **Postfix notation** is stack-friendly; **operator comes after operands**.
- ✅ **Quadruples** = `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`; **Triples** = `(op, arg1, arg2)` using instr. #s; **Indirect triples** = pointer list + triples.
- ✅ **newTemp()** generates a fresh temporary `t1, t2, t3, …` for every binary operation.
- ✅ **Boolean expressions** have two forms: **Numerical** (0/1) and **Control-flow** (short-circuit jumps).
- ✅ **Short-circuit evaluation** skips RHS of `&&` if LHS is FALSE, skips RHS of `||` if LHS is TRUE.
- ✅ **Backpatching** uses `true_list`, `false_list`, `makelist`, `merge`, and `backpatch` to fill jump targets in **one pass**.
- ✅ **Array addressing** = `base + index * width`; requires explicit multiplication in TAC.
- ✅ **Marker non-terminals (M, N)** are inserted in the grammar to capture `next_quad` between actions.
- ✅ Real-world IRs based on TAC: **LLVM IR, GNU RTL, Java Bytecode (stack form), WebAssembly**.
- ✅ **Translation Scheme** = CFG + semantic actions embedded in productions.
- ✅ Always emit a **final assignment** `$x = t_n$` to bind the result to the LHS variable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Translating Control-Flow Statements** is the intermediate-code-generation phase in which the compiler converts high-level programming constructs that alter the normal sequential flow of execution (such as selection statements like `if-then-else`, iteration statements like `while`, `do-while`, and `for`, and multi-way branching statements like `switch-case`) into an equivalent sequence of **three-address code (TAC)** instructions using conditional and unconditional jumps, labels, and explicit branching instructions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Focus:** Translation of statements such as assignment statements, mixed-mode arithmetic, Boolean expressions, and control-flow constructs (selection and iteration) into **three-address code** is a high-weightage topic. Backpatching is a *frequently-asked* technique for handling forward jumps.

### The Two Core Translation Strategies

| Strategy | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Strategy 1: One-Pass Code Generation (Using Backpatching)** | Uses lists of pending jumps (`truelist`, `falselist`, `nextlist`) whose target labels are filled in after the complete construct is parsed. |
| **Strategy 2: Short-Circuit Boolean Evaluation** | A `B ∧ C` is implemented as `if B then C else false`; a `B ∨ C` is implemented as `if B then true else C`. This naturally maps to jump code. |

## Conceptual Analogy — The Train Route Controller

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check:** Think of a compiler as a **train route controller**. A control-flow statement is like a *railway junction* on the track.
> - A **straight track** is a sequential assignment.
> - A **switch (points/switches)** is an `if` or `switch-case` — the controller checks the *condition signal* (Boolean expression) and flips the points to redirect the train to either the "true platform" (then-block) or the "false siding" (else-block).
> - A **loop track** is a `while` or `for` — the controller checks the signal at the entry; if the signal is green, the train enters the loop, completes a circuit, and checks again. If red, it continues forward.
> - **Backpatching** is like leaving a *dangling railway wire* at a junction, with a note saying "we'll connect this to platform B once we know where platform B is built."

The compiler, like the controller, must generate the equivalent of *jump instructions* on the destination track so that at runtime the program counter (the train) is redirected correctly.

## The Three Core Components of a Control-Flow Translation

> [!NOTE]
> **The Translation Triangle:** Any control-flow translation involves:
> 1. **Condition Translation** — producing jumps based on Boolean expressions.
> 2. **Block Translation** — converting statement bodies into TAC.
> 3. **Flow Linking** — connecting the start of a successor block to the exit of a predecessor block using labels and jumps.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Translation of `if S then S1`

**Syntax-Directed Translation Scheme (with Backpatching):**

The `if` statement has the following synthetic attributes that the parser manages:

| Attribute | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| `E.truelist` | List of jump instructions that should jump to the "then" label when condition `E` is true. |
| `E.falselist` | List of jump instructions that should jump to the next statement when `E` is false. |
| `S1.nextlist` | List of jump instructions at the end of `S1` that need their targets filled in. |

**Production Rules Used (Aho/Sethi/Ullman style):**

```
S → if E then S1
S → if E then S1 else S2
S → while E do S1
```

**Semantic Actions for `S → if E then S1` (without else):**

1. Translate Boolean expression `E` to obtain `E.truelist` and `E.falselist`.
2. `Backpatch(E.truelist, S1.beginlabel)` — fill in target of true-jumps with the label of `S1`.
3. `S.nextlist = Merge(E.falselist, S1.nextlist)` — propagate forward-jumps of false-exit and end-of-block.

## 2.2 Translation of `if-then-else`

**Semantic Actions for `S → if E then S1 else S2`:**

1. Translate `E` → get `E.truelist`, `E.falselist`.
2. `Backpatch(E.truelist, S1.beginlabel)`.
3. `Backpatch(E.falselist, S2.beginlabel)`.
4. Generate `goto S.next` at the end of `S1` and put it in a new list (call it `S1.afterlist`).
5. `S.nextlist = Merge(S1.afterlist, Merge(S2.nextlist, ???))` — usually just merges the after-list of S1 with the nextlist of S2.

## 2.3 Translation of `while E do S1`

```
S → while E do S1
```

**Semantic Actions:**

1. `S.begin = newlabel()` — label at the start of `E`.
2. Generate label `S1.begin = newlabel()` — label of the loop body.
3. Translate `E` → get `E.truelist`, `E.falselist`.
4. `Backpatch(E.truelist, S1.begin)` — true jumps go to loop body.
5. `Backpatch(S1.nextlist, S.begin)` — at the end of body, jump back to test.
6. `S.nextlist = E.falselist` — false exits the loop.

## 2.4 Translation of `do-while`

```
S → do S1 while E
```

**Semantic Actions:**

1. `S1.begin = newlabel()`.
2. Translate `S1` → get `S1.nextlist`.
3. `S.begin = newlabel()` (placed before `E`).
4. Translate `E` → get `E.truelist`, `E.falselist`.
5. `Backpatch(S1.nextlist, S.begin)`.
6. `Backpatch(E.truelist, S.begin)`.
7. `S.nextlist = E.falselist`.

## 2.5 Translation of `for` Loops

The `for` loop is canonically rewritten into an equivalent `while` loop using a temporary variable:

```
for (init; cond; step) body
   ≡  { init; while (cond) { body; step; } }
```

The translation is then handled by the `while` translation rule, plus handling of the `init` (assignment before loop) and `step` (assignment at end of each iteration).

## 2.6 Boolean Expression Translation — Short-Circuit Form

For Boolean expressions involving `&&` (AND), `||` (OR), and `!` (NOT), short-circuit semantics apply:

| Source Construct | Translated As |
| :--- | :--- |
| `E1 && E2` | `if E1 then E2 else false` |
| `E1 \vert\vert E2` | `if E1 then true else E2` |
| `!E` | `if E then false else true` |
| `E1 relop E2` | `if (E1 relop E2) then goto true else goto false` |

## 2.7 Backpatching Mechanism — The Definitive Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Backpatching is the **most repeated 14-mark question** in Module 3. Memorize these operations.

| Function | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| `makelist(i)` | Create a new list containing only instruction `i`; return a pointer to it. |
| `merge(p1, p2)` | Concatenate the lists pointed to by `p1` and `p2`; return pointer to the concatenated list. |
| `backpatch(p, i)` | Set the target of each instruction on the list pointed to by `p` to label `i`. |

**Three-Address Code Forms Used:**

| TAC Form | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `100: if a < b goto _` | Conditional jump with **deferred target** (to be backpatched). |
| `101: goto L` | Unconditional jump (e.g., jumping out of a block). |
| `102: L:` | A label declaration. |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of Control-Flow Translation |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Optimizing Compilers** (LLVM, GCC) | The CFG (Control-Flow Graph) built from TAC drives dead-code elimination, loop-invariant code motion, and inlining. |
| **Static Analyzers** | Tools like SonarQube, Coverity use the CFG to detect null-pointer dereferences, infinite loops, and unreachable code. |
| **JIT Compilation** (Java HotSpot, V8) | Just-in-time compilers re-translate hot byte-code into machine code using the same jump-based intermediate form. |
| **Decompilers and Reverse Engineering** | Binary analysis tools reconstruct high-level control flow from machine-level jump instructions. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Translating `if (a < b) then x = y + z`

**Source Code:**
```
if (a < b) then x = y + z
```

### Step 1 — Identify the Construct and Productions

The construct is `S → if E then S1`.

We need to translate the Boolean expression `E = (a < b)` and the assignment `S1 = (x = y + z)`.

### Step 2 — Translate the Boolean Expression `E`

Using the standard Boolean translation scheme:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Emit the comparison as TAC:} \quad & \text{if } a < b \text{ goto } \text{TRUELABEL} \\
& \text{goto FALSELABEL}
\end{aligned}
$$

In actual TAC with line numbers and deferred targets (backpatching form):

| Line | TAC Instruction | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 100 | `if a < b goto _` | This is the true-jump; target to be backpatched. |
| 101 | `goto _` | This is the false-jump; target to be backpatched. |

So at this stage:
- `E.truelist = makelist(100)` → list pointing to instruction `100`.
- `E.falselist = makelist(101)` → list pointing to instruction `101`.

### Step 3 — Generate the Label for the `then` Block

$$
\text{S1.begin} = \text{newlabel}() = 102
$$

### Step 4 — Translate the `S1` Body `x = y + z`

The body is an assignment. We use a temporary `t1` for the RHS:

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 102 | `t1 = y + z` |
| 103 | `x = t1` |

The `nextlist` of `S1` (fall-through jumps at the end of `S1`):
- Since `S1` is just two sequential assignments, there are **no fall-through jumps** at the end.
- Hence, `S1.nextlist = NULL` (empty list).

### Step 5 — Apply Backpatching for the True-Jumps

$$
\text{Backpatch}(E.\text{truelist},\ 102)
$$

This means: replace the `_` in instruction `100` with label `102`.

**After backpatching instruction 100 becomes:** `if a < b goto 102`

### Step 6 — Compute the `S.nextlist`

$$
S.\text{nextlist} = \text{Merge}(E.\text{falselist},\ S1.\text{nextlist})
$$

Since `S1.nextlist` is empty:

$$
S.\text{nextlist} = E.\text{falselist} = \{101\}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Three-Address Code

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if a < b goto 102` |
| 101 | `goto _`  *(target to be filled later — this becomes the nextlist)* |
| 102 | `t1 = y + z` |
| 103 | `x = t1` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When `a < b` is **true**, control jumps to line 102 and executes the body. When `a < b` is **false**, control falls through to line 101, which is a *forward jump* (filled by the surrounding context — usually to the statement following the `if`).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Translating `if (a < b) then x = y + z else x = y - z`

### Step 1 — Productions and Boolean Translation

Production: `S → if E then S1 else S2`, where:
- `E = (a < b)`
- `S1 = (x = y + z)`
- `S2 = (x = y - z)`

After Boolean translation we have:

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if a < b goto _` |
| 101 | `goto _` |

with `E.truelist = {100}`, `E.falselist = {101}`.

### Step 2 — Generate Labels and Translate `S1`

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 102 | `t1 = y + z` |
| 103 | `x = t1` |
| 104 | `goto _` ← *unconditional jump after `S1`, target TBD* |

Label `102` is `S1.begin`.

After translating `S1`, generate an unconditional jump to bypass `S2`. Place this jump on a list called `S1.afterlist`:

$$
S1.\text{afterlist} = \text{makelist}(104) = \{104\}
$$

### Step 3 — Generate Label for `S2` and Translate It

Label `S2.begin = newlabel() = 105`.

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 105 | `t2 = y - z` |
| 106 | `x = t2` |

`S2.nextlist = NULL` (no fall-through jumps at end).

### Step 4 — Apply Backpatching

**True-jumps point to S1.begin:**
$$
\text{Backpatch}(E.\text{truelist},\ 102) \implies \text{Instruction 100 becomes: } \texttt{if a < b goto 102}
$$

**False-jumps point to S2.begin:**
$$
\text{Backpatch}(E.\text{falselist},\ 105) \implies \text{Instruction 101 becomes: } \texttt{goto 105}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Final `S.nextlist`

$$
\begin{aligned}
S.\text{nextlist} &= \text{Merge}(S1.\text{afterlist},\ S2.\text{nextlist}) \\
&= \text{Merge}(\{104\},\ \emptyset) \\
&= \{104\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Three-Address Code

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if a < b goto 102` |
| 101 | `goto 105` |
| 102 | `t1 = y + z` |
| 103 | `x = t1` |
| 104 | `goto _` *(filled by surrounding context)* |
| 105 | `t2 = y - z` |
| 106 | `x = t2` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analysis:** Line 104's target (`_`) is intentionally left unresolved — this is the *forward-jump exit* from the entire `if-else`. The enclosing context (e.g., another nested construct) will backpatch it.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Translating `while (a < b) do x = y + z`

### Step 1 — Production and Initial Labels

Production: `S → while E do S1`.

Generate the begin label of the test expression `E`:

$$
S.\text{begin} = \text{newlabel}() = 100
$$

### Step 2 — Translate Boolean Expression `E`

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if a < b goto _` |
| 101 | `goto _` |

So `E.truelist = {100}`, `E.falselist = {101}`.

### Step 3 — Generate Body Label and Translate `S1`

Body begin label: `S1.begin = newlabel() = 102`.

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 102 | `t1 = y + z` |
| 103 | `x = t1` |

`S1.nextlist = NULL` (sequential assignments).

### Step 4 — Backpatching for True-Jumps (Enter Loop Body)

$$
\text{Backpatch}(E.\text{truelist},\ 102)
$$

Instruction 100 becomes: `if a < b goto 102`.

### Step 5 — Backpatching End-of-Body Jumps (Loop Back)

$$
\text{Backpatch}(S1.\text{nextlist},\ S.\text{begin}) = \text{Backpatch}(\{ \},\ 100)
$$

Since `S1.nextlist` is empty, this is a no-op. However, after `S1`, we **must insert** a jump back to the test:

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 104 | `goto 100` |

This unconditional jump is generated by the translation scheme *after* `S1` is processed.

### Step 6 — Compute `S.nextlist`

$$
S.\text{nextlist} = E.\text{falselist} = \{101\}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Three-Address Code

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if a < b goto 102` |
| 101 | `goto _` *(filled by enclosing context — exit of loop)* |
| 102 | `t1 = y + z` |
| 103 | `x = t1` |
| 104 | `goto 100` |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Constructs to Jump Patterns

| Source Construct | Jump Pattern in TAC | Direction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `if-then` | One conditional jump + one forward unconditional jump | Forward only |
| `if-then-else` | Two conditional jumps (true/false) + one forward unconditional | Forward only |
| `while-do` | One conditional (forward) + one backward unconditional | Forward + Backward |
| `do-while` | One conditional (backward) | Backward only |
| `for` | Backward unconditional + conditional | Forward + Backward |
| `&&` (AND) | True-jump chains to next sub-expression | Forward chaining |
| `\|\|` (OR) | False-jump chains to next sub-expression | Forward chaining |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Boolean Expression `a < b OR c < d AND e < f`

### Operator Precedence (from low to high): `OR < AND < NOT < Relop`

### Parse Tree (Precedence-Respecting)

$$
E \rightarrow E_1 \text{ OR } T
$$

$$
T \rightarrow T_1 \text{ AND } F
$$

So the expression parses as: `a < b OR (c < d AND e < f)`.

### Short-Circuit Translation Steps

**Step 1: Translate `a < b`:**

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if a < b goto _` |
| 101 | `goto _` |

`E1.truelist = {100}`, `E1.falselist = {101}`.

**Step 2: Translate `c < d`:**

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 102 | `if c < d goto _` |
| 103 | `goto _` |

`T1.truelist = {102}`, `T1.falselist = {103}`.

**Step 3: Translate `e < f`:**

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 104 | `if e < f goto _` |
| 105 | `goto _` |

`F.truelist = {104}`, `F.falselist = {105}`.

**Step 4: Backpatch the AND (`T1 AND F`):**

Rule: For `T1 AND F`, true-jumps of `T1` go to start of `F`'s code.

$$
\text{Backpatch}(T1.\text{truelist},\ 104)
$$

Instruction 102 becomes: `if c < d goto 104`.

The result's truelist = `F.truelist = {104}` and falselist = `Merge(T1.falselist, F.falselist) = {103, 105}`.

**Step 5: Backpatch the OR (`E1 OR T`):**

Rule: For `E1 OR T`, false-jumps of `E1` go to start of `T`'s code.

The combined expression `T` (which is `(c<d AND e<f)`) starts at line 102. So:

$$
\text{Backpatch}(E1.\text{falselist},\ 102)
$$

Instruction 101 becomes: `goto 102`.

### Final Boolean TAC

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if a < b goto _TRUE_` |
| 101 | `goto 102` |
| 102 | `if c < d goto 104` |
| 103 | `goto _FALSE_` |
| 104 | `if e < f goto _TRUE_` |
| 105 | `goto _FALSE_` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the logic:**
> - If `a < b` is true → jump to TRUE (short-circuit).
> - Else go to 102 → test `c < d`.
> - If `c < d` true → test `e < f`.
> - If both AND sub-tests true → TRUE; otherwise FALSE.
> This is **exactly** the short-circuit OR-AND semantics of the source expression.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control-Flow Translation Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Program Tokens] --> B[Parser and Syntax Directed Translation]
    B --> C{Control Statement Type?}
    C -->|Selection| D[Selection Translation Module]
    C -->|Iteration| E[Iteration Translation Module]
    C -->|Boolean Expr| F[Boolean Expression Module]
    D --> G[Generate True and False Lists]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Apply Backpatching Operations]
    H --> I[merge Lists and Fill Targets]
    I --> J[Output Three Address Code with Labels]
    J --> K[Next Compilation Phase Optimization]
```

## 4.2 While-Do Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Parser sees while keyword]) --> A1[Allocate S.begin label]
    A1 --> A2[Translate Boolean Expression E]
    A2 --> A3[Capture E.truelist and E.falselist]
    A3 --> A4[Allocate S1.begin label]
    A4 --> A5[Translate Loop Body S1]
    A5 --> A6[Capture S1.nextlist]
    A6 --> B1[Backpatch E.truelist with S1.begin]
    B1 --> B2[Backpatch S1.nextlist with S.begin]
    B2 --> B3[Emit goto S.begin for loop back]
    B3 --> B4[S.nextlist becomes E.falselist]
    B4 --> End([Return to Caller])
```

## 4.3 If-Then-Else Translation Flow with Backpatching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Parser sees if] --> Q1[Translate E]
    Q1 --> Q2[Allocate S1.begin label]
    Q2 --> Q3[Translate S1]
    Q3 --> Q4[Emit goto placeholder and add to S1.afterlist]
    Q4 --> Q5[Allocate S2.begin label]
    Q5 --> Q6[Translate S2]
    Q6 --> R1[Backpatch E.truelist with S1.begin]
    R1 --> R2[Backpatch E.falselist with S2.begin]
    R2 --> R3[Merge S1.afterlist with S2.nextlist into S.nextlist]
    R3 --> S1([Return to Caller])
```

## 4.4 Boolean Expression Short-Circuit OR-AND Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Translate E1] --> X2{E1 true?}
    X2 -->|Yes| Y1[Result is TRUE]
    X2 -->|No| X3[Translate T second operand]
    X3 --> X4{T true?}
    X4 -->|Yes| Y1
    X4 -->|No| Y2[Result is FALSE]
```

## 4.5 Jump List Data Structure (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph truelist ["E.truelist"]
        dirA100["Instruction 100 if a lt b goto _"]
    end
    subgraph falselist ["E.falselist"]
        dirA101["Instruction 101 goto _"]
    end
    subgraph nextlist ["S.nextlist"]
        dirA104["Instruction 104 goto _"]
    end
    B100[Backpatch] -->|target = 102| dirA100
    B101[Backpatch] -->|target = 105| dirA101
    B104[Backpatch] -->|target = next enclosing label| dirA104
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the backpatching technique used in intermediate code generation.**

**Model Answer:**

Backpatching is a technique used during syntax-directed translation to handle forward jumps whose target labels are not yet known at the time the jump instruction is emitted. Instead of emitting the actual jump target, the compiler:
1. Generates the jump with a placeholder target (e.g., `goto _`).
2. Maintains **lists** (using `makelist` and `merge`) of such pending jumps, called `truelist`, `falselist`, and `nextlist`.
3. Once the target label becomes known, the `backpatch(p, i)` operation traverses the list `p` and fills the placeholder of each instruction with the label `i`.

**Key Functions:** `makelist(i)`, `merge(p1, p2)`, `backpatch(p, i)`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse `truelist` (jumps to take when condition is true) with `nextlist` (jumps after the statement). These are *different* lists. The `nextlist` is propagated upward, while `truelist`/`falselist` are used within the same construct.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between short-circuit and non-short-circuit evaluation of Boolean expressions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Short-Circuit Evaluation | Non-Short-Circuit Evaluation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Semantics** | RHS evaluated only if LHS does not already determine the result. | Both operands are always evaluated. |
| **Code Generation** | Translates directly into jump code using `if` and `goto`. | Requires materializing `true`/`false` into temporaries. |
| **Side Effects** | Side effects of RHS are skipped if LHS determines the result. | Side effects always occur. |
| **Implementation** | Preferred in C, Java, C++ (most cases). | Used in languages like VB's `AndAlso`/`OrElse` distinctions. |
| **Example** | `false && f()` → `f()` not called. | `false AND f()` → `f()` is called. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — `(a) + (b)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the translation scheme for `if E then S1 else S2` using backpatching. Describe the role of `truelist`, `falselist`, `makelist`, `merge`, and `backpatch`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Translate the following program fragment into three-address code using short-circuit Boolean evaluation and backpatching: **(7 Marks)**

```
if (a > b) && (c < d) then
    x = y + z
else
    p = q - r
```

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Theory

The production rule is:
```
S → if E then S1 else S2
```

**Semantic Actions (executed during parsing):**

1. **Translate `E`** — this emits conditional jumps and returns:
   - `E.truelist` — list of jumps to take when `E` is true.
   - `E.falselist` — list of jumps to take when `E` is false.

2. **Allocate label** `S1.begin = newlabel()` — this becomes the target of true-jumps.

3. **Translate `S1`** — produces `S1.nextlist` (forward jumps after `S1`) and emits an unconditional jump (`goto _`) at the end of `S1`, placed in `S1.afterlist = makelist(i)`.

4. **Allocate label** `S2.begin = newlabel()` — target of false-jumps.

5. **Translate `S2`** — produces `S2.nextlist`.

6. **Backpatch true-jumps:**
$$
\text{Backpatch}(E.\text{truelist},\ S1.\text{begin})
$$

7. **Backpatch false-jumps:**
$$
\text{Backpatch}(E.\text{falselist},\ S2.\text{begin})
$$

8. **Compute final exit list:**
$$
S.\text{nextlist} = \text{Merge}(S1.\text{afterlist},\ S2.\text{nextlist})
$$

#### Part (b) — Translation

**Step 1: Translate the AND sub-expression `(a > b) && (c < d)`**

This is `E1 AND E2`. According to short-circuit rules:

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if a > b goto _` |
| 101 | `goto _` |

`E1.truelist = {100}`, `E1.falselist = {101}`.

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 102 | `if c < d goto _` |
| 103 | `goto _` |

`E2.truelist = {102}`, `E2.falselist = {103}`.

**Step 2: Backpatch AND — True-jumps of E1 go to E2's code:**

$$
\text{Backpatch}(E1.\text{truelist},\ 102)
$$

Instruction 100 becomes: `if a > b goto 102`.

**Combined `E.truelist = E2.truelist = {102}`, `E.falselist = Merge({101}, {103}) = {101, 103}`.**

**Step 3: Translate S1 = `x = y + z`**

Label `S1.begin = 104`:

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 104 | `t1 = y + z` |
| 105 | `x = t1` |
| 106 | `goto _` ← placed in `S1.afterlist = {106}` |

**Step 4: Translate S2 = `p = q - r`**

Label `S2.begin = 107`:

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 107 | `t2 = q - r` |
| 108 | `p = t2` |

`S2.nextlist = NULL`.

**Step 5: Backpatch false-jumps to S2.begin:**

$$
\text{Backpatch}(E.\text{falselist},\ 107)
$$

Instructions 101 and 103 both become `goto 107`.

**Step 6: Final TAC**

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if a > b goto 102` |
| 101 | `goto 107` |
| 102 | `if c < d goto 104` |
| 103 | `goto 107` |
| 104 | `t1 = y + z` |
| 105 | `x = t1` |
| 106 | `goto _` *(forward jump — to be backpatched by enclosing context)* |
| 107 | `t2 = q - r` |
| 108 | `p = t2` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes on This Question:**
> 1. Forgetting to **backpatch `S1.afterlist`** (the unconditional jump at line 106). Students often emit the jump but leave it unresolved, losing marks for the `nextlist` computation.
> 2. **Incorrect list merging order** for `E.falselist` of an AND expression — it must be `Merge(E1.falselist, E2.falselist)`, NOT `E2.falselist` alone.
> 3. Generating **separate jumps for both `E1.truelist` and `E2.truelist`** instead of recognizing that `E.truelist` of an AND is simply `E2.truelist` (since `E2` is only evaluated when `E1` is true).

---

### Question B — Alternative Choice `(a) + (b)` `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** With a clear semantic action sequence, explain how the `while E do S1` statement is translated into three-address code using backpatching. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Generate the three-address code with explicit label numbering and backpatching for: **(7 Marks)**

```
while (x < y) do
    if (a == b) then
        p = q + r
```

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Theory

Production: `S → while E do S1`

**Semantic Actions:**

1. **Allocate `S.begin`** — the label where the Boolean test `E` will be evaluated.

2. **Translate `E`** — produces `E.truelist` (jumps to loop body) and `E.falselist` (jumps that exit the loop).

3. **Allocate `S1.begin`** — label of the loop body.

4. **Translate `S1`** — produces `S1.nextlist` (forward jumps at the end of `S1`).

5. **Backpatch `E.truelist`** with `S1.begin` — true-jumps enter the body.

6. **Backpatch `S1.nextlist`** with `S.begin` — at the end of the body, jump back to re-test `E`.

7. **Emit `goto S.begin`** — unconditional loop-back.

8. **Compute:**
$$
S.\text{nextlist} = E.\text{falselist}
$$

#### Part (b) — Translation

**Step 1: Outer while — Allocate `S.begin = 100`**

**Step 2: Translate Boolean `x < y`:**

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if x < y goto _` |
| 101 | `goto _` |

`E.truelist = {100}`, `E.falselist = {101}`.

**Step 3: Inner `if` — Translate inner condition `a == b`:**

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 102 | `if a == b goto _` |
| 103 | `goto _` |

`E_inner.truelist = {102}`, `E_inner.falselist = {103}`.

**Step 4: Allocate label for inner body (then-block) = 104:**

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 104 | `t1 = q + r` |
| 105 | `p = t1` |

No inner `else` → `S_inner.nextlist = Merge(E_inner.falselist, body_nextlist) = {103}`.

**Step 5: Backpatch inner true-jumps:**

$$
\text{Backpatch}(E_\text{inner}.\text{truelist},\ 104)
$$

Instruction 102 → `if a == b goto 104`.

**Step 6: Emit loop-back jump at end of while-body:**

| Line | TAC |
| :---: | :--- |
| 106 | `goto 100` |

**Step 7: Backpatch outer `E.truelist`:**

$$
\text{Backpatch}(E.\text{truelist},\ S1.\text{begin}) = \text{Backpatch}(\{100\},\ 102)
$$

Instruction 100 → `if x < y goto 102`.

**Step 8: Final TAC**

| Line | TAC Instruction |
| :---: | :--- |
| 100 | `if x < y goto 102` |
| 101 | `goto _` *(loop exit — to be filled by outer context)* |
| 102 | `if a == b goto 104` |
| 103 | `goto 100` *(false-jump of inner if → loop back!)* |
| 104 | `t1 = q + r` |
| 105 | `p = t1` |
| 106 | `goto 100` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes on This Question:**
> 1. **Confusing which jumps loop back.** The `falselist` of the *inner* `if` (instruction 103) must jump back to `S.begin = 100`, NOT to line 106. This is because the false branch of the inner `if` is logically *inside* the outer `while`'s body — it still needs to loop.
> 2. **Forgetting the explicit `goto 100`** at line 106. After the body of `S1` executes successfully, control must loop back; this is **not** automatically generated by `backpatch`.
> 3. **Mixing up `nextlist` and `afterlist`.** In this nested example, the inner `if`'s `nextlist` becomes the propagator upward, but the *outer while's* `nextlist` is just `E_outer.falselist = {101}`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Control-Flow Translation:**

### A. Core Definitions
- **Backpatching** is a one-pass technique using jump lists (`truelist`, `falselist`, `nextlist`) to resolve forward jumps.
- **Short-circuit evaluation** of Boolean expressions maps directly to conditional jump code.
- **Three-address code (TAC)** is the intermediate form: each instruction has at most three operands and one operator.

### B. Three Core Backpatching Functions
- `makelist(i)` — create a singleton list containing instruction `i`.
- `merge(p1, p2)` — concatenate two lists.
- `backpatch(p, i)` — fill placeholder target of every instruction on list `p` with label `i`.

### C. Translation Pattern Memory Map
- `if E then S1` → `Backpatch(E.truelist, S1.begin)`; `S.nextlist = Merge(E.falselist, S1.nextlist)`.
- `if E then S1 else S2` → `Backpatch(E.truelist, S1.begin)`; `Backpatch(E.falselist, S2.begin)`; emit `goto _` at end of S1; `S.nextlist = Merge(S1.afterlist, S2.nextlist)`.
- `while E do S1` → `Backpatch(E.truelist, S1.begin)`; `Backpatch(S1.nextlist, S.begin)`; emit `goto S.begin`; `S.nextlist = E.falselist`.
- `do S1 while E` → `Backpatch(S1.nextlist, S.begin)`; `Backpatch(E.truelist, S.begin)`; `S.nextlist = E.falselist`.
- `for(init; cond; step) body` → rewrite as `{ init; while(cond) { body; step; } }` and apply the while rule.

### D. Boolean Operator Translation Rules
- `E1 && E2` → true-jumps of `E1` point to start of `E2`'s code; the combined result's truelist is `E2.truelist`; the combined falselist is the merge of `E1.falselist` and `E2.falselist`.
- `E1 || E2` → false-jumps of `E1` point to start of `E2`'s code; the combined truelist is the merge of `E1.truelist` and `E2.truelist`; the combined falselist is `E2.falselist`.
- `!E` → swap `truelist` and `falselist`.

### E. Most Frequently Asked Question Types
1. Translate a single `if-then-else` into TAC (3–7 marks).
2. Translate a `while` loop into TAC with explicit backpatching steps (7 marks).
3. Translate a Boolean expression with `&&` and `||` using short-circuit semantics (7–14 marks).
4. Explain `makelist`, `merge`, and `backpatch` with a worked example (3–7 marks).

### F. Common Examiner's Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- Do not materialize Boolean results into temporaries unless explicitly required.
- Always emit the unconditional `goto _` at the end of an `if-then` body to skip the `else`.
- Always emit the unconditional `goto S.begin` at the end of a `while` body to loop back.
- Do not confuse `nextlist` with `afterlist`; they are different lists with different lifetimes.
- Precedence of Boolean operators (low → high): `OR < AND < NOT < relop`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Shape - Arithmetic Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Code Shape - Arithmetic Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is "Code Shape"?

**Code Shape** in compiler construction refers to the formal structure and representation of a program's intermediate code (IR) — specifically, the geometric/topological shape that expressions and statements take once they have been parsed and are ready for optimization and code generation. Different intermediate representations (three-address code, static single assignment, tree-based IR, stack-based IR) have distinctly different "shapes."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBCRT604, Module 4):**
> *Code Shape* defines how the surface constructs of a programming language (expressions, statements, declarations) are mapped onto the intermediate representation used by the compiler. For arithmetic operators, code shape describes how binary and unary arithmetic operations are represented as IR trees, with explicit operator nodes holding operand subtrees.

In the canonical reference treatment (Appel, *Modern Compiler Implementation*), the IR is built as a hierarchy of **expression trees** composed using smart constructors `BINOP`, `CONST`, `MEM`, `TEMP`, etc. The **shape** of an IR node is determined by its root operator and the shapes of its sub-trees.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Post-Office Sorting Room

Imagine a post office where letters (values) arrive and must be processed:
- Every letter is either a **single, complete value** (a *constant* or a *named box*), OR
- A letter is a **box containing a specific instruction** plus other letters (e.g., *"ADD this letter and that letter"*).

When a clerk receives a parcel, they never try to "evaluate" it inside the sorting room — they only **classify its shape** (what kind of instruction box is this?) and then pass the unopened parcel to the next station. This is exactly what an IR tree does: it freezes an arithmetic expression into a structural tree where each node is an *instruction-class* and the leaves are *raw values*. The "shape" of `a + b * c` is a `+` node with two children: a `MEM a` leaf and a `*` node with two `CONST`/`MEM` leaves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:**
> Code Shape is *not* about execution — it is about **representation**. The same arithmetic expression can be shaped as a *tree* (Appel's IR), as a *stack* (JVM bytecode), or as a *three-address quadruple* (`t1 = b * c; t2 = a + t1`). The choice of shape directly affects what optimizations are easy to perform.

### 1.3 Arithmetic Operators in the IR

The standard set of arithmetic operators handled at the code-shape level are:

| Operator Class | IR Constructor | Operand Count | Associativity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addition | `BINOP(PLUS, e1, e2)` | 2 | Left |
| Subtraction | `BINOP(MINUS, e1, e2)` | 2 | Left |
| Multiplication | `BINOP(MUL, e1, e2)` | 2 | Left |
| Division | `BINOP(DIV, e1, e2)` | 2 | Left |
| Modulus | `BINOP(MOD, e1, e2)` | 2 | Left |
| Unary Minus | not BINOP — modelled via `BINOP(MINUS, 0, e)` | 1 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** There is **no separate unary-minus node** in standard tree-shaped IR. A negation `-x` is shaped as the binary subtraction `0 - x`. This is a classic 3-mark short-answer trap question in the KTU board exam.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Tier IR Data Model

The IR is split into two mutually recursive abstract data types:

1. **`Exp` (Expression)** — A *value-producing* tree. It can be a leaf (`CONST n`, `NAME n`, `TEMP t`, `MEM e`) or a binary operation `BINOP(op, e1, e2)`.
2. **`Stm` (Statement)** — An *action-performing* tree. It does not produce a value. Examples: `MOVE(TEMP t, e)`, `EXP(e)`, `SEQ(s1, s2)`, `LABEL n`, `JUMP`, `CJUMP`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the split?** A statement can *contain* expressions, but an expression **cannot** contain statements. This asymmetry enforces a clean shape and prevents ill-formed code (e.g., an expression that prints something).

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Translating an Arithmetic Expression

When the parser sees an expression like `a + b * c`, the translation proceeds in **post-order / recursive descent** in five structured steps:

1. **Lexical Reduction:** Recognize the tokens `a`, `+`, `b`, `*`, `c`.
2. **Precedence Binding:** Identify that `*` binds tighter than `+` (this is *parser responsibility* — but the *IR tree shape* faithfully preserves this binding).
3. **Operand-as-Leaf Creation:** Each identifier `a`, `b`, `c` becomes a `MEM(TEMP a)` leaf (because `a` is a memory location holding a value).
4. **Sub-expression Assembly:** The sub-expression `b * c` becomes `BINOP(MUL, MEM a, MEM b)`.
5. **Root Construction:** The full expression becomes the root: `BINOP(PLUS, MEM a, BINOP(MUL, MEM b, MEM c))`.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All entries below are **exam-ready facts** for the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Concept | Canonical Form / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expression Type | `Exp :: = CONST \mid NAME \mid TEMP \mid MEM(Exp) \mid BINOP(BinOp, Exp, Exp)` | Recursive on the right |
| 2 | Statement Type | `Stm :: = MOVE(Exp, Exp) \mid EXP(Exp) \mid SEQ(Stm, Stm) \mid LABEL \mid JUMP \mid CJUMP` | Recursive on the right |
| 3 | BinOp Enum | `PLUS, MINUS, MUL, DIV, AND, OR, LSHIFT, RSHIFT, ARSHIFT, XOR` | 10 binary operators |
| 4 | Unary Minus | `unary\_minus(e) := BINOP(MINUS, CONST(0), e)` | Trick question answer |
| 5 | Memory Load | Accessing variable `x` produces `MEM(TEMP x)` | `TEMP` is the address, `MEM` dereferences it |
| 6 | Array Element | `a[i]` produces `MEM(BINOP(PLUS, TEMP a, BINOP(MUL, CONST i, CONST 4)))` | Word size = 4 bytes assumed |
| 7 | Record Field | `p.x` produces `MEM(BINOP(PLUS, TEMP p, CONST offset))` | Offset known from type layout |
| 8 | Tree Height | Height of `BINOP(o, e1, e2)` = `1 + max(height(e1), height(e2))` | Used in optimization cost models |
| 9 | Canonical Statement | `a := b + c \rightarrow MOVE(MEM(TEMP a), BINOP(PLUS, MEM(TEMP b), MEM(TEMP c)))` | `MOVE(dst, src)` |
| 10 | Pure-Expression Stmt | `a + b;` (no effect) becomes `EXP(BINOP(PLUS, MEM a, MEM b))` | Kept for side-effect order |

> [!WARNING]
> **Memory-Side-Effect Rule:** Whenever a `MEM(e)` sub-expression is built where `e` contains a `CALL` or another `MEM`, the compiler must move the address into a fresh `TEMP` *first*. This is the **"Call-rule"** of code shape — a frequent 7-mark question in KTU.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The tree-shaped IR is the backbone of **LLVM**, **JVM**, and many production compilers because:
- It makes **data-flow analysis** (liveness, reaching definitions) trivial via tree traversal.
- The shape directly mirrors the **AST**, simplifying translation.
- Tree rewriting is the natural way to express **constant folding**, **strength reduction**, and **common subexpression elimination**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection:** LLVM's `Instruction` class with opcode `BinaryOperator::Add`, `Mul`, etc., is a direct descendant of this tree-shaped IR philosophy. Interview questions at Intel, NVIDIA, and Samsung R&D Bangalore frequently test exactly this mapping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Translating `a := b + c * d - e` to IR Tree

This is a **canonical worked example** — mastering it guarantees 7 marks in the KTU Part B.

**Source Expression:**
$$a \coloneqq b + c \cdot d - e$$

**Step 1 — Identify Precedence (Parser-Stage Output):**
By standard operator-precedence rules, the parse tree groups as:
$$a \coloneqq (b + (c \cdot d)) - e$$

**Step 2 — Translate the Multiplicative Sub-expression `(c · d)`:**
Create a `BINOP` node with `MUL` opcode and two `MEM` leaves:
$$N_1 = \mathrm{BINOP}(MUL,\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ c),\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ d))$$

**Step 3 — Translate the Additive Sub-expression `(b + N₁)`:**
$$N_2 = \mathrm{BINOP}(PLUS,\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ b),\ N_1)$$

**Step 4 — Translate the Outer Subtractive Expression `(N₂ − e)`:**
$$N_3 = \mathrm{BINOP}(MINUS,\ N_2,\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ e))$$

**Step 5 — Wrap in a `MOVE` Statement (the assignment shape):**
$$S = \mathrm{MOVE}(\mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ a),\ N_3)$$

**Step 6 — Final IR Tree (fully expanded):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \mathrm{MOVE}\Bigl(\mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ a),\ \mathrm{BINOP}\bigl(\mathrm{MINUS}, \\
&\qquad \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{PLUS},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ b),\ \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MUL},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ c),\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ d))), \\
&\qquad \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ e))\Bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Rule:** Count the operator nodes — there must be **3 BINOP** nodes (one per operator in the source: `*`, `+`, `-`). Each identifier contributes exactly one `MEM(TEMP name)` leaf. If the count mismatches, the shape is wrong.

### 3.2 Derivation: Translating Unary Minus `a := -b`

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-asked 3-mark short answer** for this topic in KTU past papers.

**Step 1 — Recognize:** `-b` is a unary operation, but the IR has no `NEG` node.

**Step 2 — Apply the Substitutability Rule:**
Unary minus is *semantically* equivalent to `(0 - b)`.

**Step 3 — Build the Sub-tree:**
$$N_1 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MINUS},\ \mathrm{CONST}(0),\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ b))$$

**Step 4 — Emit the Statement:**
$$S = \mathrm{MOVE}(\mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ a),\ N_1)$$

**Step 5 — Optimizer's Note:** A peephole pass detects `0 - x` and rewrites it to a single `NEG x` machine instruction — this is a **shape-preserving-to-shape-changing** optimization.

### 3.3 Derivation: Array Element `a := b[i]`

This is a **frequently-asked 7-mark problem** combining arithmetic and memory shape.

**Source:** `a := b[i]` (assuming `int` array, 4-byte words).

**Step 1 — Compute the offset:** `i * 4`.
$$\mathrm{offset} = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MUL},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ i),\ \mathrm{CONST}(4))$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective address:** `b + offset`.
$$\mathrm{addr} = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{PLUS},\ \mathrm{TEMP}(b),\ \mathrm{offset})$$

**Step 3 — Dereference to load the value:** `MEM(addr)`.
$$N_1 = \mathrm{MEM}(\mathrm{addr})$$

**Step 4 — Assign to `a`:**
$$S = \mathrm{MOVE}(\mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ a),\ N_1)$$

**Combined form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \mathrm{MOVE}\Bigl(\mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ a),\ \mathrm{MEM}\bigl(\mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{PLUS}, \\
&\qquad \mathrm{TEMP}(b),\ \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MUL},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ i),\ \mathrm{CONST}(4)))\bigr)\Bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Edge Cases in Arithmetic Code Shape

| Source Construct | Naive Shape | Correct Shape | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `-x` | `NEG x` (invalid) | `BINOP(MINUS, 0, x)` | No unary node exists |
| `a + b` where `a` is array | `BINOP(PLUS, a, b)` (value of `a`) | `BINOP(PLUS, TEMP a, b)` | `a` is the *base address*, not the value |
| `f() + g()` | `BINOP(PLUS, f(), g())` | `MOVE(t1, CALL f); MOVE(t2, CALL g); BINOP(PLUS, t1, t2)` | Call-rule forces side-effect isolation |
| `a + b` with `MEM` operands | `BINOP(PLUS, MEM a, MEM b)` | Same — but watch aliasing in optimization | Tree shape is identical |
| `2 * x` | `BINOP(MUL, CONST 2, MEM x)` | Same — but strength-reduction rewrites to `x + x` or `x << 1` | Shape is changed *after* construction |
| `i++` (side-effecting) | Expression | Not an `Exp` — emit as `MOVE(TEMP t, BINOP(PLUS, MEM i, CONST 1)); MOVE(MEM i, TEMP t)` | Side-effecting exprs must be statementized |

> [!WARNING]
> **Call-Rule Pitfall (KTU favourite 14-mark question):** Whenever a function call appears *inside* a `MEM` (or as a `BINOP` operand), it must be hoisted into a fresh `TEMP` before the memory access. Failing to do so is the single most common 4-mark deduction in valuation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Translation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code a+b*c] --> B[Lexer Tokens]
    B --> C[Parser AST]
    C --> D[IR Tree Builder]
    D --> E[Tree IR Exp and Stm]
    E --> F[Canonicalization]
    F --> G[Optimization Passes]
    G --> H[Code Generator]
    H --> I[Target Assembly]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.2 IR Tree for `a := b + c * d - e`

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[MOVE] --> DST[MEM TEMP a]
    S --> R1[BINOP MINUS]
    R1 --> R2[BINOP PLUS]
    R1 --> R5[MEM TEMP e]
    R2 --> R3[MEM TEMP b]
    R2 --> R4[BINOP MUL]
    R4 --> R6[MEM TEMP c]
    R4 --> R7[MEM TEMP d]

    style S fill:#f5b041,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R1 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style R2 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style R4 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style DST fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style R3 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style R5 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style R6 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style R7 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The orange `MOVE` is the statement root. The three **blue** internal nodes are the three `BINOP`s (one per source operator). The **green** leaves are the `MEM TEMP` nodes — one per source identifier. This visual ratio (3 BINOPs : 4 leaves) is the validation check.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Call-Rule Hoisting for `a[i] := f() + 1`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Source a i := f() + 1] --> P1[Parse to AST]
    P1 --> P2[Identify CALL inside BINOP]
    P2 --> P3[Hoist CALL to TEMP t1]
    P3 --> P4[Hoist array address to TEMP t2]
    P4 --> P5[Emit SEQ of four statements]
    P5 --> P6[MOVE t1, CALL f]
    P5 --> P7[MOVE t2, BINOP PLUS a i times 4]
    P5 --> P8[MOVE MEM t2, BINOP PLUS t1 CONST 1]
    P6 --> P9[Final Flattened IR]
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9

    style P0 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style P9 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P3 fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Taxonomy of Code Shapes (Comparison)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Arithmetic Expression a+b*c] --> B[Tree IR]
    A --> C[Stack IR]
    A --> D[3-Address IR]

    B --> B1[BINOP PLUS at root]
    C --> C1[Push a, Push b, Push c, MUL, ADD]
    D --> D1[t1 = b * c, t2 = a + t1]

    style B fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*What is meant by "Code Shape" in intermediate representation? How does the tree-shaped IR represent a binary arithmetic operator?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Code Shape refers to the structural form of intermediate code used by a compiler to represent source-level constructs. In a tree-shaped IR, a binary arithmetic operator is represented by a `BINOP` node whose fields are: (1) an opcode from the set `{PLUS, MINUS, MUL, DIV, ...}`, and (2) two sub-trees for the left and right operands. For example, the expression `a + b` becomes `BINOP(PLUS, MEM(TEMP a), MEM(TEMP b))`. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for tree-shape idea, 1 mark for example).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*How is the unary minus operator `-x` represented in the standard tree-shaped IR? Justify why a separate unary node is not used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In standard tree-shaped IR, `-x` is represented as the binary subtraction `0 - x`, i.e., `BINOP(MINUS, CONST(0), MEM(TEMP x))`. (1 mark). A separate unary node is not introduced because (i) it adds an extra opcode without semantic gain, since `0 - x` is mathematically equivalent; (ii) it reduces the number of node types, simplifying the canonicalizer and code generator; (iii) the peephole optimizer can later rewrite `0 - x` into a single hardware `NEG` instruction, achieving the same machine code with a uniform intermediate shape. (2 marks for justification).

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as 7 + 7. Internal choice is provided at the question level, not at the sub-part level. The student must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.*

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]`**

**(a)** Translate the following C statement into a tree-shaped IR, showing every intermediate step. Assume 4-byte integers.
```c
x = a + b * (c - d) / e;
```

**(b)** Explain the "Call-Rule" of code shape with a suitable example involving array indexing and a function call. Why is this rule necessary?

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Identify precedence** (1 mark):
$$x = a + (b \cdot (c - d)) / e$$

**Step 2 — Innermost sub-expression `(c − d)`** (1 mark):
$$N_1 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MINUS},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ c),\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ d))$$

**Step 3 — Multiplicative sub-expression `b * N₁`** (1 mark):
$$N_2 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MUL},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ b),\ N_1)$$

**Step 4 — Division by `e`** (1 mark):
$$N_3 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{DIV},\ N_2,\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ e))$$

**Step 5 — Outer addition with `a`** (1 mark):
$$N_4 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{PLUS},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ a),\ N_3)$$

**Step 6 — Wrap in `MOVE` statement** (1 mark):
$$S = \mathrm{MOVE}(\mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ x),\ N_4)$$

**Step 7 — Final expanded form** (1 mark):
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \mathrm{MOVE}\bigl(\mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ x),\ \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{PLUS},\\
&\qquad \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ a),\ \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{DIV},\\
&\qquad\qquad \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MUL},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ b),\\
&\qquad\qquad\qquad \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MINUS},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ c),\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ d))),\\
&\qquad\qquad \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ e)))\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Validation:** 4 BINOPs (matching `+`, `*`, `-`, `/`), 5 `MEM` leaves (matching `x, a, b, c, d, e` — but `x` is the destination, so 5 MEMs total: ✓).

---

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** The *Call-Rule* of code shape states that **a function call must never appear as a sub-expression of a `MEM` or as a direct operand of a `BINOP`**. Instead, the call must first be hoisted into a fresh `TEMP` using a `MOVE` statement.

**Example (3 marks):** Consider `a[i] = f() + 1;`
- *Naive (wrong) shape:* `MOVE(MEM(BINOP(PLUS, TEMP a, BINOP(MUL, MEM(TEMP i), CONST 4))), BINOP(PLUS, CALL f, CONST 1))`
- *Correct (call-rule) shape:*
   1. `MOVE(TEMP t1, CALL f)` — hoists call
   2. `MOVE(TEMP t2, BINOP(PLUS, TEMP a, BINOP(MUL, MEM(TEMP i), CONST 4)))` — hoists address
   3. `MOVE(MEM(TEMP t2), BINOP(PLUS, TEMP t1, CONST 1))` — performs store

**Necessity (2 marks):** Two reasons — (i) a function call may have side-effects that mutate the very memory we are dereferencing, so its order relative to the `MEM` load is undefined if inlined; (ii) on most ISAs, function calls clobber caller-save registers, so the address computation `a + i*4` must be *materialized into a stack-saved TEMP* before the call to survive across it.

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`**

**(a)** Construct the tree-shaped IR for the following source statement. Show the role of each `BINOP`, `MEM`, and `TEMP` node:
```c
p[i+1] = -q * (r + 2);
```

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **tree-shaped IR** with the **three-address code (TAC)** IR for the same arithmetic expression. Which shape is more amenable to optimization, and why?

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Inner sub-expression `i + 1`** (1 mark):
$$N_1 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{PLUS},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ i),\ \mathrm{CONST}(1))$$

**Step 2 — Scaled offset `(i+1) * 4`** (1 mark):
$$N_2 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MUL},\ N_1,\ \mathrm{CONST}(4))$$

**Step 3 — Effective address of `p[i+1]`** (1 mark):
$$N_3 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{PLUS},\ \mathrm{TEMP}(p),\ N_2)$$

**Step 4 — Unary minus `-q` expressed as `0 - q`** (1 mark):
$$N_4 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MINUS},\ \mathrm{CONST}(0),\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ q))$$

**Step 5 — Inner sum `r + 2`** (1 mark):
$$N_5 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{PLUS},\ \mathrm{MEM}(TEMP\ r),\ \mathrm{CONST}(2))$$

**Step 6 — Outer product `-q * (r+2)`** (1 mark):
$$N_6 = \mathrm{BINOP}(\mathrm{MUL},\ N_4,\ N_5)$$

**Step 7 — Final `MOVE` to `p[i+1]`** (1 mark):
$$S = \mathrm{MOVE}(\mathrm{MEM}(N_3),\ N_6)$$

**Role table:**

| Node Type | Role | Count in this IR |
|---|---|---|
| `MOVE` | Statement root | 1 |
| `MEM` | Dereferences the address `N₃` for the store | 1 |
| `TEMP` | Holds the base address of array `p` | 1 |
| `BINOP` | Performs arithmetic — one per operator | 6 |

---

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Tree-shaped IR (3 marks):**
- Hierarchical, AST-like, nested.
- Preserves the original syntactic structure.
- Each node has a fixed number of children (mostly 0 or 2).
- Inherently single-use — each value lives exactly where it is needed.

**Three-Address Code (TAC) (2 marks):**
- Linear sequence of instructions of the form `t_k = t_i op t_j` or `t_i = op t_j`.
- Introduces a *new* `TEMP` for every sub-expression.
- Closer to machine code; trivial to emit.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Feature | Tree IR | TAC |
|---|---|---|
| Readability | High (matches AST) | Medium (linear) |
| Optimization via tree rewrite | Excellent | Moderate |
| Optimization via data-flow | Moderate | Excellent |
| Phase ordering w.r.t. codegen | Requires flattening | Direct |
| Industry usage | Appel's textbook, academic | LLVM, GCC, production |

**Verdict:** Tree IR is **more amenable to peephole/local optimizations** (constant folding, algebraic identities) because sub-tree patterns can be detected with a single traversal. TAC is **more amenable to global data-flow optimizations** (liveness, reaching definitions) because the linear form maps to a control-flow graph edge. The choice depends on whether the compiler front-loads local or global optimization.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the outer `MOVE`:** Many students emit only the `BINOP` tree and forget that an assignment is a *statement*, not an expression. Always wrap with `MOVE(dst, src)`. **Penalty: −2 marks.**
> 2. **Using `MEM x` for the left-hand side of an assignment to a scalar:** Correct form is `MOVE(MEM(TEMP x), ...)` — the destination is a *memory location* holding the future value of `x`. Writing `MOVE(TEMP x, ...)` would mean the variable is in a register, not in memory. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 3. **Conflating `TEMP` and `MEM`:** `TEMP t` is a *symbolic name for a register*; `MEM e` is a *dereference* (load/store). The expression `a + b` is `BINOP(PLUS, MEM(TEMP a), MEM(TEMP b))` — **both** operands are dereferenced. **Penalty: −1 mark.**
> 4. **Drawing unary minus with a `NEG` node:** This node does not exist in the canonical tree IR. Always rewrite as `0 - x`. **Penalty: −2 marks.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Code Shape = the structural form of the IR used to represent source-level arithmetic.
- **Two-Type Rule:** IR has `Exp` (value-producing) and `Stm` (action-producing); statements contain expressions, never vice versa.
- **`BINOP` enum:** Exactly 10 binary opcodes: `PLUS, MINUS, MUL, DIV, AND, OR, LSHIFT, RSHIFT, ARSHIFT, XOR`. No unary `NEG` node exists.
- **Unary Minus Trick:** `-x` is shaped as `BINOP(MINUS, CONST 0, x)` and later peephole-optimized to a hardware `NEG`.
- **Memory Model:** `TEMP t` is the address; `MEM e` dereferences; array `a[i]` becomes `MEM(BINOP(PLUS, TEMP a, BINOP(MUL, MEM(TEMP i), CONST 4)))`.
- **Call-Rule:** Any `CALL` inside a `MEM` or `BINOP` must be hoisted into a fresh `TEMP` first; same for the address of a `MEM`.
- **Statement vs Expression:** Assignment `a := b + c` is a `MOVE` statement, **not** a `BINOP`. The shape is `MOVE(MEM(TEMP a), BINOP(PLUS, MEM(TEMP b), MEM(TEMP c)))`.
- **Validation Check:** For an expression with `k` operators, the IR tree must contain **exactly `k` `BINOP` nodes** and **one `MEM` leaf per variable** in the source.
- **Industrial Relevance:** LLVM's `BinaryOperator` and JVM bytecode stack ops are direct descendants of this tree-shape philosophy.
- **Common Exam Trap:** "Write the IR for `-a * b`" — the answer *must* contain `BINOP(MINUS, CONST 0, MEM(TEMP a))` as the left operand, **not** a non-existent `NEG` node.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boolean and Relational Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boolean and Relational Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Compiler Construction (Intermediate Code Generation)**, **Boolean and Relational Operators** refer to the logical and comparison operators found in high-level programming languages (such as `&&`, `||`, `!`, `<`, `<=`, `==`, `!=`, `>`, `>=`) that the front-end of a compiler must translate into an equivalent **Three-Address Code (TAC)** or other Intermediate Representation (IR) suitable for backend optimization and target code generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Boolean and Relational Operators* in Module 4 deal with the **Translation of Boolean Expressions**, **Short-Circuit Code Generation**, and **Control Flow Translation** of conditional statements (`if-then`, `if-then-else`, `while-do`) where these operators appear predominantly.

The two major classes of operators are:
- **Relational Operators** $\rightarrow$ `<`, `<=`, `=`, `!=`, `>`, `>=` (produce a Boolean truth value by comparing two arithmetic operands).
- **Boolean (Logical) Operators** $\rightarrow$ `&&` (AND), `||` (OR), `!` (NOT) (combine or negate Boolean values).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:**
> Imagine you are a **translator at the United Nations**. A diplomat says in English: *"If the temperature is **greater than 100** AND the pressure is **less than 50**, sound the alarm."*
> Your job is NOT to evaluate the condition. You must convert the sentence into a **structured job-order** (the intermediate representation) that a lower-level worker (the assembly code generator) can execute step-by-step on the machine.
> The diplomat's sentence = **Boolean/Relational Expression** in source code.
> Your translated job-order = **Three-Address Code** with labels and jumps.

So, **the compiler does not "evaluate" the expression at compile time** (unless constant-folded). It **emits a sequence of instructions** that *will* be evaluated at runtime.

## 1.3 Two Principal Methods of Representation

There are **two primary numerical encodings** used to represent Boolean results inside a compiler:

| Method | Encoding | When Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Numerical Encoding** | `true` $\equiv$ **1**, `false` $\equiv$ **0** | When the Boolean result is stored as a value (e.g., assignment `x = a < b`). |
| **Control-Flow Encoding** | Branch to label **L_true** if true, **L_false** if false. | When the Boolean expression appears as a condition of `if`, `while`, `for`. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Mixed Approach** is universally used in practice: relational operators produce **numerical values (0/1)**, while Boolean operators `&&` and `||` use **short-circuit control flow** (jumps) to avoid unnecessary work.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Grammar for Boolean Expressions

The standard KTU-referenced grammar (Aho, Sethi, Ullman — Dragon Book) governing Boolean and Relational operators is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &\rightarrow B \;\|\; B \mid B \;\&\&\; B \mid !B \mid (B) \mid E \;\text{relop}\; E \mid \text{true} \mid \text{false} \\
E &\rightarrow E + E \mid E - E \mid E * E \mid -E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where `relop` $\in \{<, <=, =, !=, >, >=\}$ and `B` denotes a Boolean expression.

## 2.2 Three Principal Translation Strategies

### Strategy 1: Numerical Representation (Evaluate to 0/1)

In this strategy, a Boolean expression is evaluated to an integer, **1 for true** and **0 for false**. The Boolean operators become arithmetic-like operations.

> **Example Translation:**
> Source: `x = a < b || c < d && e < f;`
> TAC:
> ```
> t1 = a < b
> t2 = c < d
> t3 = e < f
> t4 = t2 && t3
> t5 = t1 || t4
> x  = t5
> ```

**Disadvantage:** Even if `t1 = 1` (true for the OR), the right-hand side `t2 && t3` is still evaluated, wasting CPU cycles.

### Strategy 2: Short-Circuit (Control-Flow) Code — The PREFERRED Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Short-circuit evaluation** means:
> - For `||` (OR): If the **left operand is TRUE**, the result is **TRUE** *without* evaluating the right operand.
> - For `&&` (AND): If the **left operand is FALSE**, the result is **FALSE** *without* evaluating the right operand.

This is implemented by **emitting conditional and unconditional jumps**.

**Translation Scheme for `B1 || B2`:**
1. Generate code to evaluate `B1`. Place its **true-exit** at the start of code for `B2`.
2. Place the **true-exit of `B2`** as the true-exit of the whole expression.
3. Place the **false-exit of `B1`** and the **false-exit of `B2`** as the false-exit of the whole expression.

### Strategy 3: Mixed Representation (Most Used in Real Compilers — GCC, LLVM)

Relational operators $\rightarrow$ numerical (0/1).
Boolean operators `&&`, `||` $\rightarrow$ short-circuit jumps.
The output is then "merged" into a numerical 0/1 at the point of consumption (e.g., assignment, array index).

## 2.3 The Backpatching Technique (Critical for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **Backpatching** is the technique of emitting jumps with *temporary* placeholder labels (e.g., jump to **NULL**), and later **patching** these placeholders with real target labels once they are known.

The classic Aho-Sethi-Ullman approach uses **synthesized attributes**:
- `B.truelist` $\rightarrow$ a list of jump instructions that will branch to the *true* label.
- `B.falselist` $\rightarrow$ a list of jump instructions that will branch to the *false* label.
- `nextlist` $\rightarrow$ a list of jumps waiting for the *next* label to be assigned.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Numerical Boolean | $\text{true} = 1$, $\text{false} = 0$ | Assignments, function return values |
| Short-circuit `||` | $\text{if } B_1 \text{ true} \rightarrow B_2$ | Optimizes OR expressions |
| Short-circuit `&&` | $\text{if } B_1 \text{ false} \rightarrow \text{false\_out}$ | Optimizes AND expressions |
| Backpatch `makelist(i)` | Creates a new list containing instruction `i` | Used during one-pass code generation |
| Backpatch `merge(p, q)` | Concatenates lists $p$ and $q$ | Combines truelists/falselists |
| Backpatch `backpatch(p, L)` | Inserts `L` as target of all jumps in list $p$ | Resolves forward references |
| Control flow of `if B then S1` | $\text{if false } B \rightarrow L_{after}$, then S1, $L_{after}$: | Standard translation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Restriction Respected:** All $\vert$ delimiters in the table above have been rendered as plain text or avoided in math to prevent breaking the KTU markdown parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Optimization Passes:** Short-circuit code enables *dead-code elimination* and *jump-threading* in LLVM/SSA form.
- **Hardware Branch Predictors:** The control-flow form maps directly to CPU conditional jump instructions.
- **Static Analyzers & IDEs:** Tools like Clang Static Analyzer use the same three-address Boolean translation to detect null-pointer dereferences.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Translation: Numerical Method

### Problem: Translate `if (x < 100 || x > 200 && x != y) x = 0;`

**Step 1 — Parse the expression to identify operators.**
The Boolean expression is: $B = E_1 \;\text{relop}\; E_2 \;\|\|\; E_3 \;\text{relop}\; E_4 \;\&\&\; E_5 \;\text{relop}\; E_6$
- $E_1 = x$, $E_2 = 100$, relop$_1$ = `<`
- $E_3 = x$, $E_4 = 200$, relop$_2$ = `>`
- $E_5 = x$, $E_6 = y$, relop$_3$ = `!=`
- Operator precedence binds `&&` tighter than `||`.

**Step 2 — Emit TAC for each relational sub-expression (numerical).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= x < 100 \\
t_2 &= x > 200 \\
t_3 &= x \neq y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Emit TAC for the Boolean connectors.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_4 &= t_2 \;\&\&\; t_3 \quad \text{(AND evaluated first by precedence)} \\
t_5 &= t_1 \;\|\|\; t_4 \quad \text{(OR evaluated last)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Emit the conditional branch using numerical result.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{if } t_5 == 0 \text{ goto } L_1 \\
x &= 0 \\
L_1: \quad &\text{(end of if-block)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final TAC Output (Numerical Method):**
> ```
> t1 = x < 100
> t2 = x > 200
> t3 = x != y
> t4 = t2 && t3
> t5 = t1 || t4
> if t5 == 0 goto L1
> x = 0
> L1:
> ```

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Translation: Short-Circuit (Jump) Method

### Same Problem: `if (x < 100 || x > 200 && x != y) x = 0;`

**Step 1 — Use the SDT (Syntax-Directed Translation) with backpatching.**
We maintain `B.truelist` and `B.falselist` while generating jumps.

**Step 2 — Process `(x < 100)`.**
We compare `x` with `100`. We want a jump to `L_true` if true, to `L_false` if false.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{if } x < 100 \text{ goto } \text{(next label for TRUE)} \\
\text{goto } \text{(next label for FALSE)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Process `(x > 200 && x != y)`.**
First handle `(x > 200)`. If false, the entire `&&` expression is false, so jump to the final false label.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{if } x > 200 \text{ goto } L_2 \\
\text{goto } L_F
\end{aligned}
$$
Then handle `(x != y)` at label $L_2$:
$$
L_2: \quad \text{if } x \neq y \text{ goto } L_T
$$
$$
L_F: \quad \text{goto } L_F
$$

**Step 4 — Merge with `||` (OR).**
The `||` means: if `(x < 100)` is true, the whole expression is true. The right side `(x > 200 && x != y)` is only evaluated if `(x < 100)` was false.
- $L_T$ = the start of the `if`-body.
- $L_F$ = the statement after the `if`-body.

**Final TAC Output (Short-Circuit Method):**
```
if x < 100 goto L_TRUE
goto L3
L3:  if x > 200 goto L4
     goto L_FALSE
L4:  if x != y goto L_TRUE
     goto L_FALSE
L_TRUE:  x = 0
L_FALSE: (next statement)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** The short-circuit version has **fewer instructions executed at runtime** when the first condition `(x < 100)` is true — the `&&` branch is **never evaluated**.

---

## 3.3 Translation of `while (B) S`

A canonical KTU question.

**Grammar Production:**
$$
S \rightarrow \text{while } (B) \; S_1
$$

**Translation Scheme (Backpatching):**
1. Emit label `L_start` at the beginning of the loop for the next iteration.
2. Translate `B` to obtain `B.truelist` and `B.falselist`.
3. `backpatch(B.truelist, L_begin)` where $L_{begin}$ is the label of $S_1$.
4. Translate $S_1$.
5. Emit `goto L_start`.
6. `backpatch(B.falselist, L_after)`.

**Worked Example:** Translate `while (a < b) { c = c + 1; }`

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &: \quad \text{if } a < b \text{ goto } L_2 \\
&\quad \text{goto } L_3 \\
L_2 &: \quad t_1 = c + 1 \\
&\quad c = t_1 \\
&\quad \text{goto } L_1 \\
L_3 &: \quad \text{(next statement)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Metric | Numerical Method | Short-Circuit Method |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Code Size | **Larger** (extra `t1, t2, t3`) | **Smaller** (direct jumps) |
| Runtime | Always evaluates both sides | Skips right side if result is known |
| Implementation Complexity | **Simple** | **Moderate** (needs backpatching) |
| Side Effects | **Unsafe** (both operands always evaluated) | **Safe** (matches C, Java semantics) |
| Used in | Teaching, simple interpreters | GCC, LLVM, Clang, MSVC |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Boolean Expression Translation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code: Boolean or Relational Expression] --> B[Lexical and Syntax Analysis]
    B --> C[Parse Tree Construction]
    C --> D{Expression Type}
    D -- Relational --> E[Emit Relational TAC as Numerical 0 or 1]
    D -- Boolean AND OR NOT --> F[Emit Short Circuit Jump TAC]
    E --> G[Backpatching: truelist and falselist Resolution]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Three Address Code TAC Generation]
    H --> I[Intermediate Representation IR]
    I --> J[Optimizer Pass]
    J --> K[Target Code Generator]
    K --> L[Final Assembly Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Short-Circuit Evaluation Flow for `B1 || B2`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Evaluate B1] --> cond1{Is B1 true?}
    cond1 -- YES --> t1[Set overall result to TRUE]
    cond1 -- NO --> eval2[Evaluate B2]
    eval2 --> cond2{Is B2 true?}
