```mermaid
    cond2 -- YES --> t1
    cond2 -- NO --> f1[Set overall result to FALSE]
    t1 --> next[Continue Execution]
    f1 --> next
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Backpatching Lifecycle for Conditional Jumps

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[emit if B goto NULL] --> B[Add instruction to truelist via makelist]
    B --> C[Parse remaining expression]
    C --> D[Backpatch truelist with next label via backpatch]
    D --> E[Resolved Three Address Code]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: While-Loop Control-Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[L_start: Begin of Loop] --> evalB[Evaluate Boolean B]
    evalB --> branch{Is B true?}
    branch -- TRUE --> body[Execute Statement S1]
    body --> back[goto L_start]
    branch -- FALSE --> exitL[L_after: Exit Loop]
    back --> L1
```

## 4.5 Detailed Functional Architecture of the Boolean Translator Module

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FrontEndModule[Front End of Compiler]
        Lex[Lexical Analyzer] --> Syn[Syntax Analyzer]
        Syn --> PT[Parse Tree]
    end
    subgraph IntermediateCodeGen[Intermediate Code Generation Phase]
        PT --> RelTrans[Relational Translator: emits numerical TAC]
        RelTrans --> BoolTrans[Boolean Translator: emits short circuit jumps]
        BoolTrans --> BP[Backpatching Engine: truelist, falselist, nextlist]
        BP --> TACG[TAC Output Stream]
    end
    subgraph BackEndModule[Back End of Compiler]
        TACG --> Opt[Optimizer: constant folding, dead code elimination]
        Opt --> CodeGen[Code Generator]
        CodeGen --> ASM[Target Assembly]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Differentiate between Numerical Representation and Short-Circuit Code Representation of Boolean Expressions. Which one is preferred and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Numerical Representation | Short-Circuit Representation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Boolean values encoded as | **0 or 1** stored in temporaries | **Conditional jumps** to true/false labels |
| Evaluation | **Both operands always evaluated** | **Right operand skipped** if result is known |
| Side-effect safety | **Unsafe** (extra side effects may occur) | **Safe** (matches C/Java semantics) |
| Code size | **Larger** (extra temporaries) | **Smaller** (jumps only) |

**Preferred:** *Short-circuit representation* is preferred in production compilers (GCC, LLVM) because it **avoids unnecessary evaluation of the right operand**, **reduces code size**, and **preserves the language semantics of `&&` and `||`**. *(1 mark for the why)*

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, S3 CSE]`
**Define the roles of `truelist`, `falselist`, and `nextlist` in backpatching for Boolean expressions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **`B.truelist`:** A linked list of *unresolved* jump instructions that must branch to the **true-exit** of Boolean expression `B` once the label is known. *(1 mark)*
- **`B.falselist`:** A linked list of *unresolved* jump instructions that must branch to the **false-exit** of `B`. *(1 mark)*
- **`nextlist`:** A list of conditional/unconditional jumps (typically from `if-then`, `while`, etc.) waiting for the **next emitted label** to be backpatched as their target. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the **short-circuit code generation scheme** for translating Boolean expressions containing `&&` (AND) and `||` (OR). Use the grammar
$$B \rightarrow B \;\&\&\; B \mid B \;\|\|\; B \mid !B \mid E \;\text{relop}\; E$$
and describe the truelist and falselist attributes used in backpatching. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The short-circuit scheme ensures that the **right operand is evaluated only if necessary**.

**For `B1 || B2` (OR):**
- Evaluate `B1`. If `B1` is **true**, the result of the whole expression is true *without* evaluating `B2`.
- This is achieved by making the *true-exit* of `B1` point to the start of the code for `B2`, and making the *true-exit* of `B2` be the true-exit of the whole expression.
- The combined `falselist` is the merge of `B1.falselist` and `B2.falselist`.

**For `B1 && B2` (AND):**
- Evaluate `B1`. If `B1` is **false**, the result of the whole expression is false *without* evaluating `B2`.
- The *false-exit* of `B1` is made the *false-exit* of the whole expression.
- The combined `truelist` is the merge of `B1.truelist` and `B2.truelist`.

**For `!B1` (NOT):**
- Simply swap `B1.truelist` and `B1.falselist`.

**For `E1 relop E2`:**
- Emit: `if E1 relop E2 goto _` (add to `truelist`)
- Emit: `goto _` (add to `falselist`)

**Backpatching attributes:**
- `makelist(i)` creates a singleton list with jump instruction index `i`.
- `merge(p, q)` concatenates lists `p` and `q`.
- `backpatch(p, L)` fills in target label `L` for all instructions in list `p`. *(7 marks distributed across the four operators and the helper functions)*

---

**(b)** Generate **three-address code** (TAC) using the **short-circuit (jump) method** for the following C program fragment: **(7 marks)**

```c
while (a < b && c < d) {
    if (x == y || m > n)
        p = p + 1;
}
```

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Translation of the `while` header condition `a < b && c < d`:**
- We need the loop body entry label, call it $L_1$.
- The exit label, call it $L_{out}$.

**Step 2 — Translation of the `if` condition `x == y || m > n`:**
- True-exit = label of `p = p + 1;`, call it $L_2$.
- False-exit = label after the `if`-body (which is the loop end $L_{out}$).

**Step 3 — Emitted TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{start}&: \quad \text{if } a < b \text{ goto } L_3 \\
         &\quad \text{goto } L_{out} \\
L_3&: \quad \text{if } c < d \text{ goto } L_1 \\
   &\quad \text{goto } L_{out} \\
L_1&: \quad \text{if } x = y \text{ goto } L_2 \\
   &\quad \text{goto } L_4 \\
L_4&: \quad \text{if } m > n \text{ goto } L_2 \\
   &\quad \text{goto } L_{out} \\
L_2&: \quad t_1 = p + 1 \\
   &\quad p = t_1 \\
   &\quad \text{goto } L_{start} \\
L_{out}&: \quad \text{(statement after the while loop)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Grading Rubric for (b):**
- Correct `while` translation: 3 marks
- Correct `||` short-circuit: 2 marks
- Correct body and `goto L_start`: 2 marks

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, S3 CSE]`

**(a)** Explain **backpatching** in detail. Describe the `makelist`, `merge`, and `backpatch` functions and how they are used in generating three-address code for `if-then-else` statements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Backpatching** is a technique used during one-pass code generation where forward jumps are emitted with **incomplete (NULL) target labels**. As the parse progresses and target labels become known, these "holes" are patched.

**Helper Functions:**
1. `makelist(i)` $\rightarrow$ creates a new list containing the index `i` of the jump instruction that has an unresolved target. *(1 mark)*
2. `merge(p1, p2)` $\rightarrow$ concatenates two lists `p1` and `p2` and returns the head of the combined list. Used to merge truelists/falselists of sub-expressions. *(1 mark)*
3. `backpatch(p, L)` $\rightarrow$ iterates through list `p` and sets the target of every jump in the list to the resolved label `L`. *(1 mark)*

**Application to `if-then-else`:**
Grammar:
$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow \text{if } (B) \; S_1 \;\text{else } S_2 \\
S &\rightarrow \text{if } (B) \; S_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Translation Steps:**
1. Translate `B` $\rightarrow$ yields `B.truelist`, `B.falselist`.
2. Translate `S1` $\rightarrow$ yields `S1.nextlist`.
3. `backpatch(B.truelist, L_begin_of_S1)` — the true jumps of B now point to S1. *(1 mark)*
4. Emit `goto _` after S1 and add it to `S1.nextlist`.
5. `backpatch(B.falselist, L_begin_of_S2)` — false jumps point to S2 (or the end label if no else). *(1 mark)*
6. `backpatch(S1.nextlist, L_next_statement)` — fall-through jumps after S1 point to the next statement after the if. *(1 mark)*
7. Mark `L_next_statement` as the *current* label for subsequent code. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b)** Translate the following C code into **three-address code using the short-circuit (jump) method with backpatching**: **(7 marks)**

```c
if (a > b || c < d)
    x = y + z;
else
    x = y - z;
```

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Condition `(a > b || c < d)`:**
- True-exit of the `||` $\rightarrow$ label of `x = y + z`, call it $L_1$.
- False-exit $\rightarrow$ label of `x = y - z`, call it $L_2$.

**Step 2 — Final TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{if } a > b \text{ goto } L_1 \\
&\text{goto } L_3 \\
L_3&: \quad \text{if } c < d \text{ goto } L_1 \\
   &\quad \text{goto } L_2 \\
L_1&: \quad t_1 = y + z \\
   &\quad x = t_1 \\
   &\quad \text{goto } L_{next} \\
L_2&: \quad t_2 = y - z \\
   &\quad x = t_2 \\
L_{next}&: \quad \text{(statement after the if-else)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Grading Rubric for (b):**
- Correct `||` short-circuit structure: 2 marks
- Correct then-body translation: 2 marks
- Correct else-body translation: 1 mark
- Correct unconditional `goto L_next`: 2 marks

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing `truelist` and `falselist` for `&&` vs `||`.** Remember: `||` merges falselists, `&&` merges truelists.
> 2. **Forgetting the unconditional `goto` after the `if` body.** Without it, the `else` part (if any) will erroneously execute.
> 3. **Misplacing the loop-back `goto L_start` in `while`.** It must come *after* the body, not before the condition.
> 4. **Ignoring operator precedence** (`&&` binds tighter than `||`). Translating left-to-right without grouping is a 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Mixing numerical and short-circuit methods** in the same problem. KTU examiners award partial credit, but consistency is expected.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Boolean expressions** in source code must be translated into **Three-Address Code (TAC)** during intermediate code generation. ✅
- **Two main representations:** **Numerical (0/1)** and **Short-circuit (control-flow/jumps)**. ✅
- **Short-circuit semantics:** `||` skips right operand if left is **true**; `&&` skips right operand if left is **false**. ✅
- **Backpatching** uses three helper functions: `makelist(i)`, `merge(p, q)`, `backpatch(p, L)`. ✅
- **Attributes:** `B.truelist` (jumps to true-exit), `B.falselist` (jumps to false-exit), `S.nextlist` (jumps to next statement). ✅
- **For `B1 || B2`:** merge `B1.falselist` and `B2.falselist` for the new falselist; `B2.truelist` becomes the new truelist. ✅
- **For `B1 && B2`:** merge `B1.truelist` and `B2.truelist` for the new truelist; `B2.falselist` becomes the new falselist. ✅
- **For `!B1`:** swap `B1.truelist` and `B1.falselist`. ✅
- **For `E1 relop E2`:** emit `if E1 relop E2 goto _` (truelist) and `goto _` (falselist). ✅
- **`while(B) S1` translation:** backpatch `B.truelist` to $L_{begin}$ of $S_1$; emit `goto L_start` after $S_1$; backpatch `B.falselist` to $L_{after}$. ✅
- **`if-then-else` translation:** backpatch `B.truelist` to then-body; backpatch `B.falselist` to else-body; emit `goto L_next` after then-body and add to `S1.nextlist`; backpatch `S1.nextlist` to $L_{next}$. ✅
- **Mixed representation** (relational $\rightarrow$ numerical, Boolean $\rightarrow$ jumps) is the **industry standard** (GCC, LLVM, Clang). ✅
- **Real-world utility:** Enables **dead-code elimination**, **branch prediction**, and **short-circuit side-effect preservation** in C/Java. ✅

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control-Flow Constructs - Conditional Execution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control-Flow Constructs - Conditional Execution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Compiler Construction** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4), **Control-Flow Constructs** refer to the language statements that govern the order in which program instructions are executed, deviating from the default sequential flow. **Conditional Execution** is the specific sub-domain dealing with constructs that evaluate a predicate (Boolean expression) and dispatch the flow of control to one of two or more branches based on its truth value.

The principal constructs classified under conditional execution in KTU syllabus PBCRT604 are:

- **`if E then S1`**
- **`if E then S1 else S2`**
- **`while E do S`**
- **`do S while E`**
- **`case / switch` selectors**

The compiler's responsibility is to translate these high-level statements into **Three-Address Code (TAC)** sequences employing **conditional jumps** (e.g., `if a < b goto L1`) and **unconditional jumps** (e.g., `goto L2`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Address Code (TAC)**: An intermediate representation in which each instruction has at most **three operands** and combines exactly **one operator**. TAC is the canonical IR used for the synthesis of control-flow translations in the KTU Compiler Construction curriculum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Conditional execution is handled under the unit titled *"Translation of Control-Flow Statements and Boolean Expressions"*. The two principal techniques taught are the **Syntax-Directed Translation Scheme using Marker Non-terminals** and the **Backpatching Algorithm**. Both are **high-weightage** topics for KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Train Track Switch

Imagine a single railway track approaching a railway junction. Just before the junction stands a **signalman (Boolean expression `E`)**. He looks at the incoming train (current program state) and decides:

- **If the destination is Mumbai** → he flips switch A → the train proceeds on **Track S1**.
- **Otherwise (Else)** → he flips switch B → the train proceeds on **Track S2**.
- The train never stops; it merely *redirects* its momentum.

In the compiled program, the **signalman is the conditional jump instruction** (`if E goto L_true`), and the **switches are the labels** (`L_true`, `L_false`, `L_next`) embedded in the TAC. The compiler's job is to *lay the track and place the switches* in the correct order so that runtime execution mimics the source-level intent.

---

## 1.3 The Two-Phase Compilation View

Conditional execution translation is best understood as a **two-phase** process:

| Phase | Compiler Component | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1** | Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD) / Translation Scheme | A sequence of TAC instructions with **forward references** (jumps to labels not yet defined) |
| **Phase 2** | Backpatching Module | Labels resolved, jump targets filled, linear TAC produced |

> [!TIP]
> **Why two phases?** A **single-pass** compiler scanning the input from left-to-right often encounters a `goto L` *before* the label `L:` is generated. A buffer ("backpatch list") is required to patch these pending jumps later. This is the **essence of backpatching**, the most tested concept in KTU Module 4.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Mandatory Vocabulary for KTU)

- **Predicate / Boolean Expression (`E`)**: An expression evaluating to `true` or `false`. Composed using relational operators (`<, <=, ==, !=, >, >=`), logical AND (`&&`), OR (`||`), and NOT (`!`).
- **True-List (`E.truelist`)**: A list of TAC instruction indices (or labels) into which a jump to the *true* branch must be patched.
- **False-List (`E.falselist`)**: A list of TAC instruction indices into which a jump to the *false* branch must be patched.
- **Inherit Attribute `nextlist`**: Used for statement lists (`S.nextlist`); holds the indices of jumps that must target the code that follows the statement list.
- **Marker Non-Terminal `mark`**: A synthetic non-terminal inserted into the grammar to capture the current `nextquad` (index of the next TAC instruction to be generated).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Translation of `if E then S`

The grammar production used is:

$$ S \rightarrow \textbf{if } E \textbf{ then } S_1 $$

The corresponding semantic actions (executed during parsing) are:

1. Generate code for `E` → produces `E.truelist` and `E.falselist`.
2. `backpatch(E.truelist, S_1.nextlist_first)` is not correct here — the more precise formulation is: backpatch `E.truelist` to point to the start of `S_1`'s code.
3. `S.nextlist` = `E.falselist` (control falls through to the code after `S` if `E` is false).

**Resulting TAC Skeleton:**

```
    [code for E]
L1: if E_true_cond goto L2      <-- from E.truelist
    goto L3                     <-- from E.falselist (S.nextlist)
L2: [code for S1]
L3:
```

---

## 2.2 Translation of `if E then S1 else S2`

Grammar production:

$$ S \rightarrow \textbf{if } E \textbf{ then } S_1 \textbf{ else } S_2 $$

Semantic actions:

1. Generate code for `E`.
2. `backpatch(E.truelist, S_1.start)`.
3. `backpatch(E.falselist, S_2.start)`.
4. `S.nextlist` = `merge(S_1.nextlist, S_2.nextlist)`.

Additionally, a **jump must be inserted at the end of `S_1`** to bypass `S_2`. This is realized by an auxiliary production `S → S;` (a labelled statement) or by emitting `goto S.nextlist` and pushing the index onto `S.nextlist` at the end of `S_1`.

**Resulting TAC Skeleton:**

```
    [code for E]
    if E_true_cond goto L1      <-- E.truelist
    goto L2                     <-- E.falselist
L1: [code for S1]
    goto L_next                 <-- jumps over S2, falls to S.nextlist
L2: [code for S2]
L_next:
```

---

## 2.3 Boolean Expression Translation — The Aho/Sethi/Ullman Method

Boolean expressions are translated into TAC using **short-circuit evaluation**. The productions and their semantic rules are tabulated below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production Set for Boolean Expressions (Aho et al., Dragon Book style — the KTU standard reference):**

| Production | Semantic Action | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| $E \rightarrow E_1 \lor T$ | `E1.falselist = makelist(nextquad); emit('if E1.place goto ...')` — actually generates `or` logic via jumps | Logical OR |
| $E \rightarrow E_1 \land T$ | `E1.truelist = makelist(nextquad); emit('if E1.place goto ...')` | Logical AND |
| $E \rightarrow \lnot E_1$ | Swap `E.truelist` and `E.falselist` from `E_1` | Logical NOT |
| $E \rightarrow (E_1)$ | `E.truelist = E1.truelist; E.falselist = E1.falselist` | Parenthesized |
| $E \rightarrow \textbf{id}_1 \textbf{ relop } \textbf{id}_2$ | `E.truelist = makelist(nextquad); E.falselist = makelist(nextquad+1); emit('if id1 relop id2 goto _'); emit('goto _')` | Relational |
| $E \rightarrow \textbf{true}$ | `E.truelist = makelist(nextquad); emit('goto _')` | Constant True |
| $E \rightarrow \textbf{false}$ | `E.falselist = makelist(nextquad); emit('goto _')` | Constant False |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, the Dragon Book (Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman) treatment of *backpatching for Boolean expressions* is the **reference algorithm**. Ensure familiarity with the helper functions `makelist(i)`, `merge(p1, p2)`, and `backpatch(p, i)`.

---

## 2.4 The Backpatching Helper Functions (KTU Standard)

These three functions are the *backbone* of every KTU Module 4 problem. Memorize their semantics:

1. **`makelist(i)`** — Creates a new list containing only the index `i`. Returns the list pointer.
2. **`merge(p1, p2)`** — Concatenates two lists `p1` and `p2`. Returns the merged list pointer.
3. **`backpatch(p, i)`** — Fills every instruction index stored in list `p` with label `i` (i.e., patches each placeholder `_` in those instructions to point to TAC instruction `i`).

**Invariant:** After `backpatch(p, i)`, the list `p` is logically empty (in textbook pseudocode), though the underlying memory may still be deallocated by the implementer.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Translation Rule | TAC Pattern Emitted | Key Attribute Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `if E then S1` | `if E goto L1; goto L2; L1: code(S1); L2:` | `E.truelist`, `E.falselist` |
| 2 | `if E then S1 else S2` | `if E goto L1; goto L2; L1: code(S1); goto L3; L2: code(S2); L3:` | `merge(S1.nextlist, S2.nextlist)` |
| 3 | `while E do S1` | `L1: code(E); if E goto L2; goto L3; L2: code(S1); goto L1; L3:` | `S.begin` marker |
| 4 | `E1 || E2` | `backpatch(E1.falselist, E2.start); E.truelist = merge(E1.truelist, E2.truelist); E.falselist = E2.falselist` | OR short-circuit |
| 5 | `E1 && E2` | `backpatch(E1.truelist, E2.start); E.truelist = E2.truelist; E.falselist = merge(E1.falselist, E2.falselist)` | AND short-circuit |
| 6 | `!E1` | `E.truelist = E1.falselist; E.falselist = E1.truelist` | NOT swap |
| 7 | `a relop b` | `if a relop b goto _; goto _` | Two `makelist` calls |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Conditional execution translation is foundational to:

- **Optimizing Compilers** (GCC, LLVM, MSVC): enables dead-code elimination, branch prediction hints, and SSA construction.
- **Static Analyzers** (Coverity, SonarQube): conditional paths are enumerated for vulnerability detection.
- **JIT Compilers** (HotSpot, V8): conditional jumps are the lowest-level primitive emitted to machine code.
- **Hardware Synthesis** (HDL → Verilog/VHDL): high-level `if-else` directly maps to multiplexers (MUX) in synthesized circuits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — `if-then-else` Translation

### Source Program

```c
if (x < 100 || x > 200 && x != y)
    x = 0;
else
    x = 1;
```

We will generate TAC using the **backpatching** technique. Let `nextquad` denote the index of the next TAC instruction to be emitted. We assume the starting value of `nextquad = 100`.

### Step 1 — Parse the Boolean expression `E = (x < 100 || x > 200 && x != y)`

Following the operator precedence: `&&` binds tighter than `||`, so the parse tree is:

$$ E \rightarrow E_1 \;\lor\; T_2 $$

where $E_1 = (x < 100)$ and $T_2 = (x > 200 \;\&\&\; x \neq y)$.

### Step 2 — Emit TAC for `E1 = (x < 100)`

Using the production `E → id1 relop id2`:

| `nextquad` | TAC Emitted | Semantic Action |
|---|---|---|
| **100** | `if x < 100 goto _` | `E1.truelist = makelist(100)` |
| **101** | `goto _` | `E1.falselist = makelist(101)` |

Now `nextquad = 102`.

### Step 3 — Process `T2 = (x > 200 && x != y)`

`T2` parses as `T2_1 && F`. First, handle `T2_1 = (x > 200)`:

| `nextquad` | TAC Emitted | Semantic Action |
|---|---|---|
| **102** | `if x > 200 goto _` | `T2_1.truelist = makelist(102)` |
| **103** | `goto _` | `T2_1.falselist = makelist(103)` |

Now `nextquad = 104`. Then handle `F = (x != y)`:

| `nextquad` | TAC Emitted | Semantic Action |
|---|---|---|
| **104** | `if x != y goto _` | `F.truelist = makelist(104)` |
| **105** | `goto _` | `F.falselist = makelist(105)` |

Now `nextquad = 106`.

### Step 4 — Apply the `&&` Semantic Rule

Rule: `backpatch(T2_1.truelist, F.start)`, i.e., patch index **102** to the start of `F` which is **104**.

`E.truelist = F.truelist = {104}`
`E.falselist = merge(T2_1.falselist, F.falselist) = {103, 105}`

### Step 5 — Apply the `||` Semantic Rule

Rule: `backpatch(E1.falselist, T2.start)`, i.e., patch index **101** to the start of `T2` which is **102**.

`E.truelist = merge(E1.truelist, T2.truelist) = merge({100}, {104}) = {100, 104}`
`E.falselist = T2.falselist = {103, 105}`

`nextquad = 106`.

### Step 6 — Process the `if E then S1`

At this point, we need to insert a placeholder for the true-branch jump. The grammar production `S → if E then S1` calls:

`backpatch(E.truelist, nextquad)` — but the `nextquad` is the address of the *start* of `S1`. We will first emit the `S1` code and resolve labels in a marker-based scheme. For clarity, let's emit the branch skeleton first.

The compiler's flow inserts an unconditional jump at the end of `S1`. The complete generated TAC is:

| `nextquad` | TAC | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | `if x < 100 goto _` | `E.truelist` placeholder |
| 101 | `goto _` | `E1.falselist` placeholder (patches to 102) |
| 102 | `if x > 200 goto _` | `T2_1.truelist` placeholder (patches to 104) |
| 103 | `goto _` | `T2_1.falselist` → part of `E.falselist` |
| 104 | `if x != y goto _` | `F.truelist` → part of `E.truelist` |
| 105 | `goto _` | `F.falselist` → part of `E.falselist` |
| 106 | `if x < 100 goto L_TRUE` | After resolution: 100 patches to 110 |
| 106 | (after backpatch) ... | — |

Continuing with the if-then-else after backpatching `E.truelist` to point at the start of `S1` (let's call it `L_TRUE` = 110) and `E.falselist` to point at `S2` start (`L_FALSE` = 113):

| 106 | `goto L_FALSE` | from E.falselist (patches 103, 105) → 113 |
| 110 | `x = 0` | S1: then-branch |
| 111 | `goto L_END` | skip S2 |
| 113 | `x = 1` | S2: else-branch |
| 114 | `L_END:` | (sentinel) |

### Step 7 — Resolve All Backpatches (Final TAC)

After invoking `backpatch(E.truelist, 110)` and `backpatch(E.falselist, 113)`:

| 100 | `if x < 100 goto 110` | Patched |
| 101 | `goto 102` | Patched |
| 102 | `if x > 200 goto 104` | Patched |
| 103 | `goto 113` | Patched |
| 104 | `if x != y goto 110` | Patched |
| 105 | `goto 113` | Patched |
| 106 | (skipped/marker) | — |
| 110 | `x = 0` | S1 body |
| 111 | `goto 114` | jump over S2 |
| 113 | `x = 1` | S2 body |
| 114 | *(end)* | L_END |

### Step 8 — Verification of Correctness

- If `x < 100` → jump from 100 to 110 → execute `x = 0` → jump to 114. ✅
- If `x >= 100` → fall to 101 → jump to 102.
  - At 102, if `x > 200` → jump to 104.
    - At 104, if `x != y` → jump to 110 (`x = 0`). ✅
    - Else → fall to 105 → jump to 113 (`x = 1`). ✅
  - At 102, if `x <= 200` → fall to 103 → jump to 113 (`x = 1`). ✅

Logical mapping: `(x < 100) ∨ (x > 200 ∧ x ≠ y)` → `x = 0`, else `x = 1`. The translation is **semantically correct**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — `while` Loop Translation

### Source Program

```c
while (a < b) {
    a = a + 1;
}
```

### Step-by-Step TAC Generation

| Step | Action | TAC Emitted | Comments |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mark start of `E` (`S.begin = nextquad = 200`) | — | Begin of loop |
| 2 | Emit `E = (a < b)` | `if a < b goto _` (200); `goto _` (201) | `E.truelist = {200}`, `E.falselist = {201}` |
| 3 | `S1.start = nextquad = 202` | — | Body starts here |
| 4 | Emit `S1` body | `t1 = a + 1` (202); `a = t1` (203) | Body code |
| 5 | Emit jump back to loop start | `goto 200` (204) | Unconditional |
| 6 | `backpatch(E.truelist, 202)` | Patch 200 → 202 | True jump → body |
| 7 | `S.nextlist = E.falselist` | 201 remains → loop exit | — |

**Final TAC (after backpatch):**

```
200:  if a < b goto 202
201:  goto 205        <-- loop exit
202:  t1 = a + 1
203:  a = t1
204:  goto 200
205:  (next statement)
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Translation Schemes

| Property | Marker Non-Terminal Method | Backpatching Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Passes required** | 2 (one to find `mark` positions, one to emit code) | 1 (single-pass) |
| **Memory overhead** | Minimal (saves `nextquad` values) | Higher (stores instruction lists) |
| **Implementation complexity** | Lower (conceptually clean) | Higher (linked-list management) |
| **Suitability for KTU 14-mark Q** | Less common | **Highly tested** |
| **Output** | Skeleton with `goto _` placeholders | Fully resolved TAC after backpatching |
| **Standard reference** | Aho/Sethi/Ullman (Dragon Book) | Aho/Lam/Sethi/Ullman (Dragon Book 2nd Ed.) |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark problems, the examiner typically provides a small `if-then-else` or `while` source, and expects **(a)** a parse-tree or grammar production breakdown (7 marks) plus **(b)** the backpatching trace with final TAC (7 marks). Master both halves.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of the Backpatching Translation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **block-level functional architecture** of a single-pass compiler's control-flow translation module. It traces the path from source code entry to finalized TAC output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code: if E then S1 else S2] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[Token Stream]
    C --> D[Parser with SDD Actions]
    D --> E{Boolean Sub-Expression?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Emit if id1 relop id2 goto placeholder]
    F --> G[makelist nextquad for truelist]
    F --> H[makelist nextquad plus 1 for falselist]
    E -- No --> I[Statement Body Translation]
    I --> J[Emit Assignment or nested code]
    G --> K[Backpatch Helper Module]
    H --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Is branch end reached?}
    L -- True --> M[Resolve placeholder labels via backpatch and merge]
    L -- False --> D
    M --> N[Final Three Address Code with all labels resolved]
    N --> O[Optimizer Module]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:**
> - The diamonds (`{}` shapes) are decision points.
> - The parallelogram-like nodes (`[]`) represent operations on attribute lists.
> - The arrow flow `A → B → C → ...` shows the **forward** compilation direction.
> - The feedback loop `L -- False --> D` represents **recursive descent** for nested statements.

---

## 4.2 State Machine for Branch Code Generation

The following diagram models the **logical state transitions** that occur when the compiler encounters a conditional construct. Each state represents a specific TAC emission context.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> CondEvaluated: Parse E and emit if goto plus goto
    CondEvaluated --> TrueBranchCode: Backpatch truelist to Ltrue
    CondEvaluated --> FalseBranchCode: Backpatch falselist to Lfalse
    TrueBranchCode --> EmitSkipJump: Emit goto Lend
    EmitSkipJump --> FalseBranchCode
    FalseBranchCode --> MergeNextlists: merge S1 nextlist with S2 nextlist
    MergeNextlists --> Done: return S nextlist
    Done --> [*]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Three-Address Code Emission for `if-then-else`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SourceParse["Source Parse Phase"]
        SP1[Lex: if]
        SP2[Lex: id1 relop id2]
        SP3[Lex: then]
        SP4[Lex: id3 assignop id4]
        SP5[Lex: else]
        SP6[Lex: id5 assignop id6]
    end

    subgraph TACEmit["TAC Emission Phase"]
        TA1["100: if id1 relop id2 goto __"]
        TA2["101: goto __"]
        TA3["102: id3 = id4"]
        TA4["103: goto __"]
        TA5["104: id5 = id6"]
    end

    subgraph Backpatch["Backpatching Phase"]
        BP1[backpatch 100 with 102]
        BP2[backpatch 101 with 104]
        BP3[backpatch 103 with 105]
    end

    SP2 --> TA1
    SP1 --> TA1
    SP4 --> TA3
    SP6 --> TA5
    SP5 --> TA3

    TA1 --> BP1
    TA2 --> BP2
    TA3 --> BP3
    BP1 --> TA3
    BP2 --> TA5
    BP3 --> TA5
```

---

## 4.4 Data-Flow of Attribute Lists During Translation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Lexical Tokens] --> L2[Parse: E to id1 relop id2]
    L2 --> L3["Emit: if id1 relop id2 goto _"]
    L3 --> L4["E.truelist = makelist nextquad"]
    L4 --> L5["Emit: goto _"]
    L5 --> L6["E.falselist = makelist nextquad"]
    L6 --> L7["Parse S1"]
    L7 --> L8["Emit code for S1"]
    L8 --> L9["backpatch E.truelist, S1 start address"]
    L9 --> L10["Emit: goto _ (S.nextlist entry)"]
    L10 --> L11["Parse S2"]
    L11 --> L12["Emit code for S2"]
    L12 --> L13["backpatch E.falselist, S2 start address"]
    L13 --> L14["S.nextlist = merge S1.nextlist plus S2.nextlist"]
    L14 --> L15[Final TAC with all backpatches resolved]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `TA1`, `BP3`, `L15`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as node names. All label text is raw alphanumeric without markdown formatting tags to ensure clean rendering in Mermaid 10+.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between short-circuit evaluation and full evaluation of Boolean expressions. Which one is adopted by modern compilers during intermediate code generation and why?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

- **Short-circuit evaluation:** The evaluation of a Boolean expression halts as soon as the final truth value can be determined. For `E1 && E2`, if `E1` is `false`, `E2` is not evaluated.
- **Full evaluation:** All sub-expressions are always evaluated regardless of intermediate truth values.
- **Adopted by modern compilers:** Short-circuit.
- **Reasons:**
  1. Matches source-language semantics (C, C++, Java, C# all specify short-circuit).
  2. Avoids unintended side-effects (e.g., division by zero, null pointer dereference).
  3. Allows the compiler to **skip code emission** for unnecessary sub-expressions, reducing TAC size.
  4. Enables **branch-based translation** of Boolean expressions into conditional jumps.

> [!NOTE]
> Award 1 mark each for: short-circuit definition, full evaluation definition, and the 2 reasons (any 2 valid). Total 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is a backpatch list? Explain the functions `makelist`, `merge`, and `backpatch` used in the backpatching technique for Boolean expressions.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

- **Backpatch list:** A linked list (or array) of indices of TAC instructions containing *forward-reference* jumps whose target labels are not yet known at the time of emission.
- **`makelist(i)`:** Creates and returns a new list containing the single index `i` (the address of a freshly emitted jump instruction).
- **`merge(p1, p2)`:** Concatenates two lists `p1` and `p2` and returns the pointer to the merged list. Used to combine `truelist`s or `falselist`s of sub-expressions.
- **`backpatch(p, i)`:** Iterates over every index stored in list `p` and patches the corresponding jump instruction's target label to `i`. After this call, the list is conceptually exhausted.

> [!NOTE]
> 1 mark for backpatch list definition + 1 mark for `makelist` & `merge` (½ each) + 1 mark for `backpatch` explanation. Total 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Compliance:** Each 14-mark question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both sub-parts **must** be answered in full. KTU typically provides a choice between Question A and Question B within the same module.

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**Translate the following C code segment into Three-Address Code (TAC) using the backpatching technique. Show all intermediate attribute lists and the backpatching steps explicitly.**

```c
if (a == b || (c > d && e <= f))
    x = y + z;
else
    p = q - r;
```

#### (a) Construct the parse tree, write the relevant grammar productions, and identify the semantic actions to be applied at each node. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Grammar Productions (Aho/Sethi/Ullman):**

$$ S \rightarrow \textbf{if } E \textbf{ then } S_1 \textbf{ else } S_2 $$

$$ E \rightarrow E \lor T \quad \vert \quad E \land T \quad \vert \quad \lnot E \quad \vert \quad (E) \quad \vert \quad \textbf{id relop id} \quad \vert \quad \textbf{true} \quad \vert \quad \textbf{false} $$

**Parse Tree (textual form):**

$$ S \to \text{if } E \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2 $$

where $E = E_1 \lor T_2$, with:

- $E_1 = a == b$
- $T_2 = T_{2a} \land F_{2b}$, with $T_{2a} = c > d$ and $F_{2b} = e \le f$

**Semantic Actions Applied (in evaluation order):**

1. For `E1 = a == b`: emit `if a == b goto _` and `goto _`; record `E1.truelist` and `E1.falselist`.
2. For `T2a = c > d`: emit `if c > d goto _` and `goto _`; record `T2a.truelist` and `T2a.falselist`.
3. For `F2b = e <= f`: emit `if e <= f goto _` and `goto _`; record `F2b.truelist` and `F2b.falselist`.
4. Apply **AND rule**: `backpatch(T2a.truelist, F2b.start)`. `E2.truelist = F2b.truelist`; `E2.falselist = merge(T2a.falselist, F2b.falselist)`.
5. Apply **OR rule**: `backpatch(E1.falselist, E2.start)`. `E.truelist = merge(E1.truelist, E2.truelist)`; `E.falselist = E2.falselist`.
6. Emit `S1` (`x = y + z`) and `S2` (`p = q - r`).
7. `backpatch(E.truelist, S1.start)`, `backpatch(E.falselist, S2.start)`, emit `goto S.nextlist` after `S1`.

> Award: 3 marks for grammar productions, 2 marks for parse-tree description, 2 marks for the listed semantic actions in correct order. **Total: 7 marks.**

#### (b) Generate the complete TAC step-by-step with `nextquad` values starting at 100, and show all backpatching calls. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — TAC Generation Trace:**

| `nextquad` | TAC Emitted | Attribute Updates |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | `if a == b goto _` | `E1.truelist = makelist(100) = {100}` |
| 101 | `goto _` | `E1.falselist = makelist(101) = {101}` |
| 102 | `if c > d goto _` | `T2a.truelist = makelist(102) = {102}` |
| 103 | `goto _` | `T2a.falselist = makelist(103) = {103}` |
| 104 | `if e <= f goto _` | `F2b.truelist = makelist(104) = {104}` |
| 105 | `goto _` | `F2b.falselist = makelist(105) = {105}` |

**Apply `&&` rule (T2a AND F2b):**

- `backpatch(T2a.truelist = {102}, 104)` → instruction at 102 becomes `if c > d goto 104`
- `E2.truelist = F2b.truelist = {104}`
- `E2.falselist = merge({103}, {105}) = {103, 105}`

**Apply `||` rule (E1 OR E2):**

- `backpatch(E1.falselist = {101}, 102)` → instruction at 101 becomes `goto 102`
- `E.truelist = merge({100}, {104}) = {100, 104}`
- `E.falselist = E2.falselist = {103, 105}`

| 106 | `goto _` | Inserted as `S.nextlist` placeholder after S1 (emitted via marker) |
| 107 | `t1 = y + z` | S1: then-body |
| 108 | `goto _` | Skip-jump (added to S.nextlist) |
| 109 | `t2 = q - r` | S2: else-body |
| 110 | — | — |

Wait, re-ordering — `S1` code must come immediately. Let us renumber cleanly assuming backpatch happens at the end:

**Final Sequence After Code Emission and Backpatching:**

| `nextquad` | Final TAC | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 100 | `if a == b goto 107` | Patched: `E1.truelist` → start of S1 |
| 101 | `goto 102` | Patched: `E1.falselist` → start of E2 |
| 102 | `if c > d goto 104` | Patched: `T2a.truelist` → start of F2b |
| 103 | `goto 110` | Patched: in `E.falselist` → S2 start |
| 104 | `if e <= f goto 107` | Patched: in `E.truelist` → S1 start |
| 105 | `goto 110` | Patched: in `E.falselist` → S2 start |
| 107 | `t1 = y + z` | S1 body |
| 108 | `x = t1` | Assignment |
| 109 | `goto 112` | Skip S2 (added to S.nextlist) |
| 110 | `t2 = q - r` | S2 body |
| 111 | `p = t2` | Assignment |
| 112 | (next statement) | L_END |

**Backpatching Calls Summary (for the examiner's perusal):**

1. `backpatch({102}, 104)` — resolves AND.
2. `backpatch({101}, 102)` — resolves OR.
3. `backpatch({100, 104}, 107)` — true branch → S1.
4. `backpatch({103, 105}, 110)` — false branch → S2.
5. `backpatch({109}, 112)` — skip-jump after S1 → L_END.

> Award: 2 marks for the TAC trace table, 3 marks for backpatching call sequence, 2 marks for the final resolved TAC. **Total: 7 marks.**

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Supplementary 2024]`

**For the following C program fragment, generate Three-Address Code using the backpatching technique. Show all `nextquad` values, `truelist` / `falselist` updates, and the final resolved TAC.**

```c
while (m < n && p != 0) {
    if (m > p)
        m = m - p;
    else
        n = n - m;
}
```

#### (a) Write the SDD (Syntax-Directed Definition) productions for `while E do S1` and `if-then-else` with all attribute semantics. Explain how the `S.begin` marker is used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Productions with Semantic Actions:**

$$ S \rightarrow \textbf{while } M \; E \; \textbf{ do } S_1 $$

Semantic actions:
- `M.quad = nextquad` (the marker M captures the address at which loop code starts)
- `backpatch(S1.nextlist, M.quad)` — after body, jump back to start of `E`
- `S.nextlist = E.falselist` — false exit from `E` skips the loop
- `backpatch(E.truelist, M.quad + 1)` — wait, this is `S1.start`; more precisely: `backpatch(E.truelist, S1.start)`

For the marker `M`:
- `M` is a non-terminal that does not consume any input; it is a *synthetic marker* whose only role is to remember the current `nextquad` value.
- `M.quad` is an **inherited attribute** that propagates the loop-start address to `backpatch` calls inside the `while` rule.

**If-then-else SDD:**

$$ S \rightarrow \textbf{if } E \textbf{ then } S_1 \textbf{ else } S_2 $$

- `backpatch(E.truelist, S1.start)`
- `backpatch(E.falselist, S2.start)`
- `S.nextlist = merge(S1.nextlist, S2.nextlist)`
- Additionally, emit `goto S.nextlist` at the end of `S1` (or use a marker `N` to capture the end of `S1` and push its index into a list to be backpatched to the end of the entire if-then-else).

> Award: 3 marks for productions, 2 marks for marker `M` usage, 2 marks for if-then-else SDD. **Total: 7 marks.**

#### (b) Generate the complete TAC with `nextquad` starting at 200. Show all backpatch operations explicitly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — TAC Trace:**

| `nextquad` | TAC | Attribute |
|---|---|---|
| 200 | *(M.quad = 200 — loop start)* | — |
| 201 | `if m < n goto _` | `E1.truelist = {201}` |
| 202 | `goto _` | `E1.falselist = {202}` |
| 203 | `if p != 0 goto _` | `E2.truelist = {203}` |
| 204 | `goto _` | `E2.falselist = {204}` |
| 205 | `if m > p goto _` | inner `E.truelist = {205}` |
| 206 | `goto _` | inner `E.falselist = {206}` |
| 207 | `t1 = m - p` | S1 body of inner if |
| 208 | `m = t1` | — |
| 209 | `goto _` | skip S2 of inner if |
| 210 | `t2 = n - m` | S2 body of inner if |
| 211 | `n = t2` | — |
| 212 | `goto 200` | jump back to loop start (while body end) |
| 213 | *(loop exit)* | — |

**Backpatching Sequence:**

1. `backpatch({202}, 203)` — resolve short-circuit: `E1.falselist` → start of E2.
2. `backpatch({201}, 203)` — resolve AND: `E1.truelist` → start of E2 (so AND becomes correct semantics). Wait — for `&&`, `backpatch(E1.truelist, E2.start)`. Here `E1 = (m < n)`, `E2 = (p != 0)`, so `backpatch({201}, 203)` is correct.
3. Outer `E` for while: `E.truelist = {203}`; `E.falselist = {202, 204}`. Apply `merge({202}, {204}) = {202, 204}`.
4. `backpatch({205}, 207)` — inner if true → S1.
5. `backpatch({206}, 210)` — inner if false → S2.
6. `backpatch({209}, 213)` — skip-jump after S1 → past S2.
7. After body `S1` of while: emit `goto 200` (loop back).
8. `backpatch({203}, 205)` — while true → inner if.
9. `backpatch({202, 204}, 213)` — while false → loop exit.
10. `backpatch({212}, 200)` — body's `goto` to loop start (already direct, no list).

**Final Resolved TAC:**

```
200:  if m < n goto 203
201:  (replaced — see 203)
202:  goto 213            <-- part of while falselist
203:  if p != 0 goto 205  <-- merged truelist
204:  goto 213            <-- part of while falselist
205:  if m > p goto 207
206:  goto 210
207:  t1 = m - p
208:  m = t1
209:  goto 213
210:  t2 = n - m
211:  n = t2
212:  goto 200
213:  (next statement after while)
```

> Award: 2 marks for the trace, 3 marks for the backpatching call list, 2 marks for the final resolved TAC. **Total: 7 marks.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE — Module 4 (Conditional Execution):**
> 1. **Forgetting the unconditional `goto` after `S1` in if-then-else** (–1 to –2 marks). Without it, control falls through into `S2` even when `E` is true. This is the **#1 most common mistake**.
> 2. **Mixing up `truelist` and `falselist` semantics** in the AND / OR rules (–2 marks). Memorize: in `E1 && E2`, `E1.truelist` is patched to `E2.start`; in `E1 || E2`, `E1.falselist` is patched to `E2.start`.
> 3. **Omitting `nextquad` numbering** in the TAC trace (–1 mark). The KTU marking scheme *explicitly awards marks* for correctly numbered instructions.
> 4. **Not showing backpatching calls as a separate step** (–1 mark). Examiners want to see the explicit `backpatch(L, addr)` invocations, not just the final TAC.
> 5. **Confusing short-circuit translation with evaluation of arithmetic expressions** (–2 marks). Boolean expressions use **jumps**, not arithmetic `0/1` values (in the backpatching scheme).
> 6. **Skipping the `M` marker explanation** in `while` translation (–1 mark). The `S.begin` concept is a *favorite* of KTU examiners.
> 7. **Wrong precedence handling** in compound Boolean expressions (e.g., `a || b && c`) (–2 marks). Always parse `&&` before `||`; this affects the order of `truelist`/`falselist` merging.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 / Conditional Execution:**

- ☐ **Two translation methods exist:** (1) Marker non-terminals (two-pass) and (2) Backpatching (single-pass). KTU tests the **backpatching** method predominantly.
- ☐ **Three helper functions to memorize verbatim:** `makelist(i)`, `merge(p1, p2)`, `backpatch(p, i)`.
- ☐ **Four attribute lists to track:** `E.truelist`, `E.falselist`, `S.nextlist`, and the inherited `M.quad` (marker).
- ☐ **AND rule (`&&`):** `backpatch(E1.truelist, E2.start)`; `E.truelist = E2.truelist`; `E.falselist = merge(E1.falselist, E2.falselist)`.
- ☐ **OR rule (`||`):** `backpatch(E1.falselist, E2.start)`; `E.truelist = merge(E1.truelist, E2.truelist)`; `E.falselist = E2.falselist`.
- ☐ **NOT rule (`!`):** Swap `E.truelist` and `E.falselist` of the operand.
- ☐ **Relational (`a relop b`):** Emit two instructions: `if a relop b goto _` then `goto _`. Both are added to `truelist` and `falselist` respectively via `makelist`.
- ☐ **`if E then S1`:** `S.nextlist = E.falselist`; `backpatch(E.truelist, S1.start)`.
- ☐ **`if E then S1 else S2`:** `S.nextlist = merge(S1.nextlist, S2.nextlist)`; insert a `goto` at end of `S1` to bypass `S2`; backpatch `E.truelist` to `S1.start` and `E.falselist` to `S2.start`.
- ☐ **`while E do S1`:** `S.begin = M.quad`; `S.nextlist = E.falselist`; `backpatch(S1.nextlist, M.quad)`; `backpatch(E.truelist, S1.start)`.
- ☐ **Short-circuit evaluation** is the assumed semantics — never generate code that *always* evaluates both operands of `&&` or `||`.
- ☐ **Three-Address Code (TAC)** is the canonical IR. Each instruction has at most one operator and three operands (two sources + one destination, or one source + label).
- ☐ **KTU mark split for 14-mark Q:** ~7 marks for grammar/SDD/parse-tree + ~7 marks for the TAC trace + backpatching sequence + final resolved code.
- ☐ **Common operator precedence:** `!` > `&&` > `||` > relational. Affects the parse tree and hence the order of attribute updates.
- ☐ **Skip-jump after `S1` in if-then-else** is essential — do not omit it under any circumstance.
- ☐ **Reference textbook:** Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman — *Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools (Dragon Book)*, 2nd Edition, Section 6.6 (Translation of Expressions and Assignment Statements) and Section 6.7 (Translation of Control-Flow Statements).
- ☐ **GATE/placement bonus:** The same backpatching technique is used in `for` loops, `do-while` loops, and `switch-case` constructs — once you master `if-then-else` and `while`, the others follow by analogy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Loops and Iteration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Compiler Construction (PBCRT604)**, "Loops and Iteration" refers to the suite of **Loop Optimizations** and **Iteration-Based Code Transformations** performed by the optimizer phase of a compiler. Loops are the **dominant execution regions** of a program — studies show that the inner loops of scientific programs execute up to **90% of the total runtime**, making them the highest-priority targets for performance improvement.

A **loop** in intermediate code (typically three-address code / TAC) is a strongly connected subgraph in the control flow graph (CFG) where one or more **back edges** exist from a node to a dominating ancestor. **Iteration** refers to the repeated execution of these loop bodies, during which redundant computations, memory accesses, and arithmetic operations can be algebraically simplified or hoisted out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Code Optimization):**
> Loops and Iteration covers the identification of natural loops, dominator-based analysis, induction variables, strength reduction, loop-invariant code motion, and loop transformation techniques (unrolling, fusion, fission, tiling, interchange).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **loop** as a **factory assembly line** that processes the same product 1,000,000 times. An unskilled manager would let workers re-fetch the same toolbox on every iteration, re-read the same instruction manual, and re-apply the same paint mixture. A **skilled optimizer-manager** (the compiler) would:
- Take the toolbox once and place it at the workstation (**Loop-Invariant Code Motion**).
- Replace "multiply by 2" with a faster "add by itself" operation (**Strength Reduction**).
- Duplicate the line 2 times and process 4 products at once (**Loop Unrolling**).
- Combine two parallel product lines into one (**Loop Fusion**).

The product output remains identical, but throughput dramatically increases.

### 1.3 Why Loops are the Heart of Optimization

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Execution time spent in inner loops | **~90%** |
| Speedup from loop optimizations | **2× to 10×** |
| Code size overhead (unrolled) | **+10% to +40%** |

> [!NOTE]
> A compiler identifies a loop as **natural** if it has a **single entry node** (header) and at least one back edge. This structured definition enables systematic analysis using **Dominator Trees**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Loop Representation in the Compiler

A loop in the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** is characterized by:
1. A **header node (h)** — the unique entry of the loop.
2. One or more **back edges** $(n \rightarrow h)$ where $n$ dominates $h$ is **false** (i.e., $n$ is inside the loop).
3. The **set of nodes in the loop L** = all nodes that can reach $n$ without passing through $h$.

The **loop nesting depth** $d(L_i)$ governs the priority of optimization:
$$\text{Priority}(L) \propto \frac{\text{Execution Count}(L)}{\text{Code Size}(L)} \cdot \frac{1}{d(L)}$$

### 2.2 The Five Pillars of Loop Optimization

1. **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** — hoist computations that produce the same value on every iteration.
2. **Induction Variable Analysis & Strength Reduction** — replace expensive operations (multiplication, exponentiation) with cheap ones (addition).
3. **Loop Unrolling** — replicate the body to reduce branch overhead and expose ILP.
4. **Loop Fusion (Jamming)** — merge adjacent loops with identical iteration spaces.
5. **Loop Fission (Distribution) & Interchange** — split or permute nested loops to improve cache locality and vectorization.

### 2.3 Dominator-Based Loop Identification (Step Logic)

- **Step 1:** Identify the natural loops in the CFG using back edges.
- **Step 2:** Compute the **Dominator Set** $Dom(n) = \{ m \mid \text{every path from Entry to } n \text{ passes through } m \}$.
- **Step 3:** The header $h$ of a back edge $(n \rightarrow h)$ must dominate $n$? **No, wait** — actually $h$ must be in $Dom(n)$? No. The proper rule: a back edge is one where the target dominates the source in the *reverse* graph, OR equivalently, $h$ is in $Dom(n)$ for the edge to be classified as a back edge by the standard definition.
- **Step 4:** Mark the loop body via reverse DFS from $n$ stopping at $h$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Loop nesting depth | $d(L) = 1 + \max_{L_{child} \subset L} d(L_{child})$ | Prioritizes inner-loop optimization |
| Induction variable recurrence | $x_{i+1} = x_i + c$, where $c$ is loop-invariant | Foundation of strength reduction |
| Strength reduction (mult → add) | $a \cdot i \rightarrow a_0 + i \cdot c$ where $a_0 = a, c = a$ | Replaces costly multiplications |
| Loop unrolled trip count | $N' = \frac{N}{k}$, body replicated $k$ times | Reduces branch overhead by factor $k$ |
| LICM condition | $op$ has no side effects AND all reaching defs are outside $L$ | Safe code motion criterion |
| Trip count | $T = \left\lceil \frac{\text{upper} - \text{lower}}{\text{step}} \right\rceil$ | Determines unroll factor |
| Cache miss reduction (fusion) | $M_{fused} \approx \frac{M_1 + M_2}{2}$ | Approximates combined working set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** When writing absolute values or set cardinality in derivations, **never** use the bare pipe `|x|`. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` to prevent LaTeX/markdown collisions with table cell delimiters.

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

Loop optimizations are deployed in:
- **GCC / LLVM** with flags `-O2`, `-O3`, `-funroll-loops`, `-floop-interchange`.
- **BLAS / LAPACK** numerical libraries (Fortran-style loop transformations).
- **GPU kernels** (CUDA) where unrolling, tiling, and interchange are essential.
- **Embedded DSP firmware** where strength reduction drastically cuts power consumption.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Strength Reduction of an Induction Variable

Consider the canonical loop:
```c
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    A[i] = B[i] + x;
}
```

In three-address code, the array access `A[i]` is computed as base address plus offset. Let $a_1$ be the base of A. The access becomes:
$$t_1 = 4 \cdot i \quad \text{(byte offset)}$$
$$t_2 = a_1 + t_1$$
$$t_3 = \ldots$$

The multiplication $4 \cdot i$ is **redundant** on every iteration.

**Step 1 — Identify the induction variable.**
We observe that $i$ is a basic induction variable satisfying:
$$i_{i+1} = i_i + 1$$

**Step 2 — Derive a derived induction variable $j$.**
Define $j$ such that $j_i = 4 \cdot i_i$. Then:
$$j_{i+1} = 4 \cdot i_{i+1} = 4 \cdot (i_i + 1) = 4 \cdot i_i + 4 = j_i + 4$$

So $j$ also progresses by a **loop-invariant increment** $c = 4$.

**Step 3 — Initialize $j$ outside the loop.**
Before the loop, set $j_0 = 4 \cdot i_0 = 0$ (since $i_0 = 0$).

**Step 4 — Replace the multiplication with an addition.**
The original code:
$$\begin{aligned}
i &= i + 1 \\
t_1 &= 4 \cdot i \\
t_2 &= a_1 + t_1
\end{aligned}$$
becomes:
$$\begin{aligned}
i &= i + 1 \\
j &= j + 4 \\
t_2 &= a_1 + j
\end{aligned}$$

This eliminates **one multiplication per iteration**, replaced by a cheaper addition.

### 3.2 Derivation: Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) Conditions

For a definition $d: u = v \;\text{op}\; w$ inside loop $L$ to be moved to the **preheader** $P$, **all** of the following must hold:

1. $d$ **dominates all uses** $u$ in the loop.
2. The block containing $d$ **post-dominates** all loop exits (or $d$ is dead on exit).
3. No operation in $L$ that may execute before $d$ writes to $v$ or $w$.
4. No memory write to $v$ or $w$ occurs in $L$ prior to the use.

**Mathematical formulation of condition (1):**
$$\forall u \in \text{Uses}(d):\; d \in Dom(u) \;\land\; d.\text{block} \in L$$

**Mathematical formulation of condition (3):**
$$\nexists\; m \in L : m \text{ writes to } v \lor w \;\land\; m \prec d \text{ in execution order}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Loop Unrolling Cost-Benefit

For a loop with trip count $T$ and unroll factor $k$, let:
- $B$ = branch overhead per iteration (cycles).
- $C_b$ = body cost per iteration (cycles).

**Original cost (per loop invocation):**
$$C_{orig} = T \cdot (C_b + B) + B_{exit}$$

**Unrolled cost:**
$$C_{unroll} = \frac{T}{k} \cdot (k \cdot C_b + B) + B_{exit} = T \cdot C_b + \frac{T}{k} \cdot B + B_{exit}$$

**Savings:**
$$\Delta C = T \cdot B - \frac{T}{k} \cdot B = T \cdot B \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right)$$

**Speedup ratio:**
$$S_k = \frac{C_{orig}}{C_{unroll}} = \frac{T \cdot C_b + T \cdot B}{T \cdot C_b + \frac{T \cdot B}{k}}$$

**Asymptotic speedup (when $C_b \ll B$):**
$$\lim_{C_b \to 0} S_k = k$$

So in branch-bound loops, **unrolling by $k$ yields up to $k$-fold speedup** (ignoring I-cache pressure).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Apply Each Optimization

| Optimization | Best For | Risk | Hardware Sensitivity |
|---|---|---|---|
| LICM | Redundant calls in hot loops | Aliasing | Low |
| Strength Reduction | Array index multiplications | Code bloat | Low |
| Unrolling | Branch-bound, trip count known | I-cache miss | High |
| Fusion | Consecutive loops on same data | Register pressure | Medium |
| Fission | Conflict misses in single loop | Worse locality | Medium |
| Interchange | Memory stride mismatch | Wrong behavior if data-dep | High |
| Tiling | Cache-bound matrix kernels | Tile size tuning | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Loop Optimization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source IR / Three Address Code] --> B[Build Control Flow Graph]
    B --> C[Compute Dominator Tree]
    C --> D[Identify Natural Loops]
    D --> E[Compute Loop Nesting Forest]
    E --> F{Loop Identified?}
    F -->|No| G[Skip Optimization]
    F -->|Yes| H[Induction Variable Analysis]
    H --> I[Strength Reduction Pass]
    I --> J[LICM Pass]
    J --> K[Loop Transformation Pass]
    K --> L[Unrolling / Fusion / Fission / Interchange]
    L --> M[Optimized IR Output]
    H --> N[Derived IV Table]
    N --> I
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Strength Reduction Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIG[Original Loop Body]
        A1[Increment i] --> A2[Multiply 4 times i]
        A2 --> A3[Add to base address]
        A3 --> A4[Memory access]
    end
    subgraph OPT[Optimized Loop Body]
        B1[Increment i] --> B2[Increment j by 4]
        B2 --> B3[Add j to base address]
        B3 --> B4[Memory access]
    end
    ORIG --> TRANS[Strength Reduction Pass]
    TRANS --> OPT
    PRE[Preheader initializes j = 0] -.-> B2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: LICM Preheader Insertion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BEFORE[Before LICM]
        P1[Preheader] --> H1[Loop Header]
        H1 --> B1[Body Block with Invariant Code]
        B1 --> LATCH1[Loop Latch]
        LATCH1 --> H1
    end
    subgraph AFTER[After LICM]
        P2[Preheader with Hoisted Code] --> H2[Loop Header]
        H2 --> B2[Reduced Body]
        B2 --> LATCH2[Loop Latch]
        LATCH2 --> H2
    end
    BEFORE --> LICM[LICM Algorithm] --> AFTER
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams emphasize the **architectural data flow** of the optimization passes rather than physical layout, in line with KTU's block-schematic expectations for compiler internals questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a natural loop in a control flow graph. What is the role of a loop header?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A natural loop in a CFG is a set of nodes $L$ that has a **single entry node called the header $h$**, and there exists at least one **back edge** $(n \rightarrow h)$ such that $n \in L$. The header $h$ is the unique node outside the loop through which all control enters the loop; it dominates all other nodes in $L$. The header role is critical because it provides a single hoisting point for **LICM** and a single point for induction variable initialization during **strength reduction**. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is loop-invariant code? List two conditions under which it can be safely moved out of a loop.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A computation is **loop-invariant** if all its operands are either constants or have reaching definitions that lie **outside the loop body**. Two conditions for safe motion: **(1)** The operation must not have any side effects (e.g., no memory writes, no function calls with unknown behavior). **(2)** The definition must dominate all its uses in the loop, and no other definition of the same variable exists inside the loop on any path. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **strength reduction** optimization technique for induction variables with a suitable example. Derive the transformed code. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Loop Unrolling** as an optimization. For a loop of trip count $T$, body cost $C_b$, and branch overhead $B$ per iteration, derive the **speedup formula** and discuss its limits. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Strength Reduction (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Strength reduction is an optimization that replaces an **expensive operation** (e.g., multiplication, exponentiation) inside a loop with a **cheaper equivalent** (e.g., addition, shift) by leveraging the recurrence structure of induction variables.

**Example (3 marks):** Consider
```c
int A[100];
for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
    A[i] = B[i] + x;
}
```
The array offset $4 \cdot i$ is recomputed every iteration. Recognize $i$ as a **basic induction variable** with $i_{k+1} = i_k + 1$. Introduce a **derived induction variable** $j_k = 4 \cdot i_k$ with recurrence $j_{k+1} = j_k + 4$.

**Transformed TAC (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Initialize: } j_0 = 0 \\
&\text{For each iteration:} \\
&\quad i = i + 1 \\
&\quad j = j + 4 \\
&\quad t_1 = a_1 + j \\
&\quad t_2 = \text{load}(b_1 + j) \\
&\quad \text{store}(t_2 + x, t_1)
\end{aligned}$$

This eliminates one multiplication per iteration.

---

**Part (b) — Loop Unrolling (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** Loop unrolling replicates the loop body $k$ times and reduces the trip count by factor $k$. It decreases branch overhead, exposes instruction-level parallelism, and enables further optimizations like constant propagation across iterations.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Let $T$ = trip count, $C_b$ = body cost, $B$ = branch overhead.
$$C_{orig} = T(C_b + B) + B_{exit}$$
$$C_{unroll} = \frac{T}{k}(k \cdot C_b + B) + B_{exit} = T \cdot C_b + \frac{T \cdot B}{k} + B_{exit}$$
$$S_k = \frac{T(C_b + B)}{T \cdot C_b + \frac{TB}{k}} = \frac{C_b + B}{C_b + \frac{B}{k}}$$

**Limits discussion (1 mark):** As $k \to \infty$, $S_k \to 1 + \frac{B}{C_b}$. Beyond a threshold, code size growth causes **instruction cache thrashing**, increasing fetch latency. Practical unroll factors: $k = 2, 4, 8$ for general code; $k = 16$ or more for hot DSP kernels with small bodies.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** with the precise conditions required for safe motion. Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Loop Fusion (Jamming)** and **Loop Fission (Distribution)**. State the conditions under which each transformation is profitable, and show a transformation example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — LICM (7 marks):**

**Algorithm steps (3 marks):**
1. Identify the natural loop $L$ with header $h$ and preheader $P$.
2. For each definition $d: u = v \;\text{op}\; w$ inside $L$, check if all reaching definitions of $v$ and $w$ are outside $L$.
3. If so, mark $d$ as loop-invariant.
4. For each marked $d$, verify safety conditions, then move $d$ to $P$.

**Safety conditions (3 marks):**
- $d$ has no side effects (no volatile, no I/O, no memory write).
- $d$ dominates all its uses in the loop.
- No redefinition of $v$ or $w$ occurs in $L$ before any use of $d$.
- The block containing $d$ post-dominates all loop exits (or $d$ is dead at exit).

**Example (1 mark):**
```c
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    t = 10 * x;       // LOOP INVARIANT — hoist
    A[i] = t + i;
}
```
After LICM:
```c
t = 10 * x;           // moved to preheader
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    A[i] = t + i;
}
```

---

**Part (b) — Loop Fusion & Fission (7 marks):**

**Loop Fusion (Jamming) (3 marks):** Combines two adjacent loops with **identical iteration spaces** into one, improving data locality and reducing loop overhead.
**Conditions:** Same loop variable, same bounds, same step, no inter-iteration data dependency from the first to the second.

**Example fusion:**
```c
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) A[i] = B[i] + 1;   // Loop 1
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) C[i] = A[i] * 2;   // Loop 2
```
becomes:
```c
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    A[i] = B[i] + 1;
    C[i] = A[i] * 2;
}
```

**Loop Fission (Distribution) (3 marks):** Splits a single loop with **independent statement groups** into multiple loops to reduce register pressure and enable vectorization.

**Example fission:**
```c
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    A[i] = B[i] + 1;
    X[i] = Y[i] * 2;   // independent group
}
```
becomes:
```c
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) A[i] = B[i] + 1;
for (i = 0; i < N; i++) X[i] = Y[i] * 2;
```

**Profitability note (1 mark):** Fuse to improve locality when arrays fit in cache. Distribute to enable vectorization or when one group causes many spills.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Loops and Iteration:**
> 1. **Forgetting the preheader requirement:** LICM is only valid if a preheader exists. The compiler must *insert* one if missing; many students skip this step and lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing basic and derived induction variables:** A basic IV has a single in-loop definition of form $i = i + c$. Derived IVs are defined in terms of other IVs. Mixing these up costs **1–2 marks** in Part (a).
> 3. **Ignoring aliasing in LICM:** If a pointer may alias a loop-invariable memory reference, you **cannot** hoist. Always state the no-alias assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Wrong fusion conditions:** Fusing loops with **different bounds** is **illegal** — common mistake in exams.
> 5. **Unrolling: forgetting remainder handling.** When $T$ is not divisible by $k$, a peel loop is required. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** if you ignore this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **natural loop** has a unique **header** and at least one **back edge** in the CFG.
- **LICM** is safe only when the operation is side-effect free, dominates all uses, and has no redefining writes inside the loop.
- **Strength reduction** replaces $a \cdot i$ with a derived induction variable $j$ satisfying $j = j + a$ — multiplication becomes addition.
- **Induction variables** are variables whose values form an arithmetic progression across iterations: $x_{i+1} = x_i + c$.
- **Loop unrolling** replicates the body $k$ times; speedup is bounded by $1 + \frac{B}{C_b}$ as $k \to \infty$.
- **Loop fusion** merges adjacent loops with identical iteration spaces; **fission** splits one loop into multiple to relieve register pressure.
- **Loop interchange** swaps nesting levels to match the array's memory layout (improves spatial locality).
- **Loop tiling (blocking)** partitions the iteration space into cache-sized chunks for matrix kernels.
- Always verify **trip count** before applying unrolling; otherwise, use a peel loop.
- The **dominator tree** is the foundational data structure for natural loop detection.
- **Reductions** (sum, max, dot product) also have induction-like behavior and can be parallelized via **reduction recognition**.
- For KTU exams, draw the **CFG with back edges marked** whenever discussing loop identification — this fetches full marks in Part (a) of strength reduction questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PBCRT604 - Compiler Construction (Module 4: Intermediate Code Generation)**, a **Case Statement** (also called a **Switch Statement**) is a multi-way selection control structure that dispatches control flow to one of several statement bodies based on the value of a single **selector expression**. From a compiler's perspective, the problem is to translate this single high-level construct into efficient **three-address code (TAC)** using one of several established strategies: sequential `if-then-else` chains, **jump tables**, **binary search trees**, or **backpatching-based single-pass translation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PBCRT604 - Module 4):**
> Translation of **switch / case** statements is a mandatory topic under *Intermediate Code Generation*. It is tested regularly in University Examinations (ESE) under Part B (14 marks), usually combined with questions on procedure calls or array addressing.

The generic syntax in C-style pseudocode used by compiler textbooks is:

```c
switch (E) {
    case V1 : S1
    case V2 : S2
    ...
    case Vn-1 : Sn-1
    default : Sn
}
```

where:
- **E** is the **selector expression** (must be of an *ordinal* type — integer, character, or enumerated).
- **V1, V2, ..., Vn−1** are distinct **case labels** (constant integer values).
- **S1, S2, ..., Sn** are the corresponding **statement bodies**.
- **Sn** is the **default** branch (executed when no case value matches).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compiler-Designer's Concern:** The cases are typically values drawn from an *ordered* domain (e.g., 0, 1, 2, … 100). A naive translation emits one `if (E == Vi) goto Li` per case. This is *O(n)* at runtime. The real art is choosing an *efficient dispatch strategy* — jump table, binary search, or hybrid — depending on the **density** and **range** of case constants.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **case statement** like the **reception desk of a large hotel**:
- The **receptionist** (the compiler) is given a single piece of information: your *room number* (the value of the selector expression `E`).
- The receptionist has to *route* you to one of 500 rooms.
- There are three possible strategies:

  1. **Linear Search (if-else chain):** The receptionist checks a list one by one: *"Are you in room 101? No. 102? No. 103? No... 175? Yes!"* — Slow when the list is long.
  2. **Binary Search (tree of comparisons):** The receptionist splits the list in halves: *"Are you in the lower half or upper half of room numbers?"* then splits again — Faster, *O(log n)*.
  3. **Jump Table (direct lookup):** The receptionist has a wall of pigeonholes numbered 101 to 500. He looks at pigeonhole *E* and finds a slip of paper pointing to your exact room. *O(1)* — but wastes wall space if rooms are sparse.

The compiler must pick the best strategy automatically, balancing **execution speed** vs. **code size**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world examples:** The `switch` keyword in C/C++/Java, the `match` statement in Rust, the `case` blocks in Pascal, and the `when` expressions in Kotlin all rely on exactly these compiler techniques.

---

## 1.3 Why Case Statements Matter in Compilers

Case statements are a **canonical example** in compiler textbooks (Aho/Sethi/Ullman) because they:

- Force the student to master **control-flow translation** beyond simple `if-else`.
- Illustrate the **trade-off between space and time** — a key theme in code optimization.
- Connect directly to **three-address code (TAC)**, the *lingua franca* of intermediate representation.
- Demonstrate **backpatching**, which solves the *forward-reference problem* in a single pass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical & Analytical Constants to Remember (KTU Board Expectation):**
> - **Space cost of a jump table** = *range of case values* (number of entries from `Vmin` to `Vmax`).
> - **Time cost** = *1* (direct jump) vs. *O(log n)* (binary search) vs. *O(n)* (linear).
> - **Label generation rule** = one new temporary label per case body + 1 for default + 1 for exit.
> - **Number of generated labels for `n` cases** = **n + 1** (default) + 1 (next) = **n + 2**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Standard Translation Strategies

The KTU Board typically tests **all four** strategies, sometimes asking the student to **compare** them. They are listed below in increasing order of sophistication.

### Strategy 1 — Sequential `if-then-else` (Linear Search)

Translate the switch into a chain of comparisons. **Always works**, no matter how sparse the cases are. Cost: *O(n)* comparisons at runtime, *O(n)* code size.

Pseudo-code generated:

```
    t = E
    if t ≠ V1 goto L1
    goto L1
L1: code for S1
    goto next
    if t ≠ V2 goto L2
    goto L2
L2: code for S2
    goto next
    ...
    if t ≠ Vn-1 goto Ln-1
    goto Ln-1
Ln-1: code for Sn-1
    goto next
Ln: code for Sn
next:
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The unconditional jumps inside the chain (`goto L1`, `goto L2`, …) are slightly redundant; a more efficient version just falls through to the case body. The textbook pattern is to emit *two labels per case*: a "test" label and a "body" label.

### Strategy 2 — Jump Table (Direct Lookup)

Used when the case values form a **dense, contiguous range** of integers (e.g., 0, 1, 2, … 9).

Pseudo-code:

```
    goto test
L0: code for S0; goto next
L1: code for S1; goto next
...
L9: code for S9; goto next
Ld: code for Sd  (default)
next:
test: t = E
    if t < 0   goto Ld
    if t > 9   goto Ld
    goto table[t]
table: L0, L1, L2, ..., L9
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost analysis:** Runtime = *O(1)* (one bounds check + one indirect jump). Code size = *O(Vmax − Vmin + 1)* regardless of how many cases actually exist. **Wastes memory** if the range is wide and sparse.

### Strategy 3 — Binary Search Tree (Sparse Case Set)

Used when case values are **sparse but ordered** (e.g., 0, 100, 1000, 10000). The compiler builds a **binary decision tree** of comparisons.

Pseudo-code:

```
    t = E
    if t < 100   goto L1
    if t > 100   goto L2
    goto L100        // t == 100
L1:  if t < 10   goto L10_default
    goto L1000
L2:  if t < 10000 goto L10000
    goto Ldefault
L10: code for S10
...
```

Runtime = *O(log n)*, Code size = *O(n)*. **Best compromise** for sparse cases.

### Strategy 4 — Backpatching-Based Single-Pass Translation (Wirth / Stefanovic)

This is the **most exam-favoured strategy** in KTU Module 4 because it ties together:
- Intermediate code generation
- Forward-reference resolution
- The concept of *instruction lists* with *patch lists*.

The idea:
1. **Pass 1:** Emit TAC where jumps go to *symbolic labels* whose addresses are not yet known. Maintain a *list* of instructions that need to be patched for each label.
2. **Pass 2 (or at end of Pass 1):** Walk the code, assign actual addresses, then *back-patch* all the jump instructions whose targets are now known.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick:** The Wirth algorithm uses the helper macros `makelist(i)`, `backpatch(p, L)`, and `merge(p1, p2)`. The student is often asked to **trace** these lists on a sample switch statement.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU ESE on Case Statements. Memorize it.**

| **Parameter** | **Linear (if-else)** | **Jump Table** | **Binary Search** | **Backpatching** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Runtime cost (per dispatch)** | $O(n)$ comparisons | $O(1)$ — 1 bounds-check + 1 indirect jump | $O(\log n)$ comparisons | Same as target strategy |
| **Code size (instructions)** | $O(n)$ | $O(V_{\max} - V_{\min} + 1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ + patch lists |
| **Density requirement** | None | Cases must be **dense** & contiguous | Cases must be **sparse but ordered** | None |
| **Worst-case for** | Sparse case sets (slow) | Wide sparse ranges (huge table) | Dense sets (wasted comparisons) | None — general |
| **Number of new labels** | $n + 2$ | $n + 2$ | $2n$ (internal + leaves) | $n + 2$ |
| **Patch-list size (per case)** | — | — | — | $\vert \text{jumps to that label} \vert$ |
| **Total patches** | — | — | — | $2n + 1$ |
| **Symbolic-label cost** | $n + 2$ | $n + 2$ | $2n$ | $n + 2$ |
| **Number of passes** | 1 (or 2 with backpatch) | 1 | 1 | **1** (with backpatch lists) |
| **Time to pick strategy** | — | $O(n)$ to scan cases | $O(n \log n)$ to build tree | — |

### Symbol Glossary (for KTU marking scheme)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $n$ | Number of case labels in the switch |
| $V_{\min}$ | Smallest case constant |
| $V_{\max}$ | Largest case constant |
| $\Delta$ | Range $= V_{\max} - V_{\min} + 1$ |
| $t$ | Temporary variable holding the selector expression |
| $L_i$ | Label for the $i^{\text{th}}$ case body |
| $L_d$ | Label for the default case |
| $L_{\text{next}}$ | Label for the code following the switch |
| $L_{\text{test}}$ | Label for the bounds-check / dispatch point |
| $\text{makelist}(i)$ | Creates a patch list containing instruction index $i$ |
| $\text{merge}(p_1, p_2)$ | Concatenates two patch lists into one |
| $\text{backpatch}(p, L)$ | Patches every instruction in list $p$ to jump to label $L$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** In the formula sheet table above, **never write absolute-value bars `|x|` inside a markdown table cell** — the pipe character breaks the table parser. Use the LaTeX command `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead. The KTU Board is strict about neatly formatted answer sheets.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **How Case-Statement Translation Matters** |
|---|---|
| **Embedded Systems Compilers (ARM, AVR)** | Emit jump tables for `switch` in interrupt handlers; must fit in flash memory. |
| **JIT Compilers (HotSpot, V8, LuaJIT)** | Decide at *runtime* whether to use jump table vs. binary search based on case density. |
| **HDL Compilers (Verilog/VHDL `case`)** | Hardware synthesis tools **must** generate a multiplexer — a jump table becomes a physical MUX circuit. |
| **Protocol Parsers (Network Stacks)** | `switch` over TCP flags / opcodes often uses jump tables for line-speed dispatch. |
| **SQL Query Optimizers** | `CASE WHEN … END` expressions are translated into branchless or branchful plans using the same trade-offs. |
