# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering a Switch: Worked Example Used Universally in KTU Past Papers**

Consider the following switch statement (the **canonical KTU 2024 example**):

```c
switch (x + 2) {
    case 1  : a = a + 1; break;
    case 2  : a = a + 2; break;
    case 5  : a = a + 3; break;
    case 8  : a = a + 4; break;
    default : a = a + 5;
}
```

We will translate this using **all four strategies**.

---

## 3.1 Strategy 1 — Derivation of Sequential `if-then-else` TAC

### Step 1: Evaluate selector into a temporary.

$$t_1 = x + 2$$

### Step 2: Emit a chain of conditional jumps, one per case.

For each case value $V_i$, emit:

$$\text{if } t_1 \neq V_i \text{ goto } L_i^{\text{next}}$$

For the **default** (no match), emit:

$$\text{goto } L_d$$

### Step 3: Emit the case bodies, each terminated by a goto `next`.

### Step 4: Define the labels.

**Final three-address code (Step 1 → Step 4 expanded):**

```text
        t1 = x + 2
        if t1 ≠ 1  goto L1
        t2 = a + 1
        a  = t2
        goto next
L1:     if t1 ≠ 2  goto L2
        t3 = a + 2
        a  = t3
        goto next
L2:     if t1 ≠ 5  goto L3
        t4 = a + 3
        a  = t4
        goto next
L3:     if t1 ≠ 8  goto Ld
        t5 = a + 4
        a  = t5
        goto next
Ld:     t6 = a + 5
        a  = t6
next:
```

### Step 5: Count the cost.

- **Number of conditional jumps** = $n = 4$ (where $n$ = number of case labels).
- **Number of labels** = $n + 2 = 6$ (L1, L2, L3, Ld, next, and the implicit entry).
- **Runtime per dispatch** = up to **4 comparisons** in the worst case → $O(n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of each step:** Step 1 isolates the selector because it must be evaluated **exactly once** (otherwise side-effects in `x + 2` would multiply). Step 2 sequences the dispatch. Step 3 sequences the bodies. Step 4 anchors the targets. The pattern is identical to the textbook Aho/Sethi/Ullman §6.7.

---

## 3.2 Strategy 2 — Derivation of Jump-Table TAC

### Step 1: Compute the range of case values.

$$V_{\min} = 1, \quad V_{\max} = 8, \quad \Delta = V_{\max} - V_{\min} + 1 = 8$$

The cases $\{1, 2, 5, 8\}$ are **not** dense (missing 3, 4, 6, 7). For pedagogical completeness, suppose the cases were $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$ — a fully dense range.

### Step 2: Emit the body labels in a contiguous block.

```text
L1:     t2 = a + 1;  a = t2;   goto next
L2:     t3 = a + 2;  a = t3;   goto next
L3:     t4 = a + 3;  a = t4;   goto next
L4:     t5 = a + 4;  a = t5;   goto next
L5:     t6 = a + 5;  a = t6;   goto next
L6:     t7 = a + 6;  a = t7;   goto next
L7:     t8 = a + 7;  a = t8;   goto next
L8:     t9 = a + 8;  a = t9;   goto next
Ld:     t10 = a + 9; a = t10;
next:
```

### Step 3: Emit the dispatch code (bounds-check + indirect jump).

```text
        goto test
L1:     ...
L2:     ...
...
L8:     ...
Ld:     ...
next:
test:   t1  = x + 2
        if t1 < 1   goto Ld
        if t1 > 8   goto Ld
        t11 = t1 - 1
        goto table[t11]
table:  L1, L2, L3, L4, L5, L6, L7, L8
```

### Step 4: Cost derivation.

$$
\text{Instructions in code body} = 9 \times 2 = 18 \text{ (excluding jumps)}
$$

$$
\text{Entries in table} = \Delta = 8
$$

$$
\text{Runtime} = 2 \text{ comparisons} + 1 \text{ indirect jump} = O(1)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why `t1 − 1`?** The selector returns values 1..8, but array/table indexing is 0-based, so we offset by $V_{\min} = 1$. The general formula is:

$$
\text{index} = t_1 - V_{\min}
$$

---

## 3.3 Strategy 3 — Derivation of Binary-Search TAC

### Step 1: Sort the case values.

$$
\{1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 8\}
$$

### Step 2: Pick the median as the root.

The median index in a sorted array of length 4 is $\lfloor (0+3)/2 \rfloor = 1$, so the value is $2$ (we use the lower median if even). A common alternative is to pick the **upper** median $5$ — both are valid; the choice is an optimization.

**Choose root = 2** (Case 2).

### Step 3: Recursively split.

- Left subtree: $\{1\}$ — single leaf, base case.
- Right subtree: $\{5, 8\}$ — pick median = $5$ (upper median of length 2 is the second element).
  - Left of $5$: empty.
  - Right of $5$: $\{8\}$ — single leaf.

### Step 4: Build the decision tree.

```text
                  [ t1 < 2 ? ]
                  /            \
               yes              no
              /                  \
      [ t1 == 1 ? ]         [ t1 < 5 ? ]
        /       \             /        \
      yes       no          yes         no
       |         |           |           |
      L1        L2      [ t1 == 5 ? ]  [ t1 < 8 ? ]
                                ...            ...
```

### Step 5: Emit the TAC.

```text
        t1 = x + 2
        if t1 < 2   goto L1_test
        if t1 > 2   goto L2_test
        goto L2_body           // t1 == 2
L1_test: if t1 ≠ 1  goto L5_test
        goto L1_body           // t1 == 1
L5_test: if t1 < 5  goto L8_test
        if t1 > 5  goto Ld
        goto L5_body           // t1 == 5
L8_test: if t1 ≠ 8  goto Ld
        goto L8_body           // t1 == 8
L1_body: t2 = a + 1;  a = t2;  goto next
L2_body: t3 = a + 2;  a = t3;  goto next
L5_body: t4 = a + 3;  a = t4;  goto next
L8_body: t5 = a + 4;  a = t5;  goto next
Ld:      t6 = a + 5;  a = t6;
next:
```

### Step 6: Cost derivation.

$$
\text{Max comparisons} = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil = \lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2
$$

$$
\text{Labels} = 2n = 8
$$

$$
\text{Code size} = O(n) \text{ instructions}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to choose a strategy, the textbook rule of thumb is: **use a jump table if the case set is dense and the range is moderate (e.g., < 100); use binary search if the set is sparse but ordered; fall back to linear search otherwise.** GCC and LLVM use exactly this heuristic.

---

## 3.4 Strategy 4 — Backpatching-Based Single-Pass Translation

This is the most rigorous derivation and is a **favourite KTU long question**.

### Helper Functions (defined first)

| Function | Definition |
|---|---|
| $\text{makelist}(i)$ | Return a new list containing only instruction index $i$. |
| $\text{merge}(p_1, p_2)$ | Concatenate lists $p_1$ and $p_2$ and return the head. |
| $\text{backpatch}(p, L)$ | For each instruction index $i$ in list $p$, set the target of the jump at $i$ to label $L$. |

### The Algorithm (one pass, emits 3-address code with symbolic labels)

Given a switch statement, process the case labels **left to right**. Maintain:

- A current **patch list** for the code that should jump to `next`.
- For each case $V_i$, a **patch list** $L_i^{\text{list}}$ of jump instructions that need to be patched to the body of case $i$.

The skeleton (universal pattern taught in KTU):

```text
        goto test
L1: code for S1
    goto next
L2: code for S2
    goto next
...
Ln: code for Sn       // default
next:
test: t1 = E
    // emit a dispatch:  goto L1 or L2 or ... or Ln
```

### Worked Trace on the Sample Switch

For the switch example from §3.1, the **backpatch algorithm** does the following in **one pass**:

1. Emit `goto test` — instruction **#0**. Symbolically label this as `goto test`. No patches needed.
2. Emit `L1: t2 = a+1; a=t2; goto next` — instruction **#1** is `goto next`. Add it to a list `nextlist = {1}`.
3. Emit `L2: t3 = a+2; a=t3; goto next` — instruction **#3** joins `nextlist = {1, 3}`.
4. Emit `L3: t4 = a+3; a=t4; goto next` — `nextlist = {1, 3, 5}`.
5. Emit `L4: t5 = a+4; a=t5; goto next` — `nextlist = {1, 3, 5, 7}`.
6. Emit `Ld: t6 = a+5; a=t6` — no jump; default body ends naturally.
7. Emit `next:` — now **backpatch** all instructions in `nextlist` to point to `next`. So instructions #1, #3, #5, #7 all become `goto next`.
8. Emit `test: t1 = x+2` — followed by a dispatch sequence. For a **jump-table** dispatch, this would be `t11 = t1 − 1; goto table[t11]`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is elegant:** The algorithm emits code in *one* pass over the source, even though the actual jump targets are unknown until later. The patch lists act as **deferred placeholders**. The backpatch step is $O(\vert \text{nextlist} \vert)$.

### Cost Derivation for Backpatching

$$
\text{Total patch operations} = 2n + 1
$$

where the $+1$ accounts for the unconditional `goto test` emitted at the entry, and the $2n$ accounts for:
- $n$ conditional jumps (one per case) needing to be patched to either a body label or the default.
- $n$ unconditional `goto next` jumps (one per case body) needing to be patched at the end.

$$
\text{Space overhead} = O(n) \text{ list cells}
$$

$$
\text{Time overhead} = O(n) \text{ for backpatching}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Strategy

The decision is based on two measurable properties of the case set:

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{n}{V_{\max} - V_{\min} + 1} \quad \text{(density, } 0 \le \rho \le 1 \text{)}
$$

$$
\sigma \;=\; V_{\max} - V_{\min} + 1 \quad \text{(range)}
$$

| **Density $\rho$** | **Range $\sigma$** | **Recommended Strategy** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho \ge 0.5$ (dense) | small ($\sigma \le 256$) | **Jump Table** | Table size is bounded; $O(1)$ dispatch dominates. |
| $\rho < 0.5$ (sparse) | small | **Binary Search** | Few cases, few comparisons, small code. |
| $\rho < 0.5$ (sparse) | very large ($\sigma > 10^5$) | **Hashed dispatch** or **linear search** | Jump table would consume megabytes. |
| $\rho \ge 0.5$ | very large | **Hybrid: jump table for dense core + linear for outliers** | Used in GCC, Clang, MSVC. |
| 1 case only | — | **if-then-else** | Trivial; the compiler's heuristics always pick `if`. |
| $\ge 5$ cases | any | Use backpatching to translate in **one pass** | Avoids second-pass overhead. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic Trigger:** If the question says "the values are **consecutive**" or "**dense**" — answer with a **jump table**. If the values are **sparse** (e.g., 1, 100, 1000) — answer with **binary search**. If nothing is specified — answer with **sequential if-else + backpatching**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Comparison

For the running example with $n = 4$ cases $\{1, 2, 5, 8\}$:

$$
V_{\min} = 1, \quad V_{\max} = 8, \quad \sigma = 8, \quad \rho = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5
$$

| Strategy | Runtime comparisons (worst case) | Code size (instructions) | Memory (bytes) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sequential if-else | $n = 4$ | $\approx 4n = 16$ | 0 |
| Jump table | $2$ (bounds) $+ 1$ (indirect) | $\approx 2n = 8$ | $\sigma \cdot 4 = 32$ |
| Binary search | $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil = 2$ | $\approx 3n = 12$ | 0 |
| Backpatch + if-else | $n = 4$ | $\approx 4n = 16$ | $O(n)$ list cells |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For this specific case set, **binary search and jump table are tied** on runtime. The compiler would likely pick the jump table because the code is shorter and there is no recursion.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of Switch Translation

The following block diagram shows the **decision pipeline** the compiler follows when translating a case statement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parse switch_stmt] --> B[Extract Selector E]
    B --> C[Extract Case Set C = V1 V2 ... Vn]
    C --> D[Compute Vmin, Vmax, range, density]
    D --> E{Density Test}
    E -- rho high and sigma small --> F[Jump Table Strategy]
    E -- rho low sigma small --> G[Binary Search Strategy]
    E -- sigma huge or n tiny --> H[Sequential if-else Strategy]
    F --> I[Emit TAC + Jump Table]
    G --> J[Emit TAC + Decision Tree]
    H --> K[Emit TAC + Patch Lists]
    I --> L[Backpatch All Forward Jumps]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Final Three-Address Code]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a *compiler phase* operating on the parsed switch statement. The diamond `Density Test` is the *strategy-selection heuristic* (see §3.5). All three strategies funnel into a common **backpatch step** to resolve forward references.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart of a Single Switch Dispatch (Jump-Table Strategy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Entry: goto test]) --> Eval[t1 = E]
    Eval --> B1{t1 less than Vmin}
    B1 -- yes --> DefA[goto Ld]
    B1 -- no --> B2{t1 greater than Vmax}
    B2 -- yes --> DefB[goto Ld]
    B2 -- no --> Idx[t2 = t1 - Vmin]
    Idx --> Ind[goto table t2]
    Ind --> Tab{Jump Table Lookup}
    Tab -- matches V1 --> Body1[Code for S1]
    Tab -- matches V2 --> Body2[Code for S2]
    Tab -- matches Vn --> Bodyn[Code for Sn]
    Tab -- default --> Bodyd[Code for Sd]
    Body1 --> Exit([goto next])
    Body2 --> Exit
    Bodyn --> Exit
    Bodyd --> Exit
    DefA --> Bodyd
    DefB --> Bodyd
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block naming convention (Mermaid safety):** Notice every node has a unique alphanumeric ID (`Start`, `Eval`, `B1`, `Idx`, `Body1`, …). Reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` are **avoided** as IDs. Labels are written in **plain uppercase text** with no embedded markdown or HTML — this prevents parser errors during KTU examination rendering.

---

## 4.3 Binary-Search Decision Tree (for cases {1, 2, 5, 8})

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0["t1 less than 2"] --> R0Yes["t1 equals 1 ?"]
    R0 --> R0No["t1 less than 5"]
    R0Yes -- yes --> L1["L1: a = a + 1"]
    R0Yes -- no --> L2["L2: a = a + 2"]
    R0No -- yes --> R5["t1 equals 5 ?"]
    R0No -- no --> R8["t1 equals 8 ?"]
    R5 -- yes --> L5["L5: a = a + 3"]
    R5 -- no --> Ld["Ld: a = a + 5"]
    R8 -- yes --> L8["L8: a = a + 4"]
    R8 -- no --> Ld
    L1 --> Next([goto next])
    L2 --> Next
    L5 --> Next
    L8 --> Next
    Ld --> Next
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization tip:** Each internal node in the binary tree is a *comparison*; each leaf is a *case body or default*. The depth of the tree equals the worst-case number of comparisons = $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$. For 4 cases, depth = 2.

---

## 4.4 Backpatching Pipeline (Single-Pass Translation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pass 1: Emit TAC with symbolic labels] --> P2[Maintain nextlist and case-lists]
    P2 --> P3[End of switch: emit label next]
    P3 --> P4[Backpatch nextlist to label next]
    P4 --> P5[Backpatch case lists to their bodies]
    P5 --> P6[Final TAC with concrete addresses]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Without backpatching, a *forward-jumping* compiler (e.g., emitting code as it parses) would need **two full passes** over the source. Backpatching compresses this into **one pass**, saving the cost of re-scanning the input — a major optimization in early single-pass compilers like Wirth's Pascal-S.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain briefly the two main strategies used by a compiler to translate a `switch` statement into three-address code. Mention one situation where each is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The two main strategies are:

1. **Jump-Table Strategy** (also called *direct indexing*): A table is constructed containing the addresses of all case bodies. The compiler evaluates the selector, checks that it lies within the range $[V_{\min}, V_{\max}]$, computes an index $t_2 = t_1 - V_{\min}$, and performs an indirect jump `goto table[t_2]`. This gives **O(1) dispatch** and is preferred when the case values are **dense and contiguous** (e.g., 1, 2, 3, 4, 5).

2. **Binary-Search (Decision Tree) Strategy**: The compiler builds a balanced binary decision tree by sorting the case values and emitting comparisons against the median at each internal node. At runtime, the selector is compared against the medians until a leaf (case body) is reached. This gives **O(log n) dispatch** and is preferred when the case values are **sparse but ordered** (e.g., 1, 100, 10000).

A third fallback strategy is **sequential `if-then-else`**, which is universal but gives **O(n) dispatch** and is used when neither of the above is suitable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution (typical):** 1 mark for naming jump table, 1 mark for naming binary search / decision tree, 1 mark for stating the conditions under which each is preferred.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a **patch list** in the context of switch-statement translation using backpatching? Name the three operations commonly performed on patch lists.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **patch list** is a linked list (or equivalent data structure) of *instruction indices* in the emitted three-address code that have a **forward reference** — i.e., a jump target that has not yet been emitted. The list acts as a *deferred placeholder*: instead of resolving the jump immediately, the compiler records which instructions need fixing and patches them all at once when the target label is finally emitted.

The three operations performed on patch lists are:

| # | Operation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{makelist}(i)$ | Creates a new list containing only the index $i$. |
| 2 | $\text{merge}(p_1, p_2)$ | Concatenates two lists $p_1$ and $p_2$ into one, returning the head. |
| 3 | $\text{backpatch}(p, L)$ | Walks through the list $p$ and replaces the jump target of every instruction in it with the (now-known) label $L$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution (typical):** 1 mark for the definition of patch list, 1 mark for naming `makelist` and `merge`, 1 mark for explaining `backpatch`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not forget to evaluate the selector into a temporary first.** Many students jump straight to the comparisons, which is a **−1 to −2 mark deduction** because it implies that side-effects in the selector could be repeated.
> 2. **Always count labels correctly.** The expected number is **n + 2** for a switch with $n$ cases (one label per case body + default + next). Off-by-one errors cost **1 mark**.
> 3. **For jump-table answers, you must explicitly show the table itself** (e.g., `table: L1, L2, Ld, L2, L3`). Students often forget this and lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **For backpatching, you must show the contents of `nextlist` at every emission step.** The KTU Board rewards the **trace**, not just the final code. A 14-mark question expects a fully worked trace.
> 5. **Do not use `switch` syntax in the answer**; always emit **three-address code** (TAC). The TAC must use intermediate temporaries like `t1`, `t2`, etc.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Sequential if-then-else with Backpatching*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024 Set B]**

**Question A(a)** — *7 marks*
Given the following C program fragment, translate it into **three-address code** using the **sequential if-then-else strategy** with **backpatching**. Show the contents of all patch lists.

```c
switch (i - j) {
    case 1  : x = x + 1;  break;
    case 3  : x = x + 3;  break;
    case 5  : x = x + 5;  break;
    default : x = 0;
}
```

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Evaluate the selector into a temporary. We use `t1` for `i − j`.

```text
t1 = i - j
```

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Emit the case bodies left-to-right, accumulating `nextlist` (the list of jumps that need to be patched to the exit label `next`).

| Instruction # | Emitted TAC | Patch-list update |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `goto test` | — |
| 2 | `L1: t2 = x + 1` | — |
| 3 | `   x  = t2` | — |
| 4 | `   goto next` | $\text{nextlist} = \{4\}$ |
| 5 | `L3: t3 = x + 3` | — |
| 6 | `   x  = t3` | — |
| 7 | `   goto next` | $\text{nextlist} = \{4, 7\}$ |
| 8 | `L5: t4 = x + 5` | — |
| 9 | `   x  = t4` | — |
| 10 | `   goto next` | $\text{nextlist} = \{4, 7, 10\}$ |
| 11 | `Ld: x = 0` | — |

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Emit the `next` label and **backpatch** `nextlist`.

```text
next:
backpatch(nextlist, next)   ->  patches instructions #4, #7, #10
```

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Emit the dispatch code at `test`.

```text
test:  if t1 ≠ 1  goto L3      // skip past case 1
       if t1 =  1  goto L1
       if t1 ≠ 3  goto L5      // skip past case 3
       if t1 =  3  goto L3
       if t1 ≠ 5  goto Ld      // skip past case 5
       goto L5                 // must be 5
```

**Final TAC (for reference):**

```text
        goto test
L1:     t2 = x + 1
        x  = t2
        goto next
L3:     t3 = x + 3
        x  = t3
        goto next
L5:     t4 = x + 5
        x  = t4
        goto next
Ld:     x = 0
next:
test:   if t1 ≠ 1  goto L3
        goto L1
        if t1 ≠ 3  goto L5
        goto L3
        if t1 ≠ 5  goto Ld
        goto L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** Step 1 (1), Step 2 (3), Step 3 (1), Step 4 (2). The instructor will also award 1 bonus mark for the dispatch code if it is **fully correct and free of off-by-one errors**.

---

**Question A(b)** — *7 marks*
For the same code as in part (a), generate the three-address code using a **jump table** strategy. State one assumption about the case values that you must make for the jump table to be directly applicable.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Assumption (2 marks):** For a jump table to be directly applicable, the case values must be **dense and contiguous** — i.e., the set $\{V_{\min}, V_{\min}+1, \ldots, V_{\max}\}$ with no gaps. The original cases $\{1, 3, 5\}$ have gaps, so we must *fill in the missing values* with pointers to the default label $L_d$, or *remap* them.

For this problem, the range is $[1, 5]$ and there are 5 entries. We map:

| Case value | Index $(V_i - V_{\min})$ | Jump target |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | L1 |
| 2 | 1 | Ld (default — no case label) |
| 3 | 2 | L3 |
| 4 | 3 | Ld (default) |
| 5 | 4 | L5 |

**TAC emission (5 marks):**

```text
        goto test
L1:     t2 = x + 1
        x  = t2
        goto next
L3:     t3 = x + 3
        x  = t3
        goto next
L5:     t4 = x + 5
        x  = t4
        goto next
Ld:     x = 0
next:
test:   t1 = i - j
        if t1 < 1   goto Ld
        if t1 > 5   goto Ld
        t5 = t1 - 1
        goto table[t5]
table:  L1, Ld, L3, Ld, L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** Assumption (2), Selector evaluation (1), Bounds checks (1), Indirect jump (1), Table contents (1), Flow of code (1).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Binary Search + Strategy Comparison*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Set A]**

**Question B(a)** — *7 marks*
Translate the following switch statement into three-address code using a **binary-search (decision-tree) strategy**. Clearly show the decision tree.

```c
switch (k * 2) {
    case 4   : a = a + 4; break;
    case 7   : a = a + 7; break;
    case 10  : a = a + 10; break;
    case 13  : a = a + 13; break;
    case 16  : a = a + 16; break;
    default  : a = a - 1;
}
```

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Sort the case values: $\{4, 7, 10, 13, 16\}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Build the decision tree.

The median of 5 values (index 2) is **10**. Subtrees:
- Left of 10: $\{4, 7\}$ → median 7.
  - Left of 7: $\{4\}$ → leaf.
  - Right of 7: $\varnothing$ → leaf (none, but the algorithm will fall through to default).
- Right of 10: $\{13, 16\}$ → median 13.
  - Left of 13: $\varnothing$.
  - Right of 13: $\{16\}$ → leaf.

**Decision Tree:**

```text
                t1 < 10 ?
                /       \
              yes         no
              /            \
         t1 < 7 ?       t1 < 13 ?
         /     \         /       \
       yes     no      yes       no
        |       |       |         |
     t1==4?  t1==7?  t1==10?  t1 < 16 ?
       ...                  ...
```

**Step 3 (5 marks):** Emit the TAC.

```text
        t1 = k * 2
        if t1 < 10   goto L_LT10
        if t1 > 10   goto L_GT10
        goto L10                // t1 == 10
L_LT10: if t1 < 7    goto L_LT7
        if t1 > 7    goto L_default
        goto L7                 // t1 == 7
L_LT7:  if t1 ≠ 4    goto L_default
        goto L4                 // t1 == 4
L_GT10: if t1 < 13   goto L_LT13
        if t1 > 13   goto L_GT13
        goto L13                // t1 == 13
L_LT13: goto L_default
L_GT13: if t1 ≠ 16   goto L_default
        goto L16                // t1 == 16
L4:     t2 = a + 4;  a = t2;  goto next
L7:     t3 = a + 7;  a = t3;  goto next
L10:    t4 = a + 10; a = t4;  goto next
L13:    t5 = a + 13; a = t5;  goto next
L16:    t6 = a + 16; a = t6;  goto next
L_default:
        t7 = a - 1;  a = t7;
next:
```

**Cost (1 mark implicit in diagram):**

$$
\text{Max comparisons} = \lceil \log_2 5 \rceil = 3
$$

$$
\text{Labels} = 2n + 1 = 11 \text{ (approx.)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** Sorting (1), Decision tree (1), Selector evaluation (1), Comparison chain (2), Case bodies (2). Bonus 1 mark for correct label count.

---

**Question B(b)** — *7 marks*
**Compare** the four strategies for translating case statements (sequential if-else, jump table, binary search, backpatching). Use a table with the following columns: **runtime complexity, code size, when preferred, and any drawback**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Strategy | Runtime | Code Size | Preferred When | Main Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sequential if-else** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | Cases are sparse and few; range is huge. | Slow for many cases. |
| **Jump Table** | $O(1)$ | $O(V_{\max} - V_{\min} + 1)$ | Cases are **dense and contiguous**; range small-to-moderate. | Wastes space for sparse or wide ranges. |
| **Binary Search** | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | Cases are **sparse but ordered**. | Slightly more comparisons than jump table for dense sets. |
| **Backpatching** | Same as underlying strategy | Same as underlying + $O(n)$ list cells | **Always** used as a wrapper to enable single-pass translation. | Slight overhead for list maintenance. |

(1 mark per row + 1 mark for the introductory line + 1 mark for the closing summary line.)

**Closing summary (1 mark):** Modern compilers (GCC, Clang) automatically choose between jump table and binary search using density heuristics. Backpatching is used universally to translate the chosen strategy in a single pass.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam**

- ✅ A **case statement** is a *multi-way branch* on a single selector expression.
- ✅ The selector must be **evaluated exactly once** and stored in a temporary `t1` (or similar).
- ✅ The number of new labels generated is **n + 2** for a switch with $n$ case labels (one per body + default + `next`).
- ✅ **Four canonical strategies** are tested in KTU: sequential if-else, jump table, binary search, backpatching.
- ✅ **Jump table** = dense, contiguous cases. Uses `t2 = t1 - V_min` for indexing. **O(1) dispatch, O(σ) space**.
- ✅ **Binary search** = sparse but ordered cases. Uses a **decision tree** of medians. **O(log n) dispatch, O(n) space**.
- ✅ **Sequential if-else** = universal fallback. **O(n) dispatch**.
- ✅ **Backpatching** = technique to **resolve forward jump references in one pass** using `makelist`, `merge`, `backpatch`.
- ✅ **Three patch-list operations** must be memorised: `makelist(i)`, `merge(p1, p2)`, `backpatch(p, L)`.
- ✅ **Density formula**: $\rho = \dfrac{n}{\sigma}$ where $\sigma = V_{\max} - V_{\min} + 1$.
  - $\rho \ge 0.5$ → prefer **jump table**.
  - $\rho < 0.5$ → prefer **binary search**.
- ✅ **Total patch operations** in backpatching = $2n + 1$ for a switch with $n$ cases.
- ✅ **Bounds checks** in a jump table are **two comparisons** (`t1 < Vmin` and `t1 > Vmax`) — both go to the default label.
- ✅ **The table itself** must be explicitly written in the TAC (e.g., `table: L1, L2, Ld, L3`).
- ✅ **Single-pass translation** is enabled by backpatching; without it, the compiler would need **two full passes**.
- ✅ **Real-world compilers** (GCC, LLVM, HotSpot) use a *hybrid*: jump table for dense core, linear search for outliers.
- ✅ **A switch is preferred over if-else** when there are **3 or more** branches on the same selector.
- ✅ **The `default` branch** is mandatory in robust code; the compiler must always emit a label for it.
- ✅ **The `next` label** marks the point where control flows *after* the switch completes.
- ✅ **Killer trick questions** often ask: *"What if two cases have the same label?"* — the answer is that this is a **semantic error** detected at the symbol-table level, not a code-generation concern.
- ✅ **Killer trick questions** also ask: *"What if the selector has side effects?"* — the answer is to evaluate it **once into a temporary** to avoid repeating the side effects.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU-Specific Tip:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. When answering, always state the **strategy name, the runtime cost, and the justification for your choice** — that trifecta captures the maximum marks per sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure Calls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Procedure Calls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Procedure Calls — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Procedure Call**
> A **procedure call** (function call / subroutine invocation) is a fundamental control-flow construct in which the execution of the *calling procedure* is temporarily suspended and control is transferred to a *called procedure* (callee). After the callee completes its execution, control and a (possibly computed) return value are handed back to the point immediately following the call site in the caller. A compiler must generate code that implements this transfer of control, the passing of arguments, the preservation of the caller's execution state, the allocation of storage for the callee's local data, and the orderly restoration of the caller's state upon return.

In compiler terminology, every **activation** (i.e., one live execution) of a procedure requires a dedicated **activation record (AR)** — also called a **stack frame** — placed on the **run-time stack**. The set of mechanisms that together create this AR, pass parameters, save state, transfer control, and deallocate the AR is collectively called the **calling sequence**, while the reverse process is the **return sequence**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The "Office Filing Tray" Model**
> Imagine you are working at desk A and you need information from a colleague at desk B.
> 1. You write the question on a **memo slip** (the **parameters**) and hand it over.
> 2. You place a **"do not disturb" sign** on your desk along with notes about what you were doing (**saved state**).
> 3. Your colleague pulls out a fresh **filing tray** (**activation record**) for this task, puts their working notes inside, and starts work.
> 4. When finished, they place the answer in a designated **"reply" folder** (**return value**), put their tray back in the cabinet (**deallocate AR**), and signal you.
> 5. You remove your sign, read the reply, and continue from where you had stopped.
>
> The "filing tray" is the **activation record**, the "do-not-disturb sign" is the **control link**, and the orderly memo-passing is the **calling sequence**.

This is exactly what a compiler must arrange on the run-time stack whenever a procedure is invoked.

## 1.3 Scope of the Topic (KTU 2024 Module 4 Coverage)

> [!NOTE]
> The following sub-topics are expected to be mastered under Module 4:
> - Structure of an **Activation Record (AR)** and its fields.
> - **Calling sequence** and **Return sequence** — division of work between caller and callee.
> - **Parameter-passing mechanisms**: call-by-value, call-by-reference, call-by-value-result, call-by-copy-restore, call-by-name.
> - **Storage allocation strategies**: static, stack, and heap.
> - **Access to non-local variables** under **static (lexical) scope** using **access links** and the **Display table** optimisation.
> - **Dynamic scope** and its implementation using control links.

The standard reference notation in this module is given in the table below.

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $t_{top}$ | Pointer to the top of the run-time stack |
| $\text{sp}$ | Stack pointer (current top of stack) |
| $\text{fp}$ | Frame pointer (base of current activation record) |
| $\text{top}_{sp}$ | Value of stack pointer just before an AR is built |
| $\text{disp}[i]$ | Display-table entry pointing to AR of the enclosing scope at lexical depth $i$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Activation Record (Stack Frame)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Activation Record**
> An activation record is a contiguous block of memory on the run-time stack that contains all the information required for one *active* execution of a procedure. The fields of a typical AR are listed below in the order in which they normally appear in memory (low-to-high address is implementation-defined, but the logical layout is standard).

**Standard fields of an AR (logical layout, low → high address):**

1. **Temporary values** — Compiler-generated intermediates used during expression evaluation.
2. **Local variables** — Variables declared inside the procedure.
3. **Saved machine status** — Registers that must be preserved across the call (program counter, condition codes, etc.).
4. **Access link (static link)** — Pointer to the AR of the *enclosing* lexical scope. Used for static-scope non-local access.
5. **Control link (dynamic link)** — Pointer to the AR of the *caller* on the stack. Used to restore the previous stack frame on return.
6. **Return address** — Address of the instruction in the caller to which the callee must return.
7. **Actual parameters** — Values or addresses of the arguments passed to the procedure.
8. **(Optional) Return value slot** — Reserved location where the callee deposits the value to be returned to the caller.

The **"Why"** of each field:

- *Return address* — control-flow continuity.
- *Control link* — restoration of previous stack frame.
- *Access link* — enables referencing variables defined in enclosing blocks (static scope).
- *Saved machine status* — preserves caller's state.
- *Parameters & return value* — information transfer across the call boundary.
- *Temporaries & locals* — the callee's own working memory.

## 2.2 Calling Sequence & Return Sequence

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Calling Sequence**
> The calling sequence is the executable code that is generated by the compiler to perform a procedure call. It is partitioned into two parts: code that lives in the *caller* (executed just before the call) and code that lives at the *prologue* of the *callee*. The boundary is chosen so that the callee can be optimised independently of the caller's internals.

**Step-by-step logical flow of a calling sequence:**

**Caller side (before jump):**
1. Evaluate each *actual parameter* and place it in the parameter slots of the (about-to-be-created) AR, or push it onto the stack in right-to-left order.
2. Save any caller-live registers that the callee might clobber (push them).
3. Store the **return address** (address of the instruction after the `CALL`).
4. If the language uses **static (lexical) scope**, update or remember the access link so the callee can find its enclosing scope.
5. Perform the actual `CALL` instruction — pushes the return address (in many ISAs) and transfers control to the callee's entry point.

**Callee prologue (entry of callee):**
1. Save the caller's frame pointer (`fp`).
2. Set the new `fp` to the current `sp` — this is the base of the new AR.
3. Reserve space for **local variables** and **temporaries** by decrementing `sp`.
4. Save the **control link** (old `fp`) at a known offset in the new AR.
5. Save the **access link** (incoming static link from caller) at a known offset.

**Return sequence (callee epilogue + caller resumption):**

**Callee side:**
1. Place the **return value** in the agreed slot / register.
2. Restore callee-saved registers.
3. Restore `fp` from the saved control link.
4. Reset `sp` to its value just below the current AR (effectively deallocating the AR).
5. Jump to the **return address** (`RET` / `RETURN` instruction).

**Caller side (after call resumes):**
1. Pop any caller-pushed parameters from the stack.
2. Restore any caller-saved registers.
3. Use the return value.

> [!TIP]
> **Why split the work?** Some work is naturally done by the caller (evaluating actuals, saving caller-saved registers); some by the callee (allocating its own locals). Splitting allows the callee to be called from many sites without duplicating the prologue.

## 2.3 Parameter-Passing Mechanisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parameter-passing mechanism** = the convention used by the language (and implemented by the compiler) for binding actual parameters to formal parameters at call time. The choice affects semantics, side-effects, and the code generated.

| Mechanism | What is passed to callee? | Caller can detect callee's modifications? | Code complexity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Call by Value** | A *copy* of the actual's value | No (callee has its own local copy) | Lowest |
| **Call by Reference** | The *address* of the actual | Yes (aliasing possible) | Moderate |
| **Call by Value-Result (Copy-Restore)** | A copy; results copied back on return | Yes, but only the *final* value, and only for non-aliased cases | Moderate |
| **Call by Name** | A *thunk* (re-evaluated code) at each use | Depends on the actual | Highest (used in Algol 60) |

**Code-generation implications (KTU frequently asked):**

- **Call by value**: `push actual_value` onto the stack. Callee reads from the parameter slot and never writes back. Trivial to implement.
- **Call by reference**: `push address_of(actual)`. Callee treats the parameter slot as a pointer and dereferences it for both reads and writes — this is the source of the **aliasing problem**.
- **Call by value-result**: At call time, push the current value of each actual. On return, the final values in the formal parameter slots are copied back to the corresponding actuals. Algol W introduced this to side-step the inefficiency of call-by-name.
- **Call by name**: At call time, the actual parameter is *not* evaluated; instead a small anonymous routine (**thunk**) is passed that, when invoked by the callee, re-evaluates the actual in the caller's environment. Heavily used in **Algol 60** for efficiency with array bounds.

> [!WARNING]
> **Aliasing pitfall:** In call-by-reference (and partially in call-by-value-result), two formal parameters can refer to the same actual — for example, when one is a global and another is a local of the caller. The compiler must NOT keep the global in a register across the call; it must spill it.

## 2.4 Storage-Allocation Strategies

| Strategy | When used | Lifetime of an object | Pointer to objects? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Static allocation** | Global variables, constants, no recursion | Entire program run | No (addresses are fixed at compile time) |
| **Stack allocation** | Local variables, parameters, return addresses | One procedure activation | No (LIFO) |
| **Heap allocation** | Dynamic data (`malloc`/`new`), objects whose lifetime is unknown at compile time | Arbitrary, programmer-controlled | Yes (explicit pointers) |

**Procedure calls in a stack-allocated environment:**
- A new AR is pushed on entry and popped on exit.
- This is the most common model for languages like C, Pascal, Java (for the JVM stack), and the typical case assumed in Module 4.

## 2.5 Access to Non-Local Variables — Static Scope

When a procedure `p` is nested inside procedure `q` which is nested inside the program's main block, accessing a variable `x` declared in `q` from inside `p` requires a chain of pointer dereferences called the **access chain** (or **static chain**). The pointers in this chain are the **access links** stored in each AR.

**Two standard implementations:**

1. **Access links (static links)** — Each AR stores a pointer to the AR of its *enclosing* procedure. To access a variable $d$ scopes outward, the compiler follows $d$ access links.
2. **Display table** — A global array `disp[0..Dmax]` where `disp[i]` points to the AR of the most-recently-activated procedure at lexical depth $i$. Accessing a non-local variable at depth $d$ requires **at most one dereference** through `disp[d]`, drastically speeding up lookups at the cost of updating the display on every call and return.

> [!NOTE]
> The display table trade-off: $\mathcal{O}(1)$ non-local access at the cost of $\mathcal{O}(D_{max})$ entries maintained per call/return. This is a classic KTU "compare and contrast" point.

## 2.6 Dynamic Scope

In **dynamic scoping**, a reference to a non-local name `x` is bound to the *most-recent activation* (on the call stack) that declares `x` — *not* the lexically enclosing one. Implementation typically uses the **control link chain** instead of access links.

> [!TIP]
> Most modern languages use **static (lexical) scope** (C, Java, Python). Dynamic scope survives in some shell-scripting languages and in early Lisp dialects.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The activation record / calling-sequence model is the basis of every modern CPU's stack frame and the standard Application Binary Interface (ABI) of platforms such as x86-64 System V, AArch64, and RISC-V.
- Concepts of access links and display tables appear in interpreters for nested-scope languages (Python, JavaScript, Scheme).
- Understanding parameter-passing is essential for writing safe FFI (Foreign Function Interface) bindings in Rust, Python, and Java Native Interface (JNI).

## 2.8 KTU Reference Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| Concept | Key Property | KTU Buzz-words |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Activation Record | Stack-resident, one per live procedure call | "Stack frame", "AR layout" |
| Calling sequence | Split between caller & callee | "Caller part + callee prologue" |
| Return sequence | Reverse of calling sequence | "Callee epilogue + caller part" |
| Call by value | Pass copy | "No aliasing, no side-effect" |
| Call by reference | Pass address | "Aliasing possible" |
| Call by value-result | Pass copy, copy back on return | "Copy-restore semantics" |
| Call by name | Pass thunk | "Re-evaluated at each use" |
| Static allocation | Fixed at compile time | "No recursion support" |
| Stack allocation | LIFO | "Default for local variables" |
| Heap allocation | Arbitrary lifetime | "malloc / free" |
| Access link | Points to enclosing scope's AR | "Static-scope implementation" |
| Display table | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ non-local access | "Global array of AR pointers" |
| Control link | Points to caller's AR | "Dynamic-scope implementation" |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time to Access a Non-Local Variable

Let $D$ be the **lexical depth** of the *enclosing* procedure that defines the variable, and $d$ be the **lexical depth** of the *currently executing* procedure. We want an expression for the cost of accessing the variable.

### 3.1.1 Using Access Links (Static Chain)

In the access-link model, accessing a non-local variable requires walking up the static chain. The number of pointer dereferences is exactly:

$$
\text{AccessCost}_{\text{access-link}}(D, d) \;=\; d - D
$$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Let } \Delta d \;=\; d - D. \text{ This is the static nesting distance.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Each "up the chain" traversal is exactly one pointer dereference.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Therefore the total dereferences} \;=\; \Delta d \;=\; d - D. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Adding one final dereference to read the variable's value slot:} \\
\text{AccessCost}_{\text{access-link}}(D, d) \;&=\; (d - D) + 1 \;=\; d - D + 1.
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Many textbooks absorb the final read into the count and write the cost simply as $d - D$. The KTU-accepted form is **$d - D$ dereferences** (with the final read counted as part of the access, not the lookup).

### 3.1.2 Using the Display Table

With a display table of size $D_{max}+1$, the access cost is constant in the depth:

$$
\text{AccessCost}_{\text{display}}(D) \;=\; 1
$$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{At lexical depth } D, \text{ the display entry } \text{disp}[D] \text{ already points to the AR defining the variable.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{One pointer dereference retrieves the AR base address.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{One constant offset adds the variable's slot within the AR.} \\
\text{AccessCost}_{\text{display}}(D) \;&=\; 1 \text{ indirect access } + 1 \text{ constant offset} \;=\; \mathcal{O}(1).
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Speedup Factor

$$
\text{Speedup} \;=\; \frac{\text{AccessCost}_{\text{access-link}}}{\text{AccessCost}_{\text{display}}} \;=\; \frac{d - D}{1} \;=\; d - D
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For deeply nested programs (large $d-D$), the **display table** can be orders of magnitude faster than the static chain. This is the principal reason real-world interpreters maintain a display.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Mechanism

The matrix below is the canonical KTU decision aid for the question *"Which parameter-passing mechanism is appropriate for situation X?"*

| Situation | Best Mechanism | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Read-only input parameter (e.g., a threshold) | Call by value | Safety: callee cannot corrupt the caller's variable. |
| Output parameter to be filled by callee (e.g., a status code) | Call by reference | Callee must write to the caller's storage. |
| Large struct passed for read-only use | Call by reference (or by value of pointer) | Avoids full struct copy. |
| Function that needs the **current** value, modifies it, and **also** returns the modified value to the caller | Call by value-result | Cleaner semantics than reference for serialised updates. |
| Argument that must be re-evaluated in caller's environment (e.g., an expression with side effects in Algol 60) | Call by name | Thunk re-evaluates the expression in the caller's scope at each use. |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Display-Table Maintenance

**Problem.** A program has three nested procedures at depths $0, 1, 2$. Suppose procedure at depth $1$ calls the procedure at depth $2$. The display table is initially:

$$
\text{disp} \;=\; \big[\; A R_{0} \;,\; A R_{1} \;,\; \text{undefined} \;\big]
$$

**Find:** the new contents of the display table after the call to depth-$2$ procedure.

**Solution (step-by-step):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Identify the lexical depth of the callee: } d_{\text{callee}} = 2. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Save the current } \text{disp}[2] \text{ value at a known location in the callee's AR (for restoration on return).} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Update } \text{disp}[2] \text{ to point to the new } A R_{2}. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{All } \text{disp}[i] \text{ for } i < 2 \text{ remain unchanged, because they still represent the correct enclosing scopes.} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{disp} \;=\; \big[\; A R_{0} \;,\; A R_{1} \;,\; A R_{2} \;\big].
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> On return, the saved $\text{disp}[2]$ is restored, and the display returns to its prior state — exactly mirroring the LIFO nature of the stack.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Aliasing in Call-by-Reference

**Problem.** Consider:

```c
int x = 10;
void swap(int a, int b) { int t = a; a = b; b = t; }
int main(void) {
    swap(x, x);          // pass the SAME variable twice
    return 0;
}
```

Explain what happens under each parameter-passing mechanism.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Case (a) — Call by value:} \quad & \text{Formal } a \text{ and } b \text{ are distinct local copies. swap reads } 10, 10 \text{ and writes } 10, 10. \\
& \text{Result: } x = 10. \\
\text{Case (b) — Call by reference:} \quad & \text{Both } a \text{ and } b \text{ are aliases for the same address } \vert x. \\
& \text{After } t = a: t = 10. \\
& \text{After } a = b: a = 10. \\
& \text{After } b = t: b = 10. \\
& \text{Result: } x = 10. \text{ Aliasing defeats the swap!} \\
\text{Case (c) — Call by value-result:} \quad & a \text{ and } b \text{ are distinct local copies; on return, both are copied back to } x. \\
& \text{The last copy wins. If copy-back order is left-to-right, } x \text{ becomes } a = 10. \\
& \text{If right-to-left, } x \text{ becomes } b = 10. \\
& \text{In either case, } x = 10. \text{ Same outcome as reference for this pathological case.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is a classic KTU exam trap: do **not** assume call-by-reference is "always equivalent" to call-by-value-result — for non-aliased parameters they may differ in observable behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All diagrams below are written in **Mermaid** with the strict ID-alphanumeric rule and clean uppercase labels inside node quotes.

## 4.1 Activation Record — Logical Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    A["TEMPORARIES"]:::arfield
    B["LOCAL VARIABLES"]:::arfield
    C["SAVED MACHINE STATUS"]:::arfield
    D["ACCESS LINK STATIC PARENT"]:::arfield
    E["CONTROL LINK DYNAMIC PARENT"]:::arfield
    F["RETURN ADDRESS"]:::arfield
    G["PARAMETER 1"]:::arfield
    H["PARAMETER 2"]:::arfield
    I["PARAMETER N"]:::arfield
    J["RETURN VALUE SLOT"]:::arfield

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J

    classDef arfield fill:#1f2a44,stroke:#9ec5ff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading guide:** the lowest-address field is at the top (`TEMPORARIES`), the highest-address field is at the bottom (`RETURN VALUE SLOT`). The actual direction of growth is implementation-dependent; the diagram shows the *logical* layout, not a specific ISA's stack direction.

## 4.2 Call-Stack During Nested Procedure Calls

The diagram below shows a snapshot of the run-time stack while procedure `C` is executing, having been called by `B` which was called by `A` which was called by `MAIN`.

```mermaid
graph TB
    Main["MAIN AR"]:::stackbase
    Aproc["A AR control link points to MAIN AR"]:::stackmid
    Bproc["B AR control link points to A AR"]:::stackmid
    Cproc["C AR control link points to B AR CURRENT TOP"]:::stacktop

    Main -- "control link" --> Aproc
    Aproc -- "control link" --> Bproc
    Bproc -- "control link" --> Cproc

    StaticC["C access link points to enclosing scope at depth D of C"]:::staticlink
    StaticB["B access link points to enclosing scope at depth D of B"]:::staticlink

    Bproc -. "static link" .-> StaticB
    Cproc -. "static link" .-> StaticC

    classDef stackbase fill:#274d4d,stroke:#9ec5ff,color:#ffffff
    classDef stackmid fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#9ec5ff,color:#ffffff
    classDef stacktop fill:#7a1f3a,stroke:#ffd166,color:#ffffff
    classDef staticlink fill:#3a1f7a,stroke:#c8b6ff,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading guide:**

- **Solid arrows** represent **control (dynamic) links** — the chain that unwinds on `RETURN`.
- **Dotted arrows** represent **access (static) links** — the chain that the compiler follows to resolve a non-local reference under *lexical scope*.
- The topmost AR (`C AR`) is the one currently executing; its `fp` (frame pointer) is the current `fp` of the CPU.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Calling Sequence

When a physical drawing of the call mechanism is required but impractical, the following **block-level functional flow** captures the same information in a Mermaid-renderable form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CallerStart["Caller executing"]:::stage
    EvalArgs["Evaluate actual parameters and store in temp slots"]:::stage
    SaveRegs["Save caller live registers onto stack"]:::stage
    StoreRet["Store return address"]:::stage
    UpdateAccess["Compute and store access link static parent pointer"]:::stage
    CallInstr["Execute CALL instruction transfer control to callee"]:::stage
    CalleeEntry["Callee entry prologue"]:::stage
    SaveFP["Save old frame pointer control link"]:::stage
    SetFP["Set new frame pointer to current stack top"]:::stage
    ReserveLocals["Reserve space for local variables decrement stack pointer"]:::stage
    ExecBody["Callee executes its body"]:::stage
    PlaceRet["Place return value in agreed slot"]:::stage
    RestoreRegs["Restore callee saved registers"]:::stage
    RestoreFP["Restore frame pointer from control link"]:::stage
    DeallocAR["Deallocate AR by resetting stack pointer"]:::stage
    RetInstr["Execute RET instruction jump to return address"]:::stage
    CallerResume["Caller resumes after call"]:::stage
    PopArgs["Pop parameter slots and restore caller saved registers"]:::stage
    UseRet["Use return value and continue"]:::stage

    CallerStart --> EvalArgs --> SaveRegs --> StoreRet --> UpdateAccess --> CallInstr --> CalleeEntry
    CalleeEntry --> SaveFP --> SetFP --> ReserveLocals --> ExecBody
    ExecBody --> PlaceRet --> RestoreRegs --> RestoreFP --> DeallocAR --> RetInstr
    RetInstr --> CallerResume --> PopArgs --> UseRet

    classDef stage fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#9ec5ff,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Display-Table Update Across a Call

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    StateBefore["Display BEFORE call: disp = AR0 AR1 UNDEFINED"]:::before
    SaveDisp["Save disp 2 into callee AR for later restoration"]:::action
    UpdateDisp["Set disp 2 to new callee AR2"]:::action
    StateAfter["Display AFTER call: disp = AR0 AR1 AR2"]:::after
    ReturnSave["On return, restore disp 2 from saved value in callee AR"]:::action
    StateReturn["Display AFTER return: disp = AR0 AR1 UNDEFINED"]:::after

    StateBefore --> SaveDisp --> UpdateDisp --> StateAfter
    StateAfter --> ReturnSave --> StateReturn

    classDef before fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#9ec5ff,color:#ffffff
    classDef after fill:#274d4d,stroke:#9ec5ff,color:#ffffff
    classDef action fill:#7a1f3a,stroke:#ffd166,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
