# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modelled on the KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. Marks shown in brackets follow the official KTU split (Part A: 3 marks; Part B: 14 marks with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is an activation record? List any four of its fields.**

**Model Answer:**

An **activation record (AR)** is a contiguous block of memory on the run-time stack that contains all the information required for one live execution of a procedure.

Four standard fields of an AR are:
1. **Return address** — instruction in the caller to resume at after the call.
2. **Control link (dynamic link)** — pointer to the AR of the calling procedure.
3. **Access link (static link)** — pointer to the AR of the lexically enclosing scope.
4. **Local variables** — variables declared inside the procedure.

*(Other acceptable fields: parameters, temporaries, saved machine status, return value slot.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between call-by-value and call-by-reference. Mention one disadvantage of call-by-reference.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Call-by-Value | Call-by-Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| What is passed | A *copy* of the actual's value | The *address* of the actual |
| Callee's modifications visible to caller? | No | Yes |
| Aliasing possible? | No | Yes |

**Disadvantage of call-by-reference:** It can introduce **aliasing** — two formal parameters can refer to the *same* actual, leading to subtle bugs (e.g., `swap(x, x)` fails). Additionally, the callee can unintentionally modify the caller's variable, breaking encapsulation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks, internal choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an internal choice. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two fully independent alternative selections.

---

### ❖ Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Draw and explain the structure of an activation record with a neat diagram. List any six fields. (7 marks)**

**(b) What is a calling sequence? Explain how the calling sequence and the return sequence are divided between the caller and the callee with a suitable diagram. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition:** An activation record (AR) is a contiguous block of memory in the run-time stack that contains all information required for one live execution of a procedure.

**Six fields of an AR (in logical order):**

1. **Temporary values** — compiler-generated intermediates.
2. **Local variables** — variables declared inside the procedure.
3. **Saved machine status** — registers that must be preserved across the call.
4. **Access link (static link)** — pointer to the AR of the enclosing lexical scope.
5. **Control link (dynamic link)** — pointer to the AR of the caller.
6. **Return address** — address in the caller to resume at after the call.

*(Plus parameters and an optional return-value slot.)*

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    F1["TEMPORARIES"]:::f
    F2["LOCAL VARIABLES"]:::f
    F3["SAVED MACHINE STATUS"]:::f
    F4["ACCESS LINK STATIC PARENT"]:::f
    F5["CONTROL LINK DYNAMIC PARENT"]:::f
    F6["RETURN ADDRESS"]:::f
    F7["PARAMETERS"]:::f
    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 --> F5 --> F6 --> F7
    classDef f fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#9ec5ff,color:#ffffff
```

#### Model Solution for (b)

A **calling sequence** is the code executed to invoke a procedure; the **return sequence** is the code executed to resume the caller after the procedure finishes. The calling sequence is **split** between the caller and the callee.

**Caller side (pre-call):**
1. Evaluate actual parameters and place them in parameter slots.
2. Save caller-live registers.
3. Store the return address.
4. Compute and store the access link (static parent pointer).
5. Execute the `CALL` instruction.

**Callee prologue:**
1. Save the caller's frame pointer (control link).
2. Set the new frame pointer.
3. Reserve stack space for locals and temporaries.
4. Save the incoming access link.

**Callee epilogue (return):**
1. Place the return value in the agreed slot.
2. Restore callee-saved registers.
3. Restore the frame pointer from the control link.
4. Deallocate the AR by resetting the stack pointer.
5. Execute `RET` to return to the caller.

**Caller side (post-call):**
1. Pop the parameter slots.
2. Restore caller-saved registers.
3. Use the return value and continue.

**Diagram (functional flow):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1["Caller pre: evaluate actuals, save regs, store ret addr, set access link"]:::s
    C2["CALL instruction"]:::s
    P1["Callee prologue: save old fp, set new fp, reserve locals"]:::s
    P2["Callee body executes"]:::s
    E1["Callee epilogue: place return value, restore regs, restore fp, dealloc AR"]:::s
    E2["RET instruction to caller"]:::s
    C3["Caller post: pop args, restore regs, use return value"]:::s

    C1 --> C2 --> P1 --> P2 --> E1 --> E2 --> C3
    classDef s fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#9ec5ff,color:#ffffff
```

---

### ❖ Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Explain the following parameter-passing mechanisms with examples: (i) Call by value, (ii) Call by reference, (iii) Call by value-result, (iv) Call by name. (7 marks)**

**(b) What is an access link? How is it used to access non-local variables under static scoping? Explain the display table as an optimisation. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**(i) Call by value:** A *copy* of the actual's value is passed. The callee works on its own local copy; modifications do not affect the caller.

```c
void f(int a) { a = a + 1; }
int x = 5; f(x);   // x remains 5 after the call
```

**(ii) Call by reference:** The *address* of the actual is passed. The callee dereferences this address for both reads and writes; modifications **do** affect the caller. Aliasing can occur.

```c
void f(int *a) { *a = *a + 1; }
int x = 5; f(&x);  // x becomes 6 after the call
```

**(iii) Call by value-result (copy-restore):** A copy is passed at call time. The final value of the formal at return is *copied back* into the actual. Semantics are similar to call-by-reference for non-aliased cases, but the copy-back is atomic at procedure exit.

```pascal
procedure f(var a: integer); begin a := a + 1 end;
{ conceptually a copy is taken; on return the final value is restored }
```

**(iv) Call by name:** The actual parameter is *not* evaluated at call time. Instead, a **thunk** (a small closure capturing the actual's expression and its environment) is passed. Each use of the formal inside the callee re-evaluates the actual in the caller's environment. Used in Algol 60.

```algol
procedure f(a, b); integer a, b; begin ... end;
f(x, A[i])    { A[i] is re-evaluated on every use of b inside f }
```

#### Model Solution for (b)

An **access link** is a pointer stored in each activation record that points to the activation record of the **lexically enclosing** procedure.

**Accessing a non-local variable under static scope:**
1. The compiler determines the **lexical nesting depth** of both the variable's declaration site and the currently executing procedure.
2. From the current AR, the compiler follows the **static chain** (a sequence of access links) up to the AR of the procedure that declares the variable.
3. The required variable is then accessed at a known offset within that AR.

The number of pointer dereferences required is $d - D$, where $d$ is the current depth and $D$ is the declaring procedure's depth.

**Display table as an optimisation:**
A **display table** is a global array `disp[0..Dmax]` maintained by the calling sequence, where `disp[i]` points to the AR of the most recently activated procedure at lexical depth $i$. To access a non-local variable at depth $D$, the compiler performs a **single** dereference: `disp[D]` followed by the variable's offset. This reduces the worst-case access cost from $\mathcal{O}(d-D)$ to $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

The trade-off is that the display must be updated on every call and restored on every return — but this cost is constant per call, not per access.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the access link vs control link distinction.** Many students write "access link points to the caller" — this is the **control link**. The access link points to the **lexical parent**, which may not be the caller.
> 2. **Confusing call-by-reference with call-by-value-result.** They behave identically for *non-aliased* parameters but can differ for *aliased* parameters and for expressions with side-effects. Examiners often test this with a small code snippet.
> 3. **Omitting the cost analysis** of access links vs display. The expected answer includes the cost formula $d - D$ vs $1$, *and* a one-line justification of why display is faster.
> 4. **Drawing the AR with parameters at the top.** The convention used in Module 4 is the *logical* order; examiners accept any consistent ordering, but you must **state** which end is the high address.
> 5. **In the calling sequence,** students often forget that the **callee** allocates space for its *own* locals. Marking schemes explicitly allocate marks for "callee reserves space for locals".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] An **activation record (AR)** is a stack-allocated block of memory, one per live procedure invocation.
- [x] Standard AR fields: **temporaries, locals, saved machine status, access link, control link, return address, parameters, return-value slot**.
- [x] **Control link (dynamic)** → points to the *caller's* AR. **Access link (static)** → points to the *enclosing scope's* AR.
- [x] **Calling sequence** is split between caller and callee; the callee handles its own prologue and epilogue.
- [x] **Call by value** → copy passed, no side-effects.
- [x] **Call by reference** → address passed, aliasing possible.
- [x] **Call by value-result** → copy passed; final value copied back on return.
- [x] **Call by name** → thunk passed; re-evaluated in caller's environment at each use (Algol 60).
- [x] **Static allocation** = fixed addresses, no recursion. **Stack allocation** = default for local variables. **Heap allocation** = for dynamic lifetimes.
- [x] **Static (lexical) scope** is implemented using access links; the **Display table** is an $\mathcal{O}(1)$-access optimisation.
- [x] Access cost with static chain: $d - D$ dereferences. With display: $1$ dereference.
- [x] **Dynamic scope** uses the control-link chain instead of access links.
- [x] The display table entry $\text{disp}[D]$ is saved in the callee's AR on entry and restored on return — preserving the LIFO property.
- [x] Aliasing in call-by-reference is a common KTU exam trap — be ready to explain it with `swap(x, x)`.
- [x] Remember the **two diagrams** you must be able to draw: (i) the AR layout and (ii) the call-stack with control and access links annotated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Optimization - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Code Optimization - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Code Optimization** is a phase in the compilation process that attempts to improve the intermediate code (or target code) so that it consumes fewer resources — primarily **execution time, memory (space), and power** — while preserving the **semantic equivalence** (functional output) of the program. As per the Aho-Sethi-Ullman (Dragon Book) standard adopted by KTU, optimization is the science of transforming a program into a semantically equivalent program that exhibits superior runtime performance metrics.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBCRT604, Module 4), code optimization is treated as the *bridge phase* between the **Intermediate Code Generation** module and the **Code Generation** module. It acts as a transformation pipeline that consumes a *Three-Address Code (TAC)* representation and emits a *streamlined TAC* without changing the program's observable behavior.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (Board-Examiner Ready):**
> "Optimization is a program transformation technique that attempts to improve the execution efficiency of the intermediate code by applying semantics-preserving transformations such as constant folding, common subexpression elimination, dead code elimination, strength reduction, and register allocation."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Refinement" Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef preparing a complex dish** (Biriyani) for a large wedding (your program). The *raw recipe* given to you is correct, but it contains redundant steps — e.g., *"boil water → boil water again for another purpose → throw away the first batch"*, or *"grind 1 kg onions → keep aside 200g → grind another 1 kg onions"*.

A **master chef (the optimizer)** will:
1. **Reuse** the previously boiled water instead of boiling it twice → *Common Subexpression Elimination*.
2. **Pre-chop** onions during the slow-cooking phase of rice → *Instruction Scheduling*.
3. **Skip** steps that produce a dish no one is going to taste (unused garnishing) → *Dead Code Elimination*.
4. **Replace** the labor-intensive coal-fired slow roast with a pressure cooker (same taste, less time) → *Strength Reduction*.

The **wedding guest (the user/programmer) is unaware** that the recipe was altered — the biriyani tastes identical. This is the essence of **semantic-preserving optimization**: the output is unchanged, but the journey to that output becomes *cheaper, faster, and leaner*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The word *"optimization"* is somewhat misleading. We almost never produce truly *optimal* code (that is an NP-hard problem in general). What we do is *improve* the code using well-defined transformation rules.

---

## 1.3 Why Code Optimization is Needed — The Three Pillars

| Pillar | Engineering Concern | KTU Exam Hot-Point |
|---|---|---|
| **Time** (Speed) | Modern CPUs execute billions of instructions/sec; even a 5% reduction in cycles saves hours in data-centers. | "Reduce execution time" |
| **Space** (Memory) | Embedded systems (KTU favourite topic) have RAM measured in KBs. Loops must be compacted. | "Reduce code size" |
| **Power** (Energy) | Mobile/IoT devices run on batteries; fewer instructions ⇒ less battery drain. | "Reduce energy consumption" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question (Dec 2022):** *"Is it always desirable to optimize code? Justify."*
> **Model Answer:** No. Optimization increases **compilation time** and **compiler complexity**. For a one-time run script (e.g., a quick build script), optimization is wasteful. Optimization is justified when the *execution frequency × speed-up factor > compilation overhead*.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Optimizations

The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes optimization into three orthogonal axes:

### A. By Scope (Where the transformation looks)
- **Local Optimization** — confined to a **single basic block**.
- **Global Optimization** — spans an **entire function/procedure** (intraprocedural).
- **Interprocedural Optimization** — crosses function boundaries (whole-program).

### B. By Machine Dependence
- **Machine-Independent Optimization** — operates on intermediate code; works on any target. *Example: constant folding*.
- **Machine-Dependent Optimization** — exploits specific CPU features like registers, pipeline, cache. *Example: register allocation, peephole optimization*.

### C. By When They Run
- **Peephole Optimization** — tiny sliding window of 2–5 instructions.
- **Loop Optimization** — targets the hot-spot of every program: loops.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "LSG"**: **L**ocal, **S**cope-based (global), **G**rab the loop. The three scopes a student must remember for the 14-mark question.

---

## 1.5 The Optimization Pipeline — Where Exactly Does It Fit?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source Code} &\xrightarrow{\text{Lexical}} \text{Tokens} \xrightarrow{\text{Syntax}} \text{Parser Tree} \\
&\xrightarrow{\text{Semantic}} \text{Annotated AST} \xrightarrow{\text{IR-Gen}} \text{Three-Address Code} \\
&\xrightarrow{\textbf{OPTIMIZATION}} \text{Optimized TAC} \xrightarrow{\text{Code Gen}} \text{Target Machine Code}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The optimization phase is **syntax- and semantic-aware** but **machine-agnostic** at the IR level, making it portable across all target architectures (x86, ARM, RISC-V).

---

## 1.6 Principal Sources of Optimization (KTU High-Yield List)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Six" Optimization Techniques Tested Repeatedly in KTU Exams:**

1. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**
2. **Copy Propagation**
3. **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**
4. **Constant Folding / Constant Propagation**
5. **Strength Reduction**
6. **Loop Optimization** (code motion, induction variable elimination, loop unrolling)

Each of these will be analyzed in deep theoretical detail in **Section 2**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Fundamental Premise of Optimization

The mathematical contract that every optimization must honor is **semantic equivalence**, formally expressed as:

$$
\forall \, \vec{x} \in \text{Input Domain}: \quad \mathcal{P}_{\text{orig}}(\vec{x}) = \mathcal{P}_{\text{opt}}(\vec{x})
$$

where $\vec{x}$ is the vector of all program inputs, and $\mathcal{P}$ is the observable I/O behavior. Any transformation that violates this equality is a **compiler bug**, not an optimization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Semantic Equivalence" is Hard:** Compilers use *conservative approximations* — they will refuse to optimize if they cannot *prove* safety. False negatives (missing an opportunity) are acceptable; false positives (changing behavior) are forbidden.

---

## 2.2 Basic Blocks — The Atom of Optimization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Aho-Sethi-Ullman):** A **Basic Block** is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions in which **flow of control enters at the beginning and exits only at the end**, without halting or branching except possibly at the last instruction.

### 2.2.1 The Three Successors in the Flow Graph

A basic block is a node in a **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**. The CFG edges are:
- **Fall-through edge** → next physical block (no branch).
- **Jump / Conditional edge** → target label (branch taken).
- **Return edge** → exit / caller.

### 2.2.2 Algorithm to Partition TAC into Basic Blocks

**Input:** A sequence of three-address instructions.

**Output:** A list of basic blocks.

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| **1** | Determine the set of **leaders** — instructions that start a new basic block. |
| **2(a)** | The *first* instruction of the program is a leader. |
| **2(b)** | Any instruction that is the *target of a conditional/unconditional jump* is a leader. |
| **2(c)** | Any instruction that *immediately follows a conditional/unconditional jump* is a leader. |
| **3** | For each leader, its basic block consists of itself and all instructions up to (but not including) the next leader or the end of the program. |

---

## 2.3 The Six Core Transformations — Theory & "Why"

### 2.3.1 Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)

A *common subexpression* is a computation that has been **previously computed** and whose **operands have not changed** since.

**Why it works:** Computing $a \cdot b$ twice is redundant if neither $a$ nor $b$ was redefined between the two computations. We can replace the second occurrence with a reference to a temporary holding the first result.

**Engine:** **Available Expressions Analysis** (a *forward, may-gen/kill, dataflow analysis*).

### 2.3.2 Copy Propagation

After an assignment like `x = y` (a *copy statement*), subsequent uses of `x` can be replaced with `y`, provided `y` has not been redefined.

**Why it works:** Eliminates unnecessary indirection; often a *pre-cursor* to CSE — once copies are propagated, the now-equivalent expressions become obvious common subexpressions.

**Engine:** **Reaching Definitions Analysis**.

### 2.3.3 Dead Code Elimination (DCE)

A variable is **dead** at a point if it is defined but never used *live* afterward. The assignment to it can be deleted.

**Why it works:** Dead stores cost memory bandwidth and CPU cycles. In a multi-pass optimizer, CSE → Copy Prop → DCE cascade frequently erases 30–50% of intermediate instructions.

**Engine:** **Liveness Analysis** (backward dataflow analysis).

### 2.3.4 Constant Folding & Constant Propagation

- **Constant Folding:** Evaluating constant expressions at compile time.
  - Example: `x = 2 * 3 + 5` → `x = 11` (no runtime multiplication needed).
- **Constant Propagation:** Replacing a variable's uses with a known constant value.
  - Example: After `x = 5; y = x + 3` → `y = 8`.

**Why it works:** Reduces dynamic operations to static ones. Enables further folding downstream.

**Engine:** **Constant Propagation Analysis** (iterative fixed-point).

### 2.3.5 Strength Reduction

Replacing an *expensive operation* with an equivalent *cheaper one*.

| Original (Expensive) | Replacement (Cheap) | Speed-up |
|---|---|---|
| `x = a * 2` | `x = a + a` or `x = a << 1` | 2–4× |
| `x = a * 8` | `x = a << 3` | 3–5× |
| `x = a / 2` | `x = a >> 1` | 2–3× |
| `x = a ** 2` | `x = a * a` | 10–20× |
| Loop body: `i * 4` | increment by 4 each iteration | O(n) → O(1) per iter |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern x86 CPUs have fast multipliers, so strength reduction is less impactful today — but it remains **crucial for embedded ARM/RISC-V targets** (a frequent KTU viva question).

### 2.3.6 Loop Optimization — The Big Three

#### (a) **Code Motion (Loop-Invariant Code Motion — LICM)**
Moves a computation that produces the *same result on every iteration* **outside the loop**.

$$
\text{Before:} \quad \text{for } (i=0; i<n; i++) \{ x = y + z; \quad a[i] = x * i; \}
$$

$$
\text{After LICM:} \quad x = y + z; \quad \text{for } (i=0; i<n; i++) \{ a[i] = x * i; \}
$$

#### (b) **Induction Variable Elimination**
A variable whose value is a *linear function of the loop counter* (e.g., $t_1 = 4 \cdot i$) is replaced by direct manipulation of the counter. Eliminates a multiplication per iteration.

#### (c) **Strength Reduction in Loops**
Replacing a multiplication-by-constant in a loop with an increment (addition).

---

## 2.4 Peephole Optimization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A **peephole** is a small, sliding window of consecutive instructions (typically 2–5). Peephole optimization examines these instructions and replaces inefficient instruction patterns with equivalent efficient ones.

### Canonical Peephole Transformations

| # | Pattern (Before) | Replacement (After) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `t1 = id * 2` | `t1 = id + id` | Replace mul with add |
| 2 | `goto L1; L1:` | (delete both) | Useless jump |
| 3 | `if id == 0 goto L1; ...; L1: if id == 0 goto L2` | `if id == 0 goto L2; ...; L1: if id == 0 goto L2` | Merge tests |
| 4 | `t1 = a; t2 = t1` | `t1 = a; (t2 = t1 removed)` | Copy propagation |
| 5 | `MOV R1, R2; MOV R1, R2` | `MOV R1, R2` | Redundant load |
| 6 | `MUL R, 1` | `MOV R, R` (or no-op) | Multiply by identity |

> **Why Peephole Works:** Most peephole patterns are **local syntactic rewrites** — they don't require expensive global dataflow analysis. A single pass over the instruction stream suffices (with iteration until fixpoint).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Semantic Equivalence | $\forall \vec{x}: P_{\text{orig}}(\vec{x}) = P_{\text{opt}}(\vec{x})$ | Required for every optimization |
| 2 | Available Expression | $AVAIL_{\text{out}}(B) = \bigcap_{P \in pred(B)} AVAIL_{\text{out}}(P)$ | Forward, intersection-based |
| 3 | Reaches Definition | $REACH_{\text{out}}(B) = GEN(B) \cup (REACH_{\text{in}}(B) - KILL(B))$ | Forward, union-based |
| 4 | Live Variable | $LIVE_{\text{in}}(B) = USE(B) \cup (LIVE_{\text{out}}(B) - DEF(B))$ | Backward, union-based |
| 5 | Induction Variable | $t_j = c_1 \cdot i + c_0$ | Linear in loop counter $i$ |
| 6 | Loop Nesting Speedup (LICM) | $\text{Saved ops} = (n - 1) \cdot \text{hoisted ops}$ | n = number of iterations |
| 7 | Strength Reduction Gain | $\text{Cost}(*) = 4 \cdot \text{Cost}(+)$ (typical RISC) | Roughly 4× for shifts |
| 8 | Optimal Speedup Bound (Amdahl) | $S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + f/k}$ | f = optimized fraction, k = speedup of that fraction |
| 9 | Peephole Window Size | $W \in [2, 5]$ instructions | Trade-off: size vs. cost |
| 10 | Basic Block Size | $|B| \geq 1$ instruction; terminates at branch/label | Maximal straight-line sequence |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Optimization Matters |
|---|---|
| **Compilers (GCC, LLVM, MSVC)** | Enable `-O0` to `-O3` flags; LLVM's `-O3` performs 200+ distinct optimizations. |
| **Embedded Systems (ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V)** | Code size and energy dominate. KTU students do a lab on MSP430/ARM optimization. |
| **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** | Vectorization + loop unrolling; matrix multiplication speedups of 50–100×. |
| **Database Query Engines** | SQL optimizers use **cost-based optimization** to choose the cheapest query plan. |
| **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilation (Java HotSpot, V8)** | Hot paths in JavaScript/bytecode are dynamically optimized at runtime. |
| **Operating Systems Kernels** | Scheduler, syscall paths are hand-optimized in assembly. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"Give a real-world example"*, always answer with: *Modern GCC at `-O2` performs ~50 optimization passes including LICM, inlining, and vectorization, reducing SPEC CPU benchmark runtimes by 30–60% on average.*

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Basic Block Construction (Full Board-Style Solution)

> **Source TAC Program (from KTU University Exam — May 2023):**
>
> ```
> (1)  t1 = 2 * a
> (2)  t2 = b + c
> (3)  t3 = 2 * a
> (4)  if t3 > t2 goto L1
> (5)  t4 = b + c
> (6)  t5 = t4 - t1
> (7)  goto L2
> (8) L1:  t6 = a - b
> (9)  t7 = t6 * 2
> (10) L2:  t8 = t1 + t7
> (11)     return t8
> ```

### Step 1: Identify the Leaders

| Rule | Application | Leader(s) |
|---|---|---|
| **First instruction** | (1) is the program's first instruction. | (1) |
| **Target of jump** | (4) has `goto L1` ⇒ (8) is a leader. | (8) |
| **Instruction after jump** | (5) follows (4), and (7) follows (5); (11) follows (10). | (5), (7)?, (11) |

Wait — (7) `goto L2` is also a branch. (11) immediately follows the jump at (7)? Let's re-check: (7) `goto L2` jumps to label L2, and the instruction physically after (7) is (8) which is `L1: t6 = a - b`. But (7) is also a leader because the *next* physical instruction (8) is a leader (it's also a label target). Actually, the rule says "instruction immediately following a conditional/unconditional jump". So (8) is already a leader. (11) is a leader because (10) is the instruction at L2, and (11) comes after the label L2 — but (10) is not a jump. So (11) is a leader only if the previous instruction is a jump. Since (10) is `t8 = t1 + t7` (not a jump), (11) is **not** a leader by this rule. However, (11) starts a new block because (10) is the end of a block we will form from (8)–(10).

Let me redo this carefully.

**Leaders:**
- (1) — first instruction.
- (4) — it is a conditional jump, so (5) is a leader. **AND** (4) is also the *last* instruction of its block, so (4) is itself a leader? No — only targets and instructions-after-jumps are leaders. (4) is a leader only if it is the *target* of another jump, which it is not. So (4) is in the block of (1).
- (5) — immediately follows the conditional jump (4).
- (8) — target of `goto L1` from (4).
- (10) — `L2:` is the target of `goto L2` from (7).

**Re-evaluating:** The labels themselves are not separate instructions — they are markers on (8) and (10). So instruction (8) and (10) are leaders.

**Final leaders:** (1), (5), (8), (10).

### Step 2: Form the Basic Blocks

$$
B_1 = \{(1),\,(2),\,(3),\,(4)\}
$$

$$
B_2 = \{(5),\,(6),\,(7)\}
$$

$$
B_3 = \{(8),\,(9)\}
$$

$$
B_4 = \{(10),\,(11)\}
$$

### Step 3: Construct the Control Flow Graph (CFG)

**Edges:**
- $B_1 \to B_2$ — fall-through (no jump at end of $B_1$ that skips $B_2$; the jump at (4) is conditional and targets $B_3$).
- $B_1 \to B_3$ — conditional jump `if t3 > t2 goto L1`.
- $B_2 \to B_4$ — unconditional `goto L2`.
- $B_3 \to B_4$ — fall-through.
- $B_4 \to \text{EXIT}$ — `return`.

> **KTU Tip:** Draw the CFG with **directed edges** and label each edge with the *condition* (e.g., "true", "false", "fall-through"). This is the standard KTU marking scheme.

### Step 4: Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) on $B_1$

Inside $B_1$:
- (1) computes `t1 = 2 * a`.
- (3) computes `t3 = 2 * a` — operands `2` and `a` are unchanged.

**CSE Result:**
$$
(3) \;\; t3 = 2 * a \quad \Rightarrow \quad (3') \;\; t3 = t1
$$

### Step 5: CSE across $B_2$ using reaching definitions

`b` and `c` are not redefined between (2) and (5). The expression `b + c` is **available** at the entry of $B_2$.

$$
(5) \;\; t4 = b + c \quad \Rightarrow \quad (5') \;\; t4 = t2
$$

### Step 6: Copy Propagation Cascade

After CSE: `t4 = t2`. Then (6) is `t5 = t4 - t1`. By **copy propagation**, replace `t4` with `t2`:
$$
(6') \;\; t5 = t2 - t1
$$

After CSE on (3): `t3 = t1`. The condition `if t3 > t2 goto L1` becomes:
$$
(4') \;\; \text{if } t1 > t2 \text{ goto L1}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Strength Reduction in a Loop

> **Original Loop (C code):**
> ```c
> for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
>     j = 4 * i;
>     a[j] = a[j] + 1;
> }
> ```

### Step 1: Naive TAC Translation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&i = 0 \\
&L: \quad t_1 = 4 * i \\
&\quad j = t_1 \\
&\quad t_2 = 4 * i \quad \text{(redundant — CSE applies!)} \\
&\quad j = t_2 \\
&\quad t_3 = a[j] \\
&\quad t_4 = t_3 + 1 \\
&\quad a[j] = t_4 \\
&\quad i = i + 1 \\
&\quad \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Identify Induction Variable

$$
j = 4 \cdot i \quad \text{is a linear function of } i.
$$

### Step 3: Strength Reduction — Replace Multiplication with Increment

Initialize $j$ outside the loop and increment it by 4 each iteration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&i = 0 \\
&j = 0 \\
&L: \quad t_3 = a[j] \\
&\quad t_4 = t_3 + 1 \\
&\quad a[j] = t_4 \\
&\quad i = i + 1 \\
&\quad j = j + 4 \quad \text{(replaces 2 multiplications with 1 add)} \\
&\quad \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Speedup

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Original cost per iteration} &= 2 \text{ MULs} + 1 \text{ ADD} + 1 \text{ LOAD} + 1 \text{ STORE} + 1 \text{ INC} \\
&\approx 2 \cdot 4 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 12 \text{ cycles} \\
\text{Optimized cost per iteration} &= 0 \text{ MULs} + 2 \text{ ADDs} + 1 \text{ LOAD} + 1 \text{ STORE} + 1 \text{ INC} \\
&\approx 0 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 5 \text{ cycles} \\
\text{Speedup} &= \frac{12}{5} = 2.4\times
\end{aligned}
$$

> For $n = 10^6$ iterations, this saves **7 million cycles** ≈ **2.3 ms** on a 3 GHz CPU.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Constant Folding + Propagation + DCE Cascade

> **Original TAC:**
> ```
> x = 2
> y = 3
> z = x + y        # can be folded
> w = z * 1        # strength reduction: w = z
> a = 10
> if a > 5 goto L1
> t = a + b
> L1:  return z
> ```

### Step 1: Constant Folding

`x = 2`, `y = 3` ⇒ `z = x + y = 5`. Replace `z = x + y` with `z = 5`.

### Step 2: Constant Propagation

`a = 10` is a constant. The condition `if a > 5 goto L1` becomes `if 10 > 5 goto L1` = `if TRUE goto L1`. The branch is now **unconditional**.

### Step 3: Dead Code Elimination

- `w = z * 1` is reduced to `w = z`, and `w` is never used ⇒ **delete `w = z`**.
- The false branch (`t = a + b`) is **unreachable** because the branch is now unconditional. **Delete `t = a + b`**.

### Step 4: Final Optimized TAC

```
x = 2
y = 3
z = 5
a = 10
goto L1
L1:  return z
```

> **Code size reduced from 7 instructions to 5**; the unreachable branch and the unused `w` are gone.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Apply Each Optimization

| Transformation | Required Pre-Condition | Side Effect / Risk | Estimated Speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Folding** | All operands must be literals | None (safe) | 5–10× for folded exprs |
| **CSE** | Operands unchanged between computations | None (safe) | 1.2–1.5× globally |
| **Copy Propagation** | Source of copy is not modified | May expose more CSE | 1.1–1.3× |
| **DCE** | Variable is dead (no live use) | None (safe) | 1.05–1.2× |
| **Strength Reduction** | Multiplication by a known constant | Code size may increase | 1.5–3× in loops |
| **LICM** | Loop-invariant computation, no side effects | Must respect aliasing | 2–10× in tight loops |
| **Inlining** | Function called in hot path | Code bloat risk | 1.2–2× |
| **Loop Unrolling** | Known trip count, small body | 2–4× code size | 1.2–1.8× |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall (Common Mistake):** Students often claim "DCE removes the assignment `x = 5` if `x` is not used." This is **wrong** if `x` is a *global* variable or has a *side effect* (e.g., a function call inside it). DCE is conservative.

---

## 3.5 Amdahl's Law — Quantifying Optimization Benefit

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{k}}
$$

where $f$ = fraction of execution time affected by the optimization, $k$ = speedup of that fraction.

**Example:** A program spends 40% of time in a loop. We apply LICM and reduce loop time by 50% (i.e., $k = 2$).

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.4) + \frac{0.4}{2}} = \frac{1}{0.6 + 0.2} = \frac{1}{0.8} = 1.25
$$

> **Maximum possible speedup** if we optimize *100%* of the program by $\infty$ is $1 / (1 - f)$. For $f = 0.4$, max $S = 1.67$. You cannot do better, no matter what optimization you apply.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"Why do we not apply all optimizations?"* — Answer: Amdahl's law + compilation time + code bloat + risk of regression. We stop when the marginal benefit < marginal cost.

## 4.5 Cost-Benefit Trade-off Curve (Conceptual Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Optimization Level O0 to O3] --> Y[Compilation Time]
    X --> Z[Code Speed]
    X --> W[Code Size]
    X --> V[Energy Efficiency]
    Y -->|increases| Y1[Linear Increase]
    Z -->|increases| Z1[Diminishing Returns]
    W -->|varies| W1[May Increase or Decrease]
    V -->|increases| V1[Improves]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Viva:** As optimization level increases (`-O0` → `-O1` → `-O2` → `-O3`), *compilation time* grows linearly, *speed* improves with diminishing returns (Amdahl's law), *size* may bloat (`-O3` unrolling), and *energy* improves proportionally to speed.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define code optimization. List any four criteria used to judge the quality of optimized code.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Code optimization is the phase of a compiler that transforms intermediate code into semantically equivalent code with improved efficiency in terms of execution time, memory usage, and/or power consumption.

> **Four Criteria (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Execution time** — fewer cycles, faster wall-clock run.
> 2. **Memory footprint** — reduced code/data size.
> 3. **Energy consumption** — critical for embedded/battery devices.
> 4. **Compilation time overhead** — the optimization should not slow compilation unacceptably.
> 5. *(Bonus)* **Code readability/diagnosability** — heavily optimized code is harder to debug.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is a Basic Block? Explain the concept of "Leaders" used in partitioning TAC into basic blocks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Basic Block (1 mark):** A maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions in which control flow enters only at the beginning and exits only at the end, with no internal branching or halting.

> **Leaders (2 marks):** The instructions that begin a new basic block. A statement is a *leader* if:
> 1. It is the *first* statement of the program.
> 2. It is the *target* of a conditional or unconditional jump.
> 3. It *immediately follows* a conditional or unconditional jump (i.e., the next statement after a branch).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Construct the basic blocks and the control flow graph (CFG) for the following three-address code. Identify leaders and justify each. **(7 marks)**

```
(1)  p = 5
(2)  q = 1
(3)  t1 = p * q
(4)  if t1 > 10 goto L1
(5)  p = p - 1
(6)  q = q + 2
(7)  t2 = p + q
(8)  if t2 < 20 goto L2
(9)  goto L3
(10) L1:  t3 = p * 2
(11)      q = t3 - q
(12)      goto L2
(13) L2:  t4 = p - q
(14) L3:  print t4
```

**(b)** Apply **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** and **Copy Propagation** on the basic blocks obtained. Show the optimized TAC. **(7 marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Basic Block Construction (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Identify Leaders (2 marks)**

| Rule | Application | Leader |
|---|---|---|
| First instruction | (1) is the program's first. | (1) |
| Target of jump | `goto L1` from (4) ⇒ (10). | (10) |
| Target of jump | `goto L3` from (9) ⇒ (14). | (14) |
| Target of jump | `goto L2` from (12) ⇒ (13). | (13) |
| Instruction after jump | (5) follows (4). | (5) |
| Instruction after jump | (9) follows (8). | (9) |

**Leaders set: {1, 5, 9, 10, 13, 14}**

**Step 2: Form Basic Blocks (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &= \{(1), (2), (3), (4)\} \\
B_2 &= \{(5), (6), (7), (8)\} \\
B_3 &= \{(9)\} \\
B_4 &= \{(10), (11), (12)\} \\
B_5 &= \{(13)\} \\
B_6 &= \{(14)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Draw the CFG (3 marks)**

Edges:
- $B_1 \to B_2$ (fall-through, since (4) is conditional).
- $B_1 \to B_4$ (true branch: `if t1 > 10 goto L1`).
- $B_2 \to B_3$ (true branch: `if t2 < 20 goto L2`, but L2 is in B5... wait, re-check).

> **Correction:** (8) `if t2 < 20 goto L2`. L2 is at (13), which is the *first* instruction of $B_5$. So $B_2 \to B_5$ is the true branch and $B_2 \to B_3$ is the false branch (fall-through).

Final edges:
- $B_1 \to B_2$ (fall-through / false branch).
- $B_1 \to B_4$ (true branch, goto L1).
- $B_2 \to B_3$ (false branch, fall-through to (9)).
- $B_2 \to B_5$ (true branch, goto L2).
- $B_3 \to B_6$ (goto L3).
- $B_4 \to B_5$ (goto L2).
- $B_5 \to B_6$ (fall-through).
- $B_6 \to \text{EXIT}$.

**CFG Diagram (for board display):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BA[Block B1]
        BAinstr["1 p equals 5\n2 q equals 1\n3 t1 equals p times q\n4 if t1 greater than 10 goto L1"]
    end
    subgraph BB[Block B2]
        BBinstr["5 p equals p minus 1\n6 q equals q plus 2\n7 t2 equals p plus q\n8 if t2 less than 20 goto L2"]
    end
    subgraph BC[Block B3]
        BCinstr["9 goto L3"]
    end
    subgraph BD[Block B4]
        BDinstr["10 t3 equals p times 2\n11 q equals t3 minus q\n12 goto L2"]
    end
    subgraph BE[Block B5]
        BEinstr["13 t4 equals p minus q"]
    end
    subgraph BF[Block B6]
        BFinstr["14 print t4"]
    end

    BA -->|false| BB
    BA -->|true| BD
    BB -->|false| BC
    BB -->|true| BE
    BC -->|goto L3| BF
    BD -->|goto L2| BE
    BE -->|fall through| BF
    BF -->|print| EXITNODE[EXIT]
```

#### **Part (b) — Apply CSE and Copy Propagation (7 marks)**

**CSE on $B_1$ and $B_2$:**
- (3) computes `t1 = p * q`. In $B_2$, (5) modifies `p` and (6) modifies `q`. So `p * q` is **not available** at $B_2$. ❌ No CSE here.
- (7) `t2 = p + q`. After (7), no further use of `p + q`. ❌ No CSE.

**CSE on $B_4$:**
- (10) `t3 = p * 2`. The expression `p * 2` is not computed elsewhere. ❌ No CSE.

**Copy Propagation across blocks:**
- In $B_1$, (1) `p = 5` is a copy of constant 5. (3) `t1 = p * q` could become `t1 = 5 * q`, but that is constant propagation, not copy propagation in the strict sense.
- In $B_4$, (11) `q = t3 - q` is not a copy.
- **Result:** No pure copy-propagation opportunities exist. The transformation result is identical to the original TAC, but the analysis itself scores full marks.

**Optimized TAC (after CSE only):**
No change — output equals input TAC.

> **Marks distribution (7 marks for part b):**
> - Identifying expressions considered for CSE: 2 marks.
> - Justifying why CSE does/does not apply: 3 marks.
> - Concluding with the (possibly unchanged) optimized TAC: 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't skip the leader justification table.** A "blank" CFG without labeled leaders loses 2 marks immediately.
> - **Don't forget the false-branch fall-through edge.** Many students draw only true branches. KTU penalizes missing edges.
> - **Don't confuse copy propagation with constant propagation.** Copy propagation replaces `x` with `y` (variable). Constant propagation replaces `x` with a literal.
> - **Don't apply CSE across loop iterations without checking redefinition.** This is a classic 2-mark deduction.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain **Loop Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** and **Strength Reduction** with suitable TAC examples. Show the original and optimized code for the following C snippet. **(7 marks)**

```c
int sum = 0;
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    x = a + b;
    sum = sum + x * i;
}
```

**(b)** What is **Peephole Optimization**? Enumerate any **six** peephole transformations with example instruction patterns. **(7 marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — LICM and Strength Reduction (7 marks)**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

> **LICM (1 mark):** A loop optimization that moves computations producing the *same value on every iteration* (loop-invariant) to a position *outside the loop*, thus reducing redundant computation.

> **Strength Reduction (1 mark):** Replacing an expensive operation (e.g., multiplication) with an equivalent cheaper one (e.g., addition) without changing the semantics.

**Step 1: Original TAC (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{sum} = 0 \\
&i = 0 \\
&L: \quad t_1 = a + b \quad \text{(loop-invariant!)} \\
&\quad x = t_1 \\
&\quad t_2 = x * i \quad \text{(expensive mul)} \\
&\quad \text{sum} = \text{sum} + t_2 \\
&\quad i = i + 1 \\
&\quad \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply LICM (2 marks):**

`a + b` does not depend on `i`, nor is `a` or `b` redefined in the loop. Hoist it:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{sum} = 0 \\
&i = 0 \\
&t_1 = a + b \quad \text{(moved outside)} \\
&x = t_1 \\
&L: \quad t_2 = x * i \\
&\quad \text{sum} = \text{sum} + t_2 \\
&\quad i = i + 1 \\
&\quad \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Strength Reduction (1 mark):**

Recognize that $t_2 = x \cdot i$ is an induction variable. Replace multiplication with incremental addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{sum} = 0 \\
&i = 0 \\
&t_2 = 0 \quad \text{(initialization of strength-reduced var)} \\
&L: \quad \text{sum} = \text{sum} + t_2 \\
&\quad i = i + 1 \\
&\quad t_2 = t_2 + x \quad \text{(strength-reduced: ADD instead of MUL)} \\
&\quad \text{if } i < n \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Quantify Speedup (1 mark):**

For $n = 10^6$ iterations:
- Original: $2 \times 10^6$ adds + $10^6$ muls.
- Optimized: $3 \times 10^6$ adds + 0 muls.
- Speedup ≈ 2.5× (assuming MUL is 4× cost of ADD).

#### **Part (b) — Peephole Optimization (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**

> Peephole optimization is a local transformation technique that examines a small sliding window (2–5 instructions) of the generated code and replaces inefficient instruction subsequences with equivalent efficient ones. The transformation is purely syntactic and does not require global dataflow analysis.

**Six Transformations (1 mark each = 6 marks):**

| # | Before | After | Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Redundant load/store elimination** | `MOV R1, A; MOV R1, A` | `MOV R1, A` | Save 1 instruction |
| **2. Useless jump elimination** | `goto L1; ... L1: goto L2` | `goto L2; ... L1: goto L2` | Eliminates dead path |
| **3. Strength reduction** | `MUL R, 2` | `ADD R, R` | Faster (ADD is 1 cycle) |
| **4. Constant folding** | `t = 3 + 5` | `t = 8` | Eliminates runtime add |
| **5. Copy propagation** | `t1 = a; t2 = t1` | `t1 = a` (remove t2) | Removes redundancy |
| **6. Control-flow simplification** | `if x == 0 goto L; if x == 0 goto L` | `if x == 0 goto L` (remove one) | Merges duplicate tests |

> **Bonus (extra credit):** Algebraic identities — `x * 1 = x`, `x + 0 = x`, `x * 0 = 0`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Peephole Questions:**
> - **Don't define "peephole" as "global optimization."** It is *strictly local* — operating on a window of 2–5 instructions.
> - **Don't give a peephole transformation that requires a loop.** It is single-pass; loops are global.
> - **Don't confuse peephole with peephole of *assembly code*.** KTU accepts both IR and assembly peepholes, but assembly is more common in board exams.
> - **Always state the WINDOW SIZE (2–5).** Forgetting it costs 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Code Optimization Introduction**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Optimization** = semantics-preserving program transformation improving time/space/power.
- ☐ **Basic Block** = maximal straight-line sequence; flow enters at start, exits at end.
- ☐ **Leader** = first statement of a BB; identified by 3 rules (first, jump-target, after-jump).
- ☐ **CFG** = directed graph with BBs as nodes and control flow as edges.
- ☐ **Peephole** = local sliding window of 2–5 instructions; purely syntactic.
- ☐ **Loop Invariant** = a computation that produces the same result on every iteration.

### The "Big Six" Transformations
- ☐ **CSE** — eliminate recomputation of available expressions (needs *Available Expressions* analysis).
- ☐ **Copy Propagation** — replace `x` with `y` after `x = y` (needs *Reaching Definitions*).
- ☐ **DCE** — remove assignments to dead variables (needs *Liveness* analysis).
- ☐ **Constant Folding** — evaluate constant expressions at compile time.
- ☐ **Strength Reduction** — replace `*` with `<<` or `+`; `**` with `*`; `/` with `>>`.
- ☐ **LICM** — hoist loop-invariant code outside the loop (needs *reaching defs* + *aliasing* check).

### Classification
- ☐ **Local** (within BB) vs. **Global** (within function) vs. **Interprocedural** (across functions).
- ☐ **Machine-Independent** (works on IR) vs. **Machine-Dependent** (exploits CPU features).
- ☐ **Peephole** vs. **Loop Optimization** vs. **Global** (three scopes).

### Numerical / Quantification
- ☐ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/k}$ — sets the upper bound on speedup.
- ☐ **Speedup of strength reduction:** mul-to-add typically gives 2–4× in tight loops.
- ☐ **LICM savings:** $(n-1) \cdot$ hoisted-ops cycles saved per execution.
- ☐ **Peephole window size:** 2–5 instructions (standard textbook value).

### Common Board Traps
- ☐ DCE is **conservative** — never delete a store to a global or volatile.
- ☐ CSE must verify **operands unchanged** between occurrences (not just syntactically equal).
- ☐ LICM is **invalid** if the hoisted expression has side effects (e.g., function call) or if the loop may execute zero times AND the side effect matters.
- ☐ Strength reduction is **machine-dependent in performance impact** (MUL is fast on x86, slow on embedded MCUs).
- ☐ Optimization is **not always beneficial** — Amdahl's law + compile-time cost trade-off.

### Quick Mnemonics
- ☐ **"F-C-C-D-S-L"** for the Big Six: **F**old, **C**opy-prop, **C**SE, **D**CE, **S**trength, **L**ICM.
- ☐ **"B-L-C"** for scope: **B**asic-Block, **L**oop, **C**FG (intra-function).
- ☐ **"L-S-G"** for KTU's three-scope classification.

### KTU Frequently Asked Topics (High-Probability)
- ☐ Basic block construction with leader identification — **almost certain** in every exam.
- ☐ DAG-based CSE (Module 5) — builds on this introduction.
- ☐ LICM with strength reduction — **favourite 14-mark question**.
- ☐ Peephole optimization with at least 4 transformations — **standard 3-mark question**.
- ☐ Definition + criteria of optimization — **guaranteed Part A question**.

> **End of Module 4 — Code Optimization Introduction Notes**
> **Total estimated study time: 6–8 hours for full mastery.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Opportunities for Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Opportunities for Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCRT604)**, the term **"Opportunities for Optimization"** refers to the systematic identification and exploitation of redundant, inefficient, or wasteful patterns in the **intermediate code (IR)** generated by the front-end of a compiler, with the objective of producing an **equivalent target program** that consumes **less time, less memory, or less power** while preserving program semantics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "Optimization is the optional phase of compilation that transforms an intermediate representation of a program into a semantically equivalent form that executes more efficiently in terms of one or more of: **running time, memory footprint, register usage, and energy consumption**, by exploiting redundancies and structural patterns in the code."

The "opportunities" are the **detection points** at which the compiler can apply an optimization — they exist as a direct consequence of how high-level language constructs are translated into low-level intermediate instructions.

### 1.2 Classification of Optimization Opportunities

The opportunities are broadly classified by the **scope** over which they operate:

| Opportunity Class | Scope of Analysis | Granularity | Typical Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local** | Within a single basic block | Intra-block | Local optimizer |
| **Global** | Across basic blocks (within a function/procedure) | Inter-block (intraprocedural) | Global optimizer |
| **Inter-procedural** | Across function boundaries | Whole-program | Interprocedural optimizer |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 4 specifically focuses on the **Machine-Independent Optimizations** that operate on the **Three-Address Code (TAC)** intermediate representation before register allocation and target code generation.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop Cleaning" Intuition

Imagine a chef preparing a multi-course meal (the **program**) in a kitchen (the **CPU**). The chef repeats several micro-actions across recipes:

- Goes to the pantry to grab salt **every single time**, even when a bowl of salt is already on the counter.
- Re-reads a recipe card that hasn't changed since morning.
- Stirs the pot clockwise then immediately counter-clockwise (a wasted pair of motions).
- Continues to chop vegetables on a plate that was already moved to the sink.

A smart assistant (the **Optimizer**) watches the chef and says:
1. "Keep the salt bowl on the counter — don't walk to the pantry." → **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**
2. "Stop re-reading the recipe; your memory is enough." → **Loop-Invariant Code Motion**
3. "Skipping the counter-stir is harmless." → **Dead Store Elimination**
4. "That plate isn't needed anymore." → **Dead Code Elimination**

**Optimization opportunities** are exactly such redundancies that the compiler's analyzer detects in the TAC.

### 1.4 The Source of Optimization Opportunities

> [!NOTE]
> Every optimization opportunity arises because **high-level language semantics permit more freedom than the naive translation enforces**. The compiler's naive translation is *correct* but *pessimistic*.

**Primary sources:**

1. **Redundant Computation** — The same expression is recomputed in multiple places.
2. **Constant Values** — Some operands are computable at compile time but are not evaluated until runtime.
3. **Loop Behavior** — A variable changes in a *predictable, regular* pattern inside a loop.
4. **Control-Flow Merging** — Multiple paths converge; the value at the merge point is invariant.
5. **Useless Code** — Code that computes a value never used, or code that can never be reached.
6. **Operator Cost Differences** — Some operators are cheaper than others (e.g., `*` by power-of-2 → `<<`).

### 1.5 Standard Optimization Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Vocabulary)

The board expects students to use these exact terms:

- **Speedup (S)** — ratio of original execution time to optimized execution time.
- **Code-size reduction** — measured as a percentage.
- **Register pressure** — the maximum number of simultaneously live temporaries.
- **Asymptotic Complexity Improvement** — rare but most powerful (e.g., loop fusion).

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse "Optimization" with "Target Code Generation".** Target code generation is a *mandatory, semantics-preserving mapping*; optimization is an *optional, semantics-preserving transformation* whose output is *still intermediate code* (or sometimes re-generated TAC).

---

### 1.6 The Pipeline Position of the Optimization Phase

In the canonical KTU 2024 compiler pipeline (Module-1 recap), the optimizer sits **between** the Intermediate Code Generator and the Code Generator:

$$
\text{Source} \xrightarrow{\text{Lex+Parse+Semantic}} \text{ICG} \xrightarrow{\text{Optimizer}} \text{Optimized IR} \xrightarrow{\text{CodeGen}} \text{Target}
$$

The **input** to the optimizer is **Three-Address Code (TAC)** — quadruples, triples, or indirect triples.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Principles Behind ALL Optimization Opportunities

Every opportunity for optimization is rooted in **one or more** of the following three principles. These are extremely high-yield for KTU essay-type questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle 1 — The Principle of Redundancy:**
> If the *same value* is guaranteed to be available at a *later use point* without any intervening redefinition, the recomputation is *redundant* and may be eliminated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle 2 — The Principle of Constancy:**
> If the *value* of an expression is computable at *compile time* (because all operands are known constants), it should be evaluated at compile time, not at runtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle 3 — The Principle of Reachability/Liveness:**
> If a *computation's result is provably never used* (dead), or a *code point is provably never reached* (unreachable), the computation/code is *useless* and may be removed.

### 2.2 Catalog of Major Optimization Opportunities (KTU Board-Favorite List)

The following opportunities appear in almost every KTU Module-4 question paper. The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to **name the opportunity, the principle behind it, and to show a TAC-level transformation**.

#### 2.2.1 Constant Folding
- **Principle:** Constancy
- **Logic:** When all operands of an expression are constants, the operator is applied at compile time.
- **Example:**
  - Original: `t1 = 3 + 5`
  - Optimized: `t1 = 8`

#### 2.2.2 Constant Propagation
- **Principle:** Constancy → Redundancy
- **Logic:** Replace all uses of a variable that holds a known constant with that constant itself.
- **Example:**
  - Original: `t1 = 5; x = t1 * 2`
  - Optimized: `x = 5 * 2` → (then Constant Folding) → `x = 10`

#### 2.2.3 Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)
- **Principle:** Redundancy
- **Logic:** If `E` is computed at two different points with no intervening modification of its operands, the second computation can be replaced by the value already computed.
- **Example:**
  - Original: `t1 = a + b; ... ; t3 = a + b; t4 = t3 * 2`
  - Optimized: `t1 = a + b; ... ; t3 = t1; t4 = t3 * 2`

#### 2.2.4 Copy Propagation
- **Principle:** Redundancy
- **Logic:** Replace `x = u; ... use(x) ...` with `use(u)` if `u` is not redefined before the use.
- **Example:**
  - Original: `x = t1; a = x + 1`
  - Optimized: `a = t1 + 1` (after which `x` becomes dead and may be eliminated).

#### 2.2.5 Dead Code Elimination
- **Principle:** Reachability/Liveness
- **Logic:** An instruction whose result is never used is removed.
- **Example:**
  - Original: `x = 5; y = x + 2; ...; return y` (and `x` is never read again)
  - Optimized: `y = 7; return y` (after constant prop + folding + DCE)

#### 2.2.6 Unreachable Code Elimination
- **Principle:** Reachability
- **Logic:** A basic block with no predecessor in the control-flow graph cannot execute; its code is removed.
- **Example:**
  - Original: `if (0) { ...dead... }` → the `if` is provably false at compile time.

#### 2.2.7 Strength Reduction
- **Principle:** Operator cost differences
- **Logic:** Replace an expensive operator with an equivalent cheaper one.
- **Mappings:**

$$
x \times 2 \equiv x \ll 1
$$

$$
x \times 2^{k} \equiv x \ll k
$$

$$
x / 2 \equiv x \gg 1 \quad \text{(for unsigned)}
$$

$$
x \cdot 2 \equiv x + x
$$

#### 2.2.8 Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)
- **Principle:** Redundancy + Loop behavior
- **Logic:** An expression whose operands are *all* defined *outside* the loop (or are loop-invariant themselves) is *hoisted* out of the loop and computed once.
- **Example:**
  - Original: `for (i=0; i<n; i++) { t = a + b; x[i] = t * i; }`
  - Optimized: `t = a + b; for (i=0; i<n; i++) { x[i] = t * i; }`

#### 2.2.9 Induction Variable Elimination
- **Principle:** Loop behavior
- **Logic:** In a loop, if a variable changes by a *constant* amount per iteration, multiplications involving it can be replaced by a *running addition* — eliminating one multiplication per iteration.
- **Example:**
  - Original: `for (i=0; i<n; i++) { A[2*i] = ...; }`
  - Optimized: `int k = 0; for (i=0; i<n; i++) { A[k] = ...; k = k + 2; }`

#### 2.2.10 Loop Unrolling
- **Principle:** Loop overhead reduction
- **Logic:** The loop body is replicated *k* times, reducing the number of branch+compare operations.
- **Example:**
  - Original: `for (i=0; i<4; i++) A[i] = 0;`
  - Optimized: `A[0]=0; A[1]=0; A[2]=0; A[3]=0;`

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most important reference** for KTU Module 4 numerical/analytical questions. Memorize the *mapping* column.

| Optimization | TAC Pattern (Before) | Transformed TAC (After) | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constant Folding | `t = a op b` (all const) | `t = const(a op b)` | All operands are literals |
| Constant Propagation | `x = k; ... y = x op z` | `... y = k op z` | No redefinition of `x` |
| CSE | `t1 = a+b; ... t2 = a+b` | `... t2 = t1` | No redefinition of `a` or `b` |
| Copy Propagation | `x = u; ... y = x op z` | `... y = u op z` | `u` not redefined |
| Dead Code Elim. | `t = expr;` | (instruction removed) | `t` is never read afterward |
| Strength Reduction | `t = x * 2` | `t = x << 1` | Multiplier is a power of 2 |
| LICM | `t = expr;` (in loop) | `t = expr;` (hoisted outside) | Operands are loop-invariant |
| Induction-Variable | `t = c * i;` (in loop) | `t_init = 0; t = t + c;` (each iter) | `i` is a basic induction var. |
| Loop Unrolling | `for (i=0;i<N;i++) body` | `body; body; ...;` (k copies) | Trip count known or small |

### 2.4 Cost & Benefit Analysis of Optimizations (Real Engineering Utility)

In real compiler engineering, every opportunity has a **cost** (analysis time, compilation time, register pressure) and a **benefit** (runtime savings, code size reduction). This trade-off is governed by the **Pareto Principle**: typically **20% of the optimizations yield 80% of the speedup** in production compilers like GCC and LLVM.

| Optimization | Compilation Cost | Runtime Benefit | When to Apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constant Folding | Very Low | Very High | Always |
| CSE | High (build avail. expr.) | Medium-High | Almost always |
| LICM | High (data-flow) | Very High (loops) | Tight inner loops |
| Strength Reduction | Low | Medium | Hot path only |
| Dead Code Elim. | Low (liveness) | Low-Medium | Always |
| Loop Unrolling | Medium | Variable (I-cache) | Tight loops with small bodies |

### 2.5 Peephole Optimization — A Special Local Opportunity

A **peephole** is a small, sliding window of consecutive instructions (typically 2–5 instructions). The peephole optimizer scans the window and applies local pattern-based transformations. It is the *simplest yet most effective* form of optimization and is **explicitly mentioned in the KTU 2024 syllabus**.

**Standard peephole transformations:**

1. **Redundant Load/Store Elimination** — `t = x; ... t = y;` → `t = y;` (if the first `t=x` is not read)
2. **Constant Folding** (peephole form)
3. **Strength Reduction** (peephole form)
4. **Null Sequence Elimination** — `MUL R, R, 0` → `CLR R` (sets to zero)
5. **Algebraic Simplification** — `a + 0 = a`, `a * 1 = a`, `a - a = 0`
6. **Jump-to-Jump Optimization** — `goto L1; ... L1: goto L2;` → `goto L2;`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Peephole optimization is a **target-machine-aware** opportunity set, but it can also be applied at the TAC level. The "peephole" concept tests students' ability to recognize patterns in a small instruction window.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Worked Example 1: Multi-Pass Optimization on TAC

**Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question):**
Apply **all possible machine-independent optimizations** to the following TAC segment and show each pass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t1} = 5 \\
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + \text{t1} \\
&\text{t3} = \text{b} + \text{t1} \\
&\text{t4} = \text{a} + \text{t1} \quad\quad \text{(duplicate of t2)} \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} * \text{t4} \\
&\text{t6} = \text{t5} + 0 \\
&\text{t7} = \text{t5} * 1 \\
&\text{a} = \text{t7} \\
&\text{if (t6) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 1 — Constant Folding & Constant Propagation:**
`t1` is defined as `5` and never redefined, so substitute `5` for `t1` everywhere.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + 5 \\
&\text{t3} = \text{b} + 5 \\
&\text{t4} = \text{a} + 5 \quad\quad \text{(now identical to t2)} \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} * \text{t4} \\
&\text{t6} = \text{t5} + 0 \\
&\text{t7} = \text{t5} * 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 2 — Algebraic Simplification (Peephole):**
`t6 = t5 + 0` simplifies to `t6 = t5`. `t7 = t5 * 1` simplifies to `t7 = t5`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + 5 \\
&\text{t3} = \text{b} + 5 \\
&\text{t4} = \text{a} + 5 \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} * \text{t4} \\
&\text{t6} = \text{t5} \\
&\text{t7} = \text{t5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 3 — Common Subexpression Elimination:**
`a` is not redefined between `t2` and `t4`. So `t4 = t2` (the expression `a+5` is the same).

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + 5 \\
&\text{t3} = \text{b} + 5 \\
&\text{t4} = \text{t2} \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} * \text{t2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 4 — Copy Propagation:**
`t4 = t2` → replace `t4` with `t2` in `t5`. Also `t6 = t5` → replace `t6` with `t5`. Also `t7 = t5` → replace `t7` with `t5`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + 5 \\
&\text{t3} = \text{b} + 5 \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} * \text{t2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 5 — Dead Code Elimination:**
`t3` is now unused (no later read of `t3` after the propagation). Remove it.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + 5 \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} * \text{t2} \\
&\text{a} = \text{t5} \\
&\text{if (t5) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Optimized TAC (4 instructions from 9 instructions — 55.5% reduction).**

---

### 3.2 Detailed Worked Example 2: Loop Optimization — LICM + Induction Variable

**Source Code:**

```c
for (i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
    x = a + b;          //  (a, b not modified in loop)
    A[i] = x * 4;       //  i increments by 1
}
```

**Naive TAC (inside the loop):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{L1: t1 = a + b} \\
&\text{t2 = i * 4} \\
&\text{t3 = t1 * 4} \\
&\text{A[t2] = t3} \\
&\text{i = i + 1} \\
&\text{if (i <= 100) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimization 1 — LICM:**
`a` and `b` are not modified in the loop. Hoist `t1 = a + b` out.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t1 = a + b} \quad \text{(moved outside)} \\
&\text{L1: t2 = i * 4} \\
&\text{t3 = t1 * 4} \\
&\text{A[t2] = t3} \\
&\text{i = i + 1} \\
&\text{if (i <= 100) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimization 2 — Strength Reduction (for `t1 * 4`):**
`4` is a power of 2. Replace `* 4` with `<< 2`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t1 = a + b} \\
&\text{L1: t2 = i << 2} \\
&\text{t3 = t1 << 2} \\
&\text{A[t2] = t3} \\
&\text{i = i + 1} \\
&\text{if (i <= 100) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimization 3 — Induction Variable Elimination:**
`t3` is invariant — hoist it as well. Now consider `t2 = i * 4` — instead of multiplying each iteration, maintain a running sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t1 = a + b} \\
&\text{t3 = t1 << 2} \\
&\text{k = 4} \quad\quad \text{(initial value of } t2 \text{ for } i=1 \text{, i.e., } 1 \cdot 4) \\
&\text{L1: A[k] = t3} \\
&\text{k = k + 4} \\
&\text{i = i + 1} \\
&\text{if (i <= 100) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Three multiplications and one add-per-iteration removed; replaced with a single add-per-iteration.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Criteria for Each Optimization

This is a **board-exam-tested matrix** that determines *which* optimizations are valid for a *given* code segment.

| Optimization | Requires | Invalidated by | Data Structure Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constant Folding | All operands const | Operand is variable | Literal-substitution |
| Constant Propagation | RHS const, no redefinition | Reassignment before use | UD-chain |
| CSE | Same expr, no redefinition | Operand redefined | Available expressions |
| Copy Propagation | `x=u`, no redefinition | Reassignment of `u` | UD-chain |
| DCE | No subsequent use | Use after definition | Liveness analysis |
| LICM | Operands loop-invariant | Operand modified in loop | Loop nesting forest |
| Induction Var. | `i` changes by const | `i` changes by non-const | Induction-var. table |
| Strength Red. | Multiplier is `2^k` | Multiplier non-power-of-2 | Pattern matcher |
| Peephole | Local pattern in 2-5 inst. | Cross-block semantics | Sliding window |

### 3.4 The Order-of-Application Theorem (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Order of Optimizations):** The set of *valid* optimization opportunities after one pass is a *superset* of the valid opportunities *after applying a richer set*; thus optimizations should be applied **repeatedly** until a **fixed point** is reached.

Formally, for an IR $I_0$ and a set of transformation rules $T = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$:

$$
I_{k+1} = T_j(I_k) \quad \text{for some } j \in \{1, \ldots, n\}
$$

and the loop terminates when:

$$
I_{k+1} = I_k \quad \text{(fixed point)}
$$

**Worked example of why fixed-point matters:**
- A round of *constant folding* may produce a *new* constant that enables a *later* round of *constant propagation*.
- Therefore, the optimizer must iterate until no rule fires.

### 3.5 Optimization Safety Conditions (Proof Sketches)

For each opportunity, the compiler must prove a **safety condition**. Board questions often ask: *"Why is this transformation valid?"*

| Opportunity | Safety Condition |
|---|---|
| CSE | No path from `t1=a+b` to `t2=a+b` redefines `a` or `b`. |
| LICM | The expression dominates the loop exit and all operands are invariant. |
| DCE | The variable has no live use in the remainder of the function. |
| Strength Reduction | The replacement is mathematically equivalent under the type rules. |
| Constant Folding | The expression is well-defined (no division by zero, no overflow for the type). |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Optimization Opportunity Identification Pipeline

The following block-level flow diagram shows the **canonical order** in which the compiler identifies and exploits opportunities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Source Program]) --> B0[Lexical Analysis]
    B0 --> C0[Syntax Analysis - Parse Tree]
    C0 --> D0[Semantic Analysis - Decorated AST]
    D0 --> E0[Intermediate Code Generator]
    E0 --> F0[Three-Address Code TAC]
    F0 --> G0{CFG Construction}
    G0 --> H0[Basic Block Partitioning]
    H0 --> I0[Local Optimization]
    I0 --> J0[Global Optimization Data-Flow]
    J0 --> K0[Loop Identification + LICM]
    K0 --> L0[Peephole Optimization]
    L0 --> M0[Fixed-Point Check]
    M0 -- Changed --> I0
    M0 -- No Change --> N0([Optimized IR])
    N0 --> O0[Code Generator]
    O0 --> P0([Target Code])
```

### 4.2 The Three-Principle Taxonomy of Opportunities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root([Optimization Opportunities]) --> P1[Principle 1: Redundancy]
    Root --> P2[Principle 2: Constancy]
    Root --> P3[Principle 3: Reachability or Liveness]
    P1 --> P1A[Common Subexpression Elimination]
    P1 --> P1B[Loop Invariant Code Motion]
    P1 --> P1C[Copy Propagation]
    P2 --> P2A[Constant Folding]
    P2 --> P2B[Constant Propagation]
    P3 --> P3A[Dead Code Elimination]
    P3 --> P3B[Unreachable Code Elimination]
    P3 --> P3C[Useless Store Elimination]
```

### 4.3 Data-Flow Information Required for Global Opportunities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A00[CFG Entry] --> B00[Basic Block B1]
    B00 --> B01[Basic Block B2]
    B00 --> B02[Basic Block B3]
    B01 --> C00[Basic Block B4]
    B02 --> C00
    C00 --> D00[CFG Exit]
    C00 --> E00[Available Expressions IN for B4]
    E00 --> F00{Is expr in Available OUT for B4's preds?}
    F00 -- Yes --> G00[Apply CSE]
    F00 -- No --> H00[Recompute the expression]
    C00 --> I00[Use-Def Chain for x]
    I00 --> J00{Is x redefined along path?}
    J00 -- No --> K00[Apply Copy Propagation]
    J00 -- Yes --> L00[Keep Original]
```

### 4.4 Loop Optimization Opportunity Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A000[Loop Header L1] --> B000[Loop Body L1.1]
    B000 --> C000{Is expression E invariant?}
    C000 -- Yes --> D000[Hoist E outside loop - LICM]
    C000 -- No --> E000{Is i a basic induction variable?}
    E000 -- Yes --> F000[Strength Reduce multiplications]
    E000 -- No --> G000{Is trip count known and small?}
    G000 -- Yes --> H000[Unroll the loop k times]
    G000 -- No --> I000[Keep loop as is]
    B000 --> J000{Are any stores dead?}
    J000 -- Yes --> K000[Apply Dead Store Elimination]
    J000 -- No --> L000[Continue]
```

### 4.5 Peephole Optimizer — Sliding-Window Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0([TAC Instruction Stream]) --> W0[Sliding Peephole Window - 3 to 5 inst.]
    W0 --> M00{Pattern Match}
    M00 -- Matches Redundant Load or Store --> R1[Eliminate Redundant Load or Store]
    M00 -- Matches Algebraic Identity --> R2[Apply Algebraic Simplification]
    M00 -- Matches Constant Operands --> R3[Apply Constant Folding]
    M00 -- Matches Power of 2 Multiplier --> R4[Apply Strength Reduction]
    M00 -- Matches Jump to Jump --> R5[Apply Jump Chaining]
    M00 -- No Match --> R6[Slide Window by 1 Instruction]
    R1 --> W0
    R2 --> W0
    R3 --> W0
    R4 --> W0
    R5 --> W0
    R6 --> W0
    W0 -- End of Stream --> OUT0([Optimized TAC])
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1, 3 Marks]**

*Define the term "optimization opportunity" in the context of compiler design. List any four common sources of optimization opportunities.*

**Model Answer (Board-Examiner-Worthy, 3-mark depth):**

An *optimization opportunity* is a *detectable pattern* in the intermediate representation of a program that allows a **semantics-preserving transformation** to reduce runtime, memory, or energy consumption.

**Four common sources:**

1. **Redundant computation** — same expression evaluated multiple times.
2. **Compile-time constant values** — operands available at compile time.
3. **Loop behavior** — predictable, regular variation of variables.
4. **Dead/unreachable code** — instructions whose results are never used or whose paths are never taken.

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark questions, KTU board expects: **1-mark definition + 1 mark for naming 2 sources + 1 mark for naming 2 more sources with one-line explanations.** Stick to this template.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Paper, 3 Marks]**

*Differentiate between **local optimization** and **global optimization**. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Local Optimization | Global Optimization |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | A single basic block | Multiple basic blocks within a function |
| Information Used | Just the instruction sequence | Control-flow graph, data-flow info |
| Complexity | $O(n)$ per block | $O(n \cdot \text{iterations})$ for the CFG |
| Example | Peephole: `t = x + 0; → t = x;` | Common Subexpression Elimination across a CFG |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B Module-4 questions carry **14 marks**, divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers **ONE** of the two choices in full.

---

### ✅ CHOICE A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, ESE Pattern]**

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]:**
*Explain the following machine-independent optimization techniques with one example each:*
*(i) Common Subexpression Elimination*
*(ii) Constant Folding*
*(iii) Dead Code Elimination*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) — 3 marks**

A *common subexpression* is an expression `E` that appears at two (or more) distinct program points such that the value of `E` is **guaranteed to be the same** at both points (i.e., none of the operands of `E` have been redefined along any path between the two points).

If the compiler can prove this, it can:
- Compute `E` at the first point, store in a temporary `t`.
- Replace the *second and later* occurrences of `E` with `t`.

**Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{a} = \text{b} + \text{c} \\
&\text{d} = \text{a} + 4 \\
&\text{e} = \text{b} + \text{c} \quad\quad \text{(duplicate)} \\
&\text{f} = \text{e} \cdot 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimized:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{a} = \text{b} + \text{c} \\
&\text{d} = \text{a} + 4 \\
&\text{e} = \text{a} \quad\quad \text{(reused, no recomputation)} \\
&\text{f} = \text{e} \cdot 2
\end{aligned}
$$

> One multiplication/addition of `b+c` is eliminated. The information used is the **Available Expressions** data-flow set.

**(ii) Constant Folding — 2 marks**

If *all* operands of an expression are *compile-time constants*, the operator is applied at compile time.

**Example:** `t1 = 3 * 4 + 5;` → `t1 = 17;`

This is a **purely local, pattern-based** optimization that requires no data-flow analysis.

**(iii) Dead Code Elimination — 2 marks**

An instruction whose *result is never used* (i.e., the result is not in any subsequent live-range) is removed.

**Example:** `x = 5;` followed by no read of `x` → instruction removed.

The information used is **Liveness Analysis**, computed via a backward data-flow pass:

$$
\text{Live}_{\text{out}}(B) = \bigcup_{S \in \text{succ}(B)} \text{Live}_{\text{in}}(S)
$$

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]:**
*Consider the following TAC segment. Apply **constant propagation, constant folding, common subexpression elimination, copy propagation, and dead code elimination** in the correct order. Show **each pass** explicitly.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t1} = 4 \\
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + \text{t1} \\
&\text{t3} = \text{t1} \cdot 2 \\
&\text{t4} = \text{a} + \text{t1} \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} + \text{t3} \\
&\text{x} = \text{t5} \\
&\text{y} = \text{t4} \\
&\text{if (x) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Solution (Board-Examiner Format):**

**Pass 1 — Constant Propagation (substitute `t1 = 4`):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + 4 \\
&\text{t3} = 4 \cdot 2 \\
&\text{t4} = \text{a} + 4 \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} + \text{t3} \\
&\text{x} = \text{t5} \\
&\text{y} = \text{t4} \\
&\text{if (x) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 2 — Constant Folding (`t3 = 4 * 2 = 8`):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + 4 \\
&\text{t3} = 8 \\
&\text{t4} = \text{a} + 4 \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} + 8 \\
&\text{x} = \text{t5} \\
&\text{y} = \text{t4} \\
&\text{if (x) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 3 — Common Subexpression Elimination (`t4 = a + 4` is same as `t2`):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + 4 \\
&\text{t3} = 8 \\
&\text{t4} = \text{t2} \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} + 8 \\
&\text{x} = \text{t5} \\
&\text{y} = \text{t4} \\
&\text{if (x) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 4 — Copy Propagation (`t4 = t2`, `y = t4` → `y = t2`):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + 4 \\
&\text{t3} = 8 \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} + 8 \\
&\text{x} = \text{t5} \\
&\text{y} = \text{t2} \\
&\text{if (x) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 5 — Dead Code Elimination (`t3 = 8` is now unused):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t2} = \text{a} + 4 \\
&\text{t5} = \text{t2} + 8 \\
&\text{x} = \text{t5} \\
&\text{y} = \text{t2} \\
&\text{if (x) goto L1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Optimized TAC — 5 instructions reduced from 8 (37.5% reduction).**

> **Verification of safety:** None of the program semantics are altered. The variable `a` is treated as an external input whose value is not changed inside the segment, so the transformations are valid.

---

### ✅ CHOICE B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Slot 1, 14-Mark Question]**

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]:**
*With suitable examples, explain **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** and **Induction Variable Elimination** as opportunities for loop optimization.*

**Model Solution:**

**Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM) — 3.5 marks**

A statement inside a loop is **loop-invariant** if:
- All of its operands are either *constants*, or
- Variables *defined outside the loop* with no *modification* inside the loop.

Such a statement computes the *same value* on every iteration and is therefore *redundant*. LICM **hoists** (moves) the statement to the **pre-header** of the loop, so it executes exactly *once* instead of *N* times.

**Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{for (i = 0; i < 100; i = i + 1)} \\
&\quad \text{t = a + b;} \quad\quad \text{// a, b not modified in loop} \\
&\quad \text{A[i] = t;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimized:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t = a + b;} \quad\quad \text{(hoisted)} \\
&\text{for (i = 0; i < 100; i = i + 1)} \\
&\quad \text{A[i] = t;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Benefit:** The addition `a + b` is performed **once** instead of 100 times.

**Induction Variable Elimination — 3.5 marks**

A variable `i` in a loop is a **basic induction variable** if its value on each iteration is a **linear function** of the loop count (e.g., `i = i + c` for some constant `c`). Expressions like `c * i` or `c * i + d` are **derived induction variables**.

The opportunity is to:
- Maintain the derived value by **addition** instead of **multiplication** each iteration.
- Replace the multiplication with a single running update.

**Example (naive):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
