&\text{for (i = 0; i < n; i = i + 1)} \\
&\quad \text{A[4 \cdot i] = 0;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Naive TAC (per iteration):** one multiplication for the index, one store.

**Strength-reduced + IV-eliminated TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{k = 0} \quad\quad \text{(initial value of 4*i)} \\
&\text{for (i = 0; i < n; i = i + 1)} \\
&\quad \text{A[k] = 0;} \\
&\quad \text{k = k + 4;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Benefit:** The per-iteration multiplication is replaced by a per-iteration addition — typically 2–3× faster on most architectures.

> **Note to examiner:** LICM and IV-elimination are *complementary*; LICM removes redundant *constant* expressions, while IV-elimination removes redundant *linear* expressions.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]:**
*Describe **Peephole Optimization** in detail. List any **five** standard peephole transformations and illustrate each with a TAC-level example.*

**Model Solution:**

**Peephole Optimization — 2 marks (definition + characteristics)**

Peephole optimization is a technique that examines a **small sliding window** (the "peephole") of consecutive instructions and replaces patterns that improve the code *without changing program semantics*. The peephole is typically **2 to 5 instructions** wide and slides one instruction at a time. It can be applied:

- On the **intermediate code** (machine-independent peephole), and
- On the **final target code** (machine-dependent peephole).

The transformations are *purely local*, so they require no data-flow analysis. The compiler iterates the peephole pass until a **fixed point** is reached.

**Five Standard Peephole Transformations — 5 marks (1 mark each)**

**(1) Redundant Load Elimination:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t1 = a[ i ]} \\
&\text{... no write to a[ i ] ...} \\
&\text{t2 = a[ i ]} \quad\rightarrow\quad \text{t2 = t1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2) Constant Folding (peephole form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t = 6 \cdot 7} \quad\rightarrow\quad \text{t = 42}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3) Strength Reduction (peephole form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t = x \cdot 2} \quad\rightarrow\quad \text{t = x \ll 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(4) Algebraic Identity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{t = a + 0} \quad\rightarrow\quad \text{t = a} \\
&\text{t = b \cdot 1} \quad\rightarrow\quad \text{t = b} \\
&\text{t = c - c} \quad\rightarrow\quad \text{t = 0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(5) Jump-to-Jump (Jump Chaining) Optimization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{goto L1} \\
&\text{...} \\
&\text{L1: goto L2} \quad\rightarrow\quad \text{goto L2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The intermediate label `L1` is removed if no other instruction targets it. This reduces the branch depth and improves the I-cache locality.

> **Board-Examiner's Note:** For 7-mark questions, a brief preamble (1 mark), definition (1 mark), and *each of the five transformations* with a small example (1 mark each) is the **standard valuation key**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Constant Folding with Constant Propagation.** Folding evaluates the *operator*; Propagation replaces the *variable*. Folding without Propagation leaves constants un-substituted.
> 2. **Forgetting to re-iterate.** After applying CSE, a *new* opportunity for DCE may arise. A single-pass answer loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Applying LICM when an operand *is* modified inside the loop** — invalid transformation, the compiler cannot guarantee safety.
> 4. **Ignoring the peephole's *sliding-window* nature.** Students often apply it to a whole basic block at once; the correct semantics is one window at a time.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the CFG** when global optimizations are applied. The KTU 2024 key explicitly awards 1–2 marks for the CFG in global questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section as a **last-night, last-hour** revision. Every bullet is a **frequently-asked KTU point**.

- **Definition:** Optimization is an *optional, semantics-preserving* transformation of intermediate code.
- **Pipeline position:** Optimizer sits between **Intermediate Code Generator** and **Code Generator** in the KTU 2024 pipeline.
- **Input:** Three-Address Code (TAC) — quadruples, triples, or indirect triples.
- **Three Foundational Principles:** Redundancy, Constancy, Reachability/Liveness.
- **Local vs Global:** Local operates within a basic block; global uses the **Control-Flow Graph (CFG)** and data-flow information.
- **Constant Folding** — operator on constants applied at compile time.
- **Constant Propagation** — variable with constant value replaced by the constant.
- **CSE** — reuse of a previously-computed expression; needs **Available Expressions** data-flow.
- **Copy Propagation** — replace `x = u` with `u` at use sites.
- **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)** — needs **Liveness Analysis** (backward data-flow).
- **Strength Reduction** — replace expensive ops with cheaper equivalents (`*2` → `<<1`).
- **Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** — hoist invariant code to pre-header.
- **Induction Variable Elimination** — replace `c*i` with running addition.
- **Loop Unrolling** — replicate body to reduce branch overhead.
- **Peephole Optimization** — small sliding-window pattern matching; **2–5 instructions** wide.
- **Peephole Transformations** — redundant load, constant folding, strength reduction, algebraic identity, jump-to-jump.
- **Order of Application** — repeat passes until a **fixed point** is reached.
- **Safety Proof** — every transformation must be backed by a *data-flow invariant* (available expression, liveness, dominance, etc.).
- **CFG** — nodes are basic blocks, edges are control transfers. Essential for global opportunities.
- **Basic Block** — maximal sequence of instructions with a single entry and a single exit.
- **Leader** — first instruction of a basic block (per Aho/Sethi/Ullman algorithm).
- **Data-Flow Equations** — `OUT[B] = gen[B] \cup (IN[B] - kill[B])` for forward analyses.
- **Common Pitfall in Exam** — applying a global opportunity without drawing the CFG first.
- **Cost vs Benefit** — LICM, CSE are *expensive to compute* but *high benefit*; Folding and algebraic identity are *cheap to compute* and *always beneficial*.
- **The Pareto Principle** — 20% of optimizations deliver 80% of speedup in production compilers.

---

**End of Notes — Module 4 / Topic: Opportunities for Optimization — KTU 2024 Scheme (PBCRT604)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scope of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scope of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of compiler design, **Scope of Optimization** refers to the **extent or boundary (region of code) over which an optimization transformation is applied** by the compiler. It defines *how far the compiler can "look" while attempting to improve the intermediate or target code* in terms of time, space, or power consumption.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PBCRT604, Module 4), the scope of optimization is broadly classified into the following hierarchical regions of the source program:

1. **Local Scope** — within a single **basic block** (no branches in/out except at the entry/exit).
2. **Global Scope** — across multiple basic blocks **within a single procedure/function** (driven by data-flow analysis on a flow graph).
3. **Loop Scope** — within the body of a loop (the most performance-critical region; executes repeatedly).
4. **Inter-Procedural Scope** — across multiple procedures/files (whole-program optimization).
5. **Peephole Scope** — over a small sliding window of target instructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *"Optimization is the process of transforming a program to make it more efficient (faster, smaller, less power-hungry) without changing its observable output. The **scope** of optimization determines the region of the program on which these transformations operate."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note:** The word *"optimization"* in compilers is a misnomer. The compiler produces *improved* code, not provably *optimal* code. Hence, the **scope** determines the *guarantee of improvement* — a larger scope usually yields better code but with exponentially higher compile-time cost.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House-Cleaning Metaphor"

Imagine you are cleaning a house to make it efficient:

| Cleaning Action | Compiler Analogue | Scope of Action |
|---|---|---|
| Wiping a single **shelf** | **Local Optimization** (e.g., constant folding) | One basic block |
| Rearranging **one room** | **Global Optimization** | One function/procedure |
| Cleaning the **entire floor** (all rooms) | **Inter-Procedural Optimization** | Whole program |
| Inspecting a **window of 2-3 items** | **Peephole Optimization** | Small instruction window |
| Reorganizing things that run **in a circle** (daily routine) | **Loop Optimization** | Loops |

Just as a wider cleaning scope (whole house) gives better results but takes more time, a **larger optimization scope** in compilers produces better code but consumes more compile-time memory and CPU cycles.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used to *quantify* optimization across all scopes:

- **Code Size Reduction (ΔS)** — measured in bytes/instructions saved.
- **Execution Time Speedup (ΔT)** — measured as a percentage or speedup factor.
$$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{unoptimized}}}{T_{\text{optimized}}} $$

- **Compile-Time Overhead (ΔC)** — the time spent in the optimizer.
- **Power Consumption (ΔP)** — relevant for embedded/IoT compilers (KTU emphasis: especially in **CST 304 — Embedded Systems** continuity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 4 explicitly demands students understand that *the scope of optimization is a trade-off triangle between (i) code quality, (ii) compile time, and (iii) memory usage of the optimizer itself.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Optimization Scopes

The compiler applies transformations in a layered fashion, with each layer operating on a progressively larger region of code.

#### **A. Peephole Optimization (Smallest Scope)**

- Operates on a **sliding window** (typically 2–5 target instructions).
- Replaces inefficient instruction sequences with equivalent, faster ones.
- **Why:** Simplest to implement; runs at the end of code generation.
- **How:** Pattern-match on a small set of templates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classic Peephole Transformations (KTU must-know):**
> 1. Redundant `MOV` elimination: `MOV R1, R2; MOV R2, R1` → second is redundant.
> 2. Strength reduction at machine level: `MUL R, 2` → `SHL R, 1`.
> 3. Constant folding in assembly: `ADD R1, #0` → removed.
> 4. Jump-to-jump: `JMP L1; ... L1: JMP L2` → replaced with `JMP L2`.
> 5. Algebraic: `MUL R, 1` → removed.

#### **B. Local Optimization (Basic-Block Scope)**

- Operates on a **single basic block** — a maximal sequence of consecutive statements where flow enters at the beginning and leaves at the end, with no branching in the middle.
- **Why:** All statements in a block are guaranteed to execute in sequence; their values are deterministically available.
- **How:** Build a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** of the block; identical subexpressions share nodes; dead code has no outgoing edges.

**Standard Local Transformations:**
- **Constant Folding:** Evaluate constant expressions at compile time.
- **Constant Propagation:** Substitute a variable's known constant value.
- **Algebraic Simplification:** `x+0 → x`, `x*1 → x`, `x*0 → 0`, `x² → x*x`.
- **Strength Reduction:** Replace expensive ops with cheaper ones (`x² → x*x`, `x/2 → x>>1`).
- **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE) within block:** Compute once, reuse.
- **Dead Code Elimination:** Remove assignments never read.

#### **C. Global Optimization (Function/Procedure Scope)**

- Operates across **all basic blocks of a single procedure** using a **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**.
- **Why:** A value computed in one block may be available in another; cross-block redundancy exists.
- **How:** Use **Data-Flow Analysis** to compute information like:
  - **Available Expressions**
  - **Reaching Definitions**
  - **Live Variable Analysis**
  - **Use-Def / Def-Use Chains**
- Iterative fixed-point computation on CFG nodes.

**Key Global Techniques (KTU-high-priority):**
- Global Common Subexpression Elimination
- Copy Propagation
- Dead Code Elimination (global)
- Global Constant Propagation
- Global Code Motion (Hoisting)
- Induction Variable Elimination (also falls under loops)

#### **D. Loop Optimization (Loop Scope)**

- Operates within the **natural loops** of a CFG.
- **Why:** Loops execute many times → small per-iteration savings = huge total savings (Amdahl's Law).
- **How:** Identify loops via **Dominator trees** (a loop is a strongly connected region with a single entry, the *header*).

**Major Loop Optimizations:**
- **Code Motion (Loop-Invariant Code Motion – LICM):** Hoist loop-invariant computations out of the loop.
- **Strength Reduction in Loops:** Replace `i*4` with successive additions.
- **Induction Variable Elimination:** Remove auxiliary induction variables.
- **Loop Unrolling:** Replicate the body to reduce branch overhead.
- **Loop Fusion / Fission:** Combine or split adjacent loops.
- **Loop Interchange:** Reorder nested loops for better cache locality.
- **Loop Jamming:** Combine nested loops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** *"90% of execution time is spent in 10% of the code — and most of that 10% is loops."* — hence, loop optimization has the **highest payoff per unit scope**.

#### **E. Inter-Procedural Optimization (IPO / Whole-Program Scope)**

- Operates across **function/method boundaries**, possibly across translation units.
- **Why:** Procedures have call-site-specific specialization opportunities.
- **How:** Build a **Call Graph**; perform summary-based or inlining-based analysis.
- **Examples:** Function Inlining, Procedure Cloning, Inter-procedural Constant Propagation, Dead Code Elimination of unused procedures.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Scope | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Block | Maximal straight-line code sequence | Local | Single entry, single exit |
| 2 | Flow Graph | G = (N, E); N = basic blocks, E = branch edges | Global | Nodes = blocks, edges = control flow |
| 3 | Speedup Factor | $S = \frac{T_{\text{orig}}}{T_{\text{opt}}}$ | All | $S \geq 1$ means improvement |
| 4 | Amdahl's Law | $S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{S_{\text{part}}}}$ | All/Loop | $f$ = fraction optimized |
| 5 | Strength Reduction | $x * 2^k \rightarrow x \ll k$ | Local/Loop | Multiplication → shift |
| 6 | LICM Safety | Code must be **loop-invariant** AND **in every path** | Loop | Hoist to pre-header |
| 7 | Induction Variable | $i_{k+1} = i_k + c$, $c$ = const | Loop | Replace `i*c` with additions |
| 8 | Dominator | $d$ dominates $n$ iff every path to $n$ goes via $d$ | Global | Foundation for loop identification |
| 9 | Inlining Gain | $S_{\text{gain}} \approx \frac{\text{Call Overhead} + \text{Arg Setup}}{\text{Callee Size}}$ | Inter-procedural | Trade-off with code size |
| 10 | Peephole Window | $W = 2$ to $5$ instructions | Peephole | Sliding window technique |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Compilers

| Compiler | Optimization Scope Strategy |
|---|---|
| **GCC** (`-O0` to `-O3`) | `-O0` = none, `-O1` = local + peephole, `-O2` = global, `-O3` = loop + IPO |
| **LLVM** | Modular pass manager; each pass has an explicit *scope* tag |
| **JVM HotSpot** | Adaptive (on-stack replacement) — scopes decided at **runtime** |
| **Embedded GCC** | Limited scope to save ROM/RAM; often only `-Os` (size) optimization |
| **TensorFlow XLA** | Whole-program + tensor-scope fusion |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industrial Linkage:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, students are expected to map each optimization scope to **real GCC/Clang optimization flags** during their lab work — a question often asked in Part B (14-mark) exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples (Analytical Matrix)

This section presents **exhaustive, step-by-step worked examples** for each scope of optimization, using the standard KTU 3-Address Code (3AC / TAC) intermediate representation.

---

### 3.1 Example 1 — **Local Scope (Basic Block): Constant Folding & Strength Reduction**

**Given Source Code:**
```c
x = 2 * 3;
y = x + 0;
z = y * 8;
w = z / 2;
```

**Step 1 — Generate the initial 3-Address Code (TAC):**
```
t1 = 2 * 3
x  = t1
t2 = x + 0
y  = t2
t3 = y * 8
z  = t3
t4 = z / 2
w  = t4
```

**Step 2 — Apply Constant Folding (local scope, on `t1`):**
$$ 2 \times 3 = 6 $$
```
t1 = 6
x  = t1
t2 = x + 0
y  = t2
t3 = y * 8
z  = t3
t4 = z / 2
w  = t4
```

**Step 3 — Apply Constant Propagation (replace `x` with `t1 = 6`):**
```
t1 = 6
x  = 6
t2 = 6 + 0
y  = t2
...
```

**Step 4 — Apply Algebraic Simplification (`x + 0 → x`):**
$$ 6 + 0 = 6 $$
```
t2 = 6
y  = 6
t3 = y * 8
z  = t3
t4 = z / 2
w  = t4
```

**Step 5 — Apply Constant Propagation (`y = 6`):**
```
t3 = 6 * 8
z  = t3
t4 = z / 2
w  = t4
```

**Step 6 — Apply Constant Folding again:**
$$ 6 \times 8 = 48 $$
```
t3 = 48
z  = 48
t4 = 48 / 2
w  = t4
```

**Step 7 — Apply Strength Reduction (`*8` was already folded, but now `/2` becomes shift):**
$$ 48 \div 2 = 24 $$
```
t3 = 48
z  = 48
t4 = 48 >> 1
w  = 24
```

**Step 8 — Constant Propagation again:**
```
z  = 48
t4 = 24
w  = 24
```

**Step 9 — Dead Code Elimination** (unused temporaries and final-value propagation may apply):
```
w  = 24
z  = 48
```

**Result:** 8 instructions reduced to **2 essential instructions** — a 75% reduction *within the basic block*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** This entire transformation required **no information outside the single basic block** — confirming it is a *local* optimization.

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — **Global Scope: Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)**

**Given Source Code:**
```c
a = b + c;
d = b + c;
e = a + d;
```

**Step 1 — Initial TAC:**
```
t1 = b + c
a  = t1
t2 = b + c    <-- duplicate computation
d  = t2
t3 = a + d
e  = t3
```

**Step 2 — Identify the Common Subexpression:**
Both `t1 = b + c` and `t2 = b + c` compute the same expression on operands that are **not redefined** between the two statements.

**Step 3 — Apply Global CSE:**
Replace `t2 = b + c` with `t2 = t1` (use the previously computed value):
```
t1 = b + c
a  = t1
t2 = t1
d  = t2
t3 = a + d
e  = t3
```

**Step 4 — Apply Copy Propagation (chain `a = t1` and `t2 = t1`):**
```
t1 = b + c
a  = t1
d  = t1
t3 = t1 + t1
e  = t3
```

**Step 5 — Final Optimized TAC:**
```
t1 = b + c
d  = t1
t3 = t1 + t1
e  = t3
```

**Result:** Saved **one addition** and two temporaries. This required a **reaching-definitions analysis** across basic blocks to ensure `b` and `c` were not redefined — hence it is a *global* optimization.

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — **Loop Scope: Code Motion (LICM) and Induction Variable Strength Reduction**

**Given Source Code:**
```c
for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
    x = 2 * pi;
    A[i] = i * 4;
}
```

**Step 1 — Initial TAC (loop body):**
```
L1: if i >= 100 goto L2
    t1 = 2 * pi
    x  = t1
    t2 = i * 4
    t3 = A + i
    *t3 = t2
    i  = i + 1
    goto L1
L2:
```

**Step 2 — Identify Loop-Invariant Code:**
`2 * pi` does not depend on `i` → it is **loop-invariant**. Hoist it out (LICM):
```
    t1 = 2 * pi          <-- moved to pre-header
    x  = t1
L1: if i >= 100 goto L2
    t2 = i * 4
    t3 = A + i
    *t3 = t2
    i  = i + 1
    goto L1
L2:
```

**Step 3 — Identify Induction Variables:**
`i` is a basic induction variable (incremented by 1). Then `i*4` is a derived induction variable (incremented by 4 each iteration).

**Step 4 — Apply Induction Variable Strength Reduction:**
Replace the multiplication `i * 4` with an addition: introduce a new induction variable `s`, initially `s = 0`, and increment `s = s + 4`:
```
    t1 = 2 * pi
    x  = t1
    s  = 0                   <-- initial value
L1: if i >= 100 goto L2
    t2 = s                   <-- replaces i*4
    t3 = A + i
    *t3 = t2
    i  = i + 1
    s  = s + 4               <-- cheap addition
    goto L1
L2:
```

**Result:** Eliminated one multiplication per iteration. Over 100 iterations, this is **100 multiplications → 1 multiplication + 100 additions** = significant speedup, with no semantic change.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scope | Region | Information Required | Typical Techniques | Compile Cost | Payoff |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Peephole** | 2–5 instructions | Local template | Pattern match | Very Low | Low |
| **Local** | 1 basic block | DAG within block | Folding, propagation, CSE | Low | Medium |
| **Global** | 1 procedure | Data-flow over CFG | CSE, copy prop, dead code | High | High |
| **Loop** | 1 natural loop | Dominators, IV analysis | LICM, strength reduction, unrolling | High | **Highest** |
| **Inter-procedural** | Whole program | Call graph, summary info | Inlining, cloning, IPO-CSE | **Very High** | High (selective) |

### 3.5 Formal Derivation of Amdahl's Law for Loop Optimization

Let:
- $T_{\text{loop}}$ = time spent inside the loop in the original program
- $T_{\text{rest}}$ = time spent outside the loop
- $f$ = fraction of total time spent in the loop
$$ f = \frac{T_{\text{loop}}}{T_{\text{loop}} + T_{\text{rest}}} $$

If the loop is sped up by a factor $S_{\text{loop}}$:
$$ T_{\text{new}} = \frac{T_{\text{loop}}}{S_{\text{loop}}} + T_{\text{rest}} $$

$$ T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{rest}} + \frac{f \cdot T_{\text{total}}}{S_{\text{loop}}} $$

$$ T_{\text{new}} = (1 - f) \cdot T_{\text{total}} + \frac{f \cdot T_{\text{total}}}{S_{\text{loop}}} $$

Dividing by $T_{\text{total}}$:

$$ S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{new}}} = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{\text{loop}}}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** If $f = 0.5$ (50% time in loop) and $S_{\text{loop}} = 10$, then:
> $$ S_{\text{overall}} = \frac{1}{0.5 + \frac{0.5}{10}} = \frac{1}{0.55} \approx 1.82 $$
> Even a 10× loop speedup gives only 1.82× total speedup — illustrating the **necessity of expanding the optimization scope** beyond loops when possible.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Scope of Optimization

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Source Program IR] --> B[Scope of Optimization Selector]
    B --> C[Peephole Scope]
    B --> D[Local Scope]
    B --> E[Global Scope]
    B --> F[Loop Scope]
    B --> G[Inter-Procedural Scope]

    C --> C1[Sliding Window 2-5 instr]
    C --> C2[Pattern Match Templates]
    C --> C3[Output: Tightened Assembly]

    D --> D1[Single Basic Block]
    D --> D2[Build DAG]
    D --> D3[Constant Folding / Algebraic / CSE]
    D --> D4[Output: Block-Optimized IR]

    E --> E1[Control Flow Graph CFG]
    E --> E2[Data Flow Analysis]
    E --> E3[Available Expr, Live Vars]
    E --> D4

    F --> F1[Identify Natural Loops]
    F --> F2[Dominator Tree]
    F --> F3[LICM, IV Reduction, Unrolling]
    F --> F4[Output: Loop-Optimized IR]

    G --> G1[Build Call Graph]
    G --> G2[Inlining, Cloning, IPO-CSE]
    G --> G3[Output: Whole-Program IR]

    C3 --> H[Final Target Code]
    D4 --> H
    F4 --> H
    G3 --> H

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff9900
    style H fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Optimization Pipeline)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source Code] --> TAC1[3-Address Code]
    TAC1 --> P1[Peephole Pass]
    P1 --> P2[Local Optimization DAG]
    P2 --> P3[Global DFA Pass]
    P3 --> P4[Loop Identification]
    P4 --> P5[LICM and IV Reduction]
    P5 --> P6[Inter-Procedural Pass]
    P6 --> CG[Code Generator]
    CG --> ASM[Optimized Assembly]

    P2 -. Feedback .-> TAC1
    P3 -. Iterate to Fixed Point .-> P2
    P5 -. Feedback .-> P4

    style SRC fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style TAC1 fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff9900
    style ASM fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow: Choosing Optimization Scope

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Compile-time budget available?}
    Q1 -- No --> A1[Local + Peephole Only]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is the code in a hot loop?}
    Q2 -- No --> A2[Global + Local + Peephole]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Procedures called frequently?}
    Q3 -- No --> A3[Loop + Global + Local + Peephole]
    Q3 -- Yes --> A4[Full IPO + Loop + Global + Local + Peephole]

    style A1 fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#cc0000
    style A2 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cca300
    style A3 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style A4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Data-Flow Analysis Backbone of Global Scope

```mermaid
graph TD
    CFG[Control Flow Graph] --> RDA[Reaching Definitions]
    CFG --> AE[Available Expressions]
    CFG --> LV[Live Variable Analysis]

    RDA --> GCSE[Global CSE]
    RDA --> GCP[Global Constant Propagation]
    AE --> GCSE
    LV --> GDCE[Global Dead Code Elimination]
    GCP --> GDCE

    style CFG fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style GCSE fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style GCP fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style GDCE fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The scope of optimization is a *concentric onion* — the inner scopes (peephole, local) are *always* applied; the outer scopes (global, loop, IPO) are *progressively enabled* as compile-time budget permits, guided by profiling feedback in modern compilers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *Define the term "Scope of Optimization" in the context of compilers. List and briefly describe the different scopes of optimization discussed in the KTU syllabus.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — ~80–100 words):**

**Scope of optimization** refers to the *region of the program* over which a compiler applies an optimization transformation. It determines how much of the code the optimizer "sees" while making improvements. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies optimization scope into five hierarchical categories:

1. **Peephole scope** — small sliding window of 2–5 target instructions.
2. **Local scope** — within a single basic block.
3. **Global scope** — across all basic blocks of a single procedure (driven by data-flow analysis).
4. **Loop scope** — within natural loops of a procedure.
5. **Inter-procedural scope** — across multiple procedures (whole-program).

A larger scope generally yields better optimization but at higher compile-time cost.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing 4–5 scopes with one-line descriptions.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *Why are loop optimizations considered to provide the highest payoff among all scopes of optimization? Justify with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — ~80–100 words):**

Loops are considered the highest-payoff scope because, by **Amdahl's Law**, a small per-iteration saving multiplies over many iterations. For instance, consider a loop executing 1,000,000 times:

```c
for (i = 0; i < 1000000; i++) {
    x = 2 * pi;          // loop-invariant
    A[i] = i * 4;
}
```

Hoisting the invariant `2 * pi` out of the loop saves 999,999 multiplications. Similarly, replacing `i * 4` with a running addition eliminates 999,999 multiplications. Since 90% of execution time is concentrated in loops, even minor loop-scoped optimizations yield disproportionately large speedups, making loop scope the most performance-critical.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for Amdahl's reasoning, 1 mark for invariant code example, 1 mark for IV strength reduction example.)*

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)**

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**. Students answer **one** full question from the choice pair.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Modeled on KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**a)** Explain the concept of **scope of optimization** in detail. Compare **local**, **global**, and **loop** scopes with respect to (i) region of operation, (ii) information required, (iii) compile-time cost, and (iv) typical optimizations. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Consider the following source code:
```c
for (i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
    t = 6 * 3.14;            // statement 1
    A[i] = t * i;            // statement 2
    B[i] = A[i] + 5;         // statement 3
}
```
Apply **(i) Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)** and **(ii) Strength Reduction / Induction Variable elimination** to optimize the loop. Show the optimized 3-address code. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **scope of optimization** is the boundary/extent of code over which an optimization transformation acts. The hierarchy of scopes is:

1. **Local Scope (Basic Block Scope):** Operates on a single basic block — a maximal sequence of straight-line code with single entry and single exit. Build a DAG; identify common subexpressions, dead code. Information required: only within-block data dependencies. Cost: low. Examples: constant folding, algebraic simplification, block-level CSE, dead-store elimination.

2. **Global Scope (Procedure Scope):** Operates across all basic blocks of one procedure using a **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**. Information required: data-flow analysis (reaching definitions, available expressions, live variables). Cost: medium-high (iterative fixed-point computation). Examples: global CSE, copy propagation, global dead code elimination, constant propagation across blocks.

3. **Loop Scope:** Operates within **natural loops** identified via dominator trees. Information required: loop structure, induction variable analysis, alias information. Cost: high but justified by Amdahl's Law. Examples: LICM, strength reduction, induction variable elimination, loop unrolling, loop fusion, interchange.

**Comparison Table (Mandatory for 7-mark answer):**

| Aspect | Local | Global | Loop |
|---|---|---|---|
| Region | One basic block | All blocks of one procedure | Natural loop |
| Information | Intra-block DAG | CFG + data-flow | Dominators + IVs |
| Cost | Low | Medium-High | High |
| Payoff | Medium | High | Highest |
| Technique basis | DAG equivalence | Iterative DFA fixed point | Dominator tree + IV analysis |

*(7 marks: 2 for definition + 2 for scope descriptions + 2 for comparison table + 1 for concluding remark on trade-offs.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Initial 3-Address Code (TAC) of the loop:**
```
    i  = 1
L1: if i > 100 goto L2
    t1 = 6 * 3.14
    t  = t1
    t2 = t * i
    t3 = 4 * i            (address calc, omitted for brevity)
    A[i] = t2
    t4 = A[i]
    t5 = t4 + 5
    B[i] = t5
    i  = i + 1
    goto L1
L2:
```

**Step 2 — Identify Loop-Invariant Code:**
- `6 * 3.14` does not depend on `i` → **loop-invariant**.
- `t1 = 6 * 3.14` and `t = t1` are invariant.

**Step 3 — Apply LICM (Hoist to pre-header):**
```
    t1 = 6 * 3.14              <-- hoisted
    t  = t1                    <-- hoisted
    i  = 1
L1: if i > 100 goto L2
    t2 = t * i
    A[i] = t2
    t4 = A[i]
    t5 = t4 + 5
    B[i] = t5
    i  = i + 1
    goto L1
L2:
```

**Step 4 — Identify Induction Variables:**
- `i` is the basic induction variable (incremented by 1).
- `t2 = t * i` is a **derived induction variable** (increments by `t` = 18.84 each iteration).

**Step 5 — Apply Strength Reduction:**
Replace the multiplication `t * i` with an addition by introducing a new induction variable `s`:
- `s = t * 1 = t` initially
- Each iteration: `s = s + t`

**Step 6 — Final Optimized TAC:**
```
    t1 = 6 * 3.14
    t  = t1
    s  = t                     <-- new derived IV
    i  = 1
L1: if i > 100 goto L2
    t2 = s
    A[i] = t2
    t4 = A[i]
    t5 = t4 + 5
    B[i] = t5
    i  = i + 1
    s  = s + t                 <-- cheap addition
    goto L1
L2:
```

**Result:** The multiplication `t * i` is replaced by an addition `s + t` per iteration. Over 100 iterations, this saves 99 multiplications at the cost of one extra addition per iteration.

*(7 marks: 2 for LICM identification, 2 for LICM execution, 1 for IV identification, 2 for strength reduction.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — KTU 2024 Pattern]

**a)** What is **peephole optimization**? List any **six** common peephole transformations with one-line examples. Discuss its **scope** and **limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** For the following basic block:
```c
a = 3 + 5;
b = a + 0;
c = b * 1;
d = c - c;
e = d + 4;
f = e * 2;
```
Apply all possible **local optimizations** (constant folding, algebraic simplification, strength reduction, dead code elimination) sequentially. Show the 3-address code at **each** step and state which *scope* of optimization is being applied. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Peephole optimization** is a scope of optimization that operates on a small **sliding window** (typically 2–5 target/assembly instructions). It replaces inefficient instruction sequences with semantically equivalent, more efficient ones by pattern matching.

**Six Common Peephole Transformations (1 mark each for any 6):**

1. **Redundant load/store elimination:** `MOV R1, A; MOV R1, B` → second is redundant.
2. **Eliminate unreachable code:** `JMP L1; ... L1: ... ` → unreachable `JMP` removed.
3. **Jump-to-jump optimization:** `JMP L1; ... L1: JMP L2` → `JMP L2`.
4. **Strength reduction:** `MUL R, 2` → `SHL R, 1`.
5. **Constant folding in assembly:** `ADD R, 0` → removed.
6. **Algebraic identity:** `MUL R, 1` → removed.
7. **Machine idiom use:** `INC R` instead of `ADD R, 1`.
8. **Redundant stack operations:** paired `PUSH/POP` removed.

**Scope and Limitations:**

- **Scope:** Smallest scope; operates just before or after code generation.
- **Limitations:** Cannot perform global reasoning; limited to local pattern matching; may miss opportunities requiring data-flow analysis; depends on target architecture's instruction set.

*(7 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for scope + 3 for 6 transformations + 2 for limitations.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Initial TAC:**
```
t1 = 3 + 5
a  = t1
t2 = a + 0
b  = t2
t3 = b * 1
c  = t3
t4 = c - c
d  = t4
t5 = d + 4
e  = t5
t6 = e * 2
f  = t6
```

**Step 2 — Constant Folding (Scope: Local):**
$$ 3 + 5 = 8 $$
```
t1 = 8
a  = 8
t2 = a + 0
b  = t2
t3 = b * 1
c  = t3
t4 = c - c
d  = t4
t5 = d + 4
e  = t5
t6 = e * 2
f  = t6
```

**Step 3 — Constant Propagation (Scope: Local):**
Replace `a` with `8`:
```
t1 = 8
a  = 8
t2 = 8 + 0
b  = t2
...
```

**Step 4 — Algebraic Simplification (Scope: Local):**
$$ x + 0 = x, \quad x \times 1 = x, \quad x - x = 0, \quad x \times 2 = x \ll 1 $$
```
t1 = 8
a  = 8
t2 = 8
b  = 8
t3 = 8
c  = 8
t4 = 0
d  = 0
t5 = 0 + 4
e  = t5
t6 = e * 2
f  = t6
```

**Step 5 — Constant Folding again (Scope: Local):**
$$ 0 + 4 = 4 $$
```
t4 = 0
d  = 0
t5 = 4
e  = 4
t6 = 4 * 2
f  = t6
```

**Step 6 — Strength Reduction (Scope: Local — machine-dependent):**
$$ 4 \times 2 = 4 \ll 1 = 8 $$
```
t6 = 4 << 1
f  = 8
```

**Step 7 — Constant Propagation + Dead Code Elimination (Scope: Local):**
Remove unused temporaries; `d = 0` is dead if `d` is not used elsewhere.
```
f  = 8
```

**Scope used at each step:** *Local (basic-block) scope* — no cross-block information was needed. All transformations stayed within the single basic block.

*(7 marks: 1 per step × 6 steps + 1 for scope identification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "scope" with "type"** of optimization: Students often list *types* (CSE, LICM) under scope. Scope is the *region*; type is the *technique*. Always pair them: e.g., "**Loop-scope LICM**" or "**Global-scope CSE**."
> 2. **Skipping intermediate steps in TAC transformations:** Examiners explicitly award marks for *showing each transformation step*. Submitting only the final optimized code loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting compile-time cost trade-off:** Any answer on scope that does NOT mention the **compile-time vs. code-quality trade-off** will be marked down by 1 mark per KTU 2024 rubric.
> 4. **Mixing up local and global CSE:** Local CSE uses DAGs; global CSE uses data-flow analysis (available expressions). Many students wrongly attribute global CSE to DAG construction.
> 5. **Ignoring Amdahl's Law in loop optimization answers:** Always justify why loop scope is highest-payoff using Amdahl's Law — examiners specifically look for this phrase.
> 6. **Forgetting induction variable analysis prerequisite:** Strength reduction requires identifying the IV *first*; skipping the IV identification step costs 1–2 marks.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Scope of Optimization** = the *region* of code on which a transformation operates; distinct from the *type/technique* of optimization.
- **Five Scopes (in increasing size):** **Peephole → Local (Basic Block) → Global (Procedure) → Loop → Inter-Procedural (Whole Program)**.
- **Local scope** uses a **DAG**; transformations: constant folding, propagation, algebraic simplification, block-level CSE, dead code elimination.
- **Global scope** uses a **CFG** and **data-flow analysis** (reaching defs, available expressions, liveness); enables cross-block CSE, copy propagation, dead code elimination.
- **Loop scope** uses **dominator trees**; techniques: **LICM, strength reduction, IV elimination, unrolling, fusion, interchange**; **highest payoff** per Amdahl's Law.
- **Peephole scope** uses a **sliding instruction window**; techniques: pattern matching, redundant instruction elimination, jump-to-jump, strength reduction at machine level.
- **Inter-procedural scope** uses a **call graph**; techniques: inlining, cloning, IPO-CSE, IPO constant propagation; very high compile-time cost.
- **Speedup formula:** $\;S = \dfrac{T_{\text{orig}}}{T_{\text{opt}}}\;$, and overall speedup follows **Amdahl's Law**: $\;S_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/S_{\text{loop}}}\;$.
- **Strength reduction always requires two steps:** (1) identify the induction variable, (2) replace multiplication by adding a derived induction variable.
- **LICM safety rule:** Code is hoistable only if it is **loop-invariant** AND dominates all loop exits.
- **Trade-off triangle to always state in answers:** *(Code Quality) ↔ (Compile Time) ↔ (Optimizer Memory)*.
- **KTU's favorite exam line:** *"Loops, being executed repeatedly, give the highest optimization payoff per unit scope — validated by Amdahl's Law."*
- **GCC flag mapping (must memorize):**
  - `-O0` → no optimization
  - `-O1` → local + peephole
  - `-O2` → adds global + DFA-based
  - `-O3` → adds loop + aggressive vectorization
  - `-Os` → size optimization
  - `-Ofast` → adds unsafe math + IPO
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local Optimization: Local Value Numbering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Local Optimization: Local Value Numbering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Local Value Numbering (LVN)** is a classical, hash-table-driven, intra–basic-block optimization technique used in the **code generation / optimization phase** of a compiler. It systematically assigns an integer **value number (VN)** to every distinct *value* (operand or computed expression) produced within a straight-line sequence of three-address code (TAC). Two expressions are deemed **equivalent** (and hence a candidate for Common Subexpression Elimination — CSE, constant folding, and copy propagation) if and only if they evaluate to the same value under the current state of the basic block.

Formally, a **Value Number Table (VNT)** is a hash table that maps a *canonical key* — typically the triple (operator, vn(op1), vn(op2)) for binary ops or a single value number for unary ops / literals — to a small integer value number. The technique is **local** because the table is discarded at the end of every basic block; re-entering a block requires re-initialisation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Local value numbering falls under the *Local Optimisation* cluster of Module 4 and is specifically assessed for its ability to perform **Common Subexpression Elimination**, **Constant Folding**, and **Strength Reduction** within a single basic block.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a **currency exchange counter** at an international airport:

- A traveller brings a **10 USD bill** and asks for its value in INR → the clerk assigns a number (say `VN = 17`) to "ten dollars" and remembers it.
- Another traveller hands over the *same* **10 USD bill** later → the clerk instantly recognises it, looks up `VN = 17`, and **does not re-count** the money. The transaction is reduced to "give them what the previous 10-USD holder got."
- If, however, a traveller hands over a **5 EUR note**, the clerk computes a *new* exchange rate, assigns a *new* value number (say `VN = 24`), and records the mapping.

The **clerk's memory book** = the **Value Number Table**. The **tag** on every transaction = the **Value Number**. Two transactions are equivalent if and only if their tags match. This is *exactly* how LVN detects redundant computations inside a basic block.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Glossary Box)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Glossary for KTU Examinations**
>
> | Term | Meaning |
> |---|---|
> | **Basic Block** | A maximal sequence of TAC instructions with single entry/single exit and no internal branches. |
> | **Value Number (VN)** | A small integer ID assigned to a *value* (not a memory location). |
> | **Value Number Table (VNT)** | A hash table storing `(operator, vn₁, vn₂) → VN` mappings. |
> | **Symbolic Map (Scoper)** | A separate hash table mapping *variable names* → current VN they hold. |
> | **Constant Folding** | Compile-time evaluation of constant expressions (e.g., `2*3 → 6`). |
> | **Copy Propagation** | Replacing use of a copy with the original (e.g., `x = y; ... x ... → ... y ...`). |
> | **CSE** | Common Subexpression Elimination — replacing duplicate computations. |
> | **Commutative Operator** | Operators where order does not matter (`+`, `*`, `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`). |

The **expected outcome** of LVN is a TAC sequence that is *semantically identical* but executes *fewer* arithmetic/logic operations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Local Value Numbering Algorithm — Operational Theory

The algorithm is executed as a **single forward linear pass** over the three-address instructions of a basic block. Two cooperating hash tables are maintained:

1. **Value Number Table (`VNT`):** Maps the canonical key of an expression to a value number.
2. **Symbolic Map / Variable Scope Table (`Scope`):** Maps each *program variable* to the value number it currently holds.

### 2.1.1 Per-Instruction Logic (Step-by-Step)

For every three-address instruction of the form `x = y  op  z`:

**Step 1 — Operand Resolution.**
Look up `vn_y = Scope[y]` and `vn_z = Scope[z]`.

**Step 2 — Constant Folding Pre-Check.**
- If both `y` and `z` are compile-time constants whose value numbers already map to a literal, evaluate the expression at compile time.
- Replace with a fresh constant (e.g., `2 * 3 → 6`) and proceed to Step 4.

**Step 3 — Hash Lookup / Insertion.**
- Construct the **canonical key** `K = (op, min(vn_y, vn_z), max(vn_y, vn_z))` for *commutative* operators (this order-independence is what enables `a+b` ≡ `b+a` to be detected).
- For *non-commutative* operators (`-`, `/`, `<<`), use the ordered pair `(op, vn_y, vn_z)`.
- Probe `VNT`:
  - If `K` is present with value number `V`, then the expression is a **Common Subexpression** → set `vn_x = V` and mark for CSE.
  - If `K` is absent, create a **new value number** `V_new = ++counter`, store `VNT[K] = V_new`, set `vn_x = V_new`.

**Step 4 — Symbolic Map Update.**
- Update `Scope[x] = vn_x`.
- **Crucial Invalidations:** If `x` is the *only live name* for a memory location that other VN entries depended on (e.g., an array element, a pointer dereference, or a procedure call), the corresponding VNT entries must be deleted — this is the **scoper invalidation rule**.

**Step 5 — Copy Propagation Tie-In.**
If the instruction is a pure copy `x = y`, set `Scope[x] = Scope[y]` directly *without* allocating a new VN (or allocate one only for tracking) — this allows later uses of `x` to inherit the same VN as `y`.

### 2.1.2 Why "Local"? — Boundary Semantics

The VNT is initialised to empty at the **entry** of every basic block. Because data-flow information (e.g., reaching definitions, available expressions) is not consulted, the technique is *purely local* and *intra-procedural*. At the end of a block, all VNs are discarded; the next block starts fresh.

## 2.2 Worked Diagnostic: What the Algorithm Detects

| TAC Form | LVN Action | Optimisation Category |
|---|---|---|
| `t3 = a + b`; `t5 = a + b` | Second statement replaced by `t5 = t3` | **CSE** |
| `t = 2 * 3` | Replaced by `t = 6` | **Constant Folding** |
| `x = y`; `use(x)` | `use(x)` rewritten to `use(y)` | **Copy Propagation** |
| `t = a * 1` | Replaced by `t = a` | **Strength Reduction** |
| `t = a * 2` | Replaced by `t = a << 1` (optional) | **Strength Reduction** |
| `a[i] = ...` or `call f()` | Invalidate all VNT entries | **Safety Rule** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD TABLE — Memorise for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Concept | Notation / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Value Number of a literal `k` | `VN(k) = constVN[k]` (lookup) | Enables constant folding |
| 2 | Canonical key (commutative) | `K = (op, min(vn₁,vn₂), max(vn₁,vn₂))` | Detects `a+b ≡ b+a` |
| 3 | Canonical key (non-commutative) | `K = (op, vn₁, vn₂)` (ordered) | Correct for `-`, `/`, `<<` |
| 4 | CSE condition | `K ∈ VNT` | Triggers replacement |
| 5 | Constant folding condition | `op` applied to two literal VNs | Compile-time eval |
| 6 | Scoper update | `Scope[x] ← vn_x` | Tracks current value of `x` |
| 7 | Scoper invalidation | On store/call/delete VNT entries | Maintains correctness |
| 8 | Cost of LVN | **O(n)** per instruction, n = block size | Linear, hence efficient |
| 9 | Space of VNT | **O(k)** where k = distinct exprs | Bounded by block size |
| 10 | Cannot replace | Pointer deref, array ref, call | Side-effect safety |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Local value numbering is the **workhorse** of modern just-in-time (JIT) compilers (LLVM, HotSpot, V8) and static optimisers. It powers loop-invariant code motion, auto-vectorisation pre-passes, and constant propagation pipelines. In hardware-software co-design, the technique is also used in **synthesis tools** to share arithmetic units in datapath scheduling, reducing silicon area in ASIC/FPGA designs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The KTU "Poster Child" Problem

Consider the following **basic block** (B) in three-address code:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1)\quad & d = b * c \\
(2)\quad & e = a + b \\
(3)\quad & f = a + b \\
(4)\quad & g = b * c \\
(5)\quad & h = a + b \\
(6)\quad & i = 2 * 3 \\
(7)\quad & j = 2 * 3 \\
(8)\quad & k = i + j \\
(9)\quad & l = 6 + 6
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.1 Initial State of the Hash Tables

**Literal Pre-Allocation** (constants get reserved VNs):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{constVN}[2] &= 1 \\
\text{constVN}[3] &= 2 \\
\text{constVN}[6] &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

`Scope[·] = 0` for all program variables (a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h, i, j, k, l). `VNT = ∅`. `nextVN = 3` (next free value number after constants).

### 3.1.2 Instruction-by-Instruction Trace

---

#### Instruction (1): `d = b * c`

- `Scope[b] = 0`, `Scope[c] = 0`
- Canonical key (commutative `*`): `K = (*, 0, 0)`
- `VNT` lookup: **not found**
- Allocate: `nextVN = 4`. Set `VNT[(*, 0, 0)] = 4`
- `Scope[d] = 4`
- **Emitted TAC:** `d = b * c` (no change)

---

#### Instruction (2): `e = a + b`

- `Scope[a] = 0`, `Scope[b] = 0`
- Canonical key (commutative `+`): `K = (+, 0, 0)`
- `VNT` lookup: **not found**
- Allocate: `nextVN = 5`. Set `VNT[(+, 0, 0)] = 5`
- `Scope[e] = 5`
- **Emitted TAC:** `e = a + b` (no change)

---

#### Instruction (3): `f = a + b` ⭐ **CSE TRIGGERED**

- `Scope[a] = 0`, `Scope[b] = 0`
- Canonical key: `K = (+, 0, 0)`
- `VNT` lookup: **FOUND → VN = 5**
- `Scope[f] = 5` (inherits from VNT)
- **Emitted TAC:** `f = e` *(CSE applied — replaced with a copy!)*

---

#### Instruction (4): `g = b * c` ⭐ **CSE TRIGGERED**

- `Scope[b] = 0`, `Scope[c] = 0`
- Canonical key: `K = (*, 0, 0)`
- `VNT` lookup: **FOUND → VN = 4**
- `Scope[g] = 4`
- **Emitted TAC:** `g = d` *(CSE applied)*

---

#### Instruction (5): `h = a + b` ⭐ **CSE TRIGGERED**

- `Scope[a] = 0`, `Scope[b] = 0`
- Canonical key: `K = (+, 0, 0)`
- `VNT` lookup: **FOUND → VN = 5**
- `Scope[h] = 5`
- **Emitted TAC:** `h = e` *(CSE applied)*

---

#### Instruction (6): `i = 2 * 3` ⭐ **CONSTANT FOLDING**

- `2` is literal → `vn = constVN[2] = 1`
- `3` is literal → `vn = constVN[3] = 2`
- Constant-foldable: `2 * 3 = 6`
- Look up constant `6`: `constVN[6] = 3`
- `Scope[i] = 3`
- **Emitted TAC:** `i = 6` *(folded at compile time)*

---

#### Instruction (7): `j = 2 * 3` ⭐ **CSE + CONSTANT FOLDING**

- Same as (6): fold to `6`, VN = 3
- `Scope[j] = 3`
- **Emitted TAC:** `j = i` *(CSE — same value 6 already computed as `i`)*

---

#### Instruction (8): `k = i + j`

- `Scope[i] = 3`, `Scope[j] = 3`
- Canonical key: `K = (+, 3, 3)`
- `VNT` lookup: **not found**
- Allocate: `nextVN = 6`. Set `VNT[(+, 3, 3)] = 6`
- `Scope[k] = 6`
- **Emitted TAC:** `k = i + j` (no change)

---

#### Instruction (9): `l = 6 + 6` ⭐ **CSE TRIGGERED**

- `6` is literal → `vn = constVN[6] = 3`
- Canonical key: `K = (+, 3, 3)`
- `VNT` lookup: **FOUND → VN = 6**
- `Scope[l] = 6`
- **Emitted TAC:** `l = k` *(CSE applied)*

---

### 3.1.3 Final Optimised Block (Output of LVN)

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1')& \quad d = b * c \\
(2')& \quad e = a + b \\
(3')& \quad f = e \\
(4')& \quad g = d \\
(5')& \quad h = e \\
(6')& \quad i = 6 \\
(7')& \quad j = i \\
(8')& \quad k = i + j \\
(9')& \quad l = k
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Out of 9 original three-address instructions, **5 redundant computations were eliminated**, shrinking the block to 9 simpler (but cheaper) instructions. The IR count looks the same, but **runtime arithmetic operations dropped from 8 to 3** — a **62.5% reduction**.

### 3.1.4 The Final State of All Tables (Verification Snapshot)

| Instr | Variable | Scope[v] | VNT Operation | VN Allocated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | a,b,c,d,e,f,g,h,i,j,k,l | 0 | — | const: 1,2,3 |
| 1 | d | 4 | insert `(*,0,0)` | 4 |
| 2 | e | 5 | insert `(+,0,0)` | 5 |
| 3 | f | 5 | **hit** `(+,0,0)` → 5 | — |
| 4 | g | 4 | **hit** `(*,0,0)` → 4 | — |
| 5 | h | 5 | **hit** `(+,0,0)` → 5 | — |
| 6 | i | 3 | fold 2*3 = 6 | — |
| 7 | j | 3 | **hit** literal 6 | — |
| 8 | k | 6 | insert `(+,3,3)` | 6 |
| 9 | l | 6 | **hit** `(+,3,3)` → 6 | — |

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation of Hash Key Canonicalisation

Why is the key formed as `(op, min, max)` for commutative operators?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Goal:} \quad & (a + b) \stackrel{?}{=} (b + a) \\
\text{Step 1 — Fetch VNs:} \quad & v_a = \text{Scope}[a], \quad v_b = \text{Scope}[b] \\
\text{Step 2 — Canonical form:} \quad & K_1 = (+, \min(v_a, v_b), \max(v_a, v_b)) \\
& K_2 = (+, \min(v_b, v_a), \max(v_b, v_a)) \\
\text{Step 3 — Commutativity of min/max:} \quad & K_1 = K_2 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

For non-commutative operators (e.g., subtraction), ordered pairs are mandatory:

$$
K = (-, v_a, v_b) \neq (-, v_b, v_a)
$$

Violating this rule produces **incorrect CSE** (e.g., wrongly equating `a − b` with `b − a`).

## 3.3 Complexity Derivation

Let `n` = number of TAC instructions in a basic block, `m` = number of *distinct* value numbers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Per-instruction cost (avg)} &= O(1) \text{ (hash probe + hash insert)} \\
\text{Total time} &= O(n) \\
\text{Space (VNT + Scope)} &= O(m), \quad m \le 2n
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence LVN is **linear-time, linear-space** — making it one of the cheapest and most pervasive local optimisations in any production compiler.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the LVN Engine

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *interactions* between the two cooperating hash tables (`VNT` and `Scope`) and the per-instruction pipeline. It is a **block-level functional architecture flow** (per the protocol's fallback clause for non-physical drawings).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start of Basic Block]) --> B[Initialise VNT = empty]
    B --> C[Initialise Scope: all vars to VN=0]
    C --> D[Pre-load constVN: 2->1, 3->2, 6->3]
    D --> E[/Fetch next 3-address instruction: x = y op z/]

    E --> F{Is y and z both literal?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Evaluate y op z at compile time]
    G --> H[Compute canonical key for folded constant]
    H --> I
    F -- No --> J[Lookup vn_y = Scope y, vn_z = Scope z]

    J --> K{Is op commutative?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Build key = op, min, max]
    K -- No --> M[Build key = op, vn_y, vn_z]

    L --> I{VNT contains key?}
    M --> I
    H --> I

    I -- Yes CSE hit --> N[Reuse existing VN: vn_x = VNT key]
    I -- No miss --> O[Allocate new VN: nextVN++]
    O --> P[Insert VNT key = nextVN]

    N --> Q[Update Scope x = vn_x]
    P --> Q

    Q --> R{x is a pointer/array/call target?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Invalidate affected VNT entries]
    R -- No --> T{More instructions in block?}
    S --> T

    T -- Yes --> E
    T -- No --> U[Emit optimised TAC sequence]
    U --> V([End of Basic Block — discard VNT and Scope])
```

## 4.2 Architectural Data-Flow Schematic (VNT ⇄ Scope Interaction)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Instruction Stream]
        I1[d = b * c]
        I2[e = a + b]
        I3[f = a + b]
        I4[g = b * c]
        I5[h = a + b]
        I6[i = 2 * 3]
    end

    subgraph LVN[LVN Engine]
        PROBE[Hash Probe Module]
        ALLOC[VN Allocator]
        FOLD[Constant Folder]
        SCOPER[Scoper Module]
    end

    subgraph TABLES[Persistent State]
        VNT[(Value Number Table VNT)]
        SCOPE[(Symbolic Map Scope)]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Optimised TAC]
        O1[d = b * c]
        O2[e = a + b]
        O3[f = e]
        O4[g = d]
        O5[h = e]
        O6[i = 6]
    end

    I1 --> PROBE
    I2 --> PROBE
    I3 --> PROBE
    I4 --> PROBE
    I5 --> PROBE
    I6 --> FOLD
    PROBE <--> VNT
    PROBE --> SCOPER
    SCOPER <--> SCOPE
    FOLD --> PROBE
    ALLOC --> VNT
    SCOPER --> OUTPUT
    PROBE --> OUTPUT
```

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — A VN's Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Unallocated : basic block entry
    Unallocated --> Active : first definition of variable / expression
    Active --> Active : re-used by equivalent expression (CSE)
    Active --> Invalidated : pointer store / call / array write
    Active --> [*] : basic block exit (table discarded)
    Invalidated --> [*] : no longer trusted
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Invalidated** state is the safety net that prevents LVN from producing *incorrect* code when a memory alias could have been modified out-of-band. Skipping this step is one of the most common *incorrect-code* bugs in student implementations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **KTU Examiner's Note (Dec 2024 style):** Part A expects crisp, definition-grade answers. Three marks reward: (i) precise definition, (ii) one supporting example, (iii) one sentence on engineering significance.

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Set B]`

**Q:** *Define Local Value Numbering. State the two hash tables it uses and the role of each.*

**Model Answer:**
Local Value Numbering (LVN) is a basic-block-level optimisation technique that assigns a small integer *value number* to every distinct value (literal, variable, or computed expression) produced within a single basic block, using a hash table to detect and eliminate redundant computations.

It uses **two cooperating hash tables**:

1. **Value Number Table (VNT):** Maps the canonical key of an expression — typically `(operator, vn₁, vn₂)` — to its assigned value number. This is the *core* structure that flags Common Subexpressions.
2. **Symbolic Map (Scope Table):** Maps each program variable to the value number it *currently* holds. This is consulted to resolve operand VNs before any hash probe.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 each for the two tables and their roles.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Q:** *What is constant folding? Show with an example how LVN handles the TAC instruction `x = 2 * 3`.*

**Model Answer:**
**Constant folding** is a compile-time optimisation in which an expression composed entirely of literal operands is evaluated by the compiler, eliminating the runtime computation.

**LVN's handling of `x = 2 * 3`:**
- The compiler recognises that both `2` and `3` are literals; the value numbers `constVN[2] = 1` and `constVN[3] = 2` are retrieved.
- The product `2 × 3 = 6` is evaluated at compile time.
- The instruction is replaced by `x = 6`, and the constant `6` is associated with a fresh or pre-allocated value number (`VN = 3` in our scheme).
- Any subsequent `y = 2 * 3` will hit the same VN via the VNT, yielding a *further* CSE: `y = x`.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the worked example.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> **KTU Valuation Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each 14-mark question carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Awarding is keyword-driven: 1 mark per correctly identified construct, 2 marks per worked step, 1 mark for the final optimised TAC.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set A, Adapted]`

**Q:** *(a)* Explain the **Local Value Numbering algorithm** in detail. Discuss how it performs Common Subexpression Elimination, Constant Folding, and Copy Propagation within a single basic block. Specify the data structures used. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Apply the LVN algorithm to the following basic block and produce the **optimised three-address code**. Show the contents of the VNT and Scope table after every instruction. *(7 marks)*

```text
t1 = a + b
t2 = c + d
t3 = a + b
t4 = t1 + t2
t5 = a + b
t6 = c + d
t7 = t4 + 5
t8 = 2 + 3
t9 = 5 + 2
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Algorithm Explanation (7 marks)

1. **(1 mark)** Define LVN as a basic-block-scoped technique that assigns integers (value numbers) to values to detect equivalence.
2. **(1 mark)** State the two data structures: VNT (expression → VN) and Scope (variable → VN).
3. **(1 mark)** Walk through the 5-step per-instruction logic (operand resolution → key construction → hash probe → allocation/update → scoper update).
4. **(1 mark)** Explain **CSE** as a VNT hit on a previously seen expression's key.
5. **(1 mark)** Explain **Constant Folding** as the pre-step evaluation when both operands are literals.
6. **(1 mark)** Explain **Copy Propagation** as the special case `x = y` where `Scope[x]` inherits `Scope[y]` without re-allocation.
7. **(1 mark)** State the **invalidations** on pointer dereference / call / array store and the complexity `O(n)` time, `O(n)` space.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Worked Trace (7 marks)

Pre-allocated: `constVN[2]=1, constVN[3]=2, constVN[5]=3`. Initial Scope: all program variables = 0. VNT empty. nextVN = 3.

| # | Instruction | vn₁, vn₂ | Key | VNT Action | Scope after | Emitted TAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `t1 = a + b` | 0, 0 | `(+ ,0,0)` | insert → 4 | `t1=4` | `t1 = a + b` |
| 2 | `t2 = c + d` | 0, 0 | `(+ ,0,0)` | **HIT (4)** | `t2=4` | `t2 = t1` ⭐ |
| 3 | `t3 = a + b` | 0, 0 | `(+ ,0,0)` | **HIT (4)** | `t3=4` | `t3 = t1` ⭐ |
| 4 | `t4 = t1 + t2` | 4, 4 | `(+ ,4,4)` | insert → 5 | `t4=5` | `t4 = t1 + t2` |
| 5 | `t5 = a + b` | 0, 0 | `(+ ,0,0)` | **HIT (4)** | `t5=4` | `t5 = t1` ⭐ |
| 6 | `t6 = c + d` | 0, 0 | `(+ ,0,0)` | **HIT (4)** | `t6=4` | `t6 = t1` ⭐ |
| 7 | `t7 = t4 + 5` | 5, 3 | `(+ ,5,3)` | insert → 6 | `t7=6` | `t7 = t4 + 5` |
| 8 | `t8 = 2 + 3` | 1, 2 | fold → 5 | reuse const 5 (VN 3) | `t8=3` | `t8 = 5` ⭐ fold |
| 9 | `t9 = 5 + 2` | 3, 1 | fold → 5 | **HIT const 5** | `t9=3` | `t9 = t8` ⭐ CSE+fold |

**Final Optimised Block:**

```text
t1 = a + b
t2 = t1
t3 = t1
t4 = t1 + t1
t5 = t1
t6 = t1
t7 = t4 + 5
t8 = 5
t9 = t8
```

**VNT final entries:** `{(+,0,0):4, (+,4,4):5, (+,5,3):6, 5:3}`

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set C, Adapted]`

**Q:** *(a)* Differentiate between **Local Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** performed via *Available Expressions Analysis* and CSE performed via **Local Value Numbering**. State two advantages of LVN. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Consider the following basic block. Apply LVN step by step, indicating the contents of the VNT and Scope after each instruction. Mention which lines trigger CSE, constant folding, and copy propagation. *(7 marks)*

```text
(1) p = x * y
(2) q = x + y
(3) r = x * y
(4) s = y + x
(5) t = q + r
(6) u = 4 * 1
(7) v = r * 1
(8) w = 4
(9) m = w + 0
(10) n = w * 1
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — LVN vs. Data-Flow CSE (7 marks)

| Aspect | Classical CSE (Available Expressions) | Local Value Numbering (LVN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Information used** | Set-based data-flow (IN/OUT sets) | Hash-table lookup on the fly |
| **Phase** | Pre-pass analysis, then transformation | Single integrated pass |
| **Order-sensitivity** | Detects only *syntactically identical* subexprs | Detects *algebraically equivalent* (e.g., `a+b ≡ b+a`) |
| **Handles constants** | Requires separate constant prop | **Built-in constant folding** |
| **Handles copies** | Requires separate copy propagation | **Built-in** (Scope inheritance) |
| **Time complexity** | Multiple passes (O(n²) typical) | **Single O(n) pass** |
| **Space** | Bit-vector per block | Two hash tables |

**Two advantages of LVN:**
1. **Single linear pass** — drastically faster than the iterative data-flow approach.
2. **Order-insensitive** — canonical-key construction detects `a+b ≡ b+a` and `a*b ≡ b*a` automatically.

*(7 marks — 4 for the table, 2 for advantages, 1 for the conclusion.)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Worked Trace (7 marks)

Pre-allocated: `constVN[0]=1, constVN[1]=2, constVN[4]=3, constVN[5]=4`. Initial Scope: all variables = 0. VNT empty. nextVN = 4.

| # | Instruction | vn₁, vn₂ | Key | VNT Action | Scope after | Emitted TAC | Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `p = x * y` | 0, 0 | `(*,0,0)` | insert → 5 | `p=5` | `p = x * y` | — |
| 2 | `q = x + y` | 0, 0 | `(+,0,0)` | insert → 6 | `q=6` | `q = x + y` | — |
| 3 | `r = x * y` | 0, 0 | `(*,0,0)` | **HIT (5)** | `r=5` | `r = p` ⭐ | **CSE** |
| 4 | `s = y + x` | 0, 0 | `(+,min,max)=(+,0,0)` | **HIT (6)** | `s=6` | `s = q` ⭐ | **CSE (commutative)** |
| 5 | `t = q + r` | 6, 5 | `(+,5,6)` | insert → 7 | `t=7` | `t = q + r` | — |
| 6 | `u = 4 * 1` | 3, 2 | fold → 4 | `4*1=4`, VN of 4 = 4 | `u=4` | `u = 4` ⭐ | **Const. Folding + Strength Reduction** |
| 7 | `v = r * 1` | 5, 2 | fold → 5 | `r*1 = r`, VN = 5 | `v=5` | `v = r` ⭐ | **Const. Folding + Strength Reduction** |
| 8 | `w = 4` | — | (literal) | VN = 4 | `w=4` | `w = 4` | — |
| 9 | `m = w + 0` | 4, 1 | fold → 4 | `w+0 = w`, VN = 4 | `m=4` | `m = w` ⭐ | **Const. Folding + Copy Prop.** |
| 10 | `n = w * 1` | 4, 2 | fold → 4 | `w*1 = w`, VN = 4 | `n=4` | `n = w` ⭐ | **Const. Folding + Copy Prop.** |

**Final Optimised Block:**

```text
p  = x * y
q  = x + y
r  = p
s  = q
t  = q + r
u  = 4
v  = r
w  = 4
m  = w
n  = w
```

**Summary of Optimisation Triggers (1 mark each):**
- **CSE** at lines 3, 4
- **CSE (commutative)** at line 4
- **Constant Folding + Strength Reduction** at lines 6, 7, 9, 10
- **Copy Propagation** at lines 7, 9, 10

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in LVN Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to canonicalise keys for commutative operators.** Many students write `VNT[(+,a,b)]` and `VNT[(+,b,a)]` as two separate entries, missing the `a+b ≡ b+a` CSE. **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Not invalidating the VNT on pointer/array writes or procedure calls.** Producing *incorrect* code due to aliasing is a fatal flaw. **−3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing LVN's Scope Table with the Symbol Table.** The Scope Table tracks *current value numbers*, not memory addresses. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the constant-folding pre-check** and going directly to VNT lookup. This wastes the opportunity to fold `2*3 → 6` and is considered an *incomplete* optimisation. **−2 marks**.
> 5. **Writing the optimised TAC but not tabulating the VNT/Scope evolution.** The board examiner *requires* the table to award full credit for the algorithmic trace. **−3 marks** (this is the single biggest deduction).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **LVN = Local, linear, hash-based CSE + constant folding + copy propagation** within a single basic block.
- **Two data structures:** Value Number Table (`VNT`) and Symbolic Map (`Scope`).
- **Canonical key:** `(op, min(vn₁,vn₂), max(vn₁,vn₂))` for commutative ops; ordered for non-commutative.
- **Constants are pre-allocated** to fixed VNs (`constVN` array) so that they are recognised across instructions.
- **Three classifications of optimisation LVN performs:** CSE, Constant Folding, Copy Propagation (with strength reduction as a bonus).
- **Complexity:** `O(n)` time and `O(n)` space per basic block — strictly linear.
- **Safety rule:** Invalidate VNT entries on pointer stores, procedure calls, and array element modifications.
- **Local ≠ Global:** VNT is *discarded* at every basic-block boundary; no inter-block information is used.
- **Order-insensitive CSE:** Detects `a+b ≡ b+a` and `a*b ≡ b*a` because of the min/max canonical form.
- **Exam mantra:** *Probe → Hit = CSE | Miss = Allocate | Both-literals = Fold first*.
- **Engineering use:** Powers optimisations in LLVM, GCC, HotSpot, V8, and is the workhorse of JIT compilers and hardware datapath synthesis.
- **Fingerprint analogy:** Each value gets a unique integer "fingerprint" — duplicates are detected by matching fingerprints, exactly like a forensic hash.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tree-Height Balancing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Tree-Height Balancing # 🌲 Tree-Height Balancing — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Compiler Construction (PBCRT604)**, **Tree-Height Balancing** refers to the systematic process of reordering, regrouping, or restructuring the nodes of an **intermediate representation (IR) expression tree** so that the tree's height is minimized (or its node labels are balanced) for the purpose of generating **optimal machine code**. The height of an expression tree directly determines the minimum number of **temporary registers** required for its evaluation on a target machine. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):** > *Tree-Height Balancing* is the optimization technique in code generation where the compiler assigns integer labels (called **Ershov Numbers** or **Sethi-Ullman Numbers**) to the leaves and internal nodes of a syntax tree, in a way that the label of a node represents the **minimum number of registers** needed to evaluate the sub-expression rooted at that node. The tree is then evaluated in the order dictated by these labels. Two classical algorithms govern this concept: 1. **Ershov's Labeling (1958)** — assigns labels for the **arithmetic (binary) expression tree** to minimize register usage assuming operands are in memory. 2. **Sethi–Ullman Algorithm (1970)** — extends Ershov's idea and introduces the concept of *evaluating the more complex (higher-labeled) subtree first*, then the simpler subtree, to reduce register pressure. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Analogy (Real-World Intuition):** > Imagine you are a **head chef** in a kitchen with only **two stove burners** (representing CPU registers). You must cook a complex dish described as a recipe tree. Some sub-recipes (like chopping vegetables) need only **one burner**, while others (like simmering a sauce) need **two burners simultaneously**. If you naively start cooking the recipe from the top (the root), you might run out of burners halfway through, forcing you to put pots aside (spilling to memory/stack). **Tree-Height Balancing** is the art of *rearranging the recipe steps* — deciding which sub-recipe to cook first — so that you **never need more burners than you have**. The height/label of each subtree tells you *how many burners* that sub-recipe demands, and the balancing rule tells you *the safest cooking order*. ## 1.3 Why It Matters in a Compiler A modern compiler must emit **assembly or machine code** that uses the fewest registers possible. The default left-to-right or right-to-left traversal of a parse tree is often **sub-optimal**, leading to: - Excessive **spill code** (unnecessary `MOV` to/from memory). - Increased **code size**. - Slower **execution time**. Tree-Height Balancing directly addresses this by: - **Minimizing register pressure**. - **Reducing memory traffic**. - **Producing provably optimal** evaluation orders for expression trees. > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** > Tree-Height Balancing is covered under *"Code Generation"* and *"Optimal Code Generation for Expression Trees"*, and is a **favourite 14-mark ESE question** topic. --- ## 1.4 Key Terminology — At a Glance | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **Expression Tree** | A binary tree whose leaves are operands and whose internal nodes are operators. | | **Tree Height** | The length of the longest path from the root to any leaf. | | **Label (Ershov Number)** | A non-negative integer assigned to each node indicating the minimum register need. | | **Register Pressure** | The maximum number of live values (operands) at any point during evaluation. | | **Spill Code** | Extra `MOV` instructions generated when registers are insufficient. | | **Optimal Code** | Machine code requiring the minimum number of registers. | --- # 📐 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Ershov Number — The Heart of Tree-Height Balancing The **Ershov Number** (also called the **Sethi-Ullman label** or **register number**) of a node in an expression tree is defined recursively as follows: > [!NOTE] > **Ershov Labeling Rules (for binary operators, e.g., $+, -, \times, /$):** > 1. **Leaf node (operand):** Label is **1** if the operand is a **left-most leaf** (i.e., it must be fetched into a register from memory), otherwise **0** if it is the right-most leaf of a node whose left subtree is non-empty. > 2. **Internal node $N$ with children $L$ (left) and $R$ (right):** > - If $\text{label}(L) = \text{label}(R)$: then $\text{label}(N) = \text{label}(L) + 1$. > - If $\text{label}(L) \neq \text{label}(R)$: then $\text{label}(N) = \max(\text{label}(L), \text{label}(R))$. > [!IMPORTANT] > The label of the **root node** gives the **minimum number of registers** required to evaluate the entire expression without spilling. ## 2.2 The Sethi–Ullman Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic The Sethi–Ullman algorithm is the **canonical KTU-board procedure** for tree-height balancing and optimal code generation. ### Step 1: **Labeling Phase (Bottom-Up)** - Traverse the expression tree in **post-order** (left, right, root). - Apply the Ershov rules above to compute the label of every node. ### Step 2: **Code Generation Phase (Top-Down with Conditional Evaluation Order)** - For a node $N$ with children $L$ and $R$: - **If** $\text{label}(L) = \text{label}(R)$: generate code for **either** child first, but consistently use the same order. - **If** $\text{label}(L) > \text{label}(R)$: **first** generate code for the **left** subtree, then the right. - **If** $\text{label}(L) < \text{label}(R)$: **first** generate code for the **right** subtree, then the left. > [!TIP] > **The Golden Rule (KTU Board Favourite):** > *"Always evaluate the subtree with the **larger** Ershov number **first**."* > This is the **balancing step** — it ensures the more demanding subtree finishes and frees its registers before the simpler subtree begins. ### Step 2.1: **Detailed Recursive Logic** For a node $N$ with label $\ell$, the recursive code-generation function $\text{GenCode}(N, R)$ is: $$ \text{GenCode}(N, R) = \begin{cases} \text{Evaluate leaf into register } R & \text{if } N \text{ is a leaf}. \\ \text{GenCode}(L, R); \; \text{GenCode}(R_{\text{right}}, R); \; \text{OP } R \leftarrow R, R_{\text{right}} & \text{if } \text{label}(L) \geq \text{label}(R_{\text{right}}). \\ \text{GenCode}(R_{\text{right}}, R+1); \; \text{GenCode}(L, R); \; \text{OP } R \leftarrow R, R+1 & \text{if } \text{label}(L) < \text{label}(R_{\text{right}}). \end{cases} $$ where $R$ denotes the current register number, and $\text{OP}$ is the operator of node $N$. ## 2.3 The Tiling/Partitioning Insight Tree-height balancing implicitly **partitions** an expression into **tiles** — sub-expressions of bounded register need. The two primary tiling strategies are: | Strategy | Description | Best For | |----------|-------------|----------| | **Common Subexpression Tiling** | Group repeated sub-trees to evaluate once and reuse. | Loops with repeated expressions. | | **Balanced Tiling** | Partition so each tile fits in available registers. | General optimal code generation. | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cheat-Sheet Table for KTU 2024 Examinations** — memorize these. | # | Concept / Formula | Expression / Rule | Notes | |---|-------------------|-------------------|-------| | 1 | Ershov number for leaf | $\ell(\text{leaf}) = 1$ (or 0 if it's the right-child of a node whose left subtree is non-empty) | Bottom rule | | 2 | Ershov number for internal node | $\ell(N) = \begin{cases} \ell(L)+1 & \text{if } \ell(L) = \ell(R) \\ \max(\ell(L), \ell(R)) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Recursive rule | | 3 | Min. registers to evaluate tree | $\ell(\text{root})$ | Root label | | 4 | Optimal evaluation order | Evaluate the child with **larger** label first | Golden rule | | 5 | Tree height vs. registers (loose upper bound) | $\text{registers} \leq \text{height}(T) + 1$ | For skewed trees | | 6 | Optimal for fully balanced tree of height $h$ | $\text{registers} = h + 1$ | Best case | | 7 | Optimal for a "comb" tree (left-skewed) of $n$ leaves | $\text{registers} = 2$ (independent of $n$) | Best possible | | 8 | Code length optimality | Sethi–Ullman produces code of length $\ell(\text{root})$ per node | Proven optimal | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Beyond compilers, Ershov numbers are used in **parallel computing** (to balance load across processors), **query optimization in databases**, and **circuit depth minimization** in VLSI design. The same height-balancing principle appears in **AVL trees** and **red-black trees** for data structure balancing. --- # 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example (Model KTU Board Question):** > *Consider the expression* $E = (a - b) \times c + (d \times e - f) / g$. > *Apply Sethi–Ullman labeling to find the minimum number of registers required and the optimal evaluation order.* ## 3.1 Building the Expression Tree The parse tree for $E$ is constructed as follows: $$ E = \underbrace{\Big( (a-b) \times c \Big)}_{\text{T1}} \;+\; \underbrace{\Big( (d \times e - f) \Big)}_{\text{T2}} / g $$ ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Labeling (Bottom-Up Post-Order) We label each node, starting from the leaves. ### Step A — Label the leaves: For our expression, the left-most leaf at every level gets label **1**, and the right child of a node with a non-empty left subtree gets label **0** (per the refined Ershov rule). We will use the standard simple rule: **all leaves get label 1**. $$ \ell(a) = \ell(b) = \ell(c) = \ell(d) = \ell(e) = \ell(f) = \ell(g) = 1 $$ ### Step B — Label the sub-expression $(a - b)$: Node $N_1$ has children $a$ and $b$, both with $\ell = 1$. $$ \ell(N_1) = \ell(a - b) = \ell(a) + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ (Since $\ell(L) = \ell(R) = 1$, we add 1.) ### Step C — Label the sub-expression $(a - b) \times c$: Node $N_2$ has children $N_1$ ($\ell = 2$) and $c$ ($\ell = 1$). $$ \ell(N_2) = \ell\big((a-b) \times c\big) = \max(\ell(N_1), \ell(c)) = \max(2, 1) = 2 $$ ### Step D — Label the sub-expression $d \times e$: Node $N_3$ has children $d$ and $e$, both with $\ell = 1$. $$ \ell(N_3) = \ell(d \times e) = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ ### Step E — Label the sub-expression $(d \times e) - f$: Node $N_4$ has children $N_3$ ($\ell = 2$) and $f$ ($\ell = 1$). $$ \ell(N_4) = \ell\big((d \times e) - f\big) = \max(2, 1) = 2 $$ ### Step F — Label the sub-expression $\big((d \times e) - f\big) / g$: Node $N_5$ has children $N_4$ ($\ell = 2$) and $g$ ($\ell = 1$). $$ \ell(N_5) = \ell\big(((d \times e) - f) / g\big) = \max(2, 1) = 2 $$ ### Step G — Label the root $+$: Node $N_{\text{root}}$ has children $N_2$ ($\ell = 2$) and $N_5$ ($\ell = 2$). $$ \ell(N_{\text{root}}) = \ell(N_2) + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result of Labeling:** > The minimum number of registers required is $\ell(\text{root}) = \mathbf{3}$. ## 3.3 Optimal Evaluation Order (Top-Down) At the root, $\ell(N_2) = \ell(N_5) = 2$ (equal). We can evaluate either first; by convention, evaluate the **left** first. ### Inside $N_2$: $(a-b) \times c$ $\ell(N_1) = 2 > \ell(c) = 1$ → **evaluate $N_1$ first**, then $c$. ### Inside $N_1$: $(a - b)$ $\ell(a) = \ell(b) = 1$ → evaluate $a$ first, then $b$ (or any consistent order). ### Inside $N_5$: $((d \times e) - f) / g$ $\ell(N_4) = 2 > \ell(g) = 1$ → **evaluate $N_4$ first**, then $g$. ### Inside $N_4$: $(d \times e) - f$ $\ell(N_3) = 2 > \ell(f) = 1$ → **evaluate $N_3$ first**, then $f$. ## 3.4 Final Assembly Code (3 Registers, Optimal) Let $R_0, R_1, R_2$ be the three registers.
