The emitted code is: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{MOV} \; R_0, a \\ &\text{MOV} \; R_1, b \\ &\text{SUB} \; R_0, R_0, R_1 \quad &&\text{\texttt{// R0 = a - b; R0 holds result, R1 is now free}} \\ &\text{MOV} \; R_1, c \\ &\text{MUL} \; R_0, R_0, R_1 \quad &&\text{\texttt{// R0 = (a-b)*c}} \\ &\text{MOV} \; R_1, d \\ &\text{MOV} \; R_2, e \\ &\text{MUL} \; R_1, R_1, R_2 \quad &&\text{\texttt{// R1 = d * e; R2 is now free}} \\ &\text{MOV} \; R_2, f \\ &\text{SUB} \; R_1, R_1, R_2 \quad &&\text{\texttt{// R1 = d*e - f; R2 is now free}} \\ &\text{MOV} \; R_2, g \\ &\text{DIV} \; R_1, R_1, R_2 \quad &&\text{\texttt{// R1 = (d*e-f)/g}} \\ &\text{ADD} \; R_0, R_0, R_1 \quad &&\text{\texttt{// R0 = final answer; uses only 3 registers, no spill!}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Verification:** Total registers used = **3** = $\ell(\text{root})$. Total instructions = **13**. The naive left-to-right order would have required **4 registers** or many extra spill `MOV`s. Tree-height balancing saved at least **3 to 5 instructions**. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Expression Tree Patterns | Tree Shape | Example | $\ell(\text{root})$ | Optimal Reg. | Special Property | |------------|---------|---------------------|--------------|------------------| | **Single leaf** | $a$ | 1 | 1 | Base case | | **Left-skewed chain** | $(((a+b)+c)+d)+e$ | 2 | 2 | Evaluate right-most first | | **Right-skewed chain** | $a+(b+(c+(d+e)))$ | 2 | 2 | Evaluate left-most first | | **Balanced binary** | $(a+b) \times (c+d)$ | 3 | 3 | Either child first | | **"Comb" (full left-skew)** | $((((a+b)+c)+d)+e)+f$ | 2 | 2 | Right-first rule crucial | | **Deeply nested right** | $a+(b+(c+(d+e)))$ | 2 | 2 | Symmetric to comb | | **3-leaf full tree** | $(a+b)+c$ | 2 | 2 | Asymmetric but only 2 regs | --- # 🖼️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Expression Tree with Ershov Labels Below is the **Mermaid block diagram** of the expression tree for $E = (a-b) \times c + (d \times e - f) / g$ with Ershov numbers annotating every node. ```mermaid graph TD nodeRoot["+ label 3"]:::root nodeMul1["x label 2"]:::internal nodeDiv1["/ label 2"]:::internal nodeSub1["- label 2"]:::internal nodeMinus2["- label 2"]:::internal nodeMul2["x label 2"]:::internal nodeDivBy["g label 1"]:::leaf nodeC["c label 1"]:::leaf nodeF["f label 1"]:::leaf nodeA["a label 1"]:::leaf nodeB["b label 1"]:::leaf nodeD["d label 1"]:::leaf nodeE["e label 1"]:::leaf nodeRoot --- nodeMul1 nodeRoot --- nodeDiv1 nodeMul1 --- nodeSub1 nodeMul1 --- nodeC nodeSub1 --- nodeA nodeSub1 --- nodeB nodeDiv1 --- nodeMinus2 nodeDiv1 --- nodeDivBy nodeMinus2 --- nodeMul2 nodeMinus2 --- nodeF nodeMul2 --- nodeD nodeMul2 --- nodeE classDef root fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef internal fill:#a0d8ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#b6f5b6,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` **Legend:** - 🟡 **Yellow root** = the $+$ operator, label **3** (minimum registers). - 🔵 **Blue internal** = intermediate operators. - 🟢 **Green leaves** = operands, each with label **1**. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Sethi–Ullman Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Parse Tree
from Front-End"] --> B["Abstract Syntax
Tree AST"] B --> C["Phase 1
Labeling
Post-Order Traversal"] C --> D["Phase 2
Decision
Larger Label First"] D --> E["Phase 3
Code Emission
Recursive GenCode"] E --> F["Optimal Target
Machine Code"] style A fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000 style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 style C fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 style D fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 style E fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style F fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#cc4400,color:#000 ``` **Description of the flow:** 1. **Parse Tree → AST:** The front-end produces a parse tree which is converted to a leaner Abstract Syntax Tree. 2. **AST → Phase 1 (Labeling):** A post-order traversal assigns Ershov numbers to every node. 3. **Phase 1 → Phase 2 (Decision):** For every internal node, the algorithm decides which child to evaluate first, based on the **larger label first** rule — the *height balancing* step. 4. **Phase 2 → Phase 3 (Code Emission):** Recursive code generation walks the tree top-down, emitting target-machine instructions. 5. **Phase 3 → Optimal Code:** The result is provably register-optimal machine code. ## 4.3 Comparative Diagram — Skewed vs. Balanced Trees ```mermaid graph TB subgraph S1["Skewed Tree - Needs 2 Registers"] sN1["+"]:::sk sN2["+"]:::sk sN3["+"]:::sk sN4["a"]:::lf sN5["b"]:::lf sN6["c"]:::lf sN7["d"]:::lf sN1 --- sN2 sN1 --- sN5 sN2 --- sN3 sN2 --- sN4 sN3 --- sN6 sN3 --- sN7 end subgraph S2["Balanced Tree - Needs 3 Registers"] bN1["+"]:::bl bN2["x"]:::bl bN3["+"]:::bl bN4["+"]:::bl bL1["a"]:::lf2 bL2["b"]:::lf2 bL3["c"]:::lf2 bL4["d"]:::lf2 bL5["e"]:::lf2 bL6["f"]:::lf2 bL7["g"]:::lf2 bN1 --- bN2 bN1 --- bN3 bN2 --- bL1 bN2 --- bL2 bN3 --- bL3 bN3 --- bL4 end classDef sk fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef bl fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef lf fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006600,color:#000 classDef lf2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > **Observation:** The *shape* of the tree dictates register needs. The Sethi–Ullman algorithm exploits this by **re-ordering evaluation** (not re-shaping the tree) to achieve the minimum. --- # 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks) > ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, 3 Marks]** > **Define Ershov numbers. How are they used in tree-height balancing for optimal code generation?** #### **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Ershov numbers (also called Sethi–Ullman labels) are non-negative integers assigned to the nodes of an expression tree in a bottom-up fashion. The label of a node $N$ with children $L$ and $R$ is given by: $$ \ell(N) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } N \text{ is a leaf} \\ \ell(L) + 1 & \text{if } \ell(L) = \ell(R) \\ \max(\ell(L), \ell(R)) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Use in Tree-Height Balancing (1.5 Marks):** The label of a node represents the **minimum number of registers** required to evaluate the sub-expression rooted at that node. The label of the **root** gives the minimum registers needed for the whole expression. To balance the tree's evaluation height, the algorithm **always evaluates the child with the larger label first**, ensuring the more register-demanding sub-expression finishes and releases its registers before the simpler one begins. This minimizes the **maximum register pressure** and avoids spill code, yielding provably optimal code. --- > ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2, 3 Marks]** > **State and briefly explain the "Larger Label First" rule of the Sethi–Ullman algorithm. Why is it called the "tree-height balancing" rule?** #### **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Rule Statement (1.5 Marks):** For any internal node $N$ with left child $L$ and right child $R$ in a labeled expression tree, the Sethi–Ullman algorithm generates code in the following order: - If $\ell(L) > \ell(R)$: evaluate $L$ first, then $R$. - If $\ell(R) > \ell(L)$: evaluate $R$ first, then $L$. - If $\ell(L) = \ell(R)$: either order is optimal. **Why "Tree-Height Balancing" (1.5 Marks):** The label of a node is closely tied to the **height** of its sub-tree — taller sub-trees demand more registers. By always evaluating the **larger (taller) sub-tree first**, the algorithm effectively **flattens** the cumulative height of live values at any point during evaluation, preventing the live-range stack from growing too deep. This dynamic balancing of live-value heights during code emission is what gives the rule its name — *"tree-height balancing"*. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) > ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Apply the **Sethi–Ullman labeling algorithm** to the expression tree of > $E = (a + b) \times (c - (d \times e) / f) + g \times h$. > Compute the Ershov number for every node and state the **minimum number of registers** required. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Using the result of part (a), generate **optimal three-address code** for the expression. Identify all spill points if any, and justify why the code is optimal. #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Build the tree.** The expression parses into the following tree structure (described textually): - Root: $+$ - Left subtree $T_1 = (a+b) \times (c - (d \times e)/f)$ — operator $\times$ - Left child: $T_{1L} = (a+b)$ — operator $+$ - Right child: $T_{1R} = (c - (d \times e)/f)$ — operator $-$ - Right subtree $T_2 = g \times h$ — operator $\times$ **Step 2: Label the leaves.** $$ \ell(a) = \ell(b) = \ell(c) = \ell(d) = \ell(e) = \ell(f) = \ell(g) = \ell(h) = 1 $$ **Step 3: Label $d \times e$.** Let $N_1 = d \times e$. Children $d, e$ both have $\ell = 1$. $$ \ell(N_1) = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ **Step 4: Label $(d \times e) / f$.** Let $N_2 = N_1 / f$. Children: $N_1$ ($\ell = 2$), $f$ ($\ell = 1$). $$ \ell(N_2) = \max(2, 1) = 2 $$ **Step 5: Label $c - N_2$.** Let $N_3 = c - N_2$. Children: $c$ ($\ell = 1$), $N_2$ ($\ell = 2$). $$ \ell(N_3) = \max(1, 2) = 2 $$ **Step 6: Label $a + b$.** Let $N_4 = a + b$. Both children $\ell = 1$. $$ \ell(N_4) = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ **Step 7: Label $N_4 \times N_3$.** Let $N_5 = N_4 \times N_3$. Children: $N_4$ ($\ell = 2$), $N_3$ ($\ell = 2$). $$ \ell(N_5) = 2 + 1 = 3 $$ **Step 8: Label $g \times h$.** Let $N_6 = g \times h$. Both children $\ell = 1$. $$ \ell(N_6) = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ **Step 9: Label the root $+$.** Children: $N_5$ ($\ell = 3$) and $N_6$ ($\ell = 2$). $$ \ell(\text{root}) = \max(3, 2) = 3 $$ **Answer (a): Minimum number of registers = 3.** #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]** Apply the **larger-label-first** rule at every internal node. **At the root** ($+$, labels 3 and 2): evaluate $N_5$ (label 3) **first**, then $N_6$. **Inside $N_5$** ($\times$, labels 2 and 2): either order. We choose left first: $N_4$ then $N_3$. **Inside $N_4$** ($+$, labels 1 and 1): evaluate $a$ then $b$. **Inside $N_3$** ($-$, labels 1 and 2): evaluate $N_2$ (label 2) **first**, then $c$. **Inside $N_2$** ($/$, labels 2 and 1): evaluate $N_1$ (label 2) **first**, then $f$. **Inside $N_1$** ($\times$, labels 1 and 1): evaluate $d$ then $e$. **Inside $N_6$** ($\times$, labels 1 and 1): evaluate $g$ then $h$. **Generated Three-Address Code (using registers $R_0, R_1, R_2$):** $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{1. MOV } R_0, d \\ &\text{2. MOV } R_1, e \\ &\text{3. MUL } R_0, R_0, R_1 \quad &&\text{// R0 = d*e; R1 free} \\ &\text{4. MOV } R_1, f \\ &\text{5. DIV } R_0, R_0, R_1 \quad &&\text{// R0 = (d*e)/f; R1 free} \\ &\text{6. MOV } R_1, c \\ &\text{7. SUB } R_1, R_1, R_0 \quad &&\text{// R1 = c - (d*e)/f; R0 free} \\ &\text{8. MOV } R_0, a \\ &\text{9. MOV } R_2, b \\ &\text{10. ADD } R_0, R_0, R_2 \quad &&\text{// R0 = a + b; R2 free} \\ &\text{11. MUL } R_0, R_0, R_1 \quad &&\text{// R0 = (a+b)*(c - (d*e)/f)} \\ &\text{12. MOV } R_1, g \\ &\text{13. MOV } R_2, h \\ &\text{14. MUL } R_1, R_1, R_2 \quad &&\text{// R1 = g*h} \\ &\text{15. ADD } R_0, R_0, R_1 \quad &&\text{// R0 = final result} \end{aligned} $$ **No spill instructions** are needed because at every step the number of simultaneously live values never exceeds **3**, which equals $\ell(\text{root})$. **Optimality Justification:** Sethi and Ullman proved in 1970 that the algorithm produces code with the **fewest instructions** and the **fewest registers**. Any other evaluation order would either need more registers or extra spill `MOV`s. The total number of `MOV` instructions is 8 and the total computation instructions is 7, totaling **15 instructions**, which is provably minimal for this expression on a single-accumulator / multi-register machine with commutative arithmetic. --- > ### **Question B (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]** > > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **concept of tree-height balancing** with reference to code generation in a compiler. How does it relate to the **Sethi–Ullman algorithm**? Discuss the two classical rules for labeling (leaf rule and internal-node rule). > > **(b) [7 Marks]** For the expression > $E = ((a + b) - c) \times ((d / e) + (f \times g))$, > draw the expression tree, compute the Ershov label for every node, determine the **optimal evaluation order** using the "larger label first" rule, and state the **minimum register count**. #### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Concept of Tree-Height Balancing (3 Marks):** Tree-height balancing is an optimization technique in the code-generation phase of a compiler. The expression tree generated by the front-end may be arbitrarily shaped (skewed, balanced, deep, etc.). The **height** of a sub-tree determines the **lifetime depth** of its computed value — a tall sub-tree forces many intermediate results to coexist in registers, demanding high register pressure. Tree-height balancing is the process of choosing a **bottom-up labeling** of nodes and a **top-down evaluation order** that minimizes the maximum number of simultaneously-live registers. It is *not* a re-shaping of the tree, but a *re-ordering of evaluation*. **Relation to Sethi–Ullman Algorithm (2 Marks):** The Sethi–Ullman algorithm is the *practical implementation* of tree-height balancing. It consists of two phases: - **Labeling phase (post-order):** assigns Ershov numbers. - **Code-emission phase (pre-order, conditional):** uses the "larger label first" rule to generate optimal code. **Labeling Rules (2 Marks):** - **Leaf rule:** $\ell(\text{leaf}) = 1$. - **Internal-node rule:** if both children have the same label $\ell$, then the parent label is $\ell + 1$; otherwise, the parent label is the maximum of the two children's labels. #### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Tree Construction (textual description):** - Root: $\times$ - Left child $L = ((a+b) - c)$ — operator $-$ - Left grandchild: $(a+b)$ — operator $+$ - Right grandchild: $c$ (leaf) - Right child $R = ((d/e) + (f \times g))$ — operator $+$ - Left: $(d/e)$ — operator $/$ - Right: $(f \times g)$ — operator $\times$ **Step 2 — Label the leaves:** $$ \ell(a) = \ell(b) = \ell(c) = \ell(d) = \ell(e) = \ell(f) = \ell(g) = 1 $$ **Step 3 — Label $a + b$ (call it $N_1$):** Children $a, b$ both have $\ell = 1$. $$ \ell(N_1) = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ **Step 4 — Label $(a+b) - c$ (call it $N_2$):** Children: $N_1$ ($\ell = 2$), $c$ ($\ell = 1$). $$ \ell(N_2) = \max(2, 1) = 2 $$ **Step 5 — Label $d / e$ (call it $N_3$):** Both children $\ell = 1$. $$ \ell(N_3) = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ **Step 6 — Label $f \times g$ (call it $N_4$):** Both children $\ell = 1$. $$ \ell(N_4) = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ **Step 7 — Label $(d/e) + (f \times g)$ (call it $N_5$):** Children: $N_3$ ($\ell = 2$), $N_4$ ($\ell = 2$). $$ \ell(N_5) = 2 + 1 = 3 $$ **Step 8 — Label the root $\times$:** Children: $N_2$ ($\ell = 2$), $N_5$ ($\ell = 3$). $$ \ell(\text{root}) = \max(2, 3) = 3 $$ **Minimum register count = 3.** **Step 9 — Optimal Evaluation Order (Larger Label First):** - **Root** ($\times$, labels 2 and 3): evaluate $N_5$ (label 3) first, then $N_2$. - **$N_5$** ($+$, labels 2 and 2): any order; pick $N_3$ then $N_4$. - **$N_3$** ($/$, labels 1 and 1): evaluate $d$ then $e$. - **$N_4$** ($\times$, labels 1 and 1): evaluate $f$ then $g$. - **$N_2$** ($-$, labels 2 and 1): evaluate $N_1$ (label 2) first, then $c$. - **$N_1$** ($+$, labels 1 and 1): evaluate $a$ then $b$. **Final answer (b):** $\ell(\text{root}) = 3$; hence 3 registers are sufficient and necessary. The evaluation order is: $$ d \to e \to f \to g \to a \to b \to c \to \text{compute root} $$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Feedback):** > 1. **Wrong leaf label:** Students often label **all** leaves as 1. In the refined Ershov rule, the right-most leaf of a node whose left subtree is non-empty gets label **0** to reflect that it can be evaluated in-place. Using the simple rule (all leaves = 1) is acceptable for KTU but be consistent. > 2. **Confusing the "max" rule:** When $\ell(L) = \ell(R)$, students sometimes write $\ell(N) = \max(\ell(L), \ell(R))$ and miss the **$+1$** increment. The rule is: equal labels → **$+1$**; unequal → **max**. > 3. **Forgetting the "larger first" rule:** Many students compute labels correctly but then evaluate in a *naive* left-to-right order, defeating the purpose of tree-height balancing. Always justify the order. > 4. **Skipping the optimality proof sketch:** A 14-mark question often has 1–2 marks reserved for "justify why this is optimal." A one-line statement referencing Sethi–Ullman's optimality theorem suffices. > 5. **Not stating the register count explicitly:** Always end with *"Minimum registers = $\ell(\text{root})$ = …"*. --- ## 5.4 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Summary — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:** - **Tree-Height Balancing** = re-ordering of expression-tree evaluation (not re-shaping) to minimize registers. - **Ershov / Sethi–Ullman Number** = label of a node = minimum registers for that sub-tree. - **Leaf rule:** $\ell(\text{leaf}) = 1$ (or $0$ in the refined form for right-most leaves). - **Internal-node rule:** equal labels $\Rightarrow$ $+1$; unequal labels $\Rightarrow$ $\max$. - **Root label** = minimum registers for the whole expression. - **Larger label first** = the golden rule for evaluation order. - **Skewed / comb trees** = need only 2 registers regardless of depth (a key exam insight). - **Balanced full tree** of height $h$ = needs $h + 1$ registers. - **Sethi–Ullman** is provably **optimal** in both instruction count and register count. - **Spill code** is unnecessary when generated code obeys the "larger label first" rule and $\ell(\text{root}) \leq$ available registers. - **Engineering uses:** compiler back-ends, parallel scheduling, VLSI depth minimization, database query optimizers. - **KTU exam weightage:** Frequently appears in 14-mark questions under *"Code Generation"*; expect a 3-mark definitional question in Part A. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"E-L-I-M"** — > **E**rshov labels → **L**arger label first → **I**nstruction emission → **M**inimal registers. --- ✅ **End of Module 4 — Tree-Height Balancing Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme, PBCRT604)**

topics.title: Regional Optimization: Super Local Value Numbering
topics.content:
> **APJ ADJABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regional Optimization: Super Local Value Numbering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Super Local Value Numbering (SLVN)** is a regional optimization technique that extends **Local Value Numbering (LVN)** beyond a single basic block into an **Extended Basic Block (EBB)**, also called a **Super Block**. It discovers and eliminates redundant computations within a linear chain of basic blocks by tracking **value numbers**—abstract integer identifiers that group together expressions guaranteed to be computationally equivalent at runtime.

In formal compiler-theoretic terms:
$$ VN \colon \text{Expression} \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}^+ $$
$$ \text{If } e_1 \equiv e_2 \text{ (provably equal at compile time)} \iff VN(e_1) = VN(e_2) $$

The technique is called *super local* because it operates on a region *larger* than a basic block but *smaller* than the entire procedure (function). It sits hierarchically between:

| Technique | Region | Granularity |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| Local Value Numbering (LVN) | One Basic Block | Finest |
| **Super Local Value Numbering (SLVN)** | **Extended Basic Block / Super Block** | **Medium** |
| Global Value Numbering (GVN) | Whole Procedure (CFG) | Coarsest |

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extended Basic Block (EBB) / Super Block**: A maximal sequence of basic blocks $\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$ such that:
> - Control flow may enter the EBB only at the head block $B_1$
> - For every $i \geq 2$, block $B_i$ has **exactly one** predecessor inside the EBB
> - The EBB contains no cycles (it is a **DAG** of basic blocks)
> - Exits from the EBB may occur at any block (multiple exits allowed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Value Number**: A small positive integer assigned at compile time to a *value* (a variable or a computed expression). Two computations receive the same value number *iff* the compiler can prove they will produce identical results at runtime.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phi-Function ($\phi$-function)**: A synthetic pseudo-instruction inserted at the start of a non-head block inside an EBB to logically merge values arriving from multiple predecessors: $x = \phi(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$ where $x_i$ is the value reaching from the $i$-th predecessor.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator Passport" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Customs & Visa Stamps**
>
> Imagine an international airport where every traveller (a *value* in your program) is assigned a **visa stamp number** the first time they enter. If two travellers later present the *exact same passport* (i.e. the same *expression*), the customs officer simply says *"I already stamped this one — entry #VN-7"*, and the second traveller is allowed through without re-inspection.
>
> - **Local Value Numbering** = a single customs booth. Once a traveller leaves the booth, their stamp is forgotten.
> - **Super Local Value Numbering** = a row of connected booths (the EBB). A traveller can move from Booth 1 to Booth 2 to Booth 3 without losing their stamp number — provided they don't backtrack.
> - **Global Value Numbering** = the entire airport, with multiple tracks merging and splitting, requiring a complex passport-exchange (phi-functions) at every junction.

This intuition captures *why* SLVN is more powerful than LVN: it "remembers" equivalence information across the linear flow of an EBB, but it does **not** attempt to reconcile values at *merge points* in the full CFG—that is the job of GVN.

### 1.4 Why SLVN Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Justification**
>
> Empirical studies on production compilers (GCC, LLVM) have shown that **70–85% of all redundancy in real code** lies *within* extended basic blocks rather than within individual basic blocks. SLVN therefore offers:
> - Significant constant propagation
> - Strength reduction (e.g., `x*1` → `x`)
> - Algebraic simplification
> - Common subexpression elimination (CSE)
> - Copy propagation across block boundaries
>
> — all with low compile-time cost, since the algorithm runs in near-linear time on an EBB.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Foundation — The "Why" Behind SLVN

The mathematical foundation of value numbering rests on the **partition of expressions into congruence classes**:
$$ E \;=\; \bigsqcup_{i=1}^{k} C_i, \quad C_i = \{e \in E \mid VN(e) = i\} $$

Within a congruence class $C_i$, **any** computation can replace **any other** without changing program semantics. SLVN's job is to make these classes as *large* as possible by tracking value identities through the EBB.

### 2.2 Data Structures Maintained by SLVN

| # | Structure | Purpose | Notation |
|---|-----------|---------|----------|
| 1 | **Value Number Table (VNT)** | Maps each *expression* (as a canonical key) to its assigned value number | $VNT[expr] \to \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 2 | **Variable Table (VT)** | Maps each *program variable name* to the value number currently representing it | $VT[x] \to \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 3 | **Hash Function** | Collapses an expression tree to a canonical integer key for VNT lookup | $h(op, v_1, v_2)$ |
| 4 | **Scope Marker / Generation Counter** | Tracks when a value-number binding expires at EBB exits | $gen_i$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Hash Function** is the heart of every value-numbering pass. KTU examiners frequently ask students to design or trace it.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Notes |
|---------|-------------------|-------|
| Value-Number Hash for Binary Op | $h(op, v_1, v_2) = (op \cdot B + v_1) \cdot B + v_2$ | $B$ is base; $v_1, v_2$ are operand value numbers |
| Unary Op Hash | $h_{unary}(op, v) = op \cdot B + v$ | e.g. for unary minus, conversion |
| Congruence Condition | $e_1 \equiv e_2 \iff VN(e_1) = VN(e_2)$ | Equivalence at compile time |
| Phi-Function Semantic | $x = \phi(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$ | "Selects the $x_j$ flowing from the predecessor that was actually executed" |
| Strictness | $\forall e \in C_i : \text{semantics}(e) = \text{semantics}(representative of } C_i)$ | Value numbering is *semantics-preserving* |
| Algorithm Complexity | $O(N \log N)$ for $N$ instructions in EBB | Hash-table operations dominate |
| EBB Property | $B_1 \to B_2 \to \cdots \to B_n : \text{deg}^{-}_{EBB}(B_i) = 1$ for $i > 1$ | Single predecessor inside EBB |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall — Absent operator ambiguity**
> When writing the hash, students sometimes write $h(op, v_1, v_2) = op + v_1 + v_2$ which is **wrong** because it causes collisions: $a+b$ and $b+a$ would have the same key, leading to incorrect substitution. Always use a **commutative-aware** scheme or handle commutativity explicitly.

### 2.4 The SLVN Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why each step matters (intuitive explanation included)**

**Step 1 — EBB Identification**
Walk the CFG to find the maximal linear chain of basic blocks where every non-head block has exactly one predecessor in the chain. *Why?* Because the algorithm assumes single-entry flow; if multiple predecessors exist, we'd need full GVN machinery.

**Step 2 — Phi-Function Insertion**
At the start of every non-head block in the EBB, insert $\phi$-functions for all variables that are *live-in* (used before being redefined) at that block.
$$ x_{new} = \phi(x_{from\,B_{i-1}}, x_{from\,other\,preds}) $$
*Why?* The right-hand side of an instruction later in the block may need to refer to "the value of $x$ that came from the path we just walked". Without $\phi$-functions, we cannot reason about which version of $x$ is in scope.

**Step 3 — Variable Renaming (SSA-style within the EBB)**
Each definition of a variable $x$ inside the EBB receives a fresh subscripted name: $x_1, x_2, \ldots$ *Why?* So that the value-number table never confuses two distinct *lives* of the same source variable.

**Step 4 — Process Each Instruction (the LVN core, applied region-wide)**
For every three-address instruction $x = y \ op \ z$:
- (a) Look up $VT[y]$ and $VT[z]$ to get $v_1, v_2$.
- (b) Compute the hash $h = h(op, v_1, v_2)$.
- (c) If $h \in VNT$: substitute $x$ with the existing value number (i.e. replace $x = y\ op\ z$ with a copy $x = u$ where $u$ is the representative variable of that value number).
- (d) Else: assign a fresh value number $vn = \text{next}()$ and update $VNT$ and $VT[x] = vn$.

**Step 5 — Algebraic Simplification (optional but high-yield)**
Before the hash lookup, test the operands for identities:
- $x + 0 = x$, $x \cdot 1 = x$, $x \cdot 0 = 0$, $x - 0 = x$, $x \vert 0 = x$, $x \& x = x$, etc.

**Step 6 — De-Renaming & Code Emission**
Walk the EBB a second time. Wherever a definition was rewritten to a copy, replace the RHS with the source variable's current (in-EBB) name. Emit the simplified code.

**Step 7 — Scope Reset at EBB Exit**
Discard the in-EBB value-number state (or commit it to the global table if propagating into the next region).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How SLVN Helps |
|--------------------|----------------|
| Embedded DSP Code | Eliminates repeated multiply-adds in FIR filters |
| Image Processing | Catches repeated pixel-coordinate arithmetic |
| Database Query Compilers | Removes redundant `WHERE` clause computations |
| Scientific Computing (Fortran) | Folds loop-invariant-ish expressions across block boundaries |
| Modern JIT (V8, HotSpot) | Forms the basis of speculative CSE in tiered compilers |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Trace**: This is the kind of full table-trace that earns full marks (14/14) in KTU ESE. Every column update is shown explicitly — no shortcuts.

### 3.1 The Source Three-Address Code (EBB)

The following EBB contains **two** basic blocks $B_1$ and $B_2$, where $B_2$ has a single predecessor ($B_1$) inside the EBB. The first block is reached from outside the EBB; flow may exit either at the end of $B_1$ or at the end of $B_2$.

```
B1:
  a = r + s
  b = r + s           // visually identical to previous line
  c = a * 2
  d = b - a           // = 0 by algebra
  if c > 100 goto B2
  goto B3             // exit from B1
B2:
  e = a + b           // = 2*(r+s)
  f = a * 2           // = c
  g = e + 1
  h = a + b           // redundant with e
B3:                   // outside EBB
  ...
```

### 3.2 Step 1 & 2 — Phi-Function Insertion and Renaming

Because $B_2$ has *one predecessor inside the EBB* ($B_1$) and possibly other predecessors outside the EBB, we must insert a $\phi$ for every variable that is **live-in** to $B_2$ from outside.

Variables live-in to $B_2$: $\{a, b, c, d, r, s\}$. Insert:
```
B2:
  a' = φ(a_in_from_B1, a_from_other_preds)
  b' = φ(b_in_from_B1, b_from_other_preds)
  c' = φ(...)
  ...
```

For clarity of the trace, let us assume $B_2$ has only $B_1$ as predecessor inside the EBB (typical EBB setting), so the $\phi$ degenerates to a rename. We then give every definition a fresh subscript:

```
B1:
  a_1 = r_0 + s_0
  b_1 = r_0 + s_0
  c_1 = a_1 * 2
  d_1 = b_1 - a_1
B2:
  e_1 = a_1 + b_1
  f_1 = a_1 * 2
  g_1 = e_1 + 1
  h_1 = a_1 + b_1
```

### 3.3 Step 3 & 4 — Process the EBB, Instruction by Instruction

We maintain a counter $cnt$ starting at $1$. The **Value Number Table (VNT)** maps a hash triple $(op, v_1, v_2)$ to a value number $vn$. The **Variable Table (VT)** maps each renamed variable to its current $vn$.

**Convention used in the trace**: $VNT$ rows are written as `op, vn_left, vn_right → vn`. We use a base $B = 257$ (any large prime works).

We also assume the initial constants $r_0$ and $s_0$ have been assigned $VT[r_0] = 10$ and $VT[s_0] = 20$ (constants from outside the EBB).

---

**Instruction 1**: `a_1 = r_0 + s_0`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Fetch } v_1 = VT[r_0] = 10,\ v_2 = VT[s_0] = 20. \\
&\text{Compute hash } h = h(+, 10, 20) = (+ \cdot B + 10) \cdot B + 20. \\
&\text{VNT lookup: not found} \Rightarrow \text{assign new } vn = 1. \\
&VNT[+, 10, 20] = 1. \\
&VT[a_1] = 1.
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Instruction 2**: `b_1 = r_0 + s_0`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&v_1 = VT[r_0] = 10,\ v_2 = VT[s_0] = 20. \\
&h = h(+, 10, 20). \text{ Lookup: FOUND } \Rightarrow vn = 1 \text{ already exists.} \\
&\therefore b_1 \equiv a_1. \text{ Rewrite as } b_1 = a_1. \\
&VT[b_1] = 1.
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Instruction 3**: `c_1 = a_1 * 2`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&v_1 = VT[a_1] = 1,\ v_2 = VN(\text{constant } 2) = 2. \\
&h = h(*, 1, 2). \text{ Lookup: not found} \Rightarrow vn = 3. \\
&VNT[*, 1, 2] = 3. \\
&VT[c_1] = 3.
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Instruction 4**: `d_1 = b_1 - a_1`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Algebraic pre-check: } v_1 = VT[b_1] = 1,\ v_2 = VT[a_1] = 1. \\
&\text{Since } v_1 = v_2 \Rightarrow b_1 - a_1 = 0. \\
&\text{Simplify to } d_1 = 0. \\
&VN(0) = 0 \text{ (reserved). } VT[d_1] = 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Instruction 5**: `e_1 = a_1 + b_1` (first instruction of $B_2$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{After phi-renaming, } a_1, b_1 \text{ are still in scope.} \\
&v_1 = VT[a_1] = 1,\ v_2 = VT[b_1] = 1. \\
&h = h(+, 1, 1). \text{ Lookup: not found} \Rightarrow vn = 4. \\
&VNT[+, 1, 1] = 4. \\
&VT[e_1] = 4.
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Instruction 6**: `f_1 = a_1 * 2`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&v_1 = VT[a_1] = 1,\ v_2 = VN(2) = 2. \\
&h = h(*, 1, 2). \text{ Lookup: FOUND } \Rightarrow vn = 3. \\
&\therefore f_1 \equiv c_1. \text{ Rewrite as } f_1 = c_1. \\
&VT[f_1] = 3.
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Instruction 7**: `g_1 = e_1 + 1`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&v_1 = VT[e_1] = 4,\ v_2 = VN(1) = 1. \\
&h = h(+, 4, 1). \text{ Lookup: not found} \Rightarrow vn = 5. \\
&VNT[+, 4, 1] = 5. \\
&VT[g_1] = 5.
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Instruction 8**: `h_1 = a_1 + b_1`

$$
\begin{aligned}
&v_1 = VT[a_1] = 1,\ v_2 = VT[b_1] = 1. \\
&h = h(+, 1, 1). \text{ Lookup: FOUND} \Rightarrow vn = 4. \\
&\therefore h_1 \equiv e_1. \text{ Rewrite as } h_1 = e_1. \\
&VT[h_1] = 4.
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Final Value Number Table (after Step 4)

| Entry # | Operator | VN Left | VN Right | Value # | Representative |
|---------|----------|---------|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | `+` | 10 | 20 | **1** | $a_1, b_1$ (i.e. $r+s$) |
| 2 | `*` | 1 | 2 | **3** | $c_1, f_1$ (i.e. $a*2$) |
| 3 | `+` | 1 | 1 | **4** | $e_1, h_1$ (i.e. $a+a$) |
| 4 | `+` | 4 | 1 | **5** | $g_1$ |
| 5 | constant | — | — | **0** | $d_1$ (zero) |

### 3.5 Optimized Code (Step 5: De-Renaming and Emission)

Applying the substitutions, the optimized EBB is:

```
B1:
  a = r + s                  // VN#1
  b = a                      // was r+s, replaced via VN#1
  c = a * 2                  // VN#3
  d = 0                      // was b-a, simplified algebraically
  if c > 100 goto B2
  goto B3
B2:
  e = a + a                  // was a+b, simplified via VN#1
  f = c                      // was a*2, replaced via VN#3
  g = e + 1                  // VN#5
  h = e                      // was a+b, replaced via VN#4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net effect**: 8 original computations → 5 essential computations, 3 redundant operations eliminated (b = a, f = c, h = e), plus 1 algebraic simplification (d = 0). This is exactly the kind of win that real compilers achieve on inner loops.

### 3.6 Efficiency Analysis Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{SLVN}}(N) &= N \cdot \big( T_{\text{hash}} + T_{\text{lookup}} + T_{\text{update}} \big) \\
&= N \cdot O(1) \cdot c \quad \text{(hash-table operations)} \\
&= O(N)
\end{aligned}
$$

With an $O(\log N)$-balanced tree implementation, the bound rises to $O(N \log N)$. The phi-insertion step adds an extra $O(V)$ pass over live variables, but $V \leq N$ inside an EBB, so the overall complexity is $O(N \log N)$ — well within KTU's "linear-time optimization" expectation for the regional analysis layer.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The EBB / Super Block Topology (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    entry0((ENTRY)) --> B1
    B1b((B1 exit: low branch)) --> B3low[B3 - outside EBB]
    B1 --> B2
    B2h((B2 exit)) --> B3high[B3 - outside EBB]
    subgraph EBB1["Extended Basic Block EBB-1"]
        B1["B1: a=r+s; b=r+s; c=a*2; d=b-a"]
        B2["B2: e=a+b; f=a*2; g=e+1; h=a+b"]
        B1 --> B2
    end
    B1b -.outside EBB.-> B3low
    B2h -.outside EBB.-> B3high
```

### 4.2 The SLVN Algorithm Control Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: EBB Identified] --> B[Insert phi-functions at non-head blocks]
    B --> C[Rename variables SSA-style]
    C --> D[Initialize VNT and VT empty]
    D --> E{For each instruction in EBB order}
    E --> F[Lookup vn_left and vn_right from VT]
    F --> G[Apply algebraic simplifications]
    G --> H[Compute hash of op, vn_left, vn_right]
    H --> I{Hash already in VNT?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Rewrite RHS as copy of representative]
    I -- No --> K[Assign new value number]
    J --> L[Update VT for LHS]
    K --> L
    L --> M{More instructions?}
    M -- Yes --> E
    M -- No --> N[De-rename and emit optimized code]
    N --> O[Reset VNT/VT at EBB exit]
    O --> P[End]
```

### 4.3 The Value-Number Resolution Chain (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[r0] -->|VN 10| A1
    A1[r0 + s0] -->|VN 1| A2
    A2[a1] -->|VN 1| A3
    A3[b1 - a1] -->|algebra 0| A4
    A4[d1] -->|VN 0| A5
    A2 -->|VN 1| B1
    B1[a1 + b1] -->|VN 4| B2
    B2[e1] -->|VN 4| B3
    A2 -->|VN 1| C1
    C1[a1 star 2] -->|VN 3| C2
    C2[c1]
    B1 -->|VN 4 + 1| D1
    D1[e1 + 1] -->|VN 5| D2
    D2[g1]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SLVN Pass Placement

```mermaid
graph TB
    SRC[Source IR / Three-Address Code] --> CFG[Control Flow Graph Builder]
    CFG --> DOM[Dominator Frontier Analysis]
    DOM --> EBB[EBB / Super Block Identifier]
    EBB --> RENAME[Rename + Phi Insertion]
    RENAME --> SLVN[Super Local Value Numbering Core]
    SLVN --> ALG[Algebraic Simplifier]
    ALG --> EMIT[Optimized IR Emitter]
    EMIT --> NEXT[Next Optimization Pass: GVN or LICM]
    style SLVN fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EBB fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The yellow-highlighted node `SLVN` represents the actual value-numbering kernel. The blue-highlighted `EBB` node shows the pre-analysis step that defines the *region* of optimization. This block diagram mirrors the way a production compiler (LLVM's `GVN.cpp` legacy path) organizes its internal pass pipeline.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Super Local Value Numbering. How does it differ from Local Value Numbering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Super Local Value Numbering (SLVN) is a regional optimization technique that applies the principle of value numbering across an **Extended Basic Block (EBB)** — a linear chain of basic blocks where every non-head block has exactly one predecessor inside the region.

**Key differences from LVN (1 mark each):**

| Aspect | Local Value Numbering | Super Local Value Numbering |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Region | One basic block | Extended basic block (super block) |
| Phi-functions | Not required | Required at non-head blocks |
| Reach of redundancies | Limited to a single block | Catches redundancies across the EBB |
| Complexity | $O(n)$ per block | $O(n \log n)$ per EBB |

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is an Extended Basic Block? Why are phi-functions needed at its non-head blocks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Extended Basic Block (EBB)** is a maximal sequence of basic blocks $\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_n\}$ such that the head $B_1$ dominates all others in the EBB, and every $B_i$ ($i \geq 2$) has exactly one predecessor inside the EBB.

**Phi-functions are needed (1.5 marks):** because the same source-level variable may reach the start of a non-head block from **multiple predecessors** outside the EBB. A phi-function $\phi(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)$ merges these definitions into a single logical definition, allowing subsequent instructions to be value-numbered unambiguously.

**Example:** $a' = \phi(a_{B_1}, a_{B_4}, a_{B_7})$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the algebraic simplification pre-check** (Step 5) and lose 1–2 marks. Always test for $x+0$, $x\cdot 1$, $x\cdot 0$, $x-0$, $x-x$ **before** computing the hash.
> - Students often **forget to insert phi-functions** at the start of non-head blocks. This is a compulsory 2-mark deduction in KTU boards.
> - The **value table** must be shown in full — examiners award marks for **explicit tabulation**, not for descriptive prose.
> - The base of the hash function $B$ must be a **prime ≥ 257**; using $B=10$ causes collisions and is penalised.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**(a)** Explain the Super Local Value Numbering algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of (i) the value-number table, (ii) phi-functions, and (iii) the hash function.        **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply Super Local Value Numbering to the following three-address code segment over the EBB and show the **optimized code** along with the **final value-number table**.        **(7 marks)**

```
B1:
  x = a * b
  y = a * b
  z = x + 0
  w = y * 1
  u = x + y
B2:
  v = a * b
  t = u + z
```

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

(i) **Value Number Table (2 marks):** The VNT is a hash-table that records, for each expression seen so far in the EBB, the unique value number assigned to its congruence class. Lookup: $O(1)$ average. Insertion: $O(1)$ average.

(ii) **Phi-functions (2 marks):** Inserted at the start of every non-head block in the EBB. Their job is to merge definitions of the same source variable arriving from different predecessors so that the value-numbering kernel can treat the block as if it had a single "merged" definition on entry. Without phi-functions, the algorithm cannot reason about which version of a variable is in scope.

(iii) **Hash function (3 marks):** A canonical-mapping function from an expression tree to a single integer. For a binary operation:
$$ h(op, v_1, v_2) = (op \cdot B + v_1) \cdot B + v_2 $$
where $B$ is a prime $\geq 257$, $v_1, v_2$ are value numbers of the operands, and $op$ is an enumerated operator code. This must be combined with a **commutativity check** (e.g., normalize $a+b$ and $b+a$ to the same key) to avoid spurious misses.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Phi-insertion and renaming.** $B_2$ has one in-EBB predecessor, so we insert degenerated phis (= renames) for live-in variables $\{x, y, z, w, u, a, b\}$.

**Step 2: Trace.**

Assume $VT[a] = 30$, $VT[b] = 40$ (initial). Hash base $B = 257$.

| # | Instruction | $v_1$ | $v_2$ | Hash $h(op, v_1, v_2)$ | Found? | Action | New $VN$ |
|---|-------------|-------|-------|------------------------|--------|--------|----------|
| 1 | $x_1 = a \cdot b$ | 30 | 40 | $h(*, 30, 40)$ | No | new | **1** |
| 2 | $y_1 = a \cdot b$ | 30 | 40 | $h(*, 30, 40)$ | **Yes** | $y_1 = x_1$ | — (VN 1) |
| 3 | $z_1 = x_1 + 0$ | 1 | 0 (VN of literal 0) | algebraic: $x_1 + 0 = x_1$ | — | $z_1 = x_1$ | — (VN 1) |
| 4 | $w_1 = y_1 \cdot 1$ | 1 | 1 (literal 1) | algebraic: $y_1 \cdot 1 = y_1$ | — | $w_1 = y_1$ | — (VN 1) |
| 5 | $u_1 = x_1 + y_1$ | 1 | 1 | $h(+, 1, 1)$ | No | new | **2** |
| 6 | $v_1 = a \cdot b$ | 30 | 40 | $h(*, 30, 40)$ | **Yes** | $v_1 = x_1$ | — (VN 1) |
| 7 | $t_1 = u_1 + z_1$ | 2 | 1 | $h(+, 2, 1)$ | No | new | **3** |

**Final Value Number Table:**

| $VN$ | Expression | Representative |
|-------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | $a \cdot b$ | $x_1, y_1, z_1, w_1, v_1$ |
| 2 | $a \cdot b + a \cdot b$ | $u_1$ |
| 3 | $(a \cdot b + a \cdot b) + a \cdot b$ | $t_1$ |

**Optimized Code (after de-rename):**

```
B1:
  x = a * b
  y = x
  z = x
  w = y
  u = x + y
B2:
  v = x
  t = u + z
```

Notice that $u = 2x$ and $t = 3x$ at runtime — SLVN has *not* yet performed strength reduction on the additions, but the **redundant multiplications have been eliminated**, which is the principal win.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**(a)** Compare **Local Value Numbering** and **Super Local Value Numbering**. Explain why the latter is *not* sufficient as a stand-alone optimization pass in a modern compiler.        **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following CFG fragment. Identify the **Extended Basic Block(s)** and apply SLVN to each, then write the optimized code.        **(7 marks)**

```
B1:
  p = m + n
  q = m + n
  r = p - q
  if r == 0 goto B2
  goto B3
B2:
  s = m + n
  t = p + 1
  u = q + 1
B3:
  v = s + t
```

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Criterion (1 mark each) | LVN | SLVN |
|--------------------------|-----|------|
| Region | Single basic block | Extended basic block |
| Phi-functions | Not required | Required at non-head blocks |
| Renaming | Not required | Required (SSA-style) |
| Redundancy scope | Intra-block only | Inter-block (within EBB) |
| Compile-time cost | $O(n)$ per block | $O(n \log n)$ per EBB |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Moderate |
| Sufficient as a stand-alone pass? | No (very narrow scope) | No — cannot handle **merge points** where two EBBs converge, e.g. after a `if-else` |

**Why SLVN alone is insufficient (2 marks):** Consider a CFG where two distinct extended basic blocks both feed a join block $B_j$. SLVN, applied independently to each EBB, has no mechanism to compare value numbers across the join. The "same expression" computed along path A and path B may have *different value numbers* even though the expressions are equivalent. A **Global Value Numbering (GVN)** pass is required to perform this reconciliation across the full CFG.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: EBB Identification.** $B_1 \to B_2$ is a linear chain ($B_2$ has $B_1$ as its only predecessor inside this segment — $B_1$'s other exit goes to $B_3$ which is **not** in the EBB). $B_3$ is reached by *two* predecessors ($B_1$ and $B_2$), so it is the head of a **new** EBB (or the boundary of the current one).

Hence **EBB-1 = $\{B_1, B_2\}$** and **$B_3$** starts a new region (or marks the EBB exit).

**Step 2: Apply SLVN to EBB-1.**

Initial: $VT[m] = 50$, $VT[n] = 60$.

| # | Instruction | $v_1$ | $v_2$ | Action | $VN$ |
|---|-------------|-------|-------|--------|------|
| 1 | $p = m + n$ | 50 | 60 | new | **1** |
| 2 | $q = m + n$ | 50 | 60 | redundant: $q = p$ | — |
| 3 | $r = p - q$ | 1 | 1 | algebraic: $p - p = 0$ → $r = 0$ | 0 |
| 4 | $s = m + n$ (B2 entry) | 50 | 60 | redundant: $s = p$ | — |
| 5 | $t = p + 1$ | 1 | 1 | new | **2** |
| 6 | $u = q + 1$ | 1 | 1 | same hash as `t` → $u = t$ | — |

**Final VNT for EBB-1:**

| $VN$ | Expression | Representative |
|-------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | $m + n$ | $p, q, s$ |
| 2 | $(m+n) + 1$ | $t, u$ |

**Optimized code (EBB-1):**

```
B1:
  p = m + n
  q = p
  r = 0
  if r == 0 goto B2
  goto B3
B2:
  s = p
  t = p + 1
  u = t
B3:
  v = s + t          // not value-numbered here; outside EBB-1
```

If we treat $B_3$ as the head of a fresh EBB-2, the value-numbering on `v = s + t` would require fresh state, but the **cross-EBB equivalence** ($s = p$, $t = p+1$) has been preserved through the renamed variables, allowing a downstream GVN pass to continue the analysis seamlessly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU 2024**

- [ ] **SLVN = LVN on an Extended Basic Block (EBB).**
- [ ] An EBB has **one entry** (the head) and **may have multiple exits**.
- [ ] Every non-head block in the EBB has **exactly one predecessor inside the EBB**.
- [ ] **Phi-functions** must be inserted at the start of every non-head block for live-in variables.
- [ ] **Variable renaming** to SSA-style is required before the value-numbering kernel runs.
- [ ] The **hash function** for a binary op is $h(op, v_1, v_2) = (op \cdot B + v_1) \cdot B + v_2$, with $B$ a **prime ≥ 257**.
- [ ] **Commutativity** must be normalised: $a + b$ and $b + a$ must hash to the same key.
- [ ] **Algebraic pre-checks** ($x+0$, $x \cdot 1$, $x \cdot 0$, $x - x$, etc.) are tested **before** hashing.
- [ ] The Value Number Table $VNT$ maps *expressions* to value numbers; the Variable Table $VT$ maps *variables* to value numbers.
- [ ] **Two expressions are equivalent** iff they have the same value number.
- [ ] **Scope reset** is mandatory at the EBB exit — bindings do not leak to a different EBB.
- [ ] **Complexity**: $O(n)$ to $O(n \log n)$ for $n$ instructions in an EBB.
- [ ] SLVN is **strictly weaker** than GVN (cannot handle CFG merge points).
- [ ] SLVN is **strictly stronger** than LVN (catches inter-block redundancy in a linear chain).
- [ ] **Most-cited exam trick**: showing the VNT and VT in a *table* (not prose) is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
- [ ] The optimized code must be **de-renamed** to a clean, human-readable form before submission.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 — Regional Optimization: Super Local Value Numbering**
> This concludes the topic as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme for PBCRT604. For continuation, the natural next topic is **Global Value Numbering (GVN)**, which extends SLVN to the entire procedure by reconciling value numbers at CFG merge points via the dominance frontier and SSA phi-functions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loop Unrolling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Loop Unrolling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Loop Unrolling** is a well-known **loop transformation** (or *loop optimization*) technique in which the body of a loop is *replicated* (unrolled) multiple times — the **unroll factor** $k$ — within a single iteration of a new, larger loop, with the trip count adjusted accordingly. The objective is to **reduce the overhead of loop control** (increment, branch, and termination checks), **expose more instruction-level parallelism (ILP)**, and **enable further optimizations** such as instruction scheduling, register allocation, and dead-code elimination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Loop unrolling is a compiler optimization that replicates the statements inside a loop body a number of times (the unroll factor), thereby reducing the number of iterations and hence the loop-control overhead."*

The transformation is generally safe when the number of iterations is **known at compile time** (a *counted loop* with a constant trip count) or is a multiple of the unroll factor. For unknown trip counts, a *remainder loop* (or *residual loop*) is generated to handle leftover iterations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Carrying Water in Buckets":**
> 
> Imagine you have to carry **1000 cups** of water from a well to your house. The standard "loop" is: *walk to the well → fill one cup → walk back → pour it → repeat 1000 times*. That is exhausting! 
> 
> **Loop unrolling** is like deciding to use a **bigger bucket** — fill **4 cups' worth of water at once** into a single bucket, then walk back. You now only need to make **250 trips** instead of **1000**. The number of trips (loop iterations) is reduced, and the *per-trip* overhead (walking) is amortized over more useful work.
> 
> In compiler terms: the **bucket** is the *unrolled body*, the **cups-per-bucket** is the *unroll factor* $k$, and the **trips** are *loop iterations*. The compiler performs the same trick on machine instructions — bundling several iterations of useful work into one cycle of loop control.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Unroll Factor ($k$):** The number of times the original loop body is replicated. Also called the *unroll factor* or *unrolling degree*. Common values are $k = 2, 4, 8$.
- **Trip Count ($N$):** The total number of iterations of the original loop.
- **Residual / Remainder Loop:** A small leftover loop that handles the $N \bmod k$ iterations not covered by the unrolled main loop.
- **Static (Compile-Time) Unrolling:** Performed by the compiler when $N$ and $k$ are compile-time constants.
- **Dynamic (Run-Time) Unrolling:** A software pipelining / just-in-time variant used when trip counts are not known statically.
- **Prologue / Epilogue:** Code executed before/after the unrolled loop to handle alignment or peeled iterations.

## 1.4 Why It Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus High-Yield Points:**
> - Module 4 focuses on **Code Optimization** — both *Machine-Independent* and *Machine-Dependent*.
> - Loop unrolling is **explicitly mentioned** as a classic loop optimization.
> - Exam questions frequently ask: *"Explain loop unrolling with an example,"* or *"Compare loop unrolling with other loop optimizations."*
> - It is also tested in **CO4**: *Apply various code optimization techniques to improve intermediate code.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept (The "Why" and "How")

Loop unrolling is performed in three logical stages:

### Stage 1 — Determine Feasibility
- Is the loop *counted* (do we know $N$)?
- Are there any *procedure calls* inside the body that prevent replication?
- Is the *stride* (increment) constant?

### Stage 2 — Choose Unroll Factor $k$
- $k$ is typically a power of 2 ($k = 2, 4, 8$) to simplify code generation and address arithmetic.
- The compiler must balance:
  - **Reduced loop overhead** (more useful work per iteration).
  - **Larger code size** (potential *I-cache* pressure).
  - **Increased register pressure** (more live values).

### Stage 3 — Transform the Code
- Replicate the body $k$ times.
- Adjust the induction variable: $i \leftarrow i + k$ per iteration.
- Adjust the trip count: new count $\leftarrow N / k$.
- Generate a **residual loop** for $N \bmod k$ leftover iterations.

## 2.2 Worked Example: Static Unrolling with $k = 2$

### Original Loop
```c
for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
    a[i] = b[i] + c[i];
}
```

### Unrolled by $k = 2$
```c
for (i = 0; i < 100; i += 2) {
    a[i]   = b[i]   + c[i];
    a[i+1] = b[i+1] + c[i+1];
}
```

### Observations
- **Loop control instructions reduced by 50%** — branch, increment, and compare happen half as often.
- **More independent instructions** — `a[i] = b[i] + c[i]` and `a[i+1] = b[i+1] + c[i+1]` are independent, enabling CPU **pipelining / superscalar dispatch**.
- **Register reuse opportunities** — the pointer arithmetic is amortized.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **key formulas and metrics** for loop unrolling. Memorize these for the 3-mark and 14-mark KTU questions.

| Quantity | Symbol / Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Unroll Factor | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$, $k = 2^{n}$ | Number of body replications |
| Original Trip Count | $N$ | Total iterations of source loop |
| New Trip Count | $N' = \lfloor N / k \rfloor$ | Iterations of unrolled main loop |
| Residual Iterations | $r = N \bmod k$ | Iterations handled by remainder loop |
| Total Loop Overhead Reduction | $1 - \frac{1}{k}$ | Fraction of overhead eliminated |
| Code-Size Growth Factor | $\approx k$ | Approximate growth in static instruction count |
| Speedup Upper Bound | $S_{\max} \leq k$ | Ideal case (overhead fully removed) |
| Amdahl-Style Realistic Speedup | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ | $f$ = fraction of time in loop body |
| Register Pressure Bound | $L \leq R_{\text{avail}}$ | Live values $L$ must fit in available registers $R_{\text{avail}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** When writing formulas inline in the KTU exam, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the keyboard pipe `|` to avoid breaking markdown tables. Example: write $N \bmod k$ **not** $N \mid k$ (which is division).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Loop Unrolling Shines

- **Embedded DSP / Signal Processing:** Inner loops (e.g., FIR filters, FFT butterflies) are heavily unrolled for real-time guarantees.
- **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** BLAS libraries (e.g., DGEMM) use aggressive unrolling to reach near-peak FLOPs.
- **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilers:** HotSpot (Java), LLVM JIT, and GCC use *profile-guided* unrolling at runtime.
- **GPU / SIMD Code Generation:** Unrolling exposes vectorizable patterns for SSE, AVX, NEON.
- **Compiler Pedagogy:** A classic textbook example (Aho/Sethi/Ullman *Dragon Book*, Muchnick *Advanced Compiler Design*) demonstrating intermediate-code transformation.

## 2.5 Trade-Offs at a Glance

| Pro | Con |
|---|---|
| Reduces branch misprediction penalty | Increases code size (I-cache misses) |
| Exposes ILP for superscalar CPUs | Increases register pressure (spills) |
| Enables instruction scheduling | Can hurt performance if $k$ is too large |
| Amortizes induction-variable arithmetic | Requires trip-count analysis or residual loop |
| Improves software-pipelining opportunities | Complicates debugging and profiling |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Worked Example — Static Unrolling with $k = 4$

### Original Three-Address Code (Before)

The compiler's intermediate representation of the source loop:

```text
L1:  i := 0
L2:  if i >= 100 goto L_END
     t1 := 4 * i
     t2 := a[t1]
     t3 := 4 * i
     t4 := b[t1]
     t5 := t2 + t4
     t3 := 4 * i
     a[t3] := t5
     i := i + 1
     goto L2
L_END:
```

### Step 1 — Identify the Induction Variable and Trip Count
- Induction variable: $i$, initial value $0$, step $+1$, bound $100$.
- Trip count: $N = 100$.

### Step 2 — Choose Unroll Factor
- Let $k = 4$. Since $100$ is divisible by $4$, no residual loop is needed.

### Step 3 — Replicate the Body Four Times

We replicate the *useful statements* (lines 4–8 in the source IR) **four times**, with offsets $0, 1, 2, 3$ on the induction variable.

### Step 4 — Adjust the Induction Step
- The induction variable now advances by $k = 4$: $i := i + 4$.

### Step 5 — Adjust the Loop Bound
- New condition: $i < 100$ (or equivalently $i \leq 96$, depending on style).

### Resulting Three-Address Code (After)

```text
L1:   i := 0
L2:   if i >= 100 goto L_END
      t1 := 4 * i
      t2 := a[t1]
      t3 := b[t1]
      t4 := t2 + t3
      a[t1] := t4

      t5 := 4 * (i + 1)
      t6 := a[t5]
      t7 := b[t5]
      t8 := t6 + t7
      a[t5] := t8

      t9  := 4 * (i + 2)
      t10 := a[t9]
      t11 := b[t9]
      t12 := t10 + t11
      a[t9] := t12

      t13 := 4 * (i + 3)
      t14 := a[t13]
      t15 := b[t13]
      t16 := t14 + t15
      a[t13] := t16

      i := i + 4
      goto L2
L_END:
```

### Step 6 — Count Instructions to Verify Savings

**Before unrolling (per iteration):**
- 1 branch test, 1 induction add, 1 goto = **3 control instructions**.
- 1 multiply, 2 array loads, 1 add, 1 store = **5 useful instructions**.

**After unrolling (per outer iteration = 4 source iterations):**
- 1 branch test, 1 induction add, 1 goto = **3 control instructions** (same).
- $4 \times 5 = 20$ useful instructions.

Per-iteration overhead ratio:

$$
\text{Overhead Fraction} = \frac{\text{Control Ins.}}{\text{Total Ins.}} = \frac{3}{3 + 5k}
$$

Computing explicitly for $k = 4$:

$$
\text{Overhead Fraction}_{k=4} = \frac{3}{3 + 5 \cdot 4} = \frac{3}{23} \approx 13.0\%
$$

For the original loop ($k = 1$):

$$
\text{Overhead Fraction}_{k=1} = \frac{3}{3 + 5 \cdot 1} = \frac{3}{8} = 37.5\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The overhead dropped from **37.5%** to **13.0%** — a relative reduction of nearly **65%** in the fraction of time spent on loop control. This is the *quantitative* benefit loop unrolling offers.

## 3.2 Speedup Derivation (Amdahl-Style)

Let:
- $T_{\text{body}}$ = time spent in the loop body per iteration (the *useful* part).
- $T_{\text{overhead}}$ = time spent on loop control per iteration.

Total time for $N$ iterations before unrolling:

$$
T_{\text{orig}} = N \cdot (T_{\text{body}} + T_{\text{overhead}})
$$

After unrolling with factor $k$ (assume $N$ divisible by $k$):

$$
T_{\text{new}} = \frac{N}{k} \cdot (k \cdot T_{\text{body}} + T_{\text{overhead}}) = N \cdot T_{\text{body}} + \frac{N}{k} \cdot T_{\text{overhead}}
$$

The resulting speedup is:

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{orig}}}{T_{\text{new}}} = \frac{N \cdot T_{\text{body}} + N \cdot T_{\text{overhead}}}{N \cdot T_{\text{body}} + \dfrac{N}{k} \cdot T_{\text{overhead}}}
$$

Canceling $N$:

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{body}} + T_{\text{overhead}}}{T_{\text{body}} + \dfrac{T_{\text{overhead}}}{k}}
$$

Let $f = \dfrac{T_{\text{overhead}}}{T_{\text{body}} + T_{\text{overhead}}}$ denote the **fraction of time** originally spent on loop control. Then:

$$
S = \frac{1}{1 - f + \dfrac{f}{k}}
$$

This is the same form as Amdahl's Law for parallel processing — illustrating that loop unrolling has a **diminishing-returns** character: the larger $k$ becomes, the less additional speedup is obtained.

### Worked Numerical Check

Let $f = 0.375$ (matching the example above, $k=1$ case) and $k = 4$:

$$
S = \frac{1}{1 - 0.375 + \dfrac{0.375}{4}} = \frac{1}{0.625 + 0.09375} = \frac{1}{0.71875} \approx 1.39
$$

So a **39% speedup** is achievable with $k = 4$ in this configuration.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Unroll and When NOT To

| Condition | Should Unroll? | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Trip count $N$ is known at compile time | ✅ Yes | Static unrolling is straightforward |
| Loop body is small (few instructions) | ✅ Yes | Replication cost is low |
| Loop body has *no* procedure calls | ✅ Yes | Avoids code-size explosion |
| $N$ is large and overhead-dominated | ✅ Yes | Big absolute savings |
| Body has many *live variables* | ❌ No | Register pressure → spills |
| I-cache is small / code is large | ❌ No | Cache thrashing may dominate |
| Body has side effects / aliasing | ⚠️ Careful | May break dependence analysis |
| $N$ is very small | ❌ No | No useful iterations to amortize over |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The Loop-Unrolling Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Loop IR] --> B{Is Trip Count Known?}
    B -- No --> C[Conservative Unrolling or Skip]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is Body Free of Calls and Side Effects?}
    D -- No --> E[Partial or Skip Unroll]
    D -- Yes --> F[Select Unroll Factor k]
    F --> G{Replication Phase}
    G --> H[Copy Body k Times with Offsets 0,1,...,k-1]
    H --> I[Adjust Induction Step to k]
    I --> J{N mod k = 0?}
    J -- No --> K[Generate Residual Loop for N mod k]
    J -- Yes --> L[Single Unrolled Loop]
    K --> M[Emit Optimized IR]
    L --> M[Emit Optimized IR]
    E --> M
    C --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cost vs. Benefit Trade-Off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BENEFITS
        B1[Less Branch Overhead]
        B2[Exposed ILP]
        B3[Better Scheduling]
    end
    subgraph COSTS
        C1[Larger Code Size]
        C2[Register Pressure]
        C3[I-Cache Pressure]
    end
    LOOP[Loop Unrolling k] --> BENEFITS
    LOOP --> COSTS
    BENEFITS --> DECISION{Profitability Test}
    COSTS --> DECISION
    DECISION -- Pass --> OUT1[Apply Optimization]
    DECISION -- Fail --> OUT2[Keep Original Loop]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Static vs. Dynamic Loop Unrolling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Loop Unrolling] --> B[Static Unrolling]
    A --> C[Dynamic Unrolling]
    B --> B1[Compile Time]
    B --> B2[Trip Count Known]
    B --> B3[Used in Offline Compilers like GCC -O3]
    C --> C1[Run Time via JIT]
    C --> C2[Profile Guided]
    C --> C3[Used in HotSpot, LLVM JIT]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Reduction in Loop-Overhead Fraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[k equals 1] --> A1[Overhead 37.5 percent]
    B[k equals 2] --> B1[Overhead 23.1 percent]
    C[k equals 4] --> C1[Overhead 13.0 percent]
    D[k equals 8] --> D1[Overhead 7.1 percent]
    E[k equals 16] --> E1[Overhead 3.7 percent]
    A1 --> NOTE[Diminishing Returns]
    B1 --> NOTE
    C1 --> NOTE
    D1 --> NOTE
    E1 --> NOTE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Notice:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `NOTE`) and contain no reserved keywords. All labels use plain uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold, italics, or HTML inside quoted labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define loop unrolling. What is an unroll factor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Loop unrolling** is a *machine-dependent loop optimization* technique in which the body of a loop is *replicated* multiple times, and the *loop control* is adjusted accordingly. The **unroll factor** $k$ is the *number of times* the loop body is replicated inside a single iteration of the new (transformed) loop. For instance, an unroll factor of $k = 4$ means the body is repeated four times, reducing the trip count from $N$ to $N / k$, and the per-iteration overhead (branch, increment, compare) by a factor of $k$.
> 
> *(Word-count guideline for 3-mark KTU answer: ~80–100 words.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any two advantages and two disadvantages of loop unrolling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Advantages:**
> 1. **Reduces loop-control overhead** — fewer branches, comparisons, and induction-variable updates.
> 2. **Exposes instruction-level parallelism (ILP)** — independent operations from the replicated body can be issued in the same CPU cycle.
> 
> **Disadvantages:**
> 1. **Increased code size** — may cause I-cache pressure and longer compilation time.
> 2. **Higher register pressure** — more live variables may exceed available registers, causing spills to memory.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question in a KTU end-semester exam carries **14 marks** and typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**, with an *internal choice* — meaning you must answer **either (a) or (b)**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are the two alternative question choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of *loop unrolling* with a suitable example. Show the transformation for a loop of trip count 100 unrolled by a factor of 4. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the *benefits* and *limitations* of loop unrolling. How does it interact with *register allocation*? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Explanation + Example (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Loop unrolling is a *loop transformation* in which the body of a loop is **replicated** $k$ times (where $k$ is the unroll factor) and the loop-control structure is *adjusted* so that the total number of iterations is preserved. The technique reduces the *relative overhead* of loop-control instructions (branch, increment, compare) and *exposes more parallelism* for the processor to exploit.

**Original Source Loop (1 mark):**

```c
for (i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
    a[i] = b[i] + c[i];
}
```

**Unrolled by $k = 4$ (3 marks):**

```c
for (i = 0; i < 100; i += 4) {
    a[i]     = b[i]     + c[i];
    a[i + 1] = b[i + 1] + c[i + 1];
    a[i + 2] = b[i + 2] + c[i + 2];
    a[i + 3] = b[i + 3] + c[i + 3];
}
```

**Note (1 mark):** Since $N = 100$ is divisible by $k = 4$, no residual/remainder loop is required. If $N$ were not a multiple of $k$, the compiler would emit a small residual loop to handle the leftover $N \bmod k$ iterations.

---

**Part (b) — Benefits, Limitations, Register Interaction (7 marks):**

**Benefits (3 marks):**
1. **Loop-Overhead Reduction:** The number of branch, compare, and increment instructions is reduced by a factor of $k$, freeing execution units for useful work.
2. **Instruction-Level Parallelism:** Operations across the replicated body are *independent* (no write-after-read or read-after-write hazards), enabling the CPU to dispatch them in parallel in a *superscalar* pipeline.
3. **Enables Other Optimizations:** Unrolling exposes *redundancies* (e.g., `4 * i` vs `4 * (i+1)`) that the compiler can fold via *strength reduction* and *common-subexpression elimination*.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Code-Size Increase:** The static code size grows roughly $k$-fold, which may exceed the *I-cache* footprint and trigger cache misses.
2. **Compilation Time:** Larger IRs and optimization passes take longer.

**Interaction with Register Allocation (2 marks):**
Unrolling multiplies the number of *simultaneously live values* in a single iteration. For instance, in the $k = 4$ example above, four values of `b[i]`, four of `c[i]`, and four of `a[i]` are live. If the target machine has only 8 general-purpose registers, the register allocator may be forced to **spill** some values to the stack, *negating* the unrolling benefit. A safe heuristic is to limit $k$ such that $L \leq R_{\text{avail}}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat example, illustrate *static loop unrolling*. Differentiate between *static* and *dynamic* loop unrolling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following loop:
```c
for (i = 0; i < 50; i++) {
    sum = sum + a[i];
}
```
Apply *loop unrolling* with factor $k = 5$. Show the resulting three-address code. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Static vs. Dynamic Unrolling (7 marks):**

**Static Loop Unrolling (3 marks):**
Static unrolling is performed by the **compiler at compile time** when the *trip count* (number of iterations) is *known* and the loop body is *free of procedure calls and complex control flow*. The compiler replicates the body $k$ times and adjusts the induction step and bound.

**Example (2 marks):**

```c
/* Original */
for (i = 0; i < 4; i++)
    x[i] = y[i] + z[i];

/* Unrolled k = 2 */
for (i = 0; i < 4; i += 2) {
    x[i]     = y[i]     + z[i];
    x[i + 1] = y[i + 1] + z[i + 1];
}
```

**Differences Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Unrolling | Dynamic Unrolling |
|---|---|---|
| When | Compile time | Run time (JIT) |
| Trip Count | Must be known | Can be unknown |
| Tools | GCC, LLVM offline passes | HotSpot, LLVM JIT, MSVC PGO |
| Code Growth | Static, fixed | Adapts to hot loops |
| Cost | Compilation time | Run-time overhead |

---

**Part (b) — Unrolling with $k = 5$ (7 marks):**

**Original Three-Address Code (2 marks):**

```text
L1:  sum := 0
     i   := 0
L2:  if i >= 50 goto L_END
     t1 := 4 * i
     t2 := a[t1]
     sum := sum + t2
     i   := i + 1
     goto L2
L_END:
```

**Unrolled Three-Address Code (5 marks):**
Since $N = 50$ is divisible by $k = 5$, no residual loop is needed. The unrolled version:

```text
L1:   sum := 0
      i   := 0
L2:   if i >= 50 goto L_END
      t1 := 4 * i
      t2 := a[t1]
      sum := sum + t2

      t3 := 4 * (i + 1)
      t4 := a[t3]
      sum := sum + t4

      t5 := 4 * (i + 2)
      t6 := a[t5]
      sum := sum + t6

      t7 := 4 * (i + 3)
      t8 := a[t7]
      sum := sum + t8

      t9 := 4 * (i + 4)
      t10 := a[t9]
      sum := sum + t10

      i := i + 5
      goto L2
L_END:
```

**Verification (bonus, if asked):** The number of *outer iterations* is now $50 / 5 = 10$, reducing the loop-control instructions by a factor of **5** (an 80% reduction in overhead).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the residual loop** — When $N$ is not divisible by $k$, students often present the unrolled loop as if it handles all iterations. *Always mention* that leftover $N \bmod k$ iterations need a separate *remainder loop*.
> 
> 2. **Not adjusting the induction-variable step** — Many students leave `i := i + 1` after unrolling. The correct step is `i := i + k`.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring register pressure** — A common 2-mark loss in part (b) of Question A. The examiner expects you to mention that unrolling increases live variables.
> 
> 4. **Conflating strength reduction with unrolling** — These are *different* loop optimizations. Strength reduction replaces expensive ops (e.g., multiplication) with cheaper ones (e.g., addition); unrolling *replicates the body*. Do not mix them up.
> 
> 5. **Missing the difference between static and dynamic unrolling** — A favorite 3-mark short-answer trap. Memorize the table above.
> 
> 6. **No diagrams** — KTU examiners reward clear, labeled flow diagrams in 7-mark answers. Use a `flowchart` of the unrolling pipeline.
> 
> 7. **Not stating trade-offs** — A unidirectional answer (only listing benefits) will lose 1–2 marks. Always end with at least one *limitation* (e.g., code size, register pressure).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Viva & Written Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Loop unrolling is a *loop transformation* that **replicates the body** $k$ times and **reduces loop-control overhead**.
- ✅ **Unroll factor $k$:** Number of body replications; typically a power of 2.
- ✅ **Trip count after unrolling:** $N' = \lfloor N / k \rfloor$; **residual loop** handles $r = N \bmod k$.
- ✅ **Induction-variable step changes** from $+1$ to $+k$.
- ✅ **Three benefits:** (1) less overhead, (2) more ILP, (3) enables further optimizations.
- ✅ **Three drawbacks:** (1) larger code, (2) more register pressure, (3) potential I-cache thrashing.
- ✅ **Static unrolling** = compile-time, requires known trip count.
- ✅ **Dynamic unrolling** = run-time via JIT compilers and profile-guided optimization.
- ✅ **Speedup formula:** $S = \dfrac{1}{1 - f + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ (Amdahl-style, where $f$ = overhead fraction).
- ✅ **Interaction with register allocation:** Ensure $L \leq R_{\text{avail}}$; otherwise spills will undo the benefit.
- ✅ **Difference from strength reduction:** Unrolling = body replication; strength reduction = replacing expensive ops (e.g., $4i \to i + i + i + i$).
- ✅ **Difference from loop fusion:** Fusion combines adjacent loops; unrolling replicates a *single* loop's body.
- ✅ **Coined/exemplified in:** The *Dragon Book* (Aho, Sethi, Ullman) and Muchnick's *Advanced Compiler Design*.
- ✅ **Used in:** GCC (`-funroll-loops`), LLVM (`llvm.loop.unroll.*` metadata), Intel ICC, HotSpot JIT.
- ✅ **Most common exam phrasing:** *"Explain loop unrolling with an example"* (7 marks) or *"State advantages and disadvantages"* (3 marks).
- ✅ **Exam-safe analogies:** Bucket/water, batch processing, or amortized overhead — pick one and stick to it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Optimization: Finding Uninitialized Variables with Live Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Global Optimization: Finding Uninitialized Variables with Live Sets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Live Variable Analysis** is a classic **backward data-flow analysis** used in the global optimization phase of a compiler. A variable `v` is said to be **LIVE** at a program point `p` if there exists a path from `p` to a use of `v` along which `v` is **NOT** redefined (i.e., killed).

> [!NOTE]
> **Uninitialized Variable Problem** — A variable `v` is **potentially uninitialized** at program point `p` if there exists at least one path from the **ENTRY** of the program to `p` along which `v` is never assigned a value (defined) before its first use. Live-variable information is the **direct foundation** used to detect this hazardous class of bugs.

### KTU Board Examiner's Working Definition
For exam purposes, write the definition as:

> *"A variable is live at a point if its current value may be read before it is overwritten along some execution path. The live-variable sets derived from a flow graph are used by the compiler's optimization pass to detect variables that might be read while still holding an undefined (uninitialized) value."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are writing a shopping list on a small whiteboard, and your friend erases things randomly. You can only be sure the words on the board **matter** if you know someone is going to read them. Otherwise, you can safely erase them.

Now flip the problem: imagine you are about to use a pen to **read** a word from the board. The word is useful **only if**:
1. Someone wrote it on the board (initialized), **AND**
2. Nobody has erased it since (not killed), **AND**
3. You will actually read it soon (live).

**Live variables** are the words still "useful" on the board. **Uninitialized variables** are the words that you are about to read but **were never written** in the first place. The compiler walks the control-flow graph (CFG) backwards from each `USE` of a variable to figure out which variables are alive and which were forgotten at birth.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook:** *Live = "Will I be used later?"* | *Uninitialized = "Was I ever born?"* — Both are answered by walking paths in the CFG.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in This Analysis

| Metric | Symbol | KTU Standard Value / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of iterations to converge (worst case) | `k` | Height of the lattice ≤ `n+1` for `n` basic blocks |
| Lattice element (subset of `Vars`) | `⊆` | Power set lattice $\mathcal{P}(V)$ |
| Top of lattice | `⊤` | `V` (all variables live) |
| Bottom of lattice | `⊥` | `∅` (no variables live) |
| Meet operator (live analysis) | `∪` | Union (OR-semantics) |
| Meet operator (uninit analysis) | `∩` | Intersection (AND-semantics) |

---

## 1.4 KTU Course Outcome Alignment

| CO ID | Course Outcome | This Topic Addresses |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CO4** | Apply data-flow analysis to perform global optimizations | Direct — central to Module 4 |
| **CO5** | Design compilers using modern optimization techniques | Indirect — foundational abstraction |
| **CO3** | Construct intermediate representations | Indirect — IR is the substrate of analysis |

---

## 1.5 Why This Matters in Real Compilers

- **GCC / LLVM** use live-variable analysis to schedule register allocation.
- Detecting uninitialized variables statically prevents **security CVEs** (e.g., use of uninitialized stack memory leaking kernel data).
- Tools like **Coverity, Clang Static Analyzer, Infer (Facebook)** use exactly this technique to flag bugs at compile-time.
- KTU industry partners (e.g., TCS, Infosys, IBS) routinely test freshers on this concept during compiler/PL interviews.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Data-Flow Analyses Side by Side

The KTU board expects you to know that finding uninitialized variables is **structurally a forward analysis** with a **meet-over-all-paths (MOP) intersection** — the **dual** of live-variable analysis. Mastery of this duality is the single most-tested point in Module 4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Live-variable analysis (backward):** propagates *future use* information from `EXIT` back to `ENTRY`.
>
> **Uninitialized-variable analysis (forward):** propagates *past non-definition* information from `ENTRY` toward `EXIT`.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic for Live-Variable Analysis

For a basic block `B`:

1. **Identify `use[B]`** — the set of variables whose **current value** is read in `B` **before** any redefinition in `B`.
2. **Identify `def[B]`** — the set of variables that are **definitely assigned** a value in `B`.
3. Compute the transfer function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IN[B] &= use[B] \;\cup\; (OUT[B] \;-\; def[B]) \\
OUT[B] &= \bigcup_{S \in succ(B)} IN[S]
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Boundary condition:** $OUT[EXIT] = \emptyset$ (nothing is live after the program ends).
5. **Initialization:** all `IN[B] = OUT[B] = ∅`.
6. **Iterate** until no `IN[B]` changes — the fixed point is reached in $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ for a reducible CFG.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic for Uninitialized-Variable Analysis

This is the analysis that **uses** the live sets to flag the bug. For block `B`:

1. **Identify `gen[B]`** — variables used in `B` **before** they are defined anywhere along *every* incoming path. Intuitively: places where a read happens and the variable may not yet have been initialized.
2. **Identify `kill[B]`** — variables that are **definitely defined** in `B` (i.e., unconditionally assigned).
3. Transfer function (forward):

$$
\begin{aligned}
