- ✅ **Pitfall to avoid in code:** Do not write `if (lookahead == '+' || '-')` — must be separate `if/else if` branches so only one alternative is selected.
- ✅ **End-of-input marker:** Always check for `$` (or `'\0'` in C) at the end of the input to ensure the entire expression was consumed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Build a Basic Calculator that Parses Mathematical Expressions Using YACC/Equivalent, Generates Intermediate Code, and Applies Optimizations to Improve Execution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCRT604 - COMPILER CONSTRUCTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Build a Basic Calculator that Parses Mathematical Expressions Using YACC/Equivalent, Generates Intermediate Code, and Applies Optimizations to Improve Execution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Compiler Construction syllabus**, a *Calculator Compiler* is a miniature front-end-plus-middle-end software pipeline that accepts an arithmetic expression as input (a stream of characters), **lexes** it into tokens using a tool such as **Lex/Flex** (or its equivalent), **parses** the token stream according to a context-free grammar using **YACC/Bison** (or an equivalent parser generator), builds a derivation/parse tree, and then **emits an Intermediate Representation (IR)** — typically **Three-Address Code (TAC)**, **Quadruples**, or **Triples** — upon which **machine-independent optimizations** such as constant folding, constant propagation, strength reduction, copy propagation, and dead-code elimination are applied to produce a leaner IR prior to final code emission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** This topic consolidates the lexical analyzer (Module 2), the syntax analyzer / YACC (Module 3), **Intermediate Code Generation**, and **Code Optimization** (Module 4). It is the **capstone synthesis** of the course and is repeatedly asked in the **University End-Semester Examination (ESE)** as a 14-mark Part-B question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Restaurant Translator"

Imagine a tourist ordering food in a foreign language.

| Phase in the Pipeline | Restaurant Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Lexical Analysis (Lex)** | The waiter **hears** the words and chops them into known vocabulary items (*tokens*) such as "two", "plus", "cheese". |
| **Syntax Analysis (YACC)** | A **head chef** checks whether the words form a valid dish recipe (grammar). If not, an error is thrown. |
| **Semantic / AST Building** | The chef **understands** the meaning — operator precedence, types. |
| **Intermediate Code Generation** | A **standardised recipe card** is written in a kitchen-neutral language (e.g., "mix A with B at 180°C"). |
| **Optimization** | The head chef **redescribes** the recipe in fewer steps ("preheat the oven once, not twice"). |
| **Target Code** | The actual cooking steps in the local kitchen's dialect. |

The "calculator compiler" is just like this: a **kitchen-independent recipe** (the IR) is created from raw words, and then **streamlined** before it hits the stove.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is *High-Yield* for KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> The 2024 scheme (NEP-2020 aligned) emphasises **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. This topic satisfies **CO4** (*"Apply optimization techniques to intermediate code"*) and **CO3** (*"Generate intermediate code for high-level constructs"*). The university examiner *loves* asking trace-based questions such as: *"Generate TAC and apply constant folding for `a = 2 + 3 * 4 - 5`."*

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Operator Precedence Constants (Bison/YACC default):** `LEFT` (1–10), `RIGHT` (1–10), `NONASSOC` (1–10). Lower number = lower precedence.
- **Maximum Three-Address Code (TAC) per instruction:** **3** operands (`x = y op z`).
- **Standard optimisation-pass count** in textbook compilers (Aho/Sethi/Ullman): **2** primary passes (local + global), supplemented by a **peephole** pass.
- **Temporary variable naming convention:** `t1, t2, t3, …` (KTU-conventional; matches Dragon Book examples).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Stage Pipeline — A Bulleted Logical Walkthrough

### Stage 1 — Lexical Analysis (Lex / Flex)
- **Input:** Raw character stream, e.g., `3 + 4 * 2`
- **Output:** Token stream, e.g., `<NUM, 3> <PLUS, +> <NUM, 4> <MUL, *> <NUM, 2>`
- **Rule pattern:** Regular expressions matched longest-match-first.
- **Token definitions** for our calculator:
  - `digit+` → `NUM`
  - `'+'` → `PLUS`
  - `'-'` → `MINUS`
  - `'*'` → `MUL`
  - `'/'` → `DIV`
  - `'('` → `LP`
  - `')'` → `RP`
  - `'\n'` → newline (end of expression)

### Stage 2 — Syntax Analysis (YACC / Bison)
- **Input:** Token stream from Lex.
- **Output:** Parse tree (implicit, via grammar actions).
- **Grammar** (operator precedence version — the most exam-friendly):
  ```
  E : E '+' T   { $$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '+', $3); }
    | E '-' T   { $$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '-', $3); }
    | T
    ;
  T : T '*' F   { $$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '*', $3); }
    | T '/' F   { $$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '/', $3); }
    | F
    ;
  F : '(' E ')' { $$ = $2; }
    | NUM       { $$ = $1; }
    ;
  ```
- **Precedence Declarations:**
  ```
  %left '+' '-'
  %left '*' '/'
  %right UMINUS
  ```
- **Conflict Type Encountered:** *Shift-Reduce Conflict* — resolved by giving `*`/`/` higher precedence than `+`/`-`.

### Stage 3 — Intermediate Code Generation
- **Output:** Three-Address Code (TAC), a sequence of instructions each with **at most 3 operands** and **one operator**.
- **Example TAC for `2 + 3 * 4`:**
  ```
  t1 = 3 * 4
  t2 = 2 + t1
  ```

### Stage 4 — Optimisation
- **Local Optimisation** — within a basic block.
- **Peephole Optimisation** — sliding window of 2–3 TAC instructions.
- **Global Optimisation** — across basic blocks (uses data-flow analysis).

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every KTU-board-tested definition and rule for this topic. **Memorise this verbatim.**

| # | Concept | Formal Statement / Rule | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Three-Address Code Form** | At most 3 operands per statement | `x = y op z` |
| 2 | **Quadruple** | `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` — 4 fields | `Q: (op, A1, A2, R)` |
| 3 | **Triple** | `(op, arg1, arg2)` — result is implicit position | `T: (op, A1, A2)` |
| 4 | **Indirect Triple** | Triple + pointer list for easy reordering | `(op, A1, A2) [ptr]` |
| 5 | **Constant Folding** | Replace `a = 2 + 3` with `a = 5` | Eval at compile time |
| 6 | **Constant Propagation** | Substitute known constants into expressions | Replace uses of `x` by its value |
| 7 | **Strength Reduction** | Replace expensive ops with cheaper ones | `x * 2` → `x << 1` ; `x ^ 2` → `x * x` |
| 8 | **Copy Propagation** | `x = y` followed by use of `x` → use of `y` | Eliminates redundant copies |
| 9 | **Dead-Code Elimination** | Remove assignments whose results are never used | `x = 5; ... x` never read |
| 10 | **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** | `t1 = a + b; t2 = a + b` → reuse `t1` | Requires available-expression analysis |
| 11 | **Algebraic Simplification** | `x + 0 = x`, `x * 1 = x`, `x * 0 = 0` | Identity & annihilator |
| 12 | **Peephole Window Size** | Typically 2–3 instructions at a time | Sliding window |
| 13 | **TAC variable naming** | `t1, t2, t3, ...` (Dragon-Book style) | Sequential temps |
| 14 | **newtemp()** | Returns a fresh temporary name | `t_i` where `i++` |
| 15 | **emit()** | Appends a TAC instruction to the IR list | Append-to-buffer |
| 16 | **Operator Precedence Order (YACC)** | `LOW` < `+,-` < `*,/` < `^` (unary) < `UMINUS` | `%left`, `%right` |
| 17 | **Basic Block** | Maximal sequence of TAC with no branches except at the end | Leader-based partition |
| 18 | **Leader Definition** | (i) First instruction ; (ii) target of branch ; (iii) instruction after branch | Algorithm 8.4 (Dragon Book) |
| 19 | **Local vs Global Opt.** | Local = within BB ; Global = across BBs | Different algorithms |
| 20 | **Optimisation Safety** | Must preserve program semantics (same observable behaviour) | Equivalence-preserving |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Equivalence Rule** (KTU favourite): *Optimisation is a semantic-preserving transformation*. Any optimisation that changes the meaning of the program is **incorrect** and a guaranteed mark-loser.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GCC and LLVM:** Use **SSA-form** intermediate representation (Static Single Assignment) and apply the same fundamental optimisations (GVN, LICM, strength reduction, inlining).
- **V8 (Chrome) & SpiderMonkey (Firefox):** Use *inline caches* and *constant folding* at the IR level.
- **Embedded Systems:** Strength reduction and dead-code elimination are critical for fitting code into limited Flash/ROM.
- **GPU Shaders:** Constant folding reduces the GPU workload dramatically.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete YACC Source for a Calculator with TAC Emission

Below is the full, exam-ready YACC specification. **Memorise the structure**, not every character.

### 3.1.1 The Lex File (`calc.l`)

```
%{
#include "y.tab.h"
#include <stdio.h>
%}

digit   [0-9]+

%%
{digit}        { yylval = atoi(yytext); return NUM; }
"+"             { return PLUS; }
"-"             { return MINUS; }
"*"             { return MUL; }
"/"             { return DIV; }
"("             { return LP; }
")"             { return RP; }
"\n"            { return NL; }
[ \t]           { /* ignore whitespace */ }
.               { return yytext[0]; }

%%
int yywrap() { return 1; }
```

### 3.1.2 The YACC File (`calc.y`)

```
%{
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
int yylex();
int yyerror(const char *s);
%}

%token NUM
%token PLUS MINUS MUL DIV LP RP NL

%left PLUS MINUS
%left MUL DIV
%right UMINUS

%%
S : S E NL       { printf("= %d\n", $2); }
  | NL
  ;

E : E PLUS T     { $$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '+', $3); }
  | E MINUS T    { $$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '-', $3); }
  | T            { $$ = $1; }
  ;

T : T MUL F      { $$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '*', $3); }
  | T DIV F      { $$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '/', $3); }
  | F            { $$ = $1; }
  ;

F : LP E RP      { $$ = $2; }
  | NUM          { $$ = $1; }
  | MINUS F %prec UMINUS   { $$ = newtemp(); emit($$, 0, '-', $2); }
  ;

%%

int newtemp() {
    static int c = 1;
    char buf[10];
    sprintf(buf, "t%d", c++);
    return install(buf);
}

void emit(int res, int a1, char op, int a2) {
    printf("t_res = t_a1 op t_a2\n");
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is the *canonical skeleton*. The actual `emit()` in a real implementation would print a `t1 = t2 + t3` TAC line. KTU examiners accept the abstract `t_res = t_a1 op t_a2` notation provided the **semantic action logic** is correct.

### 3.1.3 Compile & Run

```
$ lex calc.l
$ yacc -d calc.y
$ gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o calc -ll
$ ./calc
2 + 3 * 4
```

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — TAC Generation Trace (Unoptimised)

**Input Expression:** `2 + 3 * 4 - 5`

**Step 1 — Parse the expression.** The grammar forces `*` to bind tighter than `+`/`-`, so the parse tree is:

```
        (-)
       /   \
     (+)    5
    /   \
   2    (*)
       /   \
      3     4
```

**Step 2 — Walk the tree bottom-up, emitting TAC at each internal node.**

At node `(*)`:
```
t1 = 3 * 4
```
At node `(+)`, the left child is the literal `2` (treated as a value), right child is `t1`:
```
t2 = 2 + t1
```
At the root `(-)`:
```
t3 = t2 - 5
```

**Final Unoptimised TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 3 * 4 \\
t_2 &= 2 + t_1 \\
t_3 &= t_2 - 5
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Applying Optimisations

We now take the unoptimised TAC from §3.2 and apply **each optimisation explicitly**.

### 3.3.1 Pass 1 — Constant Folding

We evaluate the **RHS of every TAC whose operands are all literals**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 3 * 4 \;\xrightarrow{\text{fold}}\; t_1 = 12 \\
t_2 &= 2 + t_1 \;\xrightarrow{\text{fold}}\; t_2 = 2 + 12 \;\xrightarrow{\text{fold}}\; t_2 = 14 \\
t_3 &= t_2 - 5 \;\xrightarrow{\text{fold}}\; t_3 = 14 - 5 \;\xrightarrow{\text{fold}}\; t_3 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**After Constant Folding (compact form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 12 \\
t_2 &= 14 \\
t_3 &= 9
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3.2 Pass 2 — Copy Propagation / Substitution

Since each temp is used exactly once, we can **substitute** the literal value at the use site:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_3 &= 9
\end{aligned}
$$

` t1 ` and ` t2 ` are now dead — they have no further uses.

### 3.3.3 Pass 3 — Dead-Code Elimination

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 12 \quad &\text{[dead, removed]} \\
t_2 &= 14 \quad &\text{[dead, removed]} \\
t_3 &= 9 \quad &\text{[kept, final result]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fully Optimised TAC:**

$$
t_3 = 9
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single instruction `t3 = 9` is **semantically equivalent** to the original 3-instruction TAC for this particular input. This is the essence of compiler optimisation: *less work, same answer*.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Quadruple & Triple Representation

For the unoptimised TAC of §3.2:

**Quadruples** (`(op, arg1, arg2, result)`):

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | 3 | 4 | t1 |
| (1) | `+` | 2 | t1 | t2 |
| (2) | `-` | t2 | 5 | t3 |

**Triples** (`(op, arg1, arg2)` — result = position):

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | 3 | 4 |          ← result is (0)
| (1) | `+` | 2 | (0) |        ← arg2 references triple (0)
| (2) | `-` | (1) | 5 |        ← arg1 references triple (1)

> [!NOTE]
> **Triples save space** by avoiding the `result` field, but they make **re-ordering harder** because positions shift. **Indirect Triples** fix this by adding an indirection pointer list.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Strength Reduction & Algebraic Simplification

**Input TAC (unoptimised):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= x * 8 \\
t_2 &= t_1 + 0 \\
t_3 &= a + a
\end{aligned}
$$

**Apply Strength Reduction (multiply-by-power-of-2 → shift):**

$$
t_1 = x \ll 3
$$

**Apply Algebraic Simplification (`+0`, `a+a`):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_2 &= t_1 \quad &\text{(}x + 0 = x\text{)} \\
t_3 &= a * 2 \quad &\text{(}a + a = 2 \cdot a\text{, then strength-reduce)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Optimised TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= x \ll 3 \\
t_2 &= t_1 \\
t_3 &= a \ll 1
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Peephole Optimisation (Sliding Window)

**Source TAC (3 instructions):**
```
MOV a, R1
MOV R1, b
ADD R1, c
```

**Sliding window of 2 instructions (positions 0–1):**
- `MOV a, R1` followed by `MOV R1, b` → redundant copy → can be replaced by `MOV a, b` (peephole pattern: *redundant MOV elimination*).

**Resulting TAC:**
```
MOV a, b
ADD b, c
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **peephole** is a *sliding window of 2 or 3 instructions* on the *emitted* (post-IR) code. It is the *last* optimisation pass and is local in nature.

## 3.7 Comprehensive Mapping — From Grammar Rule to TAC Emission

The following **derivation table** is the KTU-board master template:

| Grammar Production | Semantic Action | TAC Emitted |
|---|---|---|
| `E → E + T` | `$$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '+', $3);` | `$$ = $1 + $3` |
| `E → E - T` | `$$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '-', $3);` | `$$ = $1 - $3` |
| `E → T` | `$$ = $1;` | (no code) |
| `T → T * F` | `$$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '*', $3);` | `$$ = $1 * $3` |
| `T → T / F` | `$$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '/', $3);` | `$$ = $1 / $3` |
| `T → F` | `$$ = $1;` | (no code) |
| `F → ( E )` | `$$ = $2;` | (no code) |
| `F → NUM` | `$$ = $1;` | (no code) |
| `F → - F` (`%prec UMINUS`) | `$$ = newtemp(); emit($$, 0, '-', $2);` | `$$ = -$2` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (KTU Board):** Forgetting to assign `$$ = $1` for *epsilon-like* productions (`E → T`, `T → F`, `F → (E)`). This causes a **NULL pointer** semantic error and loses 2–3 marks per instance.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Expression\n2 + 3 * 4 - 5] --> B[Lexical Analyzer\nFlex or Equivalent]
    B --> C[Token Stream\nNUM PLUS NUM MUL NUM MINUS NUM]
    C --> D[Syntax Analyzer\nYACC or Bison]
    D --> E{Grammar Match}
    E -- Yes --> F[Parse Tree Built]
    E -- No --> G[Syntax Error\nReport to User]
    F --> H[Semantic Actions\nEmit TAC]
    H --> I[Intermediate Code\nThree Address Code]
    I --> J[Optimization Pass 1\nConstant Folding]
    J --> K[Optimization Pass 2\nStrength Reduction]
    K --> L[Optimization Pass 3\nDead Code Elimination]
    L --> M[Optimized TAC]
    M --> N[Final Code Generator\nMachine Code]
    M --> O[Peephole Pass\nSliding Window 2-3]
    O --> N
```

```mermaid
graph TD

    leftE1[E Plus Node\nE3 + E4]
    leaf2[Literal 2]

    leaf3[Literal 3]
    leaf4[Literal 4]
    leaf5[Literal 5]

    root --> leftE1
    root --> leaf5

    leftE1 --> leftE2
    leftE2 --> leaf3

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Visit Leaf 2\nNo TAC]

    D[Visit Multiply Node\nEmit t1 = 3 * 4]
    E[Visit Add Node\nEmit t2 = 2 + t1]
    F[Visit Leaf 5\nNo TAC]
    G[Visit Root Minus\nEmit t3 = t2 - 5]

    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> G

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        U1[t1 = 3 * 4]

        F3[t3 = 9]
    end

    subgraph CopyPass[Pass 2 Copy Propagation]
        C1[t3 = 9]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define **Three-Address Code (TAC)**. List any **two** forms of representing TAC.

**Model Answer:**

Three-Address Code (TAC) is an intermediate representation in which each instruction contains **at most three addresses (operands)** — typically in the form `x = y op z`, where `x`, `y`, `z` are variables, constants, or temporaries, and `op` is a binary operator. It is widely used because it is **machine-independent**, easy to optimise, and a clean precursor to final code generation.

**Two forms of TAC representation:**

1. **Quadruples** — A 4-field record `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`. Easy to rearrange but requires a temp-name table.
2. **Triples** — A 3-field record `(op, arg1, arg2)` where the *result* is implicit (the position of the triple itself). Saves space but is hard to re-order.
3. **Indirect Triples** — A list of pointers to triples; allows re-ordering without changing the triples themselves.

*(Any two of the above — each with a one-line description — fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is **Constant Folding**? Give a one-line example.

**Model Answer:**

**Constant Folding** is a *compile-time* local optimisation in which an expression consisting entirely of **literal constants** is **evaluated at compile time** and replaced by its computed value, eliminating the need to perform the operation at runtime.

**Example:**
- Source: `x = 2 + 3 * 4;`
- After Constant Folding: `x = 14;`

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Choice Option A

**Q:** Design a YACC specification for a simple calculator that accepts integer arithmetic expressions involving `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, and parentheses, and generates **Three-Address Code** as intermediate code. Then generate the TAC for the expression:

$$
(2 + 3) \times 4 - 5 / (6 - 1)
$$

Also apply **constant folding** and **dead-code elimination** to the generated TAC and show the final optimised code.

#### Part (a) — YACC Specification & TAC Generation (7 Marks)

**YACC Specification (calc.y):**

```
%{
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
int yylex();
int yyerror(const char *s);
int tempCount = 0;
%}

%token NUM
%left '+' '-'
%left '*' '/'
%right UMINUS

%%
E : E '+' T    { char *t = newtemp(); printf("%s = %s + %s\n", t, $1, $3); $$ = t; }
  | E '-' T    { char *t = newtemp(); printf("%s = %s - %s\n", t, $1, $3); $$ = t; }
  | T          { $$ = $1; }
  ;
T : T '*' F    { char *t = newtemp(); printf("%s = %s * %s\n", t, $1, $3); $$ = t; }
  | T '/' F    { char *t = newtemp(); printf("%s = %s / %s\n", t, $1, $3); $$ = t; }
  | F          { $$ = $1; }
  ;
F : '(' E ')'  { $$ = $2; }
  | NUM        { char *t = newtemp(); printf("%s = %d\n", t, $1); $$ = t; }
  ;
%%

char *newtemp() {
    char *buf = malloc(10);
    sprintf(buf, "t%d", ++tempCount);
    return buf;
}
int yyerror(const char *s) { printf("Error: %s\n", s); return 0; }
```

**Step-by-Step Parse Tree Traversal (Post-Order):**

The expression `(2+3)*4 - 5/(6-1)` parses as:

```
              SUB
             /   \
          MUL     DIV
         /   \   /  \
       ADD   4  5   SUB
      /   \         /   \
     2     3       6     1
```

**TAC Generation (bottom-up, post-order):**

1. Visit leaf `2` → `t1 = 2`
2. Visit leaf `3` → `t2 = 3`
3. Visit `ADD` node → `t3 = t1 + t2`
4. Visit leaf `4` → `t4 = 4`
5. Visit `MUL` node → `t5 = t3 * t4`
6. Visit leaf `5` → `t6 = 5`
7. Visit leaf `6` → `t7 = 6`
8. Visit leaf `1` → `t8 = 1`
9. Visit `SUB` node (6−1) → `t9 = t7 - t8`
10. Visit `DIV` node → `t10 = t6 / t9`
11. Visit root `SUB` → `t11 = t5 - t10`

**Unoptimised TAC Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 2 \\
t_2 &= 3 \\
t_3 &= t_1 + t_2 \\
t_4 &= 4 \\
t_5 &= t_3 * t_4 \\
t_6 &= 5 \\
t_7 &= 6 \\
t_8 &= 1 \\
t_9 &= t_7 - t_8 \\
t_{10} &= t_6 / t_9 \\
t_{11} &= t_5 - t_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Apply Optimisations (7 Marks)

**Pass 1 — Constant Folding:**

Fold every literal-only RHS:

- `t1 = 2` (already constant)
- `t2 = 3` (already constant)
- `t3 = 2 + 3 = 5`
- `t4 = 4` (already constant)
- `t5 = 5 * 4 = 20`
- `t6 = 5` (already constant)
- `t7 = 6` (already constant)
- `t8 = 1` (already constant)
- `t9 = 6 - 1 = 5`
- `t10 = 5 / 5 = 1`
- `t11 = 20 - 1 = 19`

**TAC After Constant Folding:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= 2 \\
t_2 &= 3 \\
t_3 &= 5 \\
t_4 &= 4 \\
t_5 &= 20 \\
t_6 &= 5 \\
t_7 &= 6 \\
t_8 &= 1 \\
t_9 &= 5 \\
t_{10} &= 1 \\
t_{11} &= 19
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pass 2 — Dead-Code Elimination (DCE):**

We trace the *use* of each temp. The **only live variable** is `t11` (the final result). All other temps are written but never read.

Removing dead assignments:

**Final Optimised TAC:**

$$
t_{11} = 19
$$

**Verification:**
- Original: `(2+3) * 4 - 5/(6-1) = 5*4 - 5/5 = 20 - 1 = 19` ✓
- Optimised result: `19` ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting parenthesised sub-expressions:** The expression `(2+3)*4` is *not* `2+(3*4)`. Always draw the parse tree first.
> 2. **Confusing Constant Folding with Constant Propagation:** Folding evaluates *literal-only* expressions; propagation substitutes *named* constants.
> 3. **Skipping the post-order visit:** The TAC must be emitted in the order the actions fire (post-order), not in any arbitrary order.
> 4. **DCE pitfall:** A variable is *dead* only if it has **no subsequent use**. Don't blindly delete assignments.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Choice Option B

**Q:** With a neat diagram, explain the **phases of a compiler** involved in building a calculator for mathematical expressions. Generate the **Three-Address Code** and **Quadruple representation** for the expression:

$$
a \times b + c - d / e
$$

Apply **strength reduction** and **algebraic simplification** to the generated TAC where applicable.

#### Part (a) — Compiler Phases & TAC Generation (7 Marks)

**Compiler Phases (with calculator analogy):**

| Phase | Function | Calculator Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Lexical Analysis** | Groups characters into tokens | `a*b+c` → `ID MUL ID PLUS ID` |
| 2. **Syntax Analysis** | Checks grammar, builds parse tree | Validates `E → E+T` etc. |
| 3. **Semantic Analysis** | Type checking, scope | Ensures operands are numeric |
| 4. **Intermediate Code Gen** | Emits IR (TAC) | `t1 = a*b ; t2 = t1+c` |
| 5. **Code Optimisation** | IR → improved IR | Constant fold, strength reduce |
| 6. **Code Generation** | IR → target machine code | Emit MOV/ADD/... |
| 7. **Symbol Table** | Tracks identifiers | Stores `a,b,c,d,e` |
| 8. **Error Handler** | Reports & recovers | Bad token, grammar violation |

**Parse Tree for `a*b + c - d/e`:**

```
          (-)
         /   \
       (+)    (/)
      /   \   / \
    (*)    c d   e
   /   \
  a     b
```

**Post-Order TAC Generation:**

1. Visit `a` (leaf) → load as operand
2. Visit `b` (leaf) → load as operand
3. Visit `(*)` → `t1 = a * b`
4. Visit `c` (leaf) → load as operand
5. Visit `(+)` → `t2 = t1 + c`
6. Visit `d` (leaf) → load as operand
7. Visit `e` (leaf) → load as operand
8. Visit `(/)` → `t3 = d / e`
9. Visit root `(-)` → `t4 = t2 - t3`

**Three-Address Code (TAC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a * b \\
t_2 &= t_1 + c \\
t_3 &= d / e \\
t_4 &= t_2 - t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Quadruple Representation:**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | a | b | t1 |
| (1) | `+` | t1 | c | t2 |
| (2) | `/` | d | e | t3 |
| (3) | `-` | t2 | t3 | t4 |

#### Part (b) — Apply Strength Reduction & Algebraic Simplification (7 Marks)

**Strength Reduction Rules (applicable here):**

- Multiplication/Division by a power of 2 → shift.
- Multiplication by 1 → identity.
- Addition of 0 → identity.
- Multiplication by 0 → zero.

Since the operands `a, b, c, d, e` are **runtime variables** (not compile-time constants), direct constant folding is **not** possible. We can, however, apply **structural strength reduction** if the operands match certain patterns.

**Original TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a * b \\
t_2 &= t_1 + c \\
t_3 &= d / e \\
t_4 &= t_2 - t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assume the following cases** (this is a classic KTU sub-question structure):

- **Case 1:** If `b = 1` (a known constant) → `t1 = a * 1 = a` (algebraic identity).
- **Case 2:** If `e = 2` (a known constant) → `t3 = d / 2` can be replaced with `t3 = d >> 1` (strength reduction: division by power-of-2 → shift).
- **Case 3:** If `c = 0` → `t2 = t1 + 0 = t1` (algebraic identity).

**Optimised TAC (after applying all three algebraic identities and strength reduction):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a \\
t_2 &= a \\
t_3 &= d \gg 1 \\
t_4 &= a - t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Dead-Code Cleanup:**
- `t1` and `t2` are now identical. `t1` is never used after `t2`'s definition → `t1` is dead.

**Final Clean Optimised TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_2 &= a \\
t_3 &= d \gg 1 \\
t_4 &= a - t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equivalence Check:**
- Original: `a·1 + 0 - d/2 = a - d/2` ✓
- Optimised: `a - (d>>1)` ✓ (semantically equivalent for non-negative `d`)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Strength Reduction is conditional:** Do not blindly replace `*` with `<<` unless the multiplier is a **power of 2 known at compile time**.
> 2. **Algebraic identity caveats:** `x*0 = 0` is unsafe if `x` has **side effects** (e.g., function call). In pure arithmetic IR, it is safe.
> 3. **Quadruple column order:** Some KTU answer keys use the order `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`, others `(result, op, arg1, arg2)`. **Stick to the order used in class notes** to avoid mismatched valuation.
> 4. **Don't forget the symbol table:** Even if the question does not ask, mentioning it fetches an extra mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist. It is the densest possible summary of this topic for the night-before-the-exam revision.**

### Tools & Pipeline
- [ ] **Lex/Flex** → produces `lex.yy.c` (tokenizer)
- [ ] **YACC/Bison** → produces `y.tab.c` and `y.tab.h` (parser)
- [ ] Compile both with `gcc lex.yy.c y.tab.c -o calc -ll`
- [ ] The pipeline: **Lex → Parse → AST → TAC → Optimise → Target Code**

### Grammar Essentials
- [ ] `%left`, `%right`, `%nonassoc` for precedence
- [ ] Lower-declared operator = **lower** precedence
- [ ] Resolve shift-reduce conflicts using precedence + associativity
- [ ] `E → E + T / E → T / T → T * F / T → F / F → (E) / F → NUM` is the **canonical** calc grammar
- [ ] Unary minus → use `%prec UMINUS` trick

### Semantic Actions (TAC Emission)
- [ ] `$$ = newtemp(); emit($$, $1, '+', $3);` for `E → E + T`
- [ ] `$$ = $1;` for *chain-breaking* productions (`E → T`, `T → F`, `F → (E)`)
- [ ] `newtemp()` returns `t1, t2, t3, …`

### Three-Address Code Forms
- [ ] **TAC instruction:** `x = y op z`
- [ ] **Quadruple:** `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` — 4 fields
- [ ] **Triple:** `(op, arg1, arg2)` — 3 fields, result is implicit position
- [ ] **Indirect Triple:** triples + pointer list for reorderability

### Optimisations (Memorise Each One)
- [ ] **Constant Folding:** Eval constant expressions at compile time. `2+3*4` → `14`
- [ ] **Constant Propagation:** Substitute known-constant names. `x=5; y=x*2` → `y=10`
- [ ] **Copy Propagation:** Eliminate redundant copies. `x=y; use(x)` → `use(y)`
- [ ] **Strength Reduction:** Replace expensive ops with cheaper ones. `x*2` → `x<<1`, `x/4` → `x>>2`
- [ ] **Algebraic Simplification:** `x+0=x`, `x*1=x`, `x*0=0`, `x-x=0`
- [ ] **Dead-Code Elimination:** Remove assignments whose results are unused
- [ ] **Common Subexpression Elimination:** Reuse computed expressions
- [ ] **Peephole:** Sliding 2–3 instruction window on emitted code
- [ ] **Local vs Global:** Local = within a basic block; Global = across basic blocks (data-flow)

### Basic Block Definition
- [ ] A **leader** is: (i) the first instruction, (ii) the target of a branch, (iii) the instruction following a branch
- [ ] A **basic block** is a maximal sequence of instructions starting at a leader and ending just before the next leader

### Key Safety Theorem
- [ ] **Optimisation must preserve program semantics.** Any change in observable behaviour = wrong answer.

### Common Exam Pitfalls
- [ ] Forgetting `$$ = $1` in chain rules → NULL pointer error
- [ ] Confusing fold vs propagation (fold = literals only; propagation = named variables)
- [ ] Wrong order of quadruple columns
- [ ] Skipping the parse tree before TAC generation
- [ ] Applying strength reduction without checking if multiplier is a power of 2
- [ ] Treating `t1 = t1` as dead code (it is not dead, it is a no-op — but `x = 5; x = 7` makes the first `x = 5` dead)

### Bonus Higher-Marks Material
- [ ] **SSA form** (Static Single Assignment) — every variable assigned exactly once
- [ ] **Data-flow equations** for available expressions, live variables
- [ ] **GVN** (Global Value Numbering) — advanced CSE
- [ ] **LICM** (Loop-Invariant Code Motion) — global opt for loops
